topics.title: Oil exploration and drilling; storage and transportation of crude oil and products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Oil exploration and drilling; storage and transportation of crude oil and products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Oil Exploration — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Oil exploration is the systematic search for hydrocarbon accumulations (crude oil and natural gas) beneath the Earth's surface using a sequence of geological, geophysical, and geochemical reconnaissance methods, followed by exploratory drilling to confirm the presence of a commercially viable petroleum reservoir.

The end goal of exploration is to identify a **trap** — a subsurface geological configuration where porous reservoir rock is sealed by an impermeable cap rock, allowing hydrocarbons to accumulate rather than escape to the surface.

**Standard Metrics & Classifications Used in Exploration:**

- **Wildcat Well**: An exploratory well drilled in an unproven area where no existing hydrocarbon production exists. Success rate historically hovers around **1 in 10** (≈ 10%).
- **Appraisal Well**: Drilled after a discovery to determine the areal extent, thickness, and productivity of the reservoir.
- **Development Well**: Drilled within a proven field to produce hydrocarbons commercially.
- **Onshore / Offshore**: Exploration may be conducted on land (using land rigs) or in marine environments (using jack-up rigs, semi-submersibles, or drillships).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Exploration Workflow Order:**
> Geological Survey → Geophysical Survey (Seismic) → Exploratory Drilling → Well Logging → Reservoir Evaluation → Field Development.

---

### 1.2 Drilling — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> Drilling is the engineering operation of penetrating the Earth's crust using a rotating bit attached to a drill string, circulating drilling fluid (mud) to remove cuttings, stabilize the wellbore, and maintain pressure control, in order to access hydrocarbon-bearing formations.

The complete drilling apparatus is called the **drilling rig**, which integrates hoisting, rotary, and circulating subsystems.

**Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in Drilling:**

- **ROP (Rate of Penetration)**: Expressed in meters per hour (m/hr) or feet per hour (ft/hr).
- **WOB (Weight on Bit)**: Typically **10,000 – 40,000 lbs** depending on formation hardness.
- **RPM (Rotary Speed)**: Drill string rotation, typically **60 – 200 RPM**.
- **Mud Weight**: Expressed in **pounds per gallon (ppg)** or **specific gravity (SG)**. Standard range: **8.5 – 14 ppg**.

---

### 1.3 Storage of Crude Oil and Products — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> Storage refers to the engineered containment of crude oil, intermediate refinery streams, and finished petroleum products in atmospheric or pressurized vessels to provide operational buffer capacity between upstream production, refining, and downstream distribution.

---

### 1.4 Transportation of Crude Oil and Products — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> Transportation is the movement of crude oil from wellhead to refinery (upstream logistics) and of refined products from refinery to end consumer (downstream logistics) using pipelines, marine tankers, rail tank cars, road tank trucks, or a combination thereof (intermodal).

---

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of the petroleum value chain like a **water supply system for a city**:

| Petroleum Stage | City Water Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Exploration | Surveying mountains for a freshwater spring | Finding the resource |
| Drilling | Digging the deep well & installing the pump | Extracting the resource |
| Storage | Building a water reservoir / overhead tank | Buffering supply |
| Pipeline transport | Underground water pipelines | Continuous bulk movement |
| Tanker transport | Water tanker trucks for remote areas | Flexible, long-distance delivery |
| Refining | Water treatment plant | Converting raw to usable form |

> **In Plain English:** First, geologists act like *detectives* using satellite images, gravity meters, and sound waves to find where oil might be hiding underground. Then, drilling engineers use a giant rotating drill bit — like a power drill you use at home, scaled up to thousands of horsepower — to bore a hole kilometers deep. Once oil reaches the surface, it cannot be refined instantly, so it is stored in huge steel tanks (cylindrical, with a dome roof — these are sometimes larger than a football field). Finally, the crude oil is shipped to refineries via pipelines (long underground/overground tubes) or giant ships called **VLCCs (Very Large Crude Carriers)**, which can carry **2 million barrels** of crude oil across oceans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fun Fact for Memory Retention:** A single **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** is roughly **330 m long** (longer than 3 football fields) and can carry about **2 × 10⁶ barrels** of crude oil. The largest storage tanks (floating roof type) can hold up to **1.2 million barrels** in a single unit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Methods of Oil Exploration — Structured Logic

#### A. Geological Methods (Surface & Subsurface Studies)

These are the **cheapest and least invasive** exploration methods. They rely on direct observation of surface rock outcrops and inference about subsurface structures.

1. **Surface Geology**: Mapping visible rock formations at the Earth's surface to deduce anticlines, synclines, faults, and unconformities.
2. **Subsurface Geology**: Studying rock samples from existing wells, mines, and exposed canyon walls to build stratigraphic columns.
3. **Structural Analysis**: Identifying potential **anticlinal traps** — dome-shaped folds where buoyant hydrocarbons migrate upward and become trapped beneath an impermeable seal.

> **Why it works:** Hydrocarbons, being **less dense** than formation water, migrate upward through permeable rock until they hit a non-porous cap rock. Anticlines are the most common trap geometry in petroleum geology.

#### B. Geophysical Methods

These use indirect measurements of physical properties to "see" underground structures without drilling.

1. **Seismic Survey (Most Important)**:
   - Principle: Send acoustic waves (shock waves) into the ground using explosives (onshore) or air guns (offshore).
   - Waves reflect off subsurface rock boundaries and return to surface **geophones** (onshore) or **hydrophones** (offshore).
   - **Two-Way Travel Time (TWTT)** is recorded. From this, depth is computed using the velocity model:

$$
d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

   where $d$ = depth to reflector, $v$ = seismic velocity of the layer (m/s), $t$ = two-way travel time (s).

2. **Gravity Survey**: Measures minute variations in Earth's gravitational field caused by denser rock bodies (salt domes often indicate hydrocarbon traps).
3. **Magnetic Survey**: Detects magnetic anomalies from igneous/metamorphic basement rocks; used mainly for regional reconnaissance.
4. **Electrical / Electromagnetic Survey**: Measures resistivity of subsurface formations. Hydrocarbon-bearing rocks have **high resistivity** compared to water-saturated rocks.

#### C. Geochemical Methods

- **Surface Geochemistry**: Detects micro-seeps of light hydrocarbons that escape from subsurface reservoirs and migrate to the surface. Soil and water samples are analyzed.
- **Kerogen Analysis**: Source rock evaluation using **Pyrolysis** (e.g., Rock-Eval) to determine Total Organic Carbon (TOC), hydrogen index (HI), and $T_{max}$.

---

### 2.2 Drilling Operations — Structured Logic

#### A. Types of Drilling Rigs

| Rig Type | Application | Water Depth |
|---|---|---|
| **Land Rig** | Onshore drilling | N/A |
| **Jack-up Rig** | Shallow offshore | Up to ~150 m |
| **Semi-submersible** | Deep offshore | 150 – 3000 m |
| **Drillship** | Ultra-deep offshore / exploration | Up to 4000 m |

#### B. Major Rig Components

1. **Hoisting System**: Crown block, traveling block, hook, and drilling line. Lifts the drill string.
2. **Rotary System**: Kelly, rotary table (or top drive), and drill string. Imparts rotation to the bit.
3. **Circulating System**: Mud pumps, mud pits, shale shakers, desanders, desilters. Circulates drilling mud.
4. **Power System**: Diesel engines or electric motors driving the rig.
5. **Blowout Preventer (BOP)**: Critical safety device — a stack of high-pressure valves installed on the wellhead to prevent uncontrolled flow ("blowout").

#### C. Drilling Fluid (Mud) Functions

- Clean the bit and transport cuttings to surface.
- Cool and lubricate the bit.
- Balance formation pressure (hydrostatic head).
- Stabilize the wellbore wall.
- Prevent formation damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Drilling Mud (KTU must-know):**
> - **Water-Based Mud (WBM)**: Cheapest, environmentally acceptable.
> - **Oil-Based Mud (OBM)**: Better lubricity, used in deviated wells and shale sections.
> - **Synthetic-Based Mud (SBM)**: Hybrid, environmentally safer than OBM but with similar performance.

#### D. Drilling Methods

- **Rotary Drilling** (standard): The dominant method. A rotating bit grinds the rock.
- **Directional Drilling**: The wellbore is intentionally deviated from vertical to reach targets offset from the surface location, or to access multiple reservoir zones from one platform (e.g., **horizontal drilling** in shale plays).
- **Offshore Specialized Methods**: Includes managed pressure drilling (MPD) and underbalanced drilling (UBD) for depleted reservoirs.

---

### 2.3 Storage of Crude Oil and Products

#### A. Types of Storage Tanks

| Tank Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Roof (Cone/Dome)** | Rigid top, atmospheric pressure | Low-vapor, non-volatile products (diesel, fuel oil) |
| **Floating Roof** | Roof floats on liquid surface | Crude oil, gasoline — minimizes evaporative losses |
| **LNG Spherical (Horton Sphere)** | Pressurized sphere for cryogenic gases | LPG, butane storage |
| **Bullet Tank** | Horizontal cylindrical, pressurized | Smaller LPG volumes |
| **Underground Cavern** | Salt dome or rock cavern | Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) |

> **Why Floating Roof?** Volatile products like crude oil and gasoline emit VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds). A floating roof moves up and down with the liquid level, eliminating the vapor space and drastically reducing evaporative losses and fire risk.

#### B. Tank Sizing Calculation

The working volume of a cylindrical tank is given by:

$$
V = \pi r^2 h
$$

where $r$ = tank radius, $h$ = liquid height.

For a horizontal cylindrical tank (bullet type):

$$
V = L \left[ r^2 \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{r - h}{r}\right) - (r - h)\sqrt{2rh - h^2} \right]
$$

where $L$ = tank length, $h$ = liquid depth (measured from bottom).

#### C. Storage Losses

- **Breathing Losses**: Daily thermal expansion/contraction of vapor space.
- **Working Losses**: Filling and emptying operations displacing vapor.

---

### 2.4 Transportation of Crude Oil and Products

#### A. Modes of Transportation

| Mode | Capacity | Range | Cost per barrel-mile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline** | Very high (millions of bbl/day) | Continental | **Lowest** |
| **Marine Tanker (VLCC)** | 1.5 – 2.2 million bbl | Oceanic | Lowest for long distance |
| **Rail Tank Car** | ~30,000 gal/car | Continental | Moderate |
| **Road Tank Truck** | ~5,000 – 10,000 gal | Short haul (<500 km) | Highest |
| **Barge (Inland Waterway)** | 10,000 – 30,000 bbl | Rivers/canals | Low (regional) |

#### B. Pipeline Hydraulics — Fundamental Equation

The pressure drop in a pipeline for steady-state, incompressible flow is given by the **Darcy-Weisbach Equation**:

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

where:

- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless)
- $L$ = pipe length (m)
- $D$ = pipe diameter (m)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = average flow velocity (m/s)

For laminar flow ($Re < 2000$), $f = 64 / Re$, where $Re = \rho v D / \mu$.

#### C. Key Pipeline Design Considerations

1. **Pumping Stations**: Located every **50 – 100 km** along crude pipelines to boost pressure.
2. **Batch Tracking**: In multiproduct pipelines, different products are pumped sequentially with **gel pigs** or **foam pigs** separating them.
3. **SCADA Systems**: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition monitors flow, pressure, and temperature in real time.
4. **Heating**: Waxy crude oils (e.g., those from certain offshore fields) require **insulated and heated pipelines** to maintain fluidity.

#### D. Marine Tanker Classification

- **Aframax**: 80,000 – 120,000 DWT (Deadweight Tonnage)
- **Suezmax**: 120,000 – 200,000 DWT (sized for Suez Canal)
- **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)**: 200,000 – 320,000 DWT
- **ULCC (Ultra Large Crude Carrier)**: > 320,000 DWT

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seismic depth | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$=depth, $v$=velocity, $t$=TWTT | m, m/s, s |
| 2 | Cylindrical tank volume | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $r$=radius, $h$=height | m³ |
| 3 | Darcy-Weisbach pressure drop | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | All fluid/pipe properties | Pa |
| 4 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $\mu$=dynamic viscosity | dimensionless |
| 5 | Laminar friction factor | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 6 | Buoyancy of oil | $\rho_{oil} < \rho_{water}$ | typical: 800 – 950 kg/m³ | kg/m³ |
| 7 | Mud hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho_{mud} \cdot g \cdot h$ | $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Pa |
| 8 | Pipeline flow rate (volumetric) | $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$ | $A$=cross-section | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - The **Darcy-Weisbach equation** is the backbone of every pipeline design in the petroleum industry — from the 1,800-km Keystone Pipeline to short refinery feeder lines.
> - **Seismic depth formula** underpins modern 3D and 4D seismic interpretation, which has revolutionized offshore exploration success rates to **~40 – 60%** in proven basins.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Mud Pressure Required to Balance Formation Pressure

**Problem Context:** While drilling, the mud column must exert a hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the hole equal to or slightly greater than the pore pressure of the formation, otherwise a kick or blowout can occur.

**Given:** Vertical depth $D = 3000$ m, formation pore pressure gradient $g_p = 10.5$ kPa/m, mud density to be selected.

**Find:** Minimum mud specific gravity.

**Step 1:** State the pressure-balance condition.

For a static mud column, the hydrostatic pressure at depth $D$ is:

$$
P_{mud} = \rho_{mud} \cdot g \cdot D
$$

For well control, this must equal or exceed the formation pore pressure:

$$
\rho_{mud} \cdot g \cdot D \geq P_{pore}
$$

**Step 2:** Express formation pore pressure in terms of its gradient.

$$
P_{pore} = g_p \cdot D
$$

where $g_p = 10.5$ kPa/m = 10,500 Pa/m.

**Step 3:** Substitute and solve for mud density.

$$
\rho_{mud} \cdot g \cdot D = g_p \cdot D
$$

Cancel $D$ from both sides:

$$
\rho_{mud} \cdot g = g_p
$$

Therefore:

$$
\rho_{mud} = \frac{g_p}{g}
$$

**Step 4:** Insert numerical values.

$$
\rho_{mud} = \frac{10{,}500 \text{ Pa/m}}{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}
$$

$$
\rho_{mud} \approx 1070.3 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Specific Gravity (SG).

$$
SG = \frac{\rho_{mud}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{1070.3}{1000} \approx 1.07
$$

**Step 6:** Convert SG to ppg (pounds per gallon), since ppg is the field unit used by drilling engineers.

Using the conversion factor $1 \text{ ppg} = SG \times 8.345$:

$$
\text{Mud Weight (ppg)} = 1.07 \times 8.345 \approx 8.93 \text{ ppg}
$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum mud weight required to balance the formation pressure is approximately **8.93 ppg**. In practice, an **overbalance** of 0.3 – 0.5 ppg is maintained for safety, so the actual mud weight would be around **9.3 – 9.5 ppg**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pipeline Pressure Drop for Crude Oil Transport

**Problem Context:** A 100-km crude oil pipeline, 24-inch (0.6096 m) nominal diameter, carries crude oil of density $\rho = 870$ kg/m³ and viscosity $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s at a flow velocity $v = 1.2$ m/s. Assume smooth turbulent flow and a Darcy friction factor $f = 0.018$.

**Find:** Pressure drop across the pipeline.

**Step 1:** Identify all variables clearly.

- $L = 100 \text{ km} = 100{,}000$ m
- $D = 0.6096$ m
- $\rho = 870$ kg/m³
- $v = 1.2$ m/s
- $f = 0.018$ (given for smooth turbulent flow)

**Step 2:** Write the Darcy-Weisbach equation.

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the dynamic pressure term.

$$
\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{870 \times (1.2)^2}{2} = \frac{870 \times 1.44}{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{1252.8}{2} = 626.4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the geometric ratio.

$$
\frac{L}{D} = \frac{100{,}000}{0.6096} \approx 164{,}042
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply all terms.

$$
\Delta P = 0.018 \times 164{,}042 \times 626.4
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
0.018 \times 164{,}042 = 2952.76
$$

$$
2952.76 \times 626.4 \approx 1{,}849{,}235 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\Delta P \approx 1.85 \text{ MPa} \approx 18.5 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to field units (psi).

Using $1 \text{ bar} = 14.5 \text{ psi}$:

$$
\Delta P \approx 18.5 \times 14.5 \approx 268 \text{ psi}
$$

**Conclusion:** The pipeline loses approximately **18.5 bar** (≈ 268 psi) of pressure over the 100-km length due to friction. This determines the **pump station spacing**: pumping stations must be placed such that the booster pressure compensates for this loss before reaching the next station.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> If the pipeline operates at an inlet pressure of 60 bar, the outlet pressure would drop to $60 - 18.5 = 41.5$ bar. Pump stations must be designed to boost pressure back up to 60 bar periodically along the route.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Storage Tank Selection

| Parameter | Fixed Roof | Floating Roof | Spherical (Horton) | Underground Cavern |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stored Product** | Diesel, fuel oil, lube | Crude, gasoline | LPG, butane | Crude (strategic reserve) |
| **Pressure** | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | Pressurized (~5–15 bar) | Hydrostatic (geostatic) |
| **Vapor Loss** | High | **Negligible** | Minimal | None |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium-High | High | Very High |
| **Footprint** | Large | Large (diameter up to 100 m) | Compact (spherical) | None (subterranean) |
| **Fire Risk** | Higher | Lower (no vapor space) | Moderate | Very Low |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Transportation Modes

| Parameter | Pipeline | Marine Tanker | Rail | Road Truck |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity per unit** | Unlimited (continuous) | 2 M bbl (VLCC) | 700 bbl/car | 250 bbl/truck |
| **Flexibility** | Fixed route | Oceanic routes | Moderate | Very flexible |
| **Cost ($/bbl·mile)** | **0.05 – 0.10** | 0.03 – 0.05 | 0.10 – 0.15 | 0.30 – 0.50 |
| **Environmental risk** | Spill (localized but large) | Spill (catastrophic potential) | Derailment | Accident-prone |
| **Energy required** | Pumping energy | Bunker fuel | Diesel locomotive | Diesel truck |
| **Best for** | Long-haul bulk | International trade | Cross-continent w/o pipeline | Last-mile distribution |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Petroleum Value Chain — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Geological and Geophysical Survey] --> B[Seismic Data Acquisition and Interpretation]
    B --> C{Exploratory Wildcat Well}
    C -- Hydrocarbons Found --> D[Appraisal Drilling]
    C -- Dry Hole --> C1[Plug and Abandon P and A]
    D --> E[Reservoir Evaluation and Reserve Estimation]
    E --> F[Field Development Plan]
    F --> G[Development Drilling Multiple Wells]
    G --> H[Wellhead Production Manifold]
    H --> I[Crude Oil Stabilization and Dehydration]
    I --> J[Storage Facilities Tank Farm]
    J --> K{Transportation Mode Selection}
    K --> L1[Pipeline Export Terminal]
    K --> L2[Marine Tanker Loading via SPM or Jetty]
    K --> L3[Rail Tank Car Loading]
    K --> L4[Road Tanker Loading]
    L1 --> M[Refinery Reception and Crude Distillation Unit]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
    M --> N[Refined Product Storage]
    N --> O[Product Distribution Network]
    O --> P[End Consumer Industrial, Transport, Domestic]
```

**Figure 4.1:** End-to-end functional architecture of the upstream–midstream petroleum chain, from exploration through transportation to refining.

---

### 4.2 Rotary Drilling Rig — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Diesel Engines or Electric Motors] --> R2[Draw works and Hoisting System]
    R1 --> R3[Mud Pump]
    R1 --> R4[Rotary Table or Top Drive]
    R2 --> R5[Crown Block]
    R5 --> R6[Traveling Block and Hook]
    R6 --> R7[Drill String Kelly and Drill Pipe]
    R4 --> R7
    R7 --> R8[Drill Bit at Bottom of Hole]
    R3 --> R9[Mud Pits and Shale Shakers]
    R9 --> R3
    R3 --> R7
    R7 --> R10[Annulus returns Cuttings plus Mud]
    R10 --> R9
    R8 --> R11[Open Hole Wellbore]
    R11 --> R12[Blowout Preventer BOP Stack on Wellhead]
    R12 --> R13[Wellhead and Christmas Tree]
```

**Figure 4.2:** Block-level functional architecture of a rotary drilling rig, illustrating power distribution, hoisting, rotary, and circulation subsystems.

---

### 4.3 Crude Oil Storage Tank Farm — Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Incoming Crude Pipeline] --> S2[Receiving Manifold and Metering Station]
    S2 --> T1[Floating Roof Tank 1]
    S2 --> T2[Floating Roof Tank 2]
    S2 --> T3[Floating Roof Tank 3]
    S2 --> T4[Floating Roof Tank 4]
    T1 --> P1[Booster Pump]
    T2 --> P1
    T3 --> P1
    T4 --> P1
    P1 --> S3[Outgoing Pipeline to Refinery or Tanker Loading]
    T1 --> S4[Firewater Ring Main System]
    T2 --> S4
    T3 --> S4
    T4 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Foam Pourer and Water Spray Nozzles]
```

**Figure 4.3:** Crude oil tank farm block architecture showing parallel storage tanks, common manifold, booster pumping, and integrated fire protection system.

```mermaid
    B1 -- Diesel Batch --> B2[Batch Interface Pig]
    B2 -- Kerosene Batch --> B3[Batch Interface Pig]
    B3 -- Gasoline Batch --> P2[Pump Station 2]
    P2 --> P3[Pump Station 3]
    P3 --> P4[Pump Station 4]
    P4 --> D1[Distribut]

ion Manifold]

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

> List any **three types of geological traps** in petroleum exploration and explain why hydrocarbons accumulate in them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Anticlinal Trap**: Hydrocarbons migrate upward through porous, permeable rock layers until they encounter an impermeable cap rock at the crest of an upward-arching fold. Because oil and gas are less dense than formation water, they become trapped at the highest point of the structure. (1 mark)

2. **Fault Trap**: A fault displaces a porous reservoir rock against an impermeable sealing rock, blocking the lateral migration of hydrocarbons. The sealing fault plane acts as the trap. (1 mark)

3. **Stratigraphic Trap**: Variations in rock type — such as a sandstone lens pinching out within a shale — create geometry where reservoir-quality rock is surrounded by impermeable rock, trapping hydrocarbons. (1 mark)

> **Why hydrocarbons accumulate:** Petroleum is **buoyant** (lower density than water) and **immiscible** with formation water. It migrates upward under gravity and is stopped only by an impermeable seal. This is the fundamental principle of all trap types.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

> Differentiate between a **fixed roof tank** and a **floating roof tank**. State where each is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fixed Roof Tank | Floating Roof Tank |
|---|---|---|
| Vapor space | Large vapor space above liquid | Roof rests on liquid surface — minimal vapor space |
| Evaporative losses | High | Very low |
| Fire hazard | Higher (vapor accumulation) | Lower |
| Products stored | Low-volatility (diesel, lube oil, fuel oil) | High-volatility (crude oil, gasoline, naphtha) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (mechanical seal, drainage system) |

**Preferred Use:**

- **Fixed Roof**: For less volatile products stored at ambient conditions (e.g., diesel, bitumen, lube base oil). (1 mark)
- **Floating Roof**: For crude oil and volatile refined products (gasoline, jet fuel) where **environmental regulations on VOC emissions** are stringent. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Oil Exploration and Drilling

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern, Module 1 Coverage]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **steps involved in oil exploration**, starting from geological survey to discovery of a hydrocarbon reservoir. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **rotary drilling method** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **functions of drilling fluid** and the role of the **Blowout Preventer (BOP)**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Steps in Oil Exploration (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Reconnaissance / Surface Geology (1 mark):**
Initial surveys use satellite imagery, aerial photography, and surface geological mapping to identify large-scale structural features such as anticlines, synclines, and fault systems.

**Step 2 — Geophysical Surveys (2 marks):**
- **Seismic Survey**: The dominant geophysical method. Acoustic waves are generated by vibrators or explosives (onshore) or air guns (offshore). Waves reflect off subsurface rock interfaces, and the two-way travel time is recorded by geophones/hydrophones. The data is processed into 3D subsurface images.
- **Gravity and Magnetic Surveys**: Regional reconnaissance to detect density and magnetic anomalies associated with salt domes or basement structures.

**Step 3 — Geochemical Surveys (1 mark):**
Soil and water samples are analyzed for micro-seepages of light hydrocarbons, indicating a possible active petroleum system below.

**Step 4 — Exploratory Drilling (Wildcat Well) (1.5 marks):**
A test well is drilled in the most promising location identified by the integrated studies above. Core samples and well logs are acquired.

**Step 5 — Well Logging and Testing (1 mark):**
Downhole tools (resistivity, gamma ray, sonic, neutron-density logs) measure formation properties. Drill-stem tests (DST) confirm whether hydrocarbons flow to surface.

**Step 6 — Discovery Declaration and Appraisal (0.5 marks):**
If hydrocarbons are found, the well is declared a discovery, and subsequent appraisal wells define the field's extent and reserves.

---

**(b) Rotary Drilling Method (7 marks):**

**Rotary Drilling Principle (2 marks):**
A rotating drill bit at the bottom of a string of drill pipes grinds the rock. Drilling mud is pumped down through the drill string, exits through nozzles in the bit, and carries cuttings up the annulus to the surface. This is the standard method for almost all modern oil wells.

**Block Diagram Description (1.5 marks):**
Refer to Figure 4.2 (Section 4.2). Key components:

- **Derrick/Mast**: Structural tower supporting the hoisting system.
- **Draw works**: Hoists the drill string.
- **Crown Block + Traveling Block + Hook**: Pulley system.
- **Rotary Table or Top Drive**: Imparts rotation to the drill string.
- **Drill String**: Kelly + Drill Pipes + Drill Collars + Bit.
- **Mud Pumps**: Circulate drilling fluid.
- **Mud Pits, Shale Shakers**: Receive and clean returned mud.
- **BOP Stack**: Sits on top of the wellhead for pressure control.

**Functions of Drilling Fluid (2 marks):**

1. **Cuttings transport**: Lifts rock fragments to surface.
2. **Hydrostatic pressure**: Balances formation pore pressure.
3. **Bit cooling and lubrication**: Extends bit life.
4. **Wellbore stability**: Prevents collapse of the hole.
5. **Formation damage prevention**: Seals permeable formations with filter cake.

**Role of Blowout Preventer (BOP) (1.5 marks):**
The BOP is a high-pressure safety valve assembly mounted on the wellhead after the marine riser or conductor pipe. It contains:

- **Annular preventer**: Seals around any shape in the hole (drill pipe, kelly, or open hole).
- **Ram preventers**: Pipe rams (seal around drill pipe), blind rams (seal open hole), shear rams (cut through drill pipe and seal).

When formation pressure exceeds mud hydrostatic pressure, hydrocarbons can "kick" into the well. The BOP is closed to shut in the well, preventing a catastrophic blowout such as the 2010 Deepwater Horizon incident.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Storage and Transportation (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 1 Coverage]**

**(a)** Discuss the **different types of storage tanks** used in petroleum refineries with their applications. Explain why floating roof tanks are preferred for crude oil storage. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **pipelines, marine tankers, and road tankers** as modes of crude oil transportation. Discuss the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** and its significance in pipeline design. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Storage Tanks in Refineries (7 marks):**

**Types and Applications (5 marks):**

| Tank Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Roof (Cone/Dome)** | Atmospheric, rigid steel roof | Diesel, lube oil, fuel oil, bitumen |
| **Floating Roof** | Roof floats directly on liquid surface; rim seal reduces vapor loss | Crude oil, gasoline, naphtha, jet fuel |
| **Pressure Sphere (Horton Sphere)** | Spherical pressurized vessel | LPG, butane, propane |
| **Bullet Tank** | Horizontal cylindrical, pressurized | Smaller LPG volumes, on-site storage |
| **Underground Cavern** | Salt dome or rock cavern | Strategic Petroleum Reserve, large crude storage |
| **LNG Tank** | Double-wall, cryogenic, with perlite insulation | Liquefied natural gas at -162 °C |

**Why Floating Roof Tanks Are Preferred for Crude Oil (2 marks):**

1. **Reduced Evaporative Losses**: Crude oil contains dissolved light ends (methane, ethane, propane) that evaporate readily. The floating roof eliminates the vapor space, reducing breathing and working losses by up to **90 – 95%** compared to fixed roof tanks.

2. **Environmental Compliance**: Modern environmental regulations (e.g., US EPA 40 CFR Part 60) limit VOC emissions. Floating roofs help refineries comply.

3. **Safety**: Elimination of large vapor space reduces the risk of flammable vapor accumulation and fire/explosion.

4. **Product Quality**: Reduces contamination of crude by air and moisture.

---

**(b) Transportation Modes and Pipeline Hydraulics (7 marks):**

**Comparison of Transportation Modes (3 marks):**

| Mode | Capacity | Cost per barrel-mile | Flexibility | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline** | Millions bbl/day | $0.05 – 0.10 | Low (fixed route) | Continental, high-volume |
| **Marine Tanker (VLCC)** | 2 M bbl | $0.03 – 0.05 | High (any ocean route) | International crude trade |
| **Road Tanker** | 250 bbl | $0.30 – 0.50 | Very high | Last-mile, remote, small volumes |

**Selection Criteria**: Crude oil moves from wellhead → gathering line → trunk pipeline → marine terminal → VLCC → receiving terminal → refinery pipeline → refinery. No single mode dominates globally; rather, an **intermodal logistics chain** is used.

**Darcy-Weisbach Equation (4 marks):**

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

**Significance in Pipeline Design:**

1. **Pressure Drop Calculation**: Determines the energy lost to friction per unit length of pipe, governing the **spacing of pumping stations**. Typical crude trunk lines require pumping stations every **50 – 100 km**.

2. **Pipe Diameter Selection**: Larger diameters reduce velocity (and hence $\Delta P$), but increase capital cost. Engineers optimize $D$ using economic velocity criteria (typically **1 – 3 m/s** for crude oil).

3. **Friction Factor Determination**: For laminar flow ($Re < 2000$), $f = 64/Re$ analytically. For turbulent flow, empirical correlations like **Colebrook-White** or **Moody Chart** are used.

4. **Operational Parameter**: If $\Delta P$ exceeds safe limits, fluid velocity is reduced or the pipe is cleaned (pigged) to remove wax or scale buildup, restoring flow efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Exploration" with "Drilling"**: Exploration is the *search*; drilling is the *physical penetration*. Examiners deduct marks if students use these interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Why"**: Students often list functions of drilling mud without explaining *why* each function is needed (e.g., not just "cools the bit" but "cools the bit because PDC bits degrade above 250 °C"). Always tie function to reason.
> 3. **Mixing units**: In pipeline problems, students often mix SI and US customary units. Always convert to a consistent system before computing. **1 bar = 14.5 psi = 10⁵ Pa**.
> 4. **Darcy-Weisbach without friction factor**: Stating the equation without specifying the friction factor $f$ or its source (Moody chart, Colebrook, etc.) loses 1 – 2 marks.
> 5. **Missing BOP discussion**: Many students skip the BOP in drilling diagrams. This is a **safety-critical component** and is a guaranteed mark-earning topic.
> 6. **Wrong tank type for product**: Storing gasoline in a fixed roof tank is a wrong answer; examiners look for the floating roof recommendation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Topic:**

- ☐ **Exploration Workflow**: Geological → Geophysical (Seismic primary) → Geochemical → Wildcat Drilling → Logging → Appraisal → Development.
- ☐ **Seismic Depth Equation**: $d = v \cdot t / 2$.
- ☐ **Three Types of Geological Traps**: Anticline, Fault, Stratigraphic.
- ☐ **Drilling Rig Subsystems**: Hoisting, Rotary, Circulating, Power, BOP.
- ☐ **Drilling Mud Functions**: 5 key roles — clean, cool, balance pressure, stabilize, seal.
- ☐ **BOP Components**: Annular preventer + Pipe rams + Blind rams + Shear rams.
- ☐ **Mud Weight Units**: ppg (US) and SG (SI). 1 ppg = SG × 8.345.
- ☐ **Tank Types**: Fixed roof (low volatility) vs Floating roof (high volatility/crude).
- ☐ **Floating Roof Benefit**: ~90 – 95% reduction in evaporative losses.
- ☐ **Horton Sphere**: Pressurized storage for LPG.
- ☐ **Transportation Modes**: Pipeline (cheapest bulk), VLCC (cheapest oceanic), Road (most expensive, most flexible).
- ☐ **Pipeline Hydraulics**: Darcy-Weisbach equation is the foundation.
- ☐ **VLCC Capacity**: ~2 × 10⁶ barrels; ~330 m long.
- ☐ **Largest Storage Tank**: Up to 1.2 M bbl (floating roof).
- ☐ **LNG Storage Temperature**: -162 °C.
- ☐ **Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR)**: Uses underground salt caverns.
- ☐ **Mud Pressure Formula**: $P = \rho_{mud} \cdot g \cdot h$.
- ☐ **Multiproduct Pipelines**: Use pigs as batch separators.
- ☐ **Pumping Station Spacing**: Typically 50 – 100 km for crude trunk lines.
- ☐ **Refinery–Wellhead Logistical Chain**: Pipeline → Storage → Tanker → Pipeline → Refinery.

> **Final Study Tip:** When answering any KTU question on this topic, **always draw a diagram** (mermaid sketch in the answer sheet equivalent). Examiners reward visual representation generously, and a well-labeled diagram of a drilling rig or tank farm can earn **2 – 3 bonus marks** even when text is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification, composition and evaluation of oil stock; status of petroleum industry in India; classification of petroleum refinery; preliminary petroleum processing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 — Classification, Composition & Evaluation of Oil Stock; Petroleum Industry in India; Refinery Classification; Preliminary Petroleum Processing

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification, composition and evaluation of oil stock; status of petroleum industry in India; classification of petroleum refinery; preliminary petroleum processing

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Crude Oil (Petroleum Stock)?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Crude oil is a naturally occurring, dark-coloured, viscous-to-fluid flammable liquid found beneath the earth's surface, consisting essentially of a complex mixture of hydrocarbons (paraffins, naphthenes, and aromatics) with minor amounts of sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and metallic compounds (mainly vanadium and nickel). It is the primary feedstock for a petroleum refinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Crude oil is *never* a single compound. It is a mixture whose composition determines its classification, evaluation grade, and the refinery configuration required to process it economically.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Fruit Basket" of Petroleum:**
Imagine crude oil as a mixed fruit basket brought from a farm. Just as the basket contains mangoes, bananas, grapes, and apples — each with different tastes, colours, and ripeness — crude oil contains paraffins (sweet, light), naphthenes (ringy, mid-weight), aromatics (heavy, aromatic smelling), and asphaltenes/resins (bitter, heavy). Before making a "smoothie" (fuel products), you must first **separate, classify, and grade** the raw fruit (crude oil) and then run it through preliminary cleaning (desalting) before the main processing line. This is exactly the philosophy of a petroleum refinery.

---

### 1.2 Classification of Crude Oil Stock

Crude oils are classified by chemical nature, by API gravity, and by sulfur content.

#### (a) Chemical (Hydrocarbon) Classification

| Class | Dominant Hydrocarbon | General Characteristics | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paraffinic (Paraffin-base)** | Straight & branched alkanes ($C_nH_{2n+2}$) | High wax content, high cetane number, low asphalt | Lube oils, diesel, kerosene |
| **Naphthenic (Naphthene-base)** | Cycloparaffins ($C_nH_{2n}$) | Low wax, high aromatic content, dissolves asphalt | Asphalt, gasoline (reforming stock) |
| **Aromatic (Aromatic-base)** | BTX-type structures | Poor diesel quality, high smoke point | Solvents, petrochemicals |
| **Asphaltic (Mixed-base)** | Mixture of all three + resins/asphaltenes | High sulfur, high metals, heavy residue | Fuel oil, bitumen, coke |

**Classification Index (n-d-M Method — Van Nes & Van Westen):**
The most rigorous academic classification uses refractive index ($n$) and density ($d$):

$$\%C_R = 1.9 \cdot d - 0.0012 \cdot n^{20}_{D} + \dots$$

This is the foundation of the *n-d-M analysis* taught in KTU labs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Paraffinic = "Linear Chain" (think spaghetti), Naphthenic = "Ring-shaped" (think bagels), Aromatic = "Hexagonal benzene ring" (think honeycomb), Asphaltic = "Heavy tar with all of the above".

---

#### (b) API Gravity Classification

API gravity is the standard industry measure of crude "lightness":

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$

where $SG$ is the **specific gravity** at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** relative to water at the same temperature.

| Classification | API Gravity ($^\circ$API) | Specific Gravity |
|---|---|---|
| Light crude | $> 31.1$ | $< 0.87$ |
| Medium crude | $22.3 - 31.1$ | $0.87 - 0.92$ |
| Heavy crude | $10.0 - 22.3$ | $0.92 - 1.00$ |
| Extra-heavy | $< 10.0$ | $> 1.00$ |

**Analogy:** Think of API as the *body-mass index* of crude oil. A higher API (lighter) means more gasoline-yielding; a lower API (heavier) means more residual fuel.

---

#### (c) Sulfur Content Classification

| Type | Sulfur Content (wt %) | Commercial Value |
|---|---|---|
| Sweet crude | $< 0.5$ | Premium — easier to refine |
| Sour crude | $0.5 - 1.0$ | Moderate processing needed |
| High-sour | $> 1.0$ | Expensive desulfurization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sour vs. Sweet — Why It Matters:** Sulfur poisons catalysts in catalytic crackers and hydrotreaters, corrodes distillation columns, and creates SOx emissions. Hence "sweet crudes command premium prices".

---

### 1.3 Composition of Crude Oil

Crude oil is composed of four fundamental structural types:

1. **Paraffins (Alkanes)** — $C_nH_{2n+2}$; straight & branched
2. **Naphthenes (Cycloalkanes)** — $C_nH_{2n}$, $C_nH_{2n-2}$, etc.
3. **Aromatics** — Benzene, toluene, xylene (BTX), polyaromatics
4. **Resins & Asphaltenes** — High molecular weight, polar, heteroatom-rich

Plus **non-hydrocarbon impurities:**
- **Sulfur compounds:** H₂S, mercaptans, sulfides, thiophenes
- **Nitrogen compounds:** Pyridines, quinolines
- **Oxygen compounds:** Naphthenic acids, phenols
- **Metals:** Ni, V (in porphyrin complexes), Fe, Na

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Refining technology is essentially the science of selectively cracking, rearranging, or removing these four hydrocarbon families to manufacture finished products.

---

### 1.4 Evaluation of Oil Stock

Crude oil evaluation is the **grading** process — analogous to quality control in any industry. It involves three pillars:

1. **Physical Tests** — Density (API), viscosity, pour point, flash point
2. **Distillation Tests** — ASTM D86 (atmospheric), ASTM D1160 (vacuum), TBP (true boiling point)
3. **Compositional Tests** — Elemental analysis (C, H, S, N, O), metal content, n-d-M method, SARA analysis (Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, Asphaltenes)

**Key Characterization Factor — Watson (UOP) K:**
$$K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG}$$

where $T_B$ = mean average boiling point in **°R** (Rankine) and $SG$ = specific gravity.

| K-Value Range | Crude Type |
|---|---|
| $> 12.0$ | Highly paraffinic |
| $11.5 - 12.0$ | Paraffinic |
| $11.0 - 11.5$ | Intermediate |
| $10.5 - 11.0$ | Naphthenic |
| $< 10.5$ | Highly aromatic |

---

### 1.5 Status of Petroleum Industry in India

**Indian Sector Snapshot:**

- India is the **3rd largest** energy consumer globally (after USA & China).
- **Crude oil production (2023-24):** ~30 MMT (domestic).
- **Consumption:** ~230 MMT (so India imports ~85% of crude).
- **Refining capacity:** ~250 MMTPA — among top 5 globally.

**Major Indian Refineries (Must-Know for KTU):**

| Refinery | Location | Type | Capacity (MMTPA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indian Oil — Panipat | Haryana | Complex | 15.0 |
| Reliance Jamnagar | Gujarat | Largest private complex | 68.2 |
| IOCL — Guwahati | Assam | Sector PSU | 1.0 |
| BPCL — Mumbai | Maharashtra | PSU complex | 13.0 |
| HPCL — Visakhapatnam | Andhra Pradesh | PSU complex | 11.5 |
| Mangalore Refinery (MRPL) | Karnataka | PSU | 15.0 |

**Regulatory & Strategic Bodies:**
- **MoPNG** — Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas (policy)
- **ONGC** — exploration & production (E&P)
- **OIDB** — Oil Industry Development Board (funding)
- **PCRA** — Petroleum Conservation Research Association
- **PNGRB** — Petroleum & Natural Gas Regulatory Board
- **OPEC** — global pricing influence (India is *not* a member but is a key consumer)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked about India's strategic petroleum position, always quote the **85% import dependency** and the **East Coast / West Coast refinery distribution pattern**.

---

### 1.6 Classification of Petroleum Refineries

Refineries are classified based on their **complexity**, **process configuration**, and **product slate**.

#### (a) Based on Complexity (Nelson Complexity Index, NCI)

$$\text{NCI} = \frac{\sum (\text{Capacity of secondary units})}{\text{Capacity of crude distillation}}$$

| Refinery Type | NCI | Product Slate |
|---|---|---|
| **Topping refinery** | $\approx 1.0$ | Naphtha, kerosene, diesel, FO |
| **Hydroskimming refinery** | $1.5 - 3.0$ | Above + hydrotreated products |
| **Cracking refinery** | $3.0 - 7.0$ | Above + gasoline via FCC |
| **Coking refinery** | $7.0 - 10.0$ | Above + petroleum coke |
| **Deep conversion** | $> 10.0$ | Maximum gasoline, middle distillates |

#### (b) Based on Process Configuration

| Type | Key Process | Product Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel-type** | FCC, hydrocracker, coker | Gasoline, jet, diesel |
| **Lube-type** | Solvent extraction, dewaxing | Base oils + fuels |
| **Petrochemical-type** | Steam cracker, reformer | Olefins, aromatics + fuels |
| **Asphalt-type** | Vacuum distillation | Bitumen, roofing |

---

### 1.7 Preliminary Petroleum Processing

Before the main distillation train, crude oil undergoes **four essential pre-treatment steps** (also called *front-end processing*):

1. **Desalting** — Removes dissolved salts (NaCl, MgCl₂, CaCl₂) which would otherwise:
   - Corrode overhead systems
   - Poison catalysts downstream
   - Form scales in heat exchangers
2. **Dehydration** — Removes free water and emulsified water
3. **Atmospheric Distillation** — Splits crude into naphtha, kerosene, diesel, AGO (atmospheric gas oil), and reduced crude
4. **Vacuum Distillation** — Further processes reduced crude into VGO (vacuum gas oil) and vacuum residue without cracking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Keyword:** *Preliminary processing* in the syllabus specifically refers to **desalting + atmospheric + vacuum distillation**. Always show a flowchart in answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Framework

#### 2.1.1 Crude Oil Classification — Logic Flow

Step 1: **Crude is sampled** at the wellhead and tested in the lab.

Step 2: **Specific gravity** is measured by hydrometer or pycnometer at 15.6 °C → API gravity is computed.

Step 3: **ASTM D86 distillation** is run → produces IBP, 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%, 95% cut points and FBP (final boiling point).

Step 4: **Mean Average Boiling Point (MeABP)** is calculated:

$$\text{MeABP} = \frac{T_{10} + T_{30} + T_{50} + T_{70} + T_{90}}{5}$$

Step 5: **Watson K factor** is evaluated using MeABP — this *characterizes* the crude type.

Step 6: **n-d-M method** classifies hydrocarbons into paraffinic vs naphthenic vs aromatic percentages.

Step 7: **Sulfur analysis** (ASTM D129, D2622) → sweet vs sour designation.

#### 2.1.2 Why Classification Matters — The Refinery Design Consequence

| Crude Type | Refinery Type Required | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Paraffinic (high K) | Lube-oil refinery | Wax separation is profitable |
| Naphthenic | Catalytic reformer-rich | High naphthene → aromatics yield |
| Heavy Sour (e.g., Mayan) | Coking + deep hydrotreating | High metals and sulfur |
| Light Sweet (e.g., Brent) | Topping / hydroskimming | Already "refinery-ready" |

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" Intuition:** Crude classification is *not academic* — it determines **billions of dollars** of capital investment in a refinery. A misclassified crude can render a refinery uneconomical.

---

#### 2.1.3 Preliminary Processing — Theoretical Logic

**Stage 1 — Desalting:**
- **Electrical desalting:** Crude is mixed with 5-10 vol% wash water and demulsifier in a vessel; an AC voltage (~25-35 kV) coalesces brine droplets which settle out.
- **Two-stage desalting** removes >90% salt; reduces salt content from ~1000 ptb (pounds per thousand barrels) to <5 ptb.

**Stage 2 — Atmospheric Distillation:**
- Crude preheated in a train of heat exchangers (economizers) to ~120-130 °C.
- Final heating in a **pipestill furnace** to 350-370 °C.
- Vapor-liquid separation in a **distillation column** at ~1 atm with 30-50 fractionation trays.
- Products (top → bottom): **Naphtha (IBP-150°C)**, **Kerosene (150-250°C)**, **Diesel (250-360°C)**, **AGO (360-370°C)**.
- Bottoms → **Reduced Crude (RC)** at ~370 °C.

**Stage 3 — Vacuum Distillation:**
- RC enters a vacuum furnace (380-420 °C) — temperature kept below cracking temperature (~425 °C) to avoid thermal decomposition.
- Column operates at 30-100 mmHg (absolute).
- Products: **LVGO (light vacuum gas oil, 370-450°C)**, **HVGO (heavy vacuum gas oil, 450-570°C)**, **Vacuum Residue (>570°C)**.
- Steam is injected at column bottom to lower partial pressure (steam stripping).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why vacuum?** At 570°C at atmospheric pressure, hydrocarbons crack thermally. Lowering pressure reduces boiling points so we can separate residues without cracking. *Vacuum distillation is essentially "distillation without thermal damage."*

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $^\circ \text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | API gravity (lighter = higher) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $SG_{15.6/15.6} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ at $15.6$ °C | Specific gravity | — |
| 3 | $K_{\text{Watson}} = \dfrac{\sqrt[3]{T_B\,(\text{°R})}}{SG}$ | Watson/UOP characterization K | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{MeABP} = \dfrac{T_{10}+T_{30}+T_{50}+T_{70}+T_{90}}{5}$ | Mean average boiling point | °C or °F |
| 5 | $\text{VABP} = \dfrac{T_{30}+T_{50}+T_{70}}{3}$ | Volumetric average boiling point | °C or °F |
| 6 | $\text{CABP} = \dfrac{T_{10}+T_{30}+T_{50}+T_{70}+T_{90}}{5} \cdot \text{slope corr.}$ | Cubic average boiling point | °C |
| 7 | $^\circ \text{R} = (^\circ \text{F}) + 459.67$ | Rankine conversion | °R |
| 8 | $\text{NCI} = \dfrac{\sum V_{\text{secondary}}}{V_{\text{crude}}}$ | Nelson Complexity Index | — |
| 9 | $\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{product}}}{m_{\text{crude}}} \times 100$ | Product yield | wt% |
| 10 | $d_{\text{mixture}} = \sum x_i \cdot d_i$ | Mixing rule for densities | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *API*, *Watson K*, *MeABP*, and *NCI* are the **four pillars** of any refining-tech numerical problem. Master these.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Design:** Engineers select operating temperatures, tray counts, reflux ratios from API and TBP curves.
- **Catalyst Selection:** Sulfur and metal content (Ni+V) determine whether hydrotreating or hydrocracking is needed.
- **Economic Optimization:** Refiners price crudes using API and sulfur differentials (e.g., "Dated Brent" benchmark).
- **Strategic Planning:** India's 85% import dependency drives investment in E&P (KG-D6, Rajasthan block) and refining mega-projects (Ratnagiri, Panipat).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of API Gravity from Specific Gravity

The American Petroleum Institute (API) gravity is defined to give pure water ($SG = 1$) a value of $10^\circ$ API. The inverse relationship ensures that **lighter liquids have higher API**.

**Starting relationship (definition):**
$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{A}{SG} + B$$

**Calibration with water:** Plug in $SG = 1$ and set $^\circ\text{API} = 10$:

$$10 = \frac{A}{1} + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad A + B = 10 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Calibration with a reference oil:** An oil of $SG = 0.875$ has API = 15.7:

$$15.7 = \frac{A}{0.875} + B \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Solving (i) and (ii):**
From (i): $B = 10 - A$. Substitute into (ii):

$$15.7 = \frac{A}{0.875} + 10 - A$$

$$5.7 = \frac{A}{0.875} - A = A\left(\frac{1 - 0.875}{0.875}\right) = A \cdot \frac{0.125}{0.875}$$

$$A = \frac{5.7 \times 0.875}{0.125} = 141.5$$

Therefore, $B = 10 - 141.5 = -131.5$.

$$\boxed{{}^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5}$$

This is the universally accepted API formula used across the petroleum industry and in all KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — API Calculation

**Problem:** A crude oil has a density of **0.886 g/cm³** at 15.6 °C. Calculate its API gravity and classify it.

**Solution:**

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{0.886}{1.000} = 0.886$$

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.886} - 131.5$$

$$= 159.71 - 131.5 = 28.21^{\circ}\text{API}$$

**Classification:** Since $22.3 \leq 28.21 \leq 31.1$, this is a **medium crude**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** Knowing a crude is "medium" immediately tells the refining engineer that secondary processing (FCC or hydrocracking) will be needed to convert heavy AGO/VGO fractions into gasoline.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Watson K Factor

**Problem:** A naphtha cut has a specific gravity of 0.745 and a mean average boiling point of 245 °F. Calculate the Watson K factor.

**Solution:**

Convert °F to °R:
$$T_B\,(^{\circ}R) = 245 + 459.67 = 704.67\,^{\circ}R$$

Apply Watson K formula:
$$K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG} = \frac{\sqrt[3]{704.67}}{0.745}$$

$$\sqrt[3]{704.67} = (704.67)^{0.3333}$$

Compute:
$$\ln(704.67) = 6.557$$
$$0.3333 \times 6.557 = 2.186$$
$$e^{2.186} = 8.899$$

Therefore:
$$K = \frac{8.899}{0.745} = 11.95$$

**Classification:** $K = 11.95$ falls in the **paraffinic** range (11.5 - 12.0) → cut is paraffinic naphtha, ideal for steam cracking to produce ethylene.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Nelson Complexity Index (NCI)

**Problem:** A refinery has the following unit capacities:
- Crude distillation: 10.0 MMTPA
- Vacuum distillation: 5.5 MMTPA
- FCC: 3.2 MMTPA
- Hydrocracker: 1.8 MMTPA
- Delayed coker: 1.5 MMTPA
- Naphtha reformer: 1.0 MMTPA

Calculate the NCI and classify the refinery.

**Solution:**

Secondary units (excluding crude distillation):

| Unit | Capacity (MMTPA) |
|---|---|
| Vacuum distillation | 5.5 |
| FCC | 3.2 |
| Hydrocracker | 1.8 |
| Delayed coker | 1.5 |
| Reformer | 1.0 |
| **Sum** | **13.0** |

$$\text{NCI} = \frac{13.0}{10.0} = 13.0$$

**Classification:** NCI > 10 → **Deep Conversion Refinery** (typical of Reliance Jamnagar-type mega-complexes).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Crude Properties vs. Refinery Strategy

| Property | Low Value → Strategy | High Value → Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| API gravity | Light crude → topping/hydroskimming | Heavy crude → coker + hydrocracker |
| Sulfur | Sweet → basic hydrotreating | Sour → amine treating + HDS |
| Watson K | Naphthenic → reformer-heavy | Paraffinic → steam cracker |
| Metals (Ni+V) | < 5 ppm → FCC direct | > 50 ppm → hydrodemetallization first |
| Pour point | Low → lube oil potential | High → dewaxing unit needed |
| Wax content | Low → lube oil | High → wax plant + storage |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Define the term *Watson Characterization Factor (K)*. Mention the classification of crude oil based on K values.

**Model Answer:**

The Watson K factor is a dimensionless number that characterizes a crude oil or its fractions based on boiling point and specific gravity:

$$K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG}$$

where $T_B$ is the mean average boiling point in **°R** and $SG$ is specific gravity at 15.6 °C.

**Classification based on K:**

| K-Value Range | Crude Type |
|---|---|
| $> 12.0$ | Highly paraffinic |
| $11.5 - 12.0$ | Paraffinic |
| $11.0 - 11.5$ | Intermediate |
| $10.5 - 11.0$ | Naphthenic |
| $< 10.5$ | Highly aromatic |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark for range explanation, 1 mark for the table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Distinguish between "Sweet" and "Sour" crude oils. Why are sweet crudes priced higher?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sweet Crude | Sour Crude |
|---|---|---|
| Sulfur content | $< 0.5$ wt% | $> 0.5$ wt% |
| Catalyst impact | Mild poisoning | Severe poisoning in FCC/hydrotreaters |
| Processing cost | Lower | Higher (requires amine + HDS) |
| Corrosion | Less aggressive | Aggressive to overhead systems |
| Sale price | Premium | Discount |

**Reasons for higher sweet crude prices:**
1. Lower refining cost — minimum HDS and desulfurization needed.
2. Higher yields of finished products (less sulfur carry-over).
3. Compliance with environmental norms (Euro-VI) is easier.
4. Reduced catalyst replacement and corrosion-related maintenance.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for table, 1 for pricing reason)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For "preliminary processing" questions, students **lose marks** by:
> - Forgetting the **desalting step** before the atmospheric column.
> - Confusing AGO vs LVGO vs HVGO temperature ranges.
> - Not justifying **why vacuum distillation** is used (i.e., to avoid thermal cracking).
> - Drawing block diagrams without labeling temperatures and pressures.
> - Missing **steam stripping** in vacuum columns.
>
> **Always include:** Operating temperature range, pressure, products with cut-point temperatures, and the *reason* for each step.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) (7 marks)** Discuss in detail the **classification of petroleum refineries** based on (i) complexity and (ii) product slate. Explain the Nelson Complexity Index with an example.

**(b) (7 marks)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **preliminary processing of crude oil** in a refinery. Why is desalting necessary?

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Refinery Classification:**

Petroleum refineries are classified by their *complexity*, which is quantified by the **Nelson Complexity Index (NCI)**:

$$\text{NCI} = \frac{\text{Sum of capacities of all secondary units}}{\text{Capacity of crude distillation unit}}$$

A *secondary unit* is any unit downstream of crude distillation (vacuum distillation, FCC, hydrocracker, coker, reformer, etc.). Crude distillation itself has a contribution of 1.0.

**Classification:**

| Refinery Type | NCI | Key Secondary Units | Product Slate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Topping | $\approx 1.0$ | None (only crude + product treating) | Naphtha, kerosene, diesel, fuel oil |
| Hydroskimming | $1.5 - 3.0$ | Hydrotreating, reformer | + Sulfur-free fuels |
| Cracking | $3.0 - 7.0$ | + FCC, alkylation | + Gasoline from VGO |
| Coking | $7.0 - 10.0$ | + Delayed coker | + Petroleum coke, more distillates |
| Deep conversion | $> 10.0$ | + Hydrocracker, lube complex | + Maximum middle distillates |

**Example calculation:**
A refinery has 5.0 MMTPA crude unit, 4.0 MMTPA vacuum, 2.0 MMTPA FCC, 1.0 MMTPA hydrocracker, 1.0 MMTPA coker.

$$\text{NCI} = \frac{(4.0 + 2.0 + 1.0 + 1.0)}{5.0} = 1.6$$

This corresponds to a **hydroskimming refinery**.

*(7 marks: 2 for NCI definition, 3 for classification table, 2 for example)*

---

**Part (b) — Preliminary Processing:**

The preliminary (front-end) processing of crude oil consists of the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Desalting:**
Crude oil from storage tanks contains dissolved salts (NaCl, MgCl₂, CaCl₂) typically at 100-1000 ptb (pounds per thousand barrels). Wash water (5-10 vol%) and a demulsifier are injected, and the mixture is sheared through a mixing valve to form an emulsion. The emulsion enters an **electrical desalter** operating at 25-35 kV AC, where brine droplets coalesce and settle out. Two-stage desalting reduces salts to < 5 ptb.

**Why desalting is necessary:**
- Salt hydrolysis produces HCl in the distillation overhead → severe corrosion.
- Salts form deposits on furnace tubes and column trays → reduced heat transfer.
- Salts poison downstream catalysts (FCC, reformer, hydrotreater).
- Salts contribute to atmospheric emissions (SO₂, particulate chlorides).

**Step 2 — Atmospheric Distillation:**
Desalted crude is preheated in a heat-exchanger train (economizer) to ~130 °C using product and pumparound streams. Final heating to 350-370 °C occurs in the pipestill furnace. The partially vaporized stream enters the **atmospheric distillation column** operating at ~1 atm with 30-50 fractionation trays.

**Products (top to bottom):**

| Stream | Temperature (°C) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Overhead gas + naphtha | IBP - 150 | Gasoline pool, reformer feed |
| Kerosene | 150 - 250 | Jet fuel, domestic fuel |
| Diesel (HSD) | 250 - 360 | Highway diesel, LDO |
| AGO (Atmospheric Gas Oil) | 360 - 370 | FCC / hydrocracker feed |
| Reduced crude (RC) | $> 370$ | Feed to vacuum distillation |

**Step 3 — Vacuum Distillation:**
Reduced crude enters a vacuum furnace and is heated to 380-420 °C (kept below 425 °C to prevent thermal cracking). It enters a vacuum distillation column operating at 30-100 mmHg abs with steam stripping at the bottom.

**Products:**

| Stream | Temperature (°C) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| LVGO | 370 - 450 | FCC feed, lube base stock |
| HVGO | 450 - 570 | Hydrocracker / coker feed |
| Vacuum residue | $> 570$ | Bitumen, fuel oil, coker feed |

**Block Flow Diagram:**

(See Section 4.2 for the Mermaid flow diagram of preliminary processing.)

*(7 marks: 2 for desalting + necessity, 3 for atmospheric distillation, 2 for vacuum distillation with temperatures and products)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain in detail the **chemical classification of crude oil** based on hydrocarbon composition. What is the n-d-M method?

**(b) (7 marks)** Discuss the **status of the petroleum industry in India** — its import dependency, major refineries, and key regulatory bodies.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Chemical Classification:**

Crude oils are classified chemically based on the dominant hydrocarbon family. Four major classes exist:

**1. Paraffinic (Paraffin-base) crude:**
- Dominated by straight and branched alkanes ($C_nH_{2n+2}$).
- High wax content; high pour point; high cetane number.
- Yields high-quality **lube oils** and **diesel**.
- Example: Pennsylvania crude, North Sea (Brent).

**2. Naphthenic (Naphthene-base) crude:**
- Dominated by cycloparaffins ($C_nH_{2n}$).
- Low wax; high aromatic content.
- Good feedstock for **catalytic reforming** (naphthenes → aromatics).
- Example: Some Mexican, Californian crudes.

**3. Aromatic (Aromatic-base) crude:**
- High proportion of BTX and polyaromatics.
- Poor diesel quality (low cetane).
- Suitable for **petrochemical** production.
- Example: Some Indonesian crudes.

**4. Asphaltic (Mixed-base) crude:**
- Balanced mix with high resins and asphaltenes.
- High sulfur and metal content.
- Suited for **bitumen** and **fuel oil** manufacture.
- Example: Mexican Maya, Venezuelan Boscan.

**n-d-M Method (Van Nes & Van Westen):**
This is a laboratory technique for quantifying the percentage of paraffins, naphthenes, and aromatics in a petroleum fraction. It uses:

- $n$ — refractive index at 20 °C and D-line of sodium ($n^{20}_D$)
- $d$ — density (specific gravity)
- $M$ — molecular weight (from viscosity correlation)

These three parameters, when plotted on the **n-d-M triangular chart**, give the carbon-type composition:
- $\%C_P$ — Paraffinic carbon
- $\%C_N$ — Naphthenic carbon
- $\%C_A$ — Aromatic carbon

This method is widely used in lubricant characterization and is a fundamental tool in KTU lab courses.

*(7 marks: 2 for paraffinic, 2 for naphthenic + aromatic, 2 for asphaltic + examples, 1 for n-d-M explanation)*

---

**Part (b) — Status of Petroleum Industry in India:**

**Position in the Global Scenario:**
- India is the **3rd largest energy consumer** in the world (after USA and China).
- **4th largest** LCO (light crude oil) importer globally.
- ~85% of crude oil is **imported**, making India vulnerable to global price shocks.
- Domestic crude production (~30 MMTPA) is dominated by ONGC and OIL from Mumbai High, Assam, Rajasthan, and KG Basin.

**Major Refineries:**

| Refinery | Operator | Location | Capacity (MMTPA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jamnagar (SEZ) | Reliance Industries | Gujarat | 35.2 |
| Jamnagar (DTA) | Reliance Industries | Gujarat | 33.0 |
| Panipat | Indian Oil Corp. | Haryana | 15.0 |
| Gujarat | Indian Oil Corp. | Gujarat | 13.7 |
| Mumbai | BPCL | Maharashtra | 13.0 |
| Visakhapatnam | HPCL | Andhra Pradesh | 11.5 |
| Mangalore (MRPL) | ONGC subsidiary | Karnataka | 15.0 |
| Paradip | Indian Oil Corp. | Odisha | 15.0 |
| Bina | BPCL | Madhya Pradesh | 7.8 |
| Kochi | BPCL | Kerala | 15.5 |
| Numaligarh | NRL (BPCL subsidiary) | Assam | 3.0 |

Total refining capacity: ~250 MMTPA — India is a **net exporter of refined products** despite being a crude importer.

**Regulatory and Strategic Bodies:**

| Body | Role |
|---|---|
| **MoPNG** | Policy formulation |
| **ONGC, OIL** | Exploration & Production |
| **IOCL, BPCL, HPCL** | Refining & marketing |
| **PCRA** | Conservation awareness |
| **PNGRB** | Pipelines, pricing, distribution |
| **OIDB** | Funding for industry development |
| **OPEC** (India is observer) | Global price benchmark |
| **DGH** | Directorate General of Hydrocarbons |

**Strategic Initiatives:**
- **SATAT** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) — promoting CBG (compressed biogas).
- **Ethanol Blending Programme** — E20 target by 2025.
- **Petroleum & Chemical Investment Regions (PCPIRs)** — Dahej, Visakhapatnam, Paradeep, Mangalore.
- **Strategic Petroleum Reserves** — at Vizag, Mangalore, Padur (5.33 MMT capacity).

*(7 marks: 2 for global position + import dependency, 2 for major refineries table, 2 for regulatory bodies, 1 for strategic initiatives)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **AGO** (atmospheric gas oil) with **VGO** (vacuum gas oil).
> 2. Missing the **Watson K formula** in classification questions — always include it.
> 3. In refinery classification, failing to mention **NCI = 1** for topping refineries.
> 4. Forgetting **temperature and pressure** values in distillation answers.
> 5. In India-specific questions, omitting **import percentage** (85%).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Crude oil = mixture of paraffins + naphthenes + aromatics + resins + asphaltenes + impurities (S, N, O, metals).
- ✅ Three classification axes: **Chemical (n-d-M)**, **API gravity**, **Sulfur content**.
- ✅ API = lighter the crude, higher the API; water = 10° API.
- ✅ Watson K = $\sqrt[3]{T_B(°R)} / SG$; $K > 12$ → paraffinic; $K < 10.5$ → aromatic.
- ✅ Sweet crude = S < 0.5 wt%; commands premium pricing.
- ✅ India imports ~**85%** of crude; refining capacity ~250 MMTPA.
- ✅ Largest Indian refinery = **Reliance Jamnagar** (68.2 MMTPA combined).
- ✅ Topping refinery NCI ≈ 1; Deep conversion NCI > 10.
- ✅ Preliminary processing = **Desalting → Atmospheric Distillation → Vacuum Distillation**.
- ✅ Desalter voltage = 25-35 kV AC; salt removal > 90% in two-stage.
- ✅ Atmospheric column pressure ≈ 1 atm; vacuum column pressure ≈ 30-100 mmHg.
- ✅ Atmospheric products (IBP-370°C): Naphtha, Kero, Diesel, AGO.
- ✅ Vacuum products: LVGO, HVGO, Vacuum Residue.
- ✅ Steam stripping used in vacuum columns to lower partial pressure and avoid thermal cracking at 570 °C.
- ✅ Key regulatory body for policy = **MoPNG**; for regulation = **PNGRB**.
- ✅ Strategic petroleum reserves at **Vizag, Mangalore, Padur** (5.33 MMT).

---
