topics.title: Impurities in crude oil; dehydration and desalting of crude
Electric desalter: process description, factors affecting the electric desalter; pipe still furnaces and its operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric desalter: process description, factors affecting the electric desalter; pipe still furnaces and its operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Desalter — Formal Definition

An **electric desalter** is a primary treating vessel installed immediately downstream of the crude unit charge pump and upstream of the atmospheric distillation column. Its engineering purpose is to **remove entrained water, dissolved water-soluble inorganic salts (primarily MgCl₂, CaCl₂, NaCl), and suspended solids** from the incoming crude oil charge before it enters the hot preheat train and furnace.

The desalter achieves separation by exploiting the difference in **dielectric constants** and **densities** of crude oil (continuous phase, ε ≈ 2.2) and wash water (dispersed phase, ε ≈ 80). A high-voltage alternating electric field (typically **15–35 kV AC**) is imposed between a pair of suspended electrodes. Water droplets become polarized, attract one another (dielectrophoresis), coalesce into larger drops, and gravitate to the water-boot at the bottom of the vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A single-stage desalter typically removes 90–95% of salts and reduces BS&W (Basic Sediment & Water) to **< 0.1 vol%**. Two-stage units can reach **< 1 PTB** (pounds salt per thousand barrels of oil).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salad Spinner for Crude"

Think of a salad spinner: wet lettuce leaves are tossed with paper towels and spun — centrifugal force pulls water droplets off the leaves. An **electric desalter does the same job**, but instead of centrifugal force it uses an **electric field as the separating force**.

Picture crude oil as a thick, dirty soup containing microscopic water droplets carrying dissolved table salt. The desalter:
1. **Dilutes** the soup with fresh "wash water" (just like rinsing).
2. **Sprays** it through electrodes — the water droplets, being polar, line up like tiny magnets and **clump together** into big drops.
3. Lets gravity **rain** these big drops to the bottom drain.

The "cleaned" oil floats to the top and moves forward to the furnace, while salty water is sent to the wastewater treatment plant.

---

## 1.3 Pipe Still Furnace — Formal Definition

A **pipe still furnace** (also called a **fired heater** or **crude heater**) is a direct-fired, radiant-convective heat exchanger in which crude oil (or other process fluid) flows through a continuous tube coil, absorbing thermal energy from the combustion of fuel (refinery fuel gas, naphtha, or residual fuel oil) in an adjacent firebox. It is the **prime energy consumer** in any refinery — typically accounting for **1.5–3.5% of crude throughput** as fuel and delivering heat duties of **20–150 MMBtu/hr**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "pipe still"?** Before modern furnaces, refineries used shell-and-tube "stills" (batch kettles). Today's continuous tubular coils inside a fired box are the descendants — hence the name **pipe still**.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Pressure Cooker"

Imagine heating milk on a gas stove in a long, coiled metal pipe. The pipe is inside an insulated box where a flame licks the outside. As the milk moves through the coil, it gets hotter and hotter, just like crude oil flowing through the radiant tubes. The **burners = stove**, the **tubes = the pot**, and the **stack = the chimney** venting the exhaust. Crude leaves the radiant section near its **pseudo-critical temperature** (~ 650–700 °F) ready for vaporization in the distillation tower.

---

## 1.5 Engineering Significance (Why This Topic is Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Salt left in crude → hydrolyzes to **HCl** in the overhead → severe overhead corrosion.
> - Salt + water → fouling of furnace tubes → reduced heat transfer → tube failures.
> - Salt + vanadium/sodium → catalyst poisoning in downstream hydrotreaters and FCC units.
> - Pipe still furnace efficiency → directly determines refinery **energy intensity** (EII index).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electric Desalter — Process Description (Operating Sequence)

A two-stage electrostatic desalter operates as follows:

1. **Charge Section / Mixing Valve** — Crude oil is blended with **3–7 vol% wash water** at 100–150 °C through a **mixing valve** (pressure drop ≈ **0.7–1.4 bar**) or static mixer. Demulsifier chemical (5–20 ppm) is injected upstream.
2. **Inlet Distributor** — The oil–water dispersion enters the vessel through a spreader, distributing flow evenly across the horizontal cross-section to minimize short-circuiting.
3. **Electrodes (Grid / Plate Type)** — Two sets of horizontal **electrodes** suspended from the top. The lower electrode is **grounded**, upper one is energized. A high-voltage transformer-rectifier set supplies **15–35 kV AC** (or AC/DC dual polarity).
4. **Coalescence Zone** — Polarized water droplets migrate, collide, and coalesce under the influence of the **non-uniform electric field**.
5. **Settling Zone** — Coalesced droplets gravitate downward (Stokes regime) into the **water boot**.
6. **Water Boot** — Salt water is withdrawn via interface-level control to the wastewater plant.
7. **Outlet** — Desalted oil exits through a top collector and proceeds to the second stage or to the preheat train.

---

## 2.2 Theory of Electrocoalescence

The migration velocity of a water droplet in an electric field (Kruyt, 1952) is given by:

$$v_m = \frac{E^2 \, r^2 \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r}{3 \, \mu}$$

where:
- $v_m$ = migration velocity of droplet (m/s)
- $E$ = electric field intensity (V/m)
- $r$ = droplet radius (m)
- $\varepsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity (**8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity ratio factor
- $\mu$ = viscosity of continuous (oil) phase (Pa·s)

The **dielectrophoretic force** between two adjacent droplets pulls them together:

$$F_{DEP} = 2 \, \pi \, r^3 \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_{oil} \, \left(\frac{\varepsilon_w - \varepsilon_{oil}}{\varepsilon_w + 2\varepsilon_{oil}}\right) \nabla E^2$$

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting the Electric Desalter Performance

| # | Factor | Effect on Performance | Typical Operating Range |
|---|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Operating Temperature** | ↑ T → ↓ oil viscosity → ↑ coalescence rate | 90 – 150 °C |
| 2 | **Operating Pressure** | Must prevent vaporization (water flashing) at given T | 5 – 30 bar (g) |
| 3 | **Wash Water Rate** | Insufficient → poor salt extraction; excess → emulsion upset | 3 – 7 vol % |
| 4 | **Wash Water Quality** | High TDS water → poor salt removal; fresh condensate preferred | TDS < 50 ppm |
| 5 | **Mixing Valve ΔP** | Higher → finer dispersion but risk of tight emulsion | 0.7 – 1.4 bar |
| 6 | **Electric Field Strength** | ↑ E → ↑ migration velocity (∝ E²) | 1 – 3 kV/cm |
| 7 | **Demulsifier Dose** | Lowers interfacial tension; aids water release | 5 – 20 ppm |
| 8 | **Residence Time** | Minimum 5–10 min for full coalescence | 5 – 30 min |
| 9 | **Crude API Gravity** | Lighter crudes → lower viscosity → easier desalting | > 22 °API |
| 10 | **BS&W of Feed** | High BS&W → overloading of water boot | < 1.0 vol % |
| 11 | **pH of Wash Water** | Slightly alkaline (pH 7–9) neutralizes acidic salts | 7 – 9 |
| 12 | **Interfacial Tension (IFT)** | Lower IFT → easier coalescence | 5 – 25 dyne/cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** If asked "Which factor MOST affects desalting efficiency?", the answer is **operating temperature**, because it controls viscosity (μ) in the v_m equation — a 20 °C drop can triple the oil viscosity and cripple coalescence.

---

## 2.4 Pipe Still Furnace — Process Description

A modern pipe still furnace has three distinct zones:

### A. Radiant Section (Firebox)
- Heat is transferred by **radiation** from hot gases (flame + combustion products at **1500–1800 °C**) and refractory walls.
- Contains the **process coil** at the walls and/or roof.
- Heat flux: **25,000 – 45,000 W/m²** (typical design).
- Process fluid reaches **65–75%** of the total absorbed heat here.

### B. Convection Section (Above the Radiant)
- Located above the firebox; flue gases (~ 700–900 °C) flow upward, transferring heat by **convection** to shielded tubes.
- Used for **preheating** the incoming crude (cold side of the coil), superheating steam, generating MP steam, and preheating combustion air.

### C. Stack (Chimney)
- Releases flue gases to atmosphere. **Excess air** of **10–20%** is maintained.
- **Stack temperature** is the key indicator of waste heat — modern designs keep it at **150–250 °C** to maximize efficiency.

---

## 2.5 Heat Transfer Formula Sheet

| Equation | Formula | Application |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Heat Duty** | $Q = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Total heat absorbed by oil |
| **Radiant Heat Transfer** | $Q_{rad} = \sigma \, A \, (T_g^4 - T_s^4)$ | Stefan–Boltzmann (simplified) |
| **Convective Heat Transfer** | $Q_{conv} = h_c \, A \, \Delta T_{lm}$ | Convection bank |
| **LMTD** | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Counter-flow correction |
| **Overall U** | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Tube-wall conduction |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100\%$ | Furnace performance |
| **Heat Flux (Radiant)** | $q = \dfrac{Q_{rad}}{A_{radiant}}$ | Tube-side severity |
| **Stack Loss** | $Q_{stack} = \dot{m}_{fg} \, C_{p,fg} \, (T_{stack} - T_{amb})$ | Energy wasted |
| **Excess Air** | $\%EA = \dfrac{O_{2,measured}}{21 - O_{2,measured}} \times 100$ | Combustion tuning |
| **Combustion Eqn** | $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Stoichiometric basis |

---

## 2.6 Types of Pipe Still Furnace Coils

1. **Pure Radiant (Up-fired / Down-fired)** — Oldest; oil reaches temperature in a single pass through the radiant section only.
2. **Radiant + Convection (Modern)** — Convection section preheats oil first → smaller radiant duty → lower film temperature.
3. **Double-Fired Cabin** — Burners on both sides → uniform flux, used for heavy vacuum residue service.
4. **Helical Coil (Cylindrical Heater)** — Compact, used for small capacities (lab/pilot scale).

---

## 2.7 Operating Variables of a Pipe Still Furnace

| Variable | Range / Limit | Effect |
|----------|---------------|--------|
| **Outlet Temperature (Coil Skin)** | < **593 °C** (1100 °F) — API 530 limit | Above → rapid coking, tube rupture |
| **Tube Skin Temperature** | < 540 °C (carbon steel), < 700 °C (Cr-Mo) | Material creep limit |
| **Heat Flux (Peak)** | < 31,500 W/m² (10,000 BTU/hr·ft²) — typical | Higher → hot spots |
| **Excess Air** | 10 – 20 % | More → heat loss via N₂; Less → CO, soot |
| **Draft (Negative Pressure)** | –2 to –10 mm WC | Controls airflow |
| **Stack Temperature** | 150 – 250 °C (modern) | Lower → efficiency ↑ |
| **Fuel Pressure** | 1 – 4 bar (gas) | Atomization |
| **Burner Turndown Ratio** | 4 : 1 (modern) | Operational flexibility |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Migration Velocity of a Water Droplet in an Oil Medium Under an Electric Field

We begin with the force balance on a spherical droplet of radius $r$ in a non-uniform electric field, surrounded by an oil medium of viscosity $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Identify the electric force on a polarized sphere:**

For a conducting droplet (high dielectric constant) suspended in a poorly conducting medium, the induced dipole moment is:

$$p = 4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r^3 E$$

where $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ is the permittivity of the medium and $E$ is the local field strength.

**Step 2 — Express the dielectrophoretic force:**

The DEP force on a dipole in a non-uniform field is the gradient of the energy of the dipole:

$$F_{DEP} = \nabla(p \cdot E)$$

Assuming a dilute droplet and spherical symmetry:

$$F_{DEP} = 2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r^3 \nabla E^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Stokes' drag law in the creeping-flow regime:**

For very small droplets (Reynolds number Re < 1), drag is viscous:

$$F_{drag} = 6 \pi \mu r \, v_m$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state balance $F_{DEP} = F_{drag}$:**

$$6 \pi \mu r \, v_m = 2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r^3 \, \nabla E^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v_m$:**

$$v_m = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, r^2 \, \nabla E^2}{3 \mu}$$

In a uniform field between two parallel plates separated by distance $d$ with potential difference $V$:

$$\nabla E^2 = \frac{V^2}{d^2}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{v_m = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, r^2 \, V^2}{3 \, \mu \, d^2}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Because $v_m \propto V^2$, doubling the voltage **quadruples** the migration speed. This is why high-voltage transformers (35 kV class) are used in refinery desalters.

---

## 3.2 Sample Numerical — Pipe Still Furnace Heat Duty

**Problem:** A pipe still furnace heats 50,000 kg/hr of crude oil from 200 °C to 360 °C. The crude oil specific heat varies with temperature as $C_p = 1.8 + 0.002 T$ (kJ/kg·°C, with T in °C). Calculate the **heat duty** if the furnace thermal efficiency is 88%.

**Step 1 — Average $C_p$ over the temperature range:**

$$\overline{C_p} = \frac{1}{360 - 200} \int_{200}^{360} (1.8 + 0.002 T) \, dT$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{200}^{360} (1.8 + 0.002 T) \, dT = 1.8(360 - 200) + 0.001 (360^2 - 200^2)$$

$$= 288 + 0.001 \times (129600 - 40000) = 288 + 89.6 = 377.6 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Average $C_p$:**

$$\overline{C_p} = \frac{377.6}{160} = 2.36 \; \text{kJ/kg·°C}$$

**Step 4 — Heat absorbed by oil:**

$$Q_{abs} = \dot{m} \cdot \overline{C_p} \cdot \Delta T = \frac{50000}{3600} \times 2.36 \times 160$$

$$= 13.89 \times 2.36 \times 160 = 5245 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Fuel-side duty (gross):**

$$Q_{fuel} = \frac{Q_{abs}}{\eta_{th}} = \frac{5245}{0.88} = 5960 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to MMBtu/hr (1 kW = 3412.14 BTU/hr):**

$$Q_{fuel} = 5960 \times \frac{3412.14}{10^6} = 20.34 \; \text{MMBtu/hr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result check:** A 50,000 kg/hr crude charge corresponds to ~ 9,500 m³/day (~ 60,000 BPD). A duty of 20 MMBtu/hr is right in the typical range for a small atmospheric heater (15–40 MMBtu/hr).

---

## 3.3 Sample Numerical — Stack Loss Calculation

**Given:** Flue gas flow = 25,000 kg/hr, $C_{p,fg}$ = 1.05 kJ/kg·°C, Stack T = 220 °C, Ambient = 30 °C.

$$Q_{stack} = \dot{m}_{fg} \cdot C_{p,fg} \cdot (T_{stack} - T_{amb})$$

$$= \frac{25000}{3600} \times 1.05 \times (220 - 30) = 6.944 \times 1.05 \times 190$$

$$= 1385.3 \; \text{kW} \approx 4.73 \; \text{MMBtu/hr}$$

If total fuel input is 5960 kW, the **percentage stack loss** is:

$$\% \text{Stack Loss} = \frac{1385.3}{5960} \times 100 = 23.2\%$$

This is consistent with normal pipe still losses (15–25% via stack).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Single vs. Two-Stage Desalter

| Parameter | Single-Stage | Two-Stage |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| Salt Removal | 90–95 % | 99 % + |
| Outlet BS&W | < 0.2 vol% | < 0.1 vol% |
| Outlet Salt (PTB) | 5–10 | < 1 |
| CapEx | Lower | +40–60 % |
| OpEx (wash water, demulsifier) | Baseline | +20–30 % |
| Use Case | Sweet crudes, downstream hydrotreatment | Sour crudes, FCC feed prep |
| Common Refineries | Old / small units | Modern grass-root units |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Required Transformer Voltage

For plate electrodes separated by $d = 0.25$ m, target field strength $E = 2.5 \times 10^5$ V/m:

$$V = E \cdot d = 2.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.25 = 6.25 \times 10^4 \; \text{V} = 62.5 \; \text{kV}$$

→ This explains why desalter transformers operate in the **30–80 kV** class.

---

## 3.6 Coalescence Time — Rayleigh Estimate

For two equal droplets of radius $r$ to coalesce, the drainage time of the intervening oil film is:

$$t_{coal} = \frac{3 \mu \, r}{2 \sigma} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$$

where $\sigma$ is interfacial tension and $h_0, h_f$ are initial and final film thicknesses.

Plugging in: $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $r = 50 \; \mu$m, $\sigma = 20$ dyne/cm = 0.02 N/m, $h_0/h_f = 100$:

$$t_{coal} = \frac{3 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 0.02} \times \ln(100)$$

$$= 1.875 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.605 = 8.6 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{s}$$

→ Coalescence is essentially instantaneous at the droplet scale, but **bulk residence time** of 5–30 minutes is dictated by gravity settling and flow distribution, not by the coalescence event itself.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Electric Desalter Process

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State any three factors that affect the performance of an electric desalter and explain the role of temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

Three key factors affecting electric desalter performance:

1. **Operating Temperature:** ↑ T → ↓ oil viscosity (μ) → ↑ coalescence rate per $v_m \propto 1/\mu$. Typical range 90–150 °C.
2. **Wash Water Rate:** 3–7 vol% of crude charge; insufficient water fails to dissolve salts, excess water risks re-emulsification.
3. **Electric Field Strength:** Migration velocity $v_m \propto E^2$; doubling voltage quadruples separation rate. Typical field: 1–3 kV/cm.

**Role of Temperature:** Temperature lowers crude viscosity (a 20 °C rise roughly halves viscosity) and increases the dielectric contrast between water and oil, both effects accelerate droplet migration and coalescence. However, temperature must stay below the vapor pressure of water at operating pressure to avoid flashing.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between the radiant section and the convection section of a pipe still furnace.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Radiant Section | Convection Section |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Position | Below — firebox | Above — flue gas path |
| Heat Transfer Mode | Radiation (flame + refractory) | Convection (hot gas → tubes) |
| Temperature | 1500–1800 °C (gas); tube 300–400 °C | 700–900 °C (gas); tube 150–300 °C |
| Heat Share | 65–75 % of total duty | 25–35 % of total duty |
| Function | Brings crude to final discharge temperature | Preheats crude, generates steam |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — (a) & (b) [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **process and working of an electric desalter** used in petroleum refining. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:** An electric desalter removes water-soluble salts and entrained water from crude oil by exploiting the dielectric property difference between water (ε ≈ 80) and oil (ε ≈ 2.2). When subjected to a high-voltage AC field, polar water droplets align, attract each other, coalesce into larger drops and settle out by gravity.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Crude charge** enters from the feed surge drum through the charge pump at 8–15 bar.
2. **Demulsifier** (5–20 ppm) is injected upstream to break any pre-existing tight emulsion.
3. **Wash water** (3–7 vol%, slightly alkaline, pH 7–9) is added and mixed via a **mixing valve** (ΔP = 0.7–1.4 bar) creating a fine dispersion.
4. The dispersion enters the vessel through an **inlet distributor**, ensuring uniform cross-sectional flow.
5. Inside the vessel, the **electrode grid** (top electrode energized at 15–35 kV AC; bottom grounded) creates a strong electric field (1–3 kV/cm).
6. Polarized water droplets undergo **dielectrophoretic migration** and **coalesce** into larger drops.
7. Coalesced drops settle into the **water boot** at the bottom and are withdrawn under interface-level control.
8. **Desalted oil** exits from the top collector.

**Typical Performance:** Salt removal > 95%; BS&W < 0.1 vol%; outlet salt < 5 PTB.

**Sketch:** (Horizontal cylindrical vessel, two electrodes, water boot, inlet distributor, top outlet collector, brine outlet, transformer bushing on top.)

**(b)** Explain in detail the **factors affecting the performance of an electric desalter**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Key Factors — 1 mark each, 7 factors):**

1. **Temperature** (90–150 °C) — controls viscosity; optimal T minimizes μ without vaporizing wash water.
2. **Pressure** (5–30 bar) — must keep wash water in liquid phase; vapor flashing disrupts the field.
3. **Wash water rate and quality** (3–7 vol%, low TDS) — quantity determines salt extraction efficiency.
4. **Mixing valve ΔP** — must balance dispersion (good) vs. over-emulsification (bad).
5. **Electric field strength** — $v_m \propto E^2$; high E gives faster coalescence.
6. **Demulsifier type and dose** — chemical breakers tailored to crude type.
7. **Residence time** (5–30 min) — must allow settling of coalesced droplets.
8. **Feed characteristics** (API gravity, BS&W, salt content) — heavy crudes need higher T and longer residence time.

---

### Question B — (a) & (b) [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, describe the **construction and operation of a pipe still furnace**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A pipe still furnace consists of a **rectangular (or cylindrical) refractory-lined box** divided into three zones:

1. **Radiant Section (Firebox):** Located at the bottom, lined with refractory bricks; contains burners and the radiant coil. Combustion occurs at 1500–1800 °C.
2. **Convection Section:** Located directly above the radiant section. Contains shielded tube banks. Flue gases at 700–900 °C transfer heat by convection.
3. **Stack:** Vertical chimney releasing flue gases at 150–250 °C (modern designs).

**Process Coil:** A continuous bank of welded tubes (typically 4–6 in. OD, carbon steel or 9Cr-1Mo) arranged in vertical or horizontal passes, suspended from the convection section by alloy hangers.

**Operation:**
- **Fuel** (refinery gas or liquid fuel) + **air** (10–20% excess) → burners → combustion → hot gases.
- Hot gases rise, transfer heat first by **radiation** to the radiant coil, then by **convection** to the convection coil.
- Cold crude enters the **convection section** first (preheated to ~ 250 °C), then descends to the **radiant section** where it reaches 360–400 °C for atmospheric distillation.
- Flue gases leaving at 700 °C may pass through an **air preheater** (recuperator) to recover waste heat.

**Operating Variables:** Outlet temperature, tube skin T, heat flux, excess air, draft pressure, stack T.

**(b)** A pipe still furnace heats **80,000 kg/hr of crude oil** from **180 °C to 350 °C**. The mean specific heat of crude is **2.30 kJ/kg·°C**. The furnace thermal efficiency is **86%**. Calculate: (i) the **heat absorbed** by the oil, (ii) the **fuel-side heat duty**, and (iii) the **fuel oil consumption** if the calorific value of fuel oil is **42 MJ/kg**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m} = 80{,}000$ kg/hr = $22.22$ kg/s
- $T_{in} = 180$ °C, $T_{out} = 350$ °C, $\Delta T = 170$ °C
- $C_p = 2.30$ kJ/kg·°C
- $\eta_{th} = 0.86$
- $\text{GCV}_{fuel} = 42$ MJ/kg

**(i) Heat absorbed by oil:**

$$Q_{abs} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{abs} = 22.22 \times 2.30 \times 170 = 8687 \; \text{kW} \approx 8.69 \; \text{MW}$$

$$Q_{abs} = 8.69 \times \frac{3600}{10^3} = 31.27 \; \text{GJ/hr} = 29.65 \; \text{MMBtu/hr}$$

**(ii) Fuel-side heat duty (gross):**

$$Q_{fuel} = \frac{Q_{abs}}{\eta_{th}} = \frac{8687}{0.86} = 10{,}101 \; \text{kW}$$

$$Q_{fuel} = 10.10 \; \text{MW} = 36.34 \; \text{MMBtu/hr}$$

**(iii) Fuel oil consumption:**

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{Q_{fuel}}{\text{GCV}} = \frac{10{,}101 \; \text{kJ/s}}{42{,}000 \; \text{kJ/kg}} = 0.2405 \; \text{kg/s}$$

$$= 0.2405 \times 3600 = 865.8 \; \text{kg/hr} \approx 866 \; \text{kg/hr}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the sketch** — For a 7-mark question on desalter or furnace, a labelled diagram carries **2–3 marks**. Always draw even a hand-drawn schematic.
> 2. **Forgetting the formula units** — Examiner expects consistency: SI units throughout (Pa·s, V/m, kg/s). Mixing BTU/hr with kJ/kg loses marks.
> 3. **Confusing desalting efficiency vs. salt removal %** — They are different: efficiency refers to a single-stage reduction; removal is the cumulative.
> 4. **Ignoring excess air** in furnace efficiency — Stack loss calculation requires the flue gas flow rate, which depends on %EA.
> 5. **Writing "high voltage" without numbers** — Always state the kV range (15–35 kV AC).
> 6. **Wrong direction of proportionality** — Many students write $v_m \propto E$ instead of $E^2$ — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **No mention of tube skin temperature limit** — API 530 limit of **593 °C** is a mandatory safety citation in furnace questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Electric desalter **removes salts and water** using a high-voltage AC field; typical outlet spec is **< 1 PTB salt** and **< 0.1 vol% BS&W**.
- ✅ Key equation: $v_m = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r^2 V^2}{3 \mu d^2}$ — migration velocity is **quadratically** dependent on voltage.
- ✅ Most critical operating parameter: **temperature** (controls viscosity and dielectric contrast).
- ✅ Wash water rate: **3–7 vol%**; mixing valve ΔP: **0.7–1.4 bar**; field strength: **1–3 kV/cm**.
- ✅ Two-stage desalters are standard for **FCC feed preparation** in modern refineries.
- ✅ Pipe still furnace = **direct-fired heater** with a continuous tubular coil; **radiant + convection + stack** zones.
- ✅ Radiant section delivers **65–75 %** of the heat duty via radiation from flame and refractory.
- ✅ Tube skin temperature limit (carbon steel): **≤ 540 °C**; API 530 crude outlet limit: **≤ 593 °C**.
- ✅ Furnace thermal efficiency: **85–92 %** in modern designs; stack loss is the dominant inefficiency.
- ✅ Excess air: **10–20 %**; below 5% → CO and soot; above 25% → high stack loss.
- ✅ For any furnace calculation: $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$; account for $C_p$ varying with $T$ by integrating.
- ✅ Refinery pipe stills consume **1.5–3.5 % of crude charge** as fuel — the largest single utility consumer in the refinery.
- ✅ Demulsifier dose: **5–20 ppm**; pH of wash water: **7–9**; residence time: **5–30 min**.
- ✅ **Coalescence zone → settling zone → water boot** is the canonical internal sequence of a desalter vessel.
- ✅ Always draw a labelled schematic — **2–3 marks depend on it** in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sulphuric Acid Alkylation — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulphuric Acid Alkylation** is a catalytic refining process in which light olefins (typically C₃ to C₅) are reacted with isobutane in the presence of concentrated sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄, **98–99 wt%**) as a catalyst at low temperatures (**−7 °C to +5 °C**) to produce a high-octane, branched-chain hydrocarbon product known as **alkylate**, which is a premium gasoline blending stock.

The reaction is fundamentally a **C–C bond formation** between an isoparaffin (isobutane) and an olefin (e.g., 2-butene) following the **Markovnikov addition rule**, but uniquely catalysed by a liquid acid rather than a solid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Marriage Bureau"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Matchmaking of Molecules"**
> Imagine olefins (highly reactive, "flirty" molecules with double bonds) walking into a matchmaking event. Isobutane is the stable, calm partner. Normally they wouldn't combine at room temperature. But **sulphuric acid acts as the chaperone/catalyst** that brings them together, calms them down, and facilitates a permanent bond, producing a stable, branched, high-octane "married couple" — the alkylate molecule. The reaction is so exothermic (heat-generating) that it has to be cooled continuously, just like a passionate bond that needs temperature control!

### 1.3 Treatment of Gasoline — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Treatment of Gasoline** refers to a series of physical and chemical finishing processes applied to gasoline streams to remove corrosive contaminants (mercaptans, H₂S, dissolved water), reduce gum-forming tendencies, lower sulphur content, and improve storage stability, odor, and colour, ensuring the final product meets **BS-VI/BS-VI** specifications.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spa Day for Gasoline"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Detox Spa"**
> Crude gasoline streams are like a person coming in from a long journey — dirty, smelly, and unstable. Treatment processes act like a multi-step spa:
> - **Caustic wash** = soap bath (removes acidic impurities like H₂S)
> - **Merox/Douctor sweetening** = perfume application (removes foul-smelling mercaptans)
> - **Clay contacting** = face mask (absorbs colour bodies and gums)
> - **Hydrodesulphurization** = full body detox (removes sulphur compounds)

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Process Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Acid concentration** | 98–99.5 wt% H₂SO₄ |
| **Reactor temperature** | −7 °C to +5 °C |
| **Reactor pressure** | 0.3 – 1.0 atm (gauge low) |
| **I/O ratio (Isobutane:Olefin)** | 5:1 to 15:1 (molar) |
| **Acid-to-hydrocarbon ratio** | 1:1 to 2:1 (volume) |
| **Contact time** | 5 – 40 minutes |
| **Acid consumption** | 0.5 – 1.0 lb acid/bbl alkylate |
| **Alkylate RON** | 92 – 96 |
| **Alkylate MON** | 90 – 94 |
| **Alkylate vapour pressure (RVP)** | 4 – 7 psi (low) |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Sulphuric Acid Alkylation

### 2.1.1 Primary Reaction (Isobutane + 2-Butene → Iso-octane)

The principal reaction produces **2,2,4-trimethylpentane (isooctane)**, the historical 100-octane reference fuel.

$$ \text{i-C}_4\text{H}_{10} + \text{C}_4\text{H}_8 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4, \, 0-5^\circ\text{C}} \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} \quad (\text{RON} = 100) $$

### 2.1.2 Reaction Mechanism (Carbenium Ion Pathway)

The reaction proceeds via a **two-phase liquid-liquid** mechanism:

1. **Olefin protonation** — H₂SO₄ donates a proton (H⁺) to the olefin, forming a **carbenium ion (carbocation)** in the acid phase.
2. **Hydride transfer** — The carbenium ion abstracts a hydride (H⁻) from isobutane, generating a **tert-butyl cation** and a saturated olefin-derived product.
3. **Alkylation (C–C coupling)** — The tert-butyl cation adds to another olefin molecule, forming a larger carbenium ion.
4. **Hydride transfer termination** — The larger carbenium ion abstracts a hydride from another isobutane, terminating the chain and producing the final **alkylate** while regenerating the tert-butyl cation.

### 2.1.3 Sequential Logic of Process Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Process Logic**
> 1. **Feed preparation** — Olefins (C₃=–C₅=) from FCC/coker are dried and treated to remove basic nitrogen and oxygenates.
> 2. **Mixing with isobutane** — Fresh isobutane is recycled at very high I/O ratio (5–15:1) to suppress side reactions (polymerization, olefin–olefin coupling).
> 3. **Acid–hydrocarbon contacting** — H₂SO₄ and HC are emulsified in a contactor to maximize interfacial area.
> 4. **Reaction** — Carbenium ion chain mechanism produces alkylate.
> 5. **Acid settling** — Gravity separation in a settler; acid recycled to contactor.
> 6. **Caustic wash** — Removes entrained acid from hydrocarbon phase.
> 7. **Water wash & sand/filter** — Removes residual caustic and water.
> 8. **De-isobutanizer (DIB)** — Separates unreacted iC₄ (recycled) from alkylate product.
> 9. **Acid regeneration** — Spent acid (88–90% H₂SO₄) sent to regeneration plant.

## 2.2 Process Variables — The "Big Four"

| Variable | Effect on Alkylate Quality | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Lower T → Higher RON, less cracking | −7 to +5 °C |
| **Acid strength** | Higher H₂SO₄ → better octane | 98–99.5% |
| **I/O ratio** | Higher I/O → fewer side reactions | 5:1 to 15:1 |
| **Contact time** | Optimum ~10–25 min | 5–40 min |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why low temperature is critical:** At higher temperatures, secondary reactions (olefin cracking, polymerization, disproportionation) dominate, producing lighter gases and low-octane polymer gasoline instead of clean alkylate.

## 2.3 Treatment of Gasoline — Process Taxonomy

### 2.3.1 Caustic Washing (Sour Water Treatment)

$$ \text{H}_2\text{S} + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{S} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{RSH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{RSNa} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (\text{partial}) $$

### 2.3.2 Merox Process (Sweetening) — Most KTU-Favoured

**Merox = Mercaptan Oxidation** (developed by UOP)

- Uses a fixed-bed catalyst of **activated carbon impregnated with cobalt phthalocyanine** (or chelate).
- Catalyses the air oxidation of mercaptans (RSH) to disulphides (RSSR), which are odourless and non-corrosive.

$$ 4\text{RSH} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Co-Pc, NaOH}} 2\text{RSSR} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulphur is NOT removed in Merox** — it is merely *converted* from malodorous mercaptans into less odorous disulphides. The total sulphur content remains the same.

### 2.3.3 Doctor Test (Quality Control Check)

$$ 2\text{RSH} + \text{Na}_2\text{PbO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Pb(RS)}_2 + 2\text{NaOH} $$

$$ \text{Pb(RS)}_2 + \text{S} \rightarrow \text{PbS} + \text{RSSR} \quad (\text{positive test} = \text{black ppt}) $$

A **negative Doctor test** (no black precipitate after sulphur addition) indicates gasoline is "sweet" — i.e., low in mercaptans.

### 2.3.4 Clay Treatment (Percolation/Contact Filtration)

- **Fuller's earth** or **bauxite** (natural aluminosilicates) selectively adsorb colour bodies, gum precursors, and trace polar contaminants.
- Used for thermally cracked gasolines and as a final polish.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reaction stoichiometry (general) | $i\text{-C}_4 + n\text{-C}_n^= \rightarrow \text{Alkylate}$ | Material balance |
| 2 | Sulphuric acid consumption | $A_c = \frac{V_{\text{acid spent}}}{\text{bbl alkylate}}$ | ~0.5–1.0 lb/bbl |
| 3 | I/O molar ratio | $R_{I/O} = \frac{n_{i\text{C}_4}}{n_{\text{olefin}}}$ | Control selectivity |
| 4 | Octane number (research) | $\text{RON} = f(\text{branching, MW})$ | Quality check |
| 5 | Mercaptan conversion (Merox) | $\text{Conv.} = \frac{[\text{RSH}]_{\text{in}} - [\text{RSH}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{RSH}]_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Process efficiency |
| 6 | Acid strength relationship | $S_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \geq 98\%$ | Quality gate |
| 7 | Olefin conversion | $X_{\text{olefin}} = 1 - \frac{F_{\text{out}}}{F_{\text{in}}}$ | Reactor performance |
| 8 | Yield (vol%) | $Y = \frac{V_{\text{alkylate, out}}}{V_{\text{olefin, in}}} \times 100$ | Process economics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Alkylate is the most important blending component for high-octane gasoline pools because it has **zero olefins, zero aromatics, near-zero sulphur, low RVP, and high RON/MON** — making it ideal for modern BS-VI fuels where refineries must simultaneously meet octane, sulphur, and RVP specifications.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Octane Blending Calculation for a Gasoline Pool

A refinery needs to produce **10,000 bbl/day** of finished gasoline with a target **RON = 91**. Available blendstocks:

- **Reformate**: 5000 bbl/day, RON = 98
- **FCC gasoline**: 4000 bbl/day, RON = 92
- **Alkylate**: 2000 bbl/day, RON = 94
- **Straight-run naphtha**: 1000 bbl/day, RON = 70

> **Required:** Calculate the volumetric-average RON of the pool. Identify if it meets the target.

**Step 1 — Weighted RON summation:**

The volumetric weighted average RON is given by:

$$ \text{RON}_{\text{pool}} = \frac{\sum (V_i \times \text{RON}_i)}{\sum V_i} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute volumes and RONs:**

$$ \text{RON}_{\text{pool}} = \frac{(5000)(98) + (4000)(92) + (2000)(94) + (1000)(70)}{5000 + 4000 + 2000 + 1000} $$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator term-by-term:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
5000 \times 98 &= 490{,}000 \\
4000 \times 92 &= 368{,}000 \\
2000 \times 94 &= 188{,}000 \\
1000 \times 70 &= 70{,}000
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Sum the numerator:**

$$ \sum (V_i \times \text{RON}_i) = 490{,}000 + 368{,}000 + 188{,}000 + 70{,}000 = 1{,}116{,}000 $$

**Step 5 — Sum the denominator (total volume):**

$$ \sum V_i = 5000 + 4000 + 2000 + 1000 = 12{,}000 \; \text{bbl/day} $$

**Step 6 — Final RON calculation:**

$$ \text{RON}_{\text{pool}} = \frac{1{,}116{,}000}{12{,}000} = 93.0 $$

**Step 7 — Check against target:**

$$ \text{RON}_{\text{pool}} = 93.0 \;>\; \text{RON}_{\text{target}} = 91 \quad \checkmark \;\text{SPECIFICATION MET} $$

> **Result:** The blended pool achieves a RON of **93.0**, comfortably exceeding the target of 91.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Balance on a Sulphuric Acid Alkylation Unit

A unit processes **3000 bbl/day** of mixed olefin feed (60 mol% 1-butene, 40 mol% propylene) with **recycle isobutane** at an I/O molar ratio of **8:1**. Conversion of olefins is **100%**, alkylate yield is **1.78 vol/vol of olefin fed**.

> **Required:** Compute (a) total isobutane feed rate, (b) total alkylate production rate, (c) fresh acid consumption at 0.7 lb/bbl alkylate.

**Step 1 — Determine olefin molar flow rate (assumed basis):**

Assume olefin feed: 3000 bbl/day. Average molecular weight of mixed olefins (60% C₄= + 40% C₃=):

$$ \begin{aligned}
M_{\text{mix}} &= 0.60 \times 56 \;(\text{C}_4) + 0.40 \times 42 \;(\text{C}_3) \\
&= 33.6 + 16.8 = 50.4 \; \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 2 — Compute olefin molar flow:**

$$ F_{\text{olefin}} = \frac{3000 \times 42 \times 8.34}{50.4} = \frac{1{,}050{,}840}{50.4} \approx 20{,}851 \; \text{lbmol/day} $$

(using 1 bbl ≈ 42 gal, 1 gal ≈ 8.34 lb water equivalent — for mass approx.)

**Step 3 — Total isobutane feed (I/O = 8:1):**

$$ F_{\text{iC}_4} = 8 \times F_{\text{olefin}} = 8 \times 20{,}851 = 166{,}808 \; \text{lbmol/day} $$

In volumetric terms (iC₄ density ≈ 5.0 lb/gal):

$$ V_{\text{iC}_4} = \frac{166{,}808 \times 58}{5.0 \times 42} \approx 46{,}066 \; \text{bbl/day} $$

**Step 4 — Total alkylate production:**

$$ V_{\text{alkylate}} = 1.78 \times V_{\text{olefin}} = 1.78 \times 3000 = 5340 \; \text{bbl/day} $$

**Step 5 — Fresh acid consumption:**

$$ m_{\text{acid}} = 0.7 \;\frac{\text{lb}}{\text{bbl}} \times 5340 \; \text{bbl/day} = 3738 \; \text{lb/day} \approx 1.87 \; \text{ton/day} $$

> **Result:** The unit produces **5340 bbl/day alkylate** and consumes **~1.87 tons/day** of fresh H₂SO₄.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Efficiency in Merox Sweetening

A Merox extractor treats **5000 bbl/day** of FCC gasoline. Inlet mercaptan sulphur = **350 ppmw**, outlet = **15 ppmw**.

**Step 1 — Mercaptan conversion formula:**

$$ X_{\text{RSH}} = \frac{[\text{RSH}]_{\text{in}} - [\text{RSH}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{RSH}]_{\text{in}}} \times 100 $$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$ X_{\text{RSH}} = \frac{350 - 15}{350} \times 100 = \frac{335}{350} \times 100 = 95.71\% $$

**Step 3 — Doctor test prediction:**

Since outlet mercaptan is **15 ppm < 30 ppm threshold**, the gasoline will pass the **Doctor test** (negative).

> **Result:** Merox unit achieves **95.71% mercaptan conversion**, well above the typical **90% specification**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Alkylate | Reformate | FCC Naphtha | Isomerate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RON | 92–96 | 95–102 | 88–93 | 87–92 |
| RVP (psi) | 4–5 | 7–10 | 8–12 | 12–16 |
| Sulphur (ppm) | <10 | <10 | 100–1000 | <5 |
| Olefins (vol%) | <1 | <1 | 20–40 | <1 |
| Aromatics (vol%) | <1 | 50–70 | 15–30 | <1 |
| Sensitivity (RON−MON) | 2–3 | 5–10 | 8–12 | 1–2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Alkylate has the **lowest sensitivity (RON − MON)** of all gasoline blendstocks, making it especially valuable in modern engines that operate at high MON-sensitive conditions. This is why alkylate is considered the *gold standard* of gasoline components.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Explain the role of sulphuric acid in the alkylation process. Why is 98–99% H₂SO₄ preferred over dilute acid?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sulphuric acid functions as a **liquid acid catalyst** that protonates the olefin feed to generate a **carbenium ion (carbocation)** intermediate, which then undergoes hydride transfer with isobutane and C–C coupling to form the alkylate. Concentrated H₂SO₄ (98–99 wt%) is preferred because:

1. **(1 mark)** It provides the necessary **protonating strength (Hammett acidity)** to ionize the olefin.
2. **(1 mark)** It minimizes side reactions such as **olefin polymerization and disproportionation** that are favoured at lower acid strength.
3. **(1 mark)** Dilute acid (e.g., 90%) leads to **acid-soluble oil (ASO) formation** and rapid acid consumption, raising operating costs.

---

### Question 2
**What is the Doctor test? Write the chemistry involved and explain its significance.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Doctor test** is a qualitative test to detect the presence of **mercaptans (RSH)** in gasoline.

**Chemistry (2 marks):**
- Mercaptan reacts with sodium plumbite (Na₂PbO₂) to form a colourless lead mercaptide:
$$ 2\text{RSH} + \text{Na}_2\text{PbO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Pb(RS)}_2 + 2\text{NaOH} $$
- On addition of elemental sulphur, if mercaptans are present, PbS (black precipitate) forms:
$$ \text{Pb(RS)}_2 + \text{S} \rightarrow \text{PbS (black)} + \text{RSSR} $$

**Significance (1 mark):** A **negative test (no black ppt)** indicates the gasoline is "sweet" and meets the **30 ppm mercaptan sulphur** specification, confirming that the product is non-corrosive and saleable.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Choice 1

**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, explain the **sulphuric acid alkylation process**. Discuss the major process variables and their effect on alkylate quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **treatment processes for gasoline**. Compare the **Merox process and Doctor sweetening** with respect to chemistry, equipment, and product quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Process Flow (3 marks):**
The process consists of:
1. **Feed preparation** — Olefin feed (from FCC) and isobutane (fresh + recycle) are mixed in a high I/O ratio.
2. **Contactor/Reaction section** — The mixture is contacted with **98% H₂SO₄** in a Stratco contactor at 0–5 °C.
3. **Settler** — Emulsion breaks; acid (bottom) recycled; HC phase goes forward.
4. **Caustic wash + water wash** — Removes entrained acid and alkalinity.
5. **Deisobutanizer (DIB)** — Recovers unreacted iC₄ overhead; alkylate bottoms.

**Process variables and effects (4 marks):**

| Variable | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Lower T → higher RON, suppresses side reactions (cracking, polymerization) |
| **I/O ratio** | Higher I/O → fewer olefin–olefin reactions, higher octane |
| **Acid strength** | 98–99% H₂SO₄ → best RON; lower → ASO formation |
| **Contact time** | 10–25 min optimal; too short → low conversion; too long → secondary reactions |

**Conclusion:** Alkylate produced has RON 92–96, MON 90–94, zero olefins, zero aromatics.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Treatment of Gasoline (3.5 marks):**
The main treatment processes are:
- **Caustic washing** — removes H₂S and some RSH using 5–15% NaOH.
- **Merox process** — catalytic oxidation of mercaptans to disulphides using cobalt phthalocyanine.
- **Doctor sweetening** — older lead-based oxidation of mercaptans; largely obsolete.
- **Clay treatment** — percolation of gasoline through Fuller's earth to remove colour bodies and gums.
- **Hydrodesulphurization (HDS)** — removes total sulphur using Co–Mo catalyst at 300–400 °C with H₂.

**Merox vs Doctor comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Merox | Doctor |
|---|---|---|
| Chemistry | Air oxidation of RSH to RSSR | PbO-based oxidation of RSH |
| Catalyst | Co-phthalocyanine | Sodium plumbite |
| Toxicity | Non-toxic, environmentally friendly | Uses toxic lead compounds |
| Product | Disulphide remains in gasoline (sweet) | Disulphide remains in gasoline |
| Status | Modern industry standard | Largely obsolete |

---

### Question B — Choice 2

**(a)** Explain the **reaction mechanism of sulphuric acid alkylation** with a suitable example. Discuss why the I/O ratio is kept very high. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Merox process** for gasoline sweetening with a flow diagram. State the role of cobalt phthalocyanine catalyst and discuss the difference between Merox extraction and Merox sweetening. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Mechanism — Carbenium Ion Pathway (5 marks):**

Take isobutane + 2-butene → 2,2,4-trimethylpentane as example.

**Step 1: Olefin protonation**
- The π-bond of 2-butene accepts a proton (H⁺) from H₂SO₄ to form a sec-butyl carbocation.

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{-CH=CH-CH}_3 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{-}\overset{+}{\text{CH}}\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_3 $$

**Step 2: Hydride transfer**
- The sec-butyl carbocation abstracts a hydride (H⁻) from isobutane, producing n-butane and a tert-butyl cation.

$$ \text{sec-C}_4^+ + \text{i-C}_4\text{H}_{10} \rightarrow \text{n-C}_4\text{H}_{10} + \text{t-C}_4^+ $$

**Step 3: Alkylation (C–C coupling)**
- The tert-butyl cation adds to a fresh 2-butene molecule, forming a C₈ carbenium ion.

$$ \text{t-C}_4^+ + \text{CH}_3\text{-CH=CH-CH}_3 \rightarrow \text{C}_8^+ $$

**Step 4: Hydride transfer termination**
- The C₈ carbenium ion abstracts a hydride from another isobutane, giving 2,2,4-trimethylpentane (isooctane) and regenerating the tert-butyl cation (chain propagation).

$$ \text{C}_8^+ + \text{i-C}_4\text{H}_{10} \rightarrow \text{i-C}_8\text{H}_{18} + \text{t-C}_4^+ $$

**Why high I/O ratio (2 marks):**
- High isobutane concentration **suppresses olefin–olefin reactions** (which would produce polymer gasoline and low-octane by-products).
- Ensures the dominant reaction is **isobutane + olefin → alkylate**, maximizing RON.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Merox Process Description (4 marks):**
The Merox (Mercaptan Oxidation) process is a UOP-developed catalytic process for **sweetening gasoline** by converting corrosive, malodorous mercaptans (RSH) into odourless disulphides (RSSR).

**Process flow:**
1. **Extractor** — Gasoline is contacted with **caustic solution (NaOH)** in a counter-current extractor. RSH transfers from gasoline to caustic as sodium mercaptide (RSNa).
2. **Oxidizer** — The mercaptide-rich caustic is sent to an oxidizer where **air** is sparged in presence of **cobalt phthalocyanine** catalyst supported on activated carbon.
3. **Separator** — The aqueous phase is separated from the disulphide oil; the aqueous caustic is recycled.
4. **Polishing** — Sweetened gasoline is water-washed and sent to storage.

**Chemistry:**
$$ 4\text{RS}^- + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Co-Pc}} 2\text{RSSR} + 4\text{OH}^- $$

**Role of cobalt phthalocyanine (1.5 marks):**
- It catalyses the **single-electron-transfer oxidation** of mercaptide ions.
- The cobalt centre cycles between Co²⁺ and Co³⁺ oxidation states.
- Allows reaction to proceed at ambient temperature and pressure — energy efficient.

**Merox Extraction vs Merox Sweetening (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Merox Extraction | Merox Sweetening |
|---|---|---|
| Location of oxidation | In separate oxidizer vessel | In situ in extractor (in gasoline phase) |
| Feed mercaptan level | High (>100 ppm) — needs removal | Low (<100 ppm) — direct oxidation |
| Caustic contact | Yes — mercaptans extracted first | Minimal — direct in gasoline |
| Application | Naphtha, kerosene | Cracked gasoline, lighter feeds |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse "removal" with "conversion"** — Merox does NOT remove sulphur; it converts mercaptan-S to disulphide-S. If the question asks for "sulphur removal," answer **Hydrodesulphurization (HDS)**, not Merox.
> - **Do not write "dilute H₂SO₄"** for alkylation — always specify **98–99 wt%** concentrated acid. Examiners specifically test this.
> - **Reaction mechanism steps must be in order** — protonation → hydride transfer → C–C coupling → hydride transfer termination. Skipping the hydride transfer step loses 2–3 marks.
> - **In alkylate RON calculation**, examiners often expect you to mention **both RON and MON** and the **sensitivity (RON − MON)**.
> - For Doctor test, **black precipitate = positive (bad)**, **no ppt = negative (good)** — students often flip this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Alkylation** = isobutane + olefin → high-octane alkylate via **carbenium ion** mechanism.
- **Catalyst** = 98–99% **H₂SO₄** at 0–5 °C (low T is critical).
- **I/O ratio** must be very high (5:1 to 15:1) to suppress olefin polymerization.
- **Alkylate properties:** RON 92–96, low RVP, zero olefins/aromatics, near-zero sulphur — the **gold standard** gasoline blendstock.
- **Process equipment:** Stratco contactor → acid settler → caustic wash → water wash → deisobutanizer → product.
- **Merox** = Mercaptan Oxidation, uses **Co-phthalocyanine** catalyst, converts RSH → RSSR (sulphur not removed).
- **Merox ≠ Sulphur removal**; for total S removal use **Hydrodesulphurization (HDS)**.
- **Doctor test** uses **sodium plumbite** + sulphur; black PbS = positive (mercaptan present).
- **Caustic wash** removes H₂S, phenols, and some mercaptans; uses 5–15% NaOH.
- **Clay treatment** (Fuller's earth) removes colour bodies, gum precursors, trace polar compounds.
- **Acid-soluble oil (ASO)** = red oil formed when H₂SO₄ strength drops; reduces alkylate yield and quality.
- **Spent acid** (88–90% H₂SO₄) is sent to regeneration, not directly discarded.
- **Sensitivity (RON − MON)** of alkylate is the **lowest** among all blendstocks (~2–3).
- **Alkylate has no olefins and no aromatics**, making it ideal for clean-fuel (BS-VI) gasoline pools.
- **Temperature rise** during alkylation can be controlled using autorefrigeration (evaporating iC₄) or jacket cooling.
- **Acid regeneration** recovers 98% H₂SO₄ from spent acid; small make-up acid is added.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:**
> For a 14-mark question, always: (1) draw a labelled PFD, (2) list the operating conditions in a table, (3) show the chemistry with balanced equations, (4) discuss the engineering trade-offs, and (5) give a 1-line conclusion. KTU examiners award the maximum marks to answers that are *visually structured* and *numerically quantified*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Copper chloride process and Merox sweetening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Copper chloride process and Merox sweetening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Copper Chloride Sweetening Process

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **Copper Chloride Sweetening Process** (also known as the *Cuprous Chloride Process* or *CuCl₂ Sweetening Process*) is a liquid-liquid catalytic treatment used in petroleum refineries to convert undesirable **mercaptans (RSH)** present in gasoline, kerosene, jet fuel, and similar light-to-middle distillate fractions into **disulfides (RSSR)**, thereby eliminating the foul, acidic, and corrosive nature of sulfur compounds. The chemistry relies on the reversible redox couple between cuprous (Cu⁺) and cupric (Cu²⁺) ions in a strongly alkaline aqueous medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Copper Chloride Process belongs to the *Chemical Sweetening* family — a category of processes that *convert* mercaptans (rather than physically extract them), making it distinct from extraction-based methods like Merox Extraction.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Bleach Bucket" Idea:**
Imagine you have a white shirt stained with curry (mercaptans = foul smell). Two ways to clean it:
1. **Wash it away** with detergent (extractive process — physically removes mercaptans).
2. **Bleach it in place** — the stain stays, but it becomes invisible and harmless (chemical sweetening — converts RSH to RSSR, which is odourless and non-corrosive).

The Copper Chloride Process is essentially the **"bleach-in-place"** method. The mercaptans don't leave the hydrocarbon; they are *chemically transformed* into disulfides that stay dissolved in the oil but don't smell or corrode. The "bleach" (cupric chloride) is regenerated continuously using air, just like a washing-machine bleach dispenser that refills itself.

## 1.2 The Merox Sweetening Process

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
**Merox** (an acronym for **Mercaptan Oxidation**) is a proprietary catalytic process developed by **UOP LLC (Honeywell UOP)** that removes or converts mercaptans in petroleum fractions by catalytic oxidation with air in the presence of a caustic environment. It uses an organometallic catalyst (a **phthalocyanine chelate of cobalt**, often called the *Merox catalyst*) and operates in two principal configurations:
- **Merox Extraction** — for lighter fractions (LPG, straight-run naphtha).
- **Merox Sweetening (Fixed-Bed)** — for heavier fractions (kerosene, diesel, jet fuel).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Oxygen Bar" for Sulphur:**
Picture mercaptans as foul-smelling passengers on a bus (the hydrocarbon stream). The Merox process opens a *window* (the catalyst bed) and forces them to breathe in fresh oxygen (air), after which they peacefully pair up with each other to form odourless, harmless disulphide "couples" that just ride along quietly. The catalyst acts as the *matchmaker* and the *breath-freshener* at the same time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Adoption Note:** Merox is the **single most widely installed** mercaptan-treatment process in the world, with hundreds of licensed units operating in refineries across every continent. It is, in essence, the *default* sweetening technology in modern refining.

## 1.3 Why These Processes Matter — Standard Refining Metrics

| Metric | Typical Target Value (sweet product) | Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mercaptan Sulfur content** | < **5 ppm (mg/kg)** for jet fuel; < **10 ppm** for gasoline | Corrosion, odour, doctor-test negativity |
| **Doctor test** | **Negative (SWEET)** | Standard qualitative KTU/industry pass criterion |
| **Total Sulfur (after sweetening)** | Reduced only marginally (disulfides stay) | Distinguishes sweetening from HDS |
| **Operating temperature** | CuCl: **40–60 °C**; Merox: **35–65 °C** | Mild, low-energy process |
| **Caustic strength** | **2–10 wt% NaOH** | Drives mercaptan ionisation (extraction) |
| **Air requirement (Merox)** | Stoichiometric + ~10 % excess | Critical for complete oxidation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box — Sweetening vs. Desulfurization:**
> **Sweetening** = removes only the *reactive* sulfur (mercaptans), preserving the molecule skeleton.
> **Desulfurization (HDS)** = removes *total* sulfur by breaking C–S bonds (requires H₂, high T, high P).
> A sweet product is not necessarily a low-sulfur product. **KCU 2024 examiners love this distinction.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Copper Chloride (CuCl₂) Process — Chemistry

The process operates in **two coupled reaction stages** performed simultaneously in a single contactor or in separate extractor/regenerator vessels:

### Stage 1 — Mercaptan Absorption into Caustic
In a **strong alkaline (NaOH) aqueous phase**, mercaptans (weak acids, pKₐ ≈ 10–11) are neutralised to form **sodium mercaptides**:

$$
\text{RSH} + \text{NaOH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RS}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The mercaptide ion (RS⁻) migrates into the aqueous caustic phase, leaving the hydrocarbon raffinate *mercaptan-free*.

### Stage 2 — Catalytic Oxidation by Cupric Chloride
The dissolved mercaptide is then oxidised by **cupric chloride (CuCl₂)** to the corresponding **disulfide**, while Cu²⁺ is reduced to Cu⁺ (cuprous chloride):

$$
4\,\text{RS}^-\text{Na}^+ + 2\,\text{CuCl}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{RSSR} + 4\,\text{NaCl} + 2\,\text{CuCl}
$$

The cuprous chloride is **insoluble** in the caustic phase and precipitates as a sludge — this is the chief operational drawback of the classical process. A modified version keeps Cu⁺ soluble using complexing agents (e.g., NH₃ or amines) — the **Chloroamine Process** variant.

### Stage 3 — Catalyst Regeneration by Air
The precipitated CuCl is re-oxidised back to CuCl₂ by air in the regenerator:

$$
4\,\text{CuCl} + 4\,\text{HCl} + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 4\,\text{CuCl}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The regenerated CuCl₂ is recycled to the contactor — closing the catalytic loop. The overall net reaction across the loop is:

$$
4\,\text{RSH} + \text{O}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{NaOH, CuCl}_2}\; 2\,\text{RSSR} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Net stoichiometry is identical to Merox** — both processes fundamentally oxidise mercaptans to disulfides using O₂. The difference lies in the *catalyst system* (homogeneous Cu salt vs. heterogeneous Co-phthalocyanine).

## 2.2 Merox Process — Chemistry

### Two Principal Configurations

**A) Merox Extraction (for light ends — LPG, LR naphtha)**
1. Mercaptans extracted from hydrocarbon into **10–20 % caustic** at the bottom of an extraction column.
2. Mercaptide-rich caustic sent to a **regenerator** where air is bubbled in the presence of **Merox catalyst (Co-phthalocyanine disulphonate)** dissolved in caustic.
3. Disulfides separate as an insoluble oil phase and are decanted; regenerated caustic is recycled.

**B) Merox Fixed-Bed Sweetening (for kerosene, diesel, jet fuel)**
1. Caustic is **pre-impregnated** into a **solid Merox catalyst** (Co-phthalocyanine on activated carbon support).
2. Hydrocarbon + air are co-currently passed down through the fixed bed at **35–65 °C, 4–8 bar (g)**.
3. Mercaptans react in a single step on the catalyst surface — no separate caustic circulation needed.

### Governing Reaction (Universal Merox Stoichiometry)
$$
2\,\text{RSH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Merox catalyst, NaOH}}\; \text{RSSR} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

For mercaptans with high molecular weight (heavy kerosene, diesel), the disulfide RSSR is oil-soluble and stays in the product — this is acceptable because RSSR is sweet (non-corrosive) and non-odorous.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cobalt phthalocyanine?** The macrocyclic Co²⁺/Co³⁺ redox couple, sandwiched inside a planar N₄-donor phthalocyanine ring, mimics biological oxidation enzymes (cytochrome P-450). The ligand environment prevents Co(OH)₂ precipitation that would otherwise occur in caustic, and tunes the redox potential (~ +0.4 V vs. NHE) — *just right* for selective mercaptide oxidation without burning the hydrocarbon.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stoichiometric O₂ for Merox** | $n_{O_2} = \dfrac{m_{S}}{32{,}000} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{m_{S}}{64{,}000}$ | $m_S$ = mass of mercaptan S to be removed (kg); gives moles O₂ |
| 2 | **Air required (theoretical)** | $V_{air} = \dfrac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} \times 22.4$ | Nm³ at STP; 21 % O₂ in air |
| 3 | **Actual air (with excess)** | $V_{air,\,act} = 1.10 \times V_{air}$ | Typical 10 % excess for complete conversion |
| 4 | **Caustic-to-oil ratio (extraction)** | $\phi = \dfrac{Q_{caustic}}{Q_{oil}}$ | Typical **0.05 – 0.20** (v/v) |
| 5 | **Distribution coefficient (extraction)** | $K_D = \dfrac{C_{RSH,\,aq}}{C_{RSH,\,oil}}$ | Higher K_D ⇒ easier extraction; function of pKₐ, pH |
| 6 | **Conversion (sweetening)** | $X_{RSH} = \dfrac{C_{RSH,\,in} - C_{RSH,\,out}}{C_{RSH,\,in}}$ | Dimensionless; target **X ≥ 0.95** |
| 7 | **Net CuCl₂ mass balance (Cu-Cl process)** | $m_{CuCl_2,\,recirc} = m_{S} \times \dfrac{134.5}{32} = 4.20\,m_S$ | Recirculation rate per kg of S removed |
| 8 | **Disulfide molecular weight** | $M_{RSSR} = 2\,M_{RSH} - 2$ | Loss of 2 H atoms during oxidation |
| 9 | **Merox catalyst loading (fixed bed)** | $0.5 - 2.0$ wt% Co on activated carbon | Commercial specification |
| 10 | **Operating T window** | $T_{op} = 308\text{–}338\,\text{K}$ | i.e., **35–65 °C** |
| 11 | **Pressure (fixed-bed Merox)** | $P = 4\text{–}8\,\text{bar(g)}$ | Ensures O₂ remains dissolved |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas drive the **Process Design Basis** package of any new sweetening unit. KTU 2024 questions often test the stoichiometric air calculation (rows 1–3) as a 7-mark sub-question in Part B.

## 2.4 Operational Comparison — CuCl vs. Merox

| Aspect | Copper Chloride | Merox |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst state** | Homogeneous (in caustic) | Heterogeneous (fixed bed) or homogeneous |
| **Catalyst** | Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ redox | Co-phthalocyanine |
| **Caustic consumption** | Moderate | Very low (fixed-bed) |
| **Sludge formation** | Yes (CuCl precipitate) — major drawback | None |
| **Sweetening vs. extraction** | Sweetening only | Both modes available |
| **Air requirement** | Yes (for Cu⁺ → Cu²⁺) | Yes (direct oxidant) |
| **Modern relevance** | Largely obsolete, replaced by Merox | Industry standard (since 1958) |
| **Product sulfur** | Slight net increase (added O) | Slight net increase |
| **Energy intensity** | Low | Very low (no reboiler, no furnace) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stoichiometric Derivation — Theoretical Air for Merox Sweetening

**Problem Framework:** A kerosene stream contains **180 ppm (by mass)** of mercaptan sulfur, with a flow rate of **120 m³/h** at a density of **810 kg/m³**. Determine the **theoretical molar O₂ requirement**, the **standard volumetric air rate (Nm³/h)**, and the **actual air rate with 10 % excess** for a fixed-bed Merox unit operating at a 95 % conversion target.

### Step 1 — Mass flow rate of the hydrocarbon

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = Q \cdot \rho = 120\,\text{m}^3/\text{h} \times 810\,\text{kg}/\text{m}^3
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = 97{,}200\,\text{kg}/\text{h}
$$

### Step 2 — Mass flow rate of mercaptan sulfur entering

Given **180 ppm** means 180 mg of S per kg of oil (= 180 × 10⁻⁶ kg S / kg oil).

$$
\dot{m}_{S,\,in} = 97{,}200 \times 180 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{S,\,in} = 17.496\,\text{kg S/h}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass of sulfur actually removed (95 % conversion)

$$
\dot{m}_{S,\,removed} = 0.95 \times 17.496
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{S,\,removed} = 16.6212\,\text{kg S/h}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert sulfur mass to moles

Atomic mass of S = 32 g/mol.

$$
n_{S} = \dfrac{16.6212 \times 10^3}{32}
$$

$$
n_{S} = 519.41\,\text{mol S/h (equivalent)}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Merox stoichiometry

Reaction: $2\,\text{RSH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{RSSR} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

For every 2 moles of RSH oxidised, $\tfrac{1}{2}$ mole of O₂ is consumed. Hence each **mole of sulfur** (i.e., 1 mole of RSH, since each RSH contains 1 S) requires $\tfrac{1}{4}$ mole O₂.

$$
n_{O_2,\,theor} = \dfrac{1}{4} \times n_{S} = \dfrac{519.41}{4}
$$

$$
n_{O_2,\,theor} = 129.85\,\text{kmol O}_2/\text{h}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert moles of O₂ to volume at STP

At STP, 1 kmol of ideal gas = **22.414 Nm³**.

$$
V_{O_2} = 129.85 \times 22.414
$$

$$
V_{O_2} = 2{,}910.7\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to theoretical air (21 % O₂ by volume)

$$
V_{air,\,theor} = \dfrac{V_{O_2}}{0.21}
$$

$$
V_{air,\,theor} = \dfrac{2{,}910.7}{0.21} = 13{,}860.6\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

### Step 8 — Apply 10 % excess for actual air requirement

$$
V_{air,\,act} = 1.10 \times 13{,}860.6
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{air,\,act} \approx 15{,}246.7\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Always explicitly state the **stoichiometric ratio** (1 mole S ≡ 0.25 mole O₂). A common mistake is using $\tfrac{1}{2}$ mole O₂ per mole of S, which over-estimates the air by **2×**. Marks are lost on this one step alone.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Parameter Sensitivity

A Merox/CuCl process engineer must check sensitivity to four operating levers. The matrix below maps each lever to the **direction of effect** on key performance indicators.

| Operating Lever | ↑ Mercaptan Conversion | ↓ Caustic Consumption | ↓ Disulfide in Product | ↓ Capex/Opex |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Temperature (35 → 65 °C)** | **Improves** (faster kinetics) | Neutral | Slight ↑ (more RSSR in oil) | Slight ↑ (mild steel OK) |
| **↑ NaOH concentration (2 → 10 %)** | **Improves** (better extraction) | **Worsens** (drag-out losses) | Improves for extraction mode | ↑ Opex (NaOH cost) |
| **↑ Air excess (0 → 30 %)** | Improves up to ~15 %; **plateaus** | Neutral | **Worsens** (oxidation of hydrocarbons, gum) | ↑ Compressor cost |
| **↑ LHSV in fixed bed (1 → 5 h⁻¹)** | **Worsens** (less contact time) | Neutral | Worsens | Improves (smaller reactor) |
| **Switching to fixed-bed Merox** | Improves for kero/diesel | **Dramatically improves** | Worsens (RSSR stays) | Slightly higher Capex |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off (LHSV vs. Conversion):** Liquid Hourly Space Velocity $LHSV = Q_{liq}/V_{bed}$ is the lever that controls reactor volume. For sweetening, KTU's typical design range is **LHSV = 1 – 4 h⁻¹** for fixed-bed Merox. Lower LHSV ⇒ bigger reactor, higher conversion.

## 3.3 Derivation — Merox Catalyst Activity Check (Turnover Number)

**Conceptual derivation (no numerical exam-style weight):**
For a fixed-bed Merox unit, the *Turnover Number (TON)* of the cobalt site is given by:

$$
\text{TON} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{RSH,\,converted}}{\dot{n}_{Co,\,in bed}} = \dfrac{Q_{oil} \cdot C_{RSH,\,in} \cdot X_{RSH}}{m_{cat} \cdot w_{Co} / M_{Co}}
$$

where $w_{Co}$ is the mass fraction of Co in the catalyst (typically 0.5–1.5 %) and $M_{Co}$ = 58.93 g/mol. Commercial Merox catalysts sustain **TON > 10⁵** before deactivation — this is what makes the process economically viable.

> [!NOTE]
> This TON expression is rarely asked directly in KTU 2024 Part B, but is excellent context for viva and design-project components.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Copper Chloride Sweetening — Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Oil with RSH] --> B[Contactor Vessel with NaOH and CuCl2]
    B --> C[Sweetened Oil Raffinate]
    B --> D[Spent Caustic with NaRS and CuCl sludge]
    D --> E[Regenerator with Air Blower]
    E --> F[Reoxidised CuCl2 solution]
    F --> B
    E --> G[Vent Gas N2 plus residual O2]
    E --> H[Disulfide Byproduct Decant]
    H --> I[Disulfide Storage or Recycle]
```

## 4.2 Merox Fixed-Bed Sweetening — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrocarbon Feed plus Mercaptans] --> M1[Feed Mixer]
    AIR[Compressed Air from Blower] --> M1
    M1 --> R1[Merox Fixed Bed Reactor Co phthalocyanine on carbon]
    R1 --> S1[Separator Knock out drum]
    S1 --> P1[Sweetened Product to Storage]
    S1 --> G1[Off gas vent O2 depleted]
    R1 --> W1[Caustic Wash Optional polisher]
    W1 --> P1
    W1 --> CW[Spent Caustic to Regenerator]
    CW --> CR[Merox Caustic Regenerator with Air]
    CR --> BLOW[Air Blower]
    CR --> RC[Regenerated Caustic Recycle to W1]
```

## 4.3 Merox Extraction Mode — Two-Column Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed LPG or Naphtha with RSH] --> EX[Extraction Column NaOH solution]
    NAOH[Fresh NaOH 10 to 20 percent] --> EX
    EX --> P1[Sweetened Oil to Storage]
    EX --> CL[Caustic with NaRS leaves bottom]
    CL --> RG[Regenerator Air plus Merox catalyst]
    AIR2[Air] --> RG
    RG --> RSSR[Disulfide Decanter]
    RG --> NAOH2[Regenerated NaOH Recycle to EX]
    RSSR --> ST[Disulfide Storage]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Functional Architecture (CuCl vs. Merox)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Hydrocarbon Feed plus Mercaptans] --> D{Refinery Sweetening Choice}
    D -- Legacy small unit --> P1[Copper Chloride Homogeneous Loop]
    D -- Modern standard --> P2[Merox Fixed Bed or Extraction]
    P1 --> P1A[Contactor NaOH plus CuCl2]
    P1A --> P1B[Regenerator with Air CuCl to CuCl2]
    P1B --> P1A
    P1A --> O1[Sweetened Oil plus RSSR remains]
    P1B --> O1B[CuCl Sludge Waste Stream]
    P2 --> P2A[Extraction Tower or Fixed Bed]
    P2A --> O2[Sweetened Oil plus RSSR remains]
    P2A --> O2B[Vent Gas and Disulfide Byproduct]
    O1 --> END[Sweet Product Meets Doctor Negative]
    O2 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Viva:**
> - The **contactor / reactor** is the *heart* — always highlight it.
> - The **air loop** in both processes is what regenerates the oxidant.
> - The **disulfide exit** distinguishes *sweetening* from *extraction* (where the disulfide is removed from the oil).
> - In **fixed-bed Merox**, no separate regenerator exists for the catalyst — the air is injected *with* the oil.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between 'sweetening' and 'desulfurization' of petroleum fractions. State, with reason, which category the Copper Chloride Process belongs to.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Sweetening* removes only the reactive, foul-smelling, corrosive **mercaptan (RSH)** sulfur by converting it (or extracting it) from the hydrocarbon. The total sulfur content is barely reduced. *(1 mark)*
- *Desulfurization (HDS)* removes the **total sulfur** (including thiophenes, sulfides, disulfides) by reacting with H₂ over a Co-Mo / Ni-Mo catalyst at high T/P, breaking C–S bonds. Total sulfur drops drastically. *(1 mark)*
- The **Copper Chloride Process is a sweetening process** because it converts mercaptans to disulfides (RSSR) using Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ catalysis in caustic — the total S in the product is virtually unchanged. *(1 mark)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**What is the Merox catalyst? Why is a phthalocyanine ligand preferred over simple cobalt salts in the Merox process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Merox catalyst** is a **cobalt(II) phthalocyanine disulphonate** chelate, often supported on activated carbon (heterogeneous) or dissolved in caustic (homogeneous). *(1 mark)*
- **Why phthalocyanine?**
  - The macrocyclic N₄ donor ligand **stabilises Co²⁺/Co³⁺** against precipitation as Co(OH)₂ in the strongly alkaline (pH > 13) environment. *(1 mark)*
  - It **tunes the Co redox potential** to ~ +0.4 V vs. NHE — ideal for selective mercaptide oxidation without over-oxidising hydrocarbons. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 marks) — Comprehensive Question on Copper Chloride**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of balanced chemical equations, explain the **two-stage chemistry** of the Copper Chloride Sweetening Process. Show clearly how the catalyst is regenerated. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A straight-run naphtha of density **740 kg/m³** flowing at **80 m³/h** contains **240 ppm (mass)** of mercaptan sulfur. Calculate the **theoretical mass of CuCl₂** required per hour if the conversion target is **92 %**. Take atomic masses Cu = 63.5, Cl = 35.5, S = 32. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Two-Stage Chemistry of CuCl₂ Sweetening (7 marks)**

**Stage 1 — Absorption in Caustic:**
Mercaptans are weak acids (pKₐ ≈ 10–11). In a **2–6 wt% NaOH** aqueous phase, they dissociate to form **sodium mercaptides**, which are water-soluble:

$$
\text{RSH} + \text{NaOH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RS}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

*(1.5 marks — balanced equation + 1-line explanation)*

**Stage 2 — Oxidation by Cupric Chloride:**
The mercaptide ion is oxidised by **cupric chloride (CuCl₂)** to the corresponding **disulfide**, while Cu²⁺ is reduced to **insoluble cuprous chloride (CuCl)**:

$$
4\,\text{RS}^-\text{Na}^+ + 2\,\text{CuCl}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{RSSR} + 4\,\text{NaCl} + 2\,\text{CuCl}\,\downarrow
$$

The disulfide, being non-polar, migrates back into the oil phase, leaving the aqueous phase loaded with NaCl and CuCl sludge. *(2 marks)*

**Catalyst Regeneration (closing the loop):**
The precipitated CuCl is re-oxidised to CuCl₂ in a separate regenerator using air:

$$
4\,\text{CuCl} + 4\,\text{HCl} + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 4\,\text{CuCl}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The regenerated CuCl₂ solution is recycled to the contactor. *(2 marks)*

**Net Reaction (combining all stages):**

$$
4\,\text{RSH} + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{RSSR} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

*(1.5 marks — net equation + 1-line conclusion that the net stoichiometry parallels Merox)*

---

**(b) Mass of CuCl₂ Required per Hour (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate of oil (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = 80 \times 740 = 59{,}200\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of mercaptan sulfur entering (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_{S,\,in} = 59{,}200 \times 240 \times 10^{-6} = 14.208\,\text{kg S/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of S actually removed (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_{S,\,removed} = 0.92 \times 14.208 = 13.0714\,\text{kg S/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to moles (1 mark):**

$$
n_S = \dfrac{13.0714 \times 10^3}{32} = 408.48\,\text{mol S/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric ratio of CuCl₂ to S (1.5 marks):**
From the oxidation half-reaction: $2\,\text{CuCl}_2$ consumes $4\,\text{RS}^-$ (i.e., 4 moles of S). Hence:

$$
\dfrac{n_{CuCl_2}}{n_S} = \dfrac{2}{4} = 0.5
$$

$$
n_{CuCl_2} = 0.5 \times 408.48 = 204.24\,\text{mol/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mass of CuCl₂ (1.5 marks):**
$M_{CuCl_2} = 63.5 + 2(35.5) = 134.5\,\text{g/mol}$.

$$
m_{CuCl_2} = 204.24 \times 134.5 = 27{,}470\,\text{g/h} = 27.47\,\text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{CuCl_2} \approx 27.47\,\text{kg/h}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Comprehensive Question on Merox

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Merox Sweetening Process** with a neat flow diagram. Compare **fixed-bed** and **extraction** modes, stating which feeds each is suitable for. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **kerosene stream** with flow rate **150 m³/h**, density **805 kg/m³**, and **mercaptan sulfur content of 210 ppm (mass)** is to be sweetened in a **fixed-bed Merox unit**. The unit operates at **55 °C, 6 bar(g)**, with **95 % conversion** and **10 % excess air**. Calculate:
  (i) the **actual volumetric air flow rate at operating conditions (m³/h)**, and
  (ii) the **disulfide (RSSR) mass produced per hour**, assuming the average mercaptan is **n-butyl mercaptan (C₄H₉SH, M = 90 g/mol)**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Merox Sweetening — Modes Comparison (7 marks)**

*See the schematic in Section 4.2 (Fixed-Bed) and Section 4.3 (Extraction).*

**Merox (Mercaptan Oxidation)** is a UOP-licensed catalytic process that converts mercaptans to disulfides in the presence of a **cobalt phthalocyanine catalyst** and **caustic (NaOH)**:

$$
2\,\text{RSH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RSSR} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

*(2 marks — reaction + catalyst identity)*

**Mode 1 — Fixed-Bed Merox Sweetening:**
- A **solid Merox catalyst** (Co-phthalocyanine on activated carbon) is pre-impregnated with concentrated caustic.
- Hydrocarbon + air are **co-currently** passed down through the fixed bed at **35–65 °C, 4–8 bar(g)**.
- Single-vessel operation; no external caustic circulation.
- **Suitable for: kerosene, jet fuel, diesel, atmospheric gas oil** — heavier fractions where extracted disulfides would re-dissolve in oil anyway. *(2.5 marks)*

**Mode 2 — Merox Extraction:**
- Multi-vessel system: an **extraction column** transfers mercaptans into **10–20 % caustic**, which is then sent to a **regenerator** with air + dissolved Merox catalyst.
- Disulfides form an **insoluble oil layer** on top of the caustic in the regenerator and are decanted.
- **Suitable for: LPG, light straight-run naphtha** — light fractions where the extracted disulfides must be removed to meet low-sulfur specifications. *(2.5 marks)*

---

**(b) Air Flow Rate and Disulfide Production (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate of oil (0.5 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = 150 \times 805 = 120{,}750\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of mercaptan sulfur entering (0.5 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_{S,\,in} = 120{,}750 \times 210 \times 10^{-6} = 25.358\,\text{kg S/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of S removed at 95 % conversion (0.5 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_{S,\,removed} = 0.95 \times 25.358 = 24.090\,\text{kg S/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Moles of RSH removed (since each RSH has 1 S atom) (0.5 mark):**

$$
n_{RSH} = \dfrac{24.090 \times 10^3}{32} = 752.81\,\text{mol/h} = 0.7528\,\text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply stoichiometry for O₂: $2\,\text{RSH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow$ RSSR + H₂O (0.5 mark):**

$$
n_{O_2,\,theor} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{n_{RSH}}{2} = \dfrac{n_{RSH}}{4}
$$

$$
n_{O_2,\,theor} = \dfrac{0.7528}{4} = 0.1882\,\text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical air at STP (0.5 mark):**

$$
V_{air,\,STP} = \dfrac{0.1882}{0.21} \times 22.414 = 20.090\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply 10 % excess (0.5 mark):**

$$
V_{air,\,actual,\,STP} = 1.10 \times 20.090 = 22.099\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert from STP to operating conditions (T = 55 + 273 = 328 K, P = 6 + 1 = 7 bar(a) = 7 × 10⁵ Pa) (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_2 = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}
$$

With $P_1 = 1.013\,\text{bar(a)}$, $T_1 = 273\,\text{K}$:

$$
V_{air,\,op} = 22.099 \times \dfrac{1.013}{7} \times \dfrac{328}{273}
$$

$$
V_{air,\,op} = 22.099 \times 0.1447 \times 1.2015
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{air,\,op} \approx 3.84\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}}
$$

**Step 9 — Disulfide mass produced (1 mark):**
From stoichiometry: $n_{RSSR} = n_{RSH}/2 = 0.3764\,\text{kmol/h}$.
Molar mass of dibutyl disulfide: $M = 2(90) - 2 = 178\,\text{g/mol}$.

$$
m_{RSSR} = 0.3764 \times 178 = 67.00\,\text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{RSSR} \approx 67.0\,\text{kg/h}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Merox / CuCl Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ¼ factor in O₂ stoichiometry.** Many students write $n_{O_2} = n_{RSH}/2$. The correct ratio is 1 mole RSH : 0.25 mole O₂ (because $\tfrac{1}{2}$ O₂ is shared between 2 RSH).
> 2. **Mixing sweetening with HDS.** Examiners specifically test the difference. Always state explicitly that sweetening *converts* RSH, while HDS *removes* all S.
