> 3. **Forgetting operating-condition correction.** Air flow must be reported at *operating* T, P, not just STP/NTP. Losing 1–2 marks here is common.
> 4. **Not mentioning catalyst regeneration.** In the CuCl process, failing to show the Cu⁺ → Cu²⁺ re-oxidation step costs ~2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing Merox Extraction with Merox Sweetening.** They are *different modes*; examiners expect you to state which feeds they apply to.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Copper Chloride & Merox**

- **Copper Chloride Process** is a *chemical sweetening* method using **Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ redox** in **NaOH**; converts RSH → RSSR; suffers from **CuCl sludge** formation.
- **Merox (Mercaptan Oxidation)** is a UOP process using **cobalt phthalocyanine** catalyst; available as **fixed-bed** (heavy distillates) and **extraction** (LPG/naphtha) modes.
- **Net reaction (both processes):** $2\,\text{RSH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{RSSR} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- **Stoichiometric air:** 1 mole of S removed requires **0.25 mole O₂** ≡ **1.19 Nm³ air per mole S** (theoretical).
- **Fixed-bed Merox** operates at **35–65 °C, 4–8 bar(g)**, LHSV 1–4 h⁻¹, with 10 % excess air.
- **Caustic concentration:** **2–10 wt% NaOH** (fixed-bed); **10–20 wt% NaOH** (extraction).
- **Doctor test negativity** is the qualitative pass criterion; quantitative target is **< 5–10 ppm mercaptan S**.
- **Merox catalyst** = **Co-phthalocyanine disulphonate**; Co²⁺/Co³⁺ redox tuned to ~ +0.4 V vs. NHE.
- **Sweetening ≠ Desulfurization:** Total S in product is *not* reduced significantly; only the reactive RSH form is removed/converted.
- **Industry status:** Merox has *largely replaced* the older CuCl process due to no sludge, lower caustic consumption, and ease of fixed-bed operation.
- **Disulfide byproducts** (RSSR) are **odourless, non-corrosive**, but remain in the oil (sweetening) or are decanted (extraction).
- **Design equation to remember:** $LHSV = Q_{liq}/V_{bed}$; **Turnover number** of Merox catalyst is typically **> 10⁵** before deactivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process of synthesis gas production by steam reforming of naphtha
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process of synthesis gas production by steam reforming of naphtha

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesis Gas (Syngas):** A gaseous mixture predominantly composed of **hydrogen (H₂)** and **carbon monoxide (CO)** in varying molar ratios, produced by the controlled reaction of a hydrocarbon feedstock with steam at high temperature over a nickel-based catalyst. Syngas serves as the primary building block for producing ammonia, methanol, Fischer–Tropsch fuels, and hydrogen.

**Steam Reforming of Naphtha** is the endothermic catalytic process in which desulfurized naphtha (a mixture of hydrocarbons roughly represented as **CₙH₂ₙ₊₂**, average formula ≈ **CH₂.₂**) is reacted with superheated steam inside tubular reformer furnaces packed with a promoted nickel catalyst to produce a crude synthesis gas. The overall process is highly endothermic, requiring external heat input typically supplied by burning fuel in the radiant section of the primary reformer furnace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Naphtha?** When natural gas (methane) is scarce or uneconomical — as in regions dependent on crude oil refining — **liquid petroleum fractions like naphtha** become the practical alternative feedstock for syngas plants. Naphtha has a higher H/C ratio advantage for H₂ production but demands more rigorous **desulfurization** because nickel reforming catalysts are extremely sulfur-sensitive (poisoning threshold: < 0.5 ppm S).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you have a block of paraffin wax (a long hydrocarbon chain) and you want to break it into useful building blocks (H₂ and CO molecules) so you can rebuild them into ammonia fertilizer or clean fuel. The process is analogous to **"cooking a long hydrocarbon chain in superheated steam inside a long hot pipe filled with a nickel 'grater'"**:

- The **nickel catalyst** acts like a grater that cracks and rearranges molecules.
- The **steam** is the reactive "steam-cooker" that oxidizes the carbon into CO and liberates hydrogen.
- The **furnace fire** outside the tubes supplies the large amount of heat (endothermic reaction).
- Any leftover sulfur compounds in the naphtha "poison" the nickel grater, so the naphtha must be **ultra-purified first**.
- The crude gas leaving the reformer is then "scrubbed" of excess CO₂ and trace CO to yield pure syngas.

## 1.3 Salient Physical Constants and Process Metrics

- Standard reformer outlet temperature: **800 °C – 850 °C**
- Operating pressure: **20 – 40 bar**
- Steam-to-carbon molar ratio (S/C): **2.5 – 4.0** (industrial best practice ≈ **3.0 – 3.5**)
- Heat of reforming reaction (naphtha basis): **≈ +1200 kJ per kg of carbon**
- Nickel catalyst loading: **≈ 10 – 25 wt% Ni** on α-Al₂O₃ support
- Sulfur poisoning limit: **< 0.5 ppmw** in feed
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Chemistry of Steam Reforming

### 2.1.1 Primary Reforming Reaction (Strongly Endothermic)

The generalized reaction of a paraffinic hydrocarbon with steam is:

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni cat., } \Delta} n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2
$$

For **naphtha**, taking an average empirical formula **CH₂.₂** (typical mid-range naphtha):

$$
\text{CH}_{2.2} + 1.1\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + 2.1\,\text{H}_2 \quad\quad \Delta H_{298} = +49.7 \text{ kcal/mol}
$$

### 2.1.2 Water–Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction (Moderately Exothermic)

Reached downstream in the shift converter:

$$
\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longleftrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad\quad \Delta H_{298} = -9.8 \text{ kcal/mol}
$$

### 2.1.3 Methanation (Highly Exothermic — Undesirable Side Reaction)

$$
\text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Le Chatelier's Principle Application:** Steam reforming is favored by **high temperature** (endothermic equilibrium shifts right with heat addition) and **low pressure** (more moles of gas produced). However, plant economics require elevated pressure to reduce downstream compression costs — hence the compromise at **20–40 bar** and high S/C ratio to push equilibrium toward H₂.

## 2.2 The Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C) — The Critical Process Lever

$$
\text{S/C} = \frac{\text{Moles of steam fed}}{\text{Moles of carbon in hydrocarbon feed}}
$$

- Increasing S/C → shifts WGS and reforming equilibria toward H₂, suppresses coking.
- Decreasing S/C below 2.5 → carbon deposition ("coking") on catalyst → tube hot spots → tube failure.
- Industrial optimum: **S/C ≈ 3.0 to 3.5**.

## 2.3 Why Catalyst Choice Matters

| Catalyst Zone | Active Metal | Support | Promoter | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Reformer | Ni (10–25%) | α-Al₂O₃ / CaAl₂O₃ | K₂O, MgO | Hydrocarbon–steam reaction |
| High-Temp Shift (HTS) | Fe₂O₃ | Cr₂O₃ | — | Converts CO → H₂ at 350–450 °C |
| Low-Temp Shift (LTS) | CuO | ZnO–Al₂O₃ | — | Final CO cleanup at 200–250 °C |
| Methanation | Ni | Al₂O₃ | — | Removes trace CO, CO₂ to < 10 ppm |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steam Reforming (generic) | CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ + n H₂O → n CO + (2n+1) H₂ | — | Strongly endothermic |
| 2 | Water–Gas Shift | CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ | — | Exothermic, ΔH = −9.8 kcal/mol |
| 3 | Naphtha empirical formula | CH₂.₂ (typical mid-range) | — | Used for material balance |
| 4 | Steam-to-Carbon Ratio | S/C = (mol H₂O) / (mol C) | mol/mol | **2.5 – 4.0** (industrial ≈ 3.0–3.5) |
| 5 | Primary reformer outlet T | T_out | °C | **800 – 850** |
| 6 | Operating pressure | P | bar | **20 – 40** |
| 7 | Catalyst sulfur tolerance | [S] in feed | ppmw | **< 0.5** |
| 8 | Reformer heat duty (approx) | Q ≈ 1200 kJ per kg C | kJ/kg | Furnace duty basis |
| 9 | Reformer tube skin T | T_skin | °C | ≤ 900 °C (creep limit) |
| 10 | Equilibrium constant, WGS | K_p = (p_CO₂·p_H₂)/(p_CO·p_H₂O) | — | Function of T |

> [!WARNING]
> When solving numerical problems, always **convert molar quantities using the empirical formula** — examiners frequently test whether students correctly compute the moles of carbon from a given naphtha feed rate (in kg/h) by dividing by the average molecular weight (≈ 14.2 for CH₂.₂).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ammonia Industry:** Syngas with **H₂ : N₂ = 3 : 1** ratio → ammonia synthesis (Haber–Bosch).
- **Methanol Industry:** Syngas with **(H₂ − CO₂) : (CO + CO₂) = 2 : 1** ratio → methanol synthesis.
- **Hydrogen Production:** After shift + CO₂ removal + methanation, near-pure H₂ for refinery hydroprocessing (HDO, HDC, hydrocracking).
- **Fischer–Tropsch:** Syngas with **H₂ : CO ≈ 2 : 1** for synthetic diesel/wax production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Material Balance for a Naphtha Steam Reformer

### Problem Statement
A naphtha reformer is fed with **1000 kg/h** of naphtha having empirical formula **CH₂.₂** at a steam-to-carbon ratio (S/C) of **3.0**. The primary reformer achieves **95% conversion of carbon to CO**. Calculate:
1. Moles of naphtha fed per hour
2. Moles of steam fed per hour
3. Moles of CO and H₂ produced (per hour)
4. Total moles of dry syngas leaving reformer

### Step 1 — Molecular Weight of Naphtha

Average molecular weight of CH₂.₂:

$$
M_{\text{naphtha}} = (1 \times 12) + (2.2 \times 1) = 12 + 2.2 = 14.2 \text{ g/mol}
$$

> Conversion logic: The empirical formula contains 1 carbon atom (12 g/mol) and 2.2 hydrogen atoms (2.2 g/mol). Summing gives the molar mass.

### Step 2 — Moles of Naphtha Fed

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{naphtha}} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg/h}}{14.2 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 70.42 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

### Step 3 — Moles of Carbon Available

Since each mole of naphtha has 1 mole of carbon:

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{C,feed}} = 70.42 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

### Step 4 — Moles of Steam Required (S/C = 3.0)

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{steam}} = 3.0 \times \dot{n}_{\text{C,feed}} = 3.0 \times 70.42 = 211.27 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

### Step 5 — Moles of CO Produced (95% carbon conversion)

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{CO}} = 0.95 \times 70.42 = 66.90 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

### Step 6 — Moles of H₂ Produced

Stoichiometry gives 2.1 mol H₂ per mol naphtha reacted. At 95% conversion:

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} = 2.1 \times 0.95 \times 70.42 = 140.49 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

### Step 7 — Total Dry Syngas Leaving Reformer

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{dry syngas}} = \dot{n}_{\text{CO}} + \dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} = 66.90 + 140.49 = 207.39 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

### Step 8 — Syngas Composition (mole %)

$$
\%\,\text{CO} = \frac{66.90}{207.39} \times 100 = 32.26 \%
$$

$$
\%\,\text{H}_2 = \frac{140.49}{207.39} \times 100 = 67.74 \%
$$

> The H₂ : CO ratio is ≈ **2.1**, which is typical for primary reformer exit gas.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Enthalpy / Heat Duty Derivation

The reformer furnace must supply enthalpy to:
- Heat the feed (naphtha + steam) from inlet temperature to outlet temperature.
- Provide the reaction enthalpy of the endothermic reforming reaction.

### Simplified Heat Duty Formula

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{furnace}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot C_{p,\text{avg}} \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) + \dot{n}_{\text{reacted}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}$ = mass flow of combined naphtha + steam (kg/h)
- $C_{p,\text{avg}}$ = average specific heat (kJ/kg·K) ≈ **2.5 kJ/kg·K** for steam-naphtha mixture
- $T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}$ = temperature rise ≈ **700 K** (room temp to 800 °C outlet)
- $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}$ = +49.7 kcal/mol for CH₂.₂ reforming

### Numerical Check

For our feed (1000 kg/h naphtha + 211.27 kmol/h steam = 211.27 × 18 = 3802.9 kg/h steam), total feed mass ≈ **4802.9 kg/h**.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{sensible}} = 4802.9 \times 2.5 \times 700 = 8.40 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{reaction}} = 0.95 \times 70.42 \times 49700 = 3.32 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{total}} = 8.40 + 3.32 = 11.72 \text{ GJ/h}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The reformer requires roughly **11.7 GJ/h** of heat input — justifying the large radiant-section furnace with multiple burners.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables on Syngas Quality

| Variable | Increase → Effect on H₂ Yield | Increase → Effect on CO Yield | Increase → Effect on Coking Risk | Practical Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (T) | ↑ (via WGS shift to right at low T is favored; reforming favored at high T — net H₂ ↑) | ↑ | ↑ at higher T (carbon gasification) | 850 °C (tube metallurgy) |
| Pressure (P) | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (favors CH₄ formation) | 40 bar (compression economy) |
| S/C Ratio | ↑ (more H₂ from WGS) | ↓ | ↓ (steam gasifies C) | 4.0 (steam cost) |
| Space Velocity | ↓ conversion | ↓ conversion | ↑ if too low | 1500–2000 h⁻¹ |
| Feed Sulfur | Catalyst deactivation | Catalyst deactivation | ↑ | < 0.5 ppmw |

## 3.4 Carbon Deposition Reactions to Remember

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \longrightarrow n\,\text{C} + (n+1)\,\text{H}_2 \quad \text{(thermal cracking)}
$$

$$
2\,\text{CO} \longleftrightarrow \text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \quad \text{(Boudouard reaction)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steam gasification of carbon (the protective reaction):**
> $$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$$
> This is why maintaining **S/C ≥ 2.5** prevents catalyst coking.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Conversion Logic Summary

1. Identify empirical formula → compute molar mass.
2. Convert mass feed → molar feed → molar carbon.
3. Apply S/C → compute steam requirement.
4. Apply conversion → compute product moles via stoichiometry.
5. Sum products → total dry syngas.
6. Compute mole fractions → report composition.
7. Compute heat duty → separate sensible and reaction enthalpy contributions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Naphtha-to-Syngas Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["NAPHTHA FEED TANK"] --> nodeB["HYDRODESULFURIZATION UNIT"]
    nodeB -->|"S less than 0.5 ppmw"| nodeC["STEAM MIXER"]
    nodeM["STEAM SUPERHEATER 500 C"] --> nodeC
    nodeC -->|"S/C equals 3.0 to 3.5"| nodeD["PRIMARY REFORMER TUBULAR FURNACE"]
    nodeF["FURNACE BURNERS NATURAL GAS OR OFFGAS"] -->|"radiant heat"| nodeD
    nodeD -->|"820 C 30 bar crude syngas"| nodeE["SECONDARY REFORMER AIR INJECTION"]
    nodeE -->|"1000 C residual CH4 less than 0.5 percent"| nodeG["HEAT RECOVERY BOILER WASTE HEAT"]
    nodeG -->|"steam generation"| nodeM
    nodeG -->|"gas cooling"| nodeH["HIGH TEMP SHIFT CONVERTER Fe2O3 Cr2O3 400 C"]
    nodeH -->|"CO down from 12 to 3 percent"| nodeI["LOW TEMP SHIFT CONVERTER CuO ZnO 220 C"]
    nodeI -->|"CO less than 0.3 percent"| nodeJ["CO2 REMOVAL BENFIELD MEA ABSORPTION"]
    nodeJ -->|"CO2 stripped for urea"| nodeK["METHANATION REACTOR Ni cat 280 C"]
    nodeK -->|"CO plus CO2 less than 10 ppm"| nodeL["PURE SYNGAS COMPRESSION AND STORAGE"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["AMMONIA PLANT OR METHANOL PLANT OR H2 PLANT"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Each Block Exists

| Block | Function | Critical Control Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| **Naphtha Feed Tank** | Buffers feedstock | Level, temperature |
| **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** | Removes S, N, metals | Sulfur slip < 0.5 ppmw |
| **Steam Mixer** | Combines feed + steam | S/C ratio = 3.0–3.5 |
| **Primary Reformer Furnace** | Reforming reaction (endothermic) | T_out = 820 °C, ΔT tube ≤ 30 °C |
| **Furnace Burners** | Supply heat | Firing rate, O₂ trim |
| **Secondary Reformer** | Air/O₂ injection for partial oxidation + CH₄ reduction | Air ratio, T_out ≈ 1000 °C |
| **Heat Recovery Boiler** | Generates high-pressure steam | Steam drum pressure |
| **High-Temp Shift (HTS)** | Converts bulk CO to H₂ | T_in ≈ 350 °C, T_out ≈ 430 °C |
| **Low-Temp Shift (LTS)** | Final CO cleanup | T_out ≈ 220 °C |
| **CO₂ Removal** | Chemical absorption of CO₂ | Lean solvent flow, reboiler duty |
| **Methanation** | Removes trace CO and CO₂ | T_out ≈ 280 °C, < 10 ppm outlet |
| **Syngas Compressor** | Pressurizes for downstream | Discharge pressure (varies by use) |

## 4.3 Schematic of Reformer Tube Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1["FURNACE HOT GAS RADIANT SECTION"] -->|"heat flux 80 kW per m2"| nodeT2["OUTER REFORMER TUBE Incoloy 800H"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["ANNULAR REFRACTORY LINING"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4["NICKEL CATALYST BED INSIDE TUBE 10 to 25 wt Ni"]
    nodeT4 -->|"feed gas inlet top to bottom"| nodeT5["PIGTAIL OUTLET TO HEADER"]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6["TRANSFER LINE TO SECONDARY REFORMER"]
```

## 4.4 Process Block Diagram — Mass Flow Stream Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["STREAM 1 NAPHTHA 1000 kg h CH 2.2"] --> nodeP1["PRIMARY REFORMER"]
    nodeS2["STREAM 2 STEAM 211 kmol h S C 3.0"] --> nodeP1
    nodeP1 --> nodeS3["STREAM 3 CRUDE SYNGAS 70 kmol CO 145 kmol H2 22 kmol unreacted CH4"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeP2["SHIFT CONVERTERS HTS plus LTS"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeS4["STREAM 4 SHIFTED GAS CO2 enriched"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeP3["CO2 ABSORPTION"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeS5["STREAM 5 PURE SYNGAS 99 percent plus H2 N2"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeP4["END USE AMMONIA METHANOL H2"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** What is synthesis gas? State its two main components and write any two industrial uses.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesis gas (syngas)** is a gaseous mixture of **hydrogen (H₂)** and **carbon monoxide (CO)** in varying ratios, produced by steam reforming of hydrocarbons or gasification of coal/biomass.
>
> **Two industrial uses:**
> 1. **Ammonia manufacture** — after adjusting H₂:N₂ = 3:1, the syngas is fed to the Haber–Bosch converter.
> 2. **Methanol manufacture** — syngas with adjusted (H₂−CO₂):(CO+CO₂) = 2:1 ratio is fed to a Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 50–100 bar, 220–280 °C.
>
> *(Other acceptable uses: Fischer–Tropsch synthesis, oxo-alcohols, town gas, pure H₂ production.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** Why is naphtha desulfurized before steam reforming?

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Naphtha contains organic sulfur compounds (mercaptans, sulfides, thiophenes) that **chemically poison the nickel reforming catalyst** by forming inactive nickel sulfide (NiS) on active sites. Even **trace sulfur (< 0.5 ppmw)** drastically reduces catalyst activity and selectivity. Desulfurization is therefore carried out via **hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** using Co-Mo or Ni-Mo catalysts at 300–400 °C, followed by **ZnO guard beds** as a polishing step to bring sulfur levels below 0.1 ppmw.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **process flow for the production of synthesis gas by steam reforming of naphtha**. Describe the function of each major unit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of operating variables** (temperature, pressure, steam-to-carbon ratio) on the performance of a primary steam reformer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a)

The naphtha-to-syngas process consists of the following sequential units:

1. **Feedstock Desulfurization (HDS Unit):** Naphtha feed is mixed with hydrogen and passed over a **Co-Mo / Ni-Mo catalyst** at 300–400 °C, 30–40 bar. Organic sulfur is converted to H₂S and absorbed in a **ZnO guard bed** to reduce sulfur to **< 0.5 ppmw**.

2. **Steam Mixing Section:** Desulfurized naphtha is mixed with **superheated steam** at 400–500 °C in a steam-to-carbon molar ratio of **3.0–3.5**.

3. **Primary Reformer (Tubular Furnace):** The mixture flows through **Incoloy-800H reformer tubes** packed with **Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst promoted by K₂O**. External firing at 850–900 °C supplies heat for the strongly endothermic reforming reaction:
   $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2$$
   Outlet gas: ≈ 820 °C, 30 bar, residual CH₄ ≈ 8–10%.

4. **Secondary Reformer:** Process air is injected for partial oxidation of remaining CH₄. Temperature rises to **1000 °C**; residual CH₄ drops to **< 0.5%**.

5. **Heat Recovery Boiler (Waste Heat Section):** Syngas is cooled, generating high-pressure steam that is reused in the process.

6. **Shift Conversion (HTS + LTS):** Two-stage catalytic conversion:
   - **HTS (Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃)** at 350–430 °C — bulk CO → CO₂ + H₂.
   - **LTS (CuO–ZnO–Al₂O₃)** at 200–250 °C — final CO polishing to < 0.3%.

7. **CO₂ Removal:** **Benfield (hot K₂CO₃)** or **MEA/MDEA** absorption strips CO₂ to < 0.1%.

8. **Methanation:** Final trace removal of CO and CO₂ to **< 10 ppm** using a **Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst** at 280 °C.

9. **Compression & Dispatch:** Pure syngas compressed and sent to ammonia/methanol/H₂ plant.

#### Model Answer — (b)

| Variable | Effect on Conversion | Effect on H₂ Purity | Effect on Coking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature ↑** | ↑ (endothermic favored) | ↑ | ↓ (steam gasifies C) |
| **Pressure ↑** | ↓ (Le Chatelier — more moles product) | ↓ | ↑ |
| **S/C Ratio ↑** | ↑ | ↑ (more H₂ via WGS) | ↓ (steam gasifies C) |
| **Space Velocity ↑** | ↓ (less contact time) | ↓ | ↑ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial compromise:** Operate at **T = 820 °C, P = 30 bar, S/C = 3.5** — a balance between thermodynamic favorability, mechanical design (tube metallurgy), catalyst life, and steam economy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to specify the **two equilibrium effects** separately — the reforming reaction (high T, low P favored) versus the WGS reaction (low T favored). Examiners give full credit only when both effects are clearly distinguished.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with **balanced chemical equations** the **primary reforming reaction, water–gas shift reaction, and methanation reaction** occurring in the naphtha-to-syngas process. Indicate whether each is endothermic or exothermic. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A naphtha reformer is fed with **2000 kg/h** of naphtha (empirical formula **CH₂.₂**, M = 14.2) at **S/C = 3.5**. If **92% of carbon is converted to CO** in the primary reformer, calculate: (i) kmol/h of steam fed, (ii) kmol/h of CO and H₂ produced, (iii) mol % composition of dry syngas. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a)

**1. Primary Reforming (Strongly Endothermic, ΔH ≈ +49.7 kcal/mol):**
$$
\text{CH}_{2.2} + 1.1\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni cat., }\Delta} \text{CO} + 2.1\,\text{H}_2
$$

**2. Water–Gas Shift (Exothermic, ΔH ≈ −9.8 kcal/mol):**
$$
\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longleftrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2
$$

**3. Methanation (Highly Exothermic, ΔH ≈ −49 kcal/mol):**
$$
\text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
$$
\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Methanation is **undesirable in the reformer** (consumes valuable H₂), but it is **deliberately used downstream** in the methanation reactor to scrub residual CO and CO₂ to < 10 ppm.

#### Model Answer — (b)

**Step 1 — Moles of naphtha:**
$$
\dot{n}_{\text{naphtha}} = \frac{2000}{14.2} = 140.85 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of carbon (= naphtha moles):**
$$
\dot{n}_{\text{C}} = 140.85 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 3 — (i) Moles of steam fed (S/C = 3.5):**
$$
\dot{n}_{\text{steam}} = 3.5 \times 140.85 = 493.0 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 4 — (ii) CO produced (92% carbon conversion):**
$$
\dot{n}_{\text{CO}} = 0.92 \times 140.85 = 129.58 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 5 — H₂ produced (2.1 mol per mol reacted):**
$$
\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} = 2.1 \times 0.92 \times 140.85 = 272.12 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Dry syngas composition:**
$$
\dot{n}_{\text{total dry}} = 129.58 + 272.12 = 401.70 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
\text{mol }\%\,\text{CO} = \frac{129.58}{401.70} \times 100 = 32.26\%
$$

$$
\text{mol }\%\,\text{H}_2 = \frac{272.12}{401.70} \times 100 = 67.74\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A common student mistake is to **forget to multiply H₂ moles by 2.1** (the stoichiometric ratio for CH₂.₂). Examiners specifically look for whether the student has **used the empirical formula** to derive the H₂ stoichiometry, not assumed CH₄-like stoichiometry (which would give 3 mol H₂). The 2.1 factor is the **distinguishing mark of naphtha reforming**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Syngas** = mixture of **H₂ + CO**; ratio depends on downstream use (3:1 for ammonia, 2:1 for methanol).
- [ ] **Naphtha empirical formula** ≈ **CH₂.₂**, M = **14.2 g/mol**.
- [ ] **Three key reactions**: Reforming (endothermic), WGS (exothermic), Methanation (exothermic, undesirable in reformer, useful as polishing step).
- [ ] **Operating conditions**: T = 800–850 °C, P = 20–40 bar, S/C = 2.5–4.0.
- [ ] **S/C < 2.5** → coking; **S/C > 4.0** → wasted steam.
- [ ] **Catalyst**: **Ni (10–25%) on α-Al₂O₃** with **K₂O promoter**.
- [ ] **Sulfur tolerance**: feed must be **< 0.5 ppmw S** (achieved by HDS + ZnO guard bed).
- [ ] **Process units (in order)**: HDS → Steam Mixer → Primary Reformer → Secondary Reformer → HTS → LTS → CO₂ Removal → Methanation → Compression.
- [ ] **Shift converters**: **HTS = Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃** (350–430 °C); **LTS = CuO–ZnO–Al₂O₃** (200–250 °C).
- [ ] **CO₂ removal**: Benfield (K₂CO₃) hot process or MEA/MDEA absorption.
- [ ] **Methanation** reduces CO + CO₂ to **< 10 ppm** before syngas dispatch.
- [ ] **Hydrogen produced per mole CH₂.₂ reacted** = **2.1 mol H₂** (key stoichiometric constant for problems).
- [ ] **Reformer tube material**: Incoloy-800H (high-temperature creep resistance).
- [ ] **Heat duty** of primary reformer ≈ **11–13 GJ per 1000 kg naphtha** (approximate scale-up benchmark).
- [ ] **Industrial S/C standard**: **3.0–3.5** (KTU most-likely numerical value).
- [ ] **CO:H₂ ratio after primary reformer** ≈ **1 : 2.1** (typical before shift conversion).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacture of methanol from synthesis gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacture of methanol from synthesis gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Methanol (CH₃OH)** is the simplest of the saturated monohydric alcohols, also known as **methyl alcohol, wood spirit, or carbinol**. In industrial petrochemical practice, it is manufactured on a large scale by the **catalytic hydrogenation of carbon monoxide (and carbon dioxide) present in synthesis gas (syngas)** — a gaseous mixture of H₂, CO, and CO₂ obtained primarily from the **steam reforming of natural gas, partial oxidation of heavy hydrocarbons, or gasification of coal/coke**.

The reaction set forms the backbone of the **C1-chemistry** stream of petrochemicals and is conducted at elevated temperature (typically **220 – 280 °C**) and pressure (**50 – 100 bar**) over a **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃** based heterogeneous catalyst.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The manufacture of methanol from synthesis gas bridges petroleum refining (syngas generation) with petrochemical manufacture. Students must clearly differentiate between **syngas generation** (feed preparation) and **methanol synthesis loop** (reaction, separation, recycle).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a **"chemical soup"** — a hot, pressurised gas made of three simple ingredients:
- **H₂ (hydrogen)** — the "fuel" and reducing agent.
- **CO (carbon monoxide)** — the "carbon backbone donor."
- **CO₂ (carbon dioxide)** — the "secondary carbon donor" that needs more hydrogen.

You bubble this soup through a bed of **catalyst pellets** that act like molecular "matchmakers". Each CO molecule is paired with two H₂ molecules; their atoms rearrange and a single, stable liquid drop — **CH₃OH (methanol)** — condenses out of the gas. The heat released during this matchmaking (the reaction is exothermic) must be continuously removed to keep the matchmaker (catalyst) alive.

In essence:
> **Syngas + Catalyst + Heat Removal → Methanol**

### 1.3 Why Methanol Matters in Industry

Methanol is one of the **top-five commodity petrochemicals globally** (production > **110 million metric tonnes/year** in 2024). Its applications include:
- **Formaldehyde production** (≈ 35 % of global methanol) — for resins and plastics.
- **MTBE / TAME** gasoline blending oxygenates.
- **Acetic acid** synthesis (Monsanto / Cativa processes).
- **Olefin-to-gasoline / MTO / MTP** processes — converting methanol to ethylene/propylene.
- **Methyl esters, methylamines, dimethyl ether (DME).**
- Emerging use as a **marine fuel, hydrogen carrier, and clean-burn fuel.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Worth Memorising**
> - Molecular weight of methanol: **32.04 g/mol**
> - Boiling point: **64.7 °C**
> - Density (at 20 °C): **0.791 g/cm³**
> - Flash point: **11 °C** (highly flammable — safety-critical for plant design)
> - Heat of combustion: **−726 kJ/mol**
> - Auto-ignition temperature: **464 °C**

### 1.4 Historical Evolution (Board Exam Favourite)

| Era | Pressure Range | Catalyst | Conversion per Pass |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1923 – BASF (high pressure) | **250 – 350 bar** | ZnO / Cr₂O₃ | ~ 14 % |
| 1966 – ICI (low pressure) | **50 – 100 bar** | Cu / ZnO / Al₂O₃ | ~ 16 – 25 % |
| 1990s – Lurgi / Topsoe | **50 – 80 bar** | Improved Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ | > 50 % (with recycle) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern methanol plants (capacity > **5,000 MTPD**) operate at the lowest practical pressure to economise compression cost, while still using the more active but heat-sensitive copper catalyst.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Reaction Engineering

The methanol synthesis is **NOT a single reaction** but a **reaction network** of competing equilibria:

**Primary synthesis reaction (dominant):**
$$
\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH}
$$
$$
\Delta H_{298} = -90.8 \text{ kJ/mol}, \quad \Delta n = -2 \text{ moles}
$$

**Secondary (CO₂) hydrogenation:**
$$
\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
$$
\Delta H_{298} = -49.5 \text{ kJ/mol}, \quad \Delta n = -3 \text{ moles}
$$

**Reverse water-gas shift (RWGS) — always present:**
$$
\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
$$
\Delta H_{298} = +41.3 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Logic — Why These Conditions?

1. **Exothermic reactions** (negative ΔH) → Lower temperature favours **higher equilibrium conversion** (Le Chatelier's principle).
2. **Mole-reducing reactions** (Δn < 0) → Higher pressure shifts equilibrium **towards methanol**.
3. **Catalyst constraint** — Cu-based catalysts **deactivate above ≈ 300 °C** (sintering of copper crystallites) and need a **minimum ≈ 220 °C** for adequate kinetic rate.
4. **Engineering compromise** → Operation at **230 – 270 °C** and **50 – 100 bar** balances equilibrium conversion, catalyst life, and compressor cost.

### 2.3 The Optimum Syngas Ratio — The "M-value" or "Stoichiometric Number"

Define the **module M** (also called stoichiometric number or stoichiometric factor):
$$
M = \frac{\left[\text{H}_2 - \text{CO}_2\right]}{\left[\text{CO} + \text{CO}_2\right]}
$$
where all concentrations are in **moles**.

- **For pure methanol yield, M = 2.0** is stoichiometrically ideal.
- **Modern plants operate at M = 2.05 – 2.10** (slight excess H₂ to suppress side reactions and coke).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Any question asking "what should be the H₂:CO ratio for methanol synthesis?" → Answer is **2:1 (molar)** for the dominant CO-hydrogenation reaction. For the CO₂ reaction, it is **3:1**.

### 2.4 Sources of Synthesis Gas — KTU Examination Perspective

| Source | Typical H₂:CO Ratio | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Steam reforming of natural gas (CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂) | ≈ **3:1** (CO-rich after shift) | Most common; preferred for methanol |
| Partial oxidation of heavy oil | ≈ **1.7:1** (CO-rich) | Needs shift converter to adjust ratio |
| Coal/coke gasification | ≈ **0.5 – 1:1** | Excess CO; requires extensive shift |
| CO₂ reforming of methane | ≈ **1:1** | Emerging route (DRM) |

The CO-rich syngas is passed through a **water-gas shift (WGS) reactor** to adjust H₂:CO ratio:
$$
\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2
$$

### 2.5 Catalysts — The Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ System

Modern low-pressure methanol synthesis uses a **ternary catalyst**:
- **Cu (copper, ~ 60 – 70 %)** → Active site; provides the metallic surface for H₂ dissociation and CO adsorption.
- **ZnO (zinc oxide, ~ 20 – 30 %)** → Structural promoter; disperses Cu crystallites, prevents sintering, stores oxygen.
- **Al₂O₃ (alumina, ~ 5 – 10 %)** → Acidic promoter; prevents Cu crystallite migration, extends catalyst life.

Optional fourth promoter: **MgO, Cr₂O₃, or ZrO₂** for improved thermal stability and resistance to poisons (especially **S and Cl**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Sulphur is the #1 catalyst poison.** Even **1 ppm** of H₂S in the syngas deactivates Cu-based catalyst almost irreversibly. Therefore, syngas must pass through a **ZnO guard bed (sulphur guard)** before entering the methanol reactor.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{[\text{H}_2] - [\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}] + [\text{CO}_2]}$ | Stoichiometric number (module M) | Dimensionless; ideal = 2.0 |
| 2 | $\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ | Primary reaction | ΔH = −90.8 kJ/mol |
| 3 | $\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Secondary reaction | ΔH = −49.5 kJ/mol |
| 4 | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | WGS equilibrium (syngas ratio adjust) | ΔH = −41.3 kJ/mol |
| 5 | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$ | Steam-methane reforming | Endothermic, ΔH = +206 kJ/mol |
| 6 | $K_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}}{P_{\text{CO}} \cdot P_{\text{H}_2}^{\,2}}$ | Equilibrium constant expression (primary rxn) | Function of T; decreases with T |
| 7 | $X_{\text{CO}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{CO, in}} - n_{\text{CO, out}}}{n_{\text{CO, in}}}$ | Fractional conversion of CO | 0.16 – 0.25 per pass |
| 8 | $S_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH, out}}}{\dot{n}_{\text{CO, consumed}}}$ | Selectivity to methanol | 0.99+ on Cu catalyst |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{space-time}} = \dfrac{\text{kg CH}_3\text{OH}}{\text{kg cat} \cdot \text{h}}$ | Catalyst productivity | Modern ≈ 0.5 – 1.0 |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{removed}} = -\Delta H_r \cdot F_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}$ | Heat duty of methanol reactor | kW; critical for reactor design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** Throughout this sheet, the symbol `vert` is used in place of the vertical pipe to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. For example, modulus is written as `$\vert x \vert$` instead of `$|x|$`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel / H₂-CO Adjustment for Syngas

**Problem Statement:** A partial-oxidation unit produces syngas with composition (dry basis): **H₂ = 45 %, CO = 50 %, CO₂ = 5 %** (mol). Determine the **module M** and the **moles of H₂ to be added (per 100 mol syngas)** so that the methanol reactor operates at M = 2.05.

**Step 1 — Compute the existing module M using the formula:**
$$
M = \frac{[\text{H}_2] - [\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}] + [\text{CO}_2]}
$$

Substituting values:
$$
M = \frac{45 - 5}{50 + 5} = \frac{40}{55} = 0.727
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:** The current syngas is highly **CO-rich** (M = 0.727 << 2.0). To make it methanol-grade, we must add H₂.

Let `x` be the moles of H₂ to add per 100 mol syngas.

**Step 3 — Write the new module equation (CO and CO₂ remain unchanged; only H₂ increases):**
$$
M_{\text{new}} = \frac{(45 + x) - 5}{50 + 5} = \frac{40 + x}{55}
$$

**Step 4 — Set M_new = 2.05 and solve for x:**
$$
\frac{40 + x}{55} = 2.05
$$

$$
40 + x = 2.05 \times 55
$$

$$
40 + x = 112.75
$$

$$
x = 72.75 \text{ moles of H}_2 \text{ per 100 mol syngas}
$$

**Step 5 — Final adjusted composition (after adding 72.75 mol H₂):**
- Total moles = 100 + 72.75 = 172.75
- New H₂ = (45 + 72.75)/172.75 = **68.0 %**
- CO = 50/172.75 = **28.9 %**
- CO₂ = 5/172.75 = **2.9 %**
- Verification: M = (68.0 − 2.9)/(28.9 + 2.9) = 65.1/31.8 ≈ **2.05** ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Material Balance on a Once-Through Methanol Reactor

**Problem Statement:** A methanol synthesis reactor is fed with a syngas of the following composition (mol): H₂ = 68 %, CO = 28 %, CO₂ = 3 %, inerts (CH₄ + N₂) = 1 %. The reactor operates at **T = 250 °C, P = 80 bar**. Per-pass CO conversion is **20 %**, and of this, 95 % goes to methanol via CO-hydrogenation and 5 % is consumed via the reverse water-gas shift (RWGS) side reaction. Compute: (a) methanol yield per 100 mol feed, (b) total H₂ consumption, (c) composition of the exit gas.

**Take Basis:** 100 mol feed.

**Step 1 — Reaction stoichiometry:**
- Primary: CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH (consumes 1 CO + 2 H₂)
- RWGS side: CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O (consumes 1 H₂, produces 1 CO)

**Step 2 — Moles of CO consumed:**
$$
\text{CO reacted} = 0.20 \times 28 = 5.6 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Split between two pathways:**
- Primary: 5.6 × 0.95 = **5.32 mol CO → 5.32 mol CH₃OH** (and 10.64 mol H₂ consumed)
- RWGS: 5.6 × 0.05 = **0.28 mol CO produced** (and 0.28 mol H₂ + 0.28 mol CO₂ consumed → 0.28 mol H₂O + 0.28 mol CO)

**Step 4 — Net change in CO (RWGS adds back some CO? No — interpretation note):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention:** In this derivation, the "5 % consumed via RWGS" means the CO undergoes RWGS to form CO₂ again. So RWGS net effect: CO is consumed and then reappears as CO₂. For simplicity in mass balance, treat RWGS as: 0.28 mol CO consumed (so CO₂ increases by 0.28 mol, H₂O increases by 0.28 mol, H₂ decreases by 0.28 mol).

**Step 5 — Total H₂ consumed:**
$$
\text{H}_2 \text{ consumed} = (5.32 \times 2) + 0.28 = 10.64 + 0.28 = 10.92 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 6 — Exit gas composition (per 100 mol feed):**
- H₂: 68 − 10.92 = **57.08 mol**
- CO: 28 − 5.32 − 0.28 = **22.40 mol**
- CO₂: 3 + 0.28 = **3.28 mol**
- H₂O (vapour): 0 + 0.28 = **0.28 mol**
- CH₃OH (vapour): 0 + 5.32 = **5.32 mol**
- Inerts: **1.00 mol**
- **Total exit:** 57.08 + 22.40 + 3.28 + 0.28 + 5.32 + 1.00 = **89.36 mol**

**Step 7 — Methanol yield:**
$$
\text{Yield per 100 mol feed} = 5.32 \text{ mol CH}_3\text{OH} = 5.32 \times 32.04 = 170.5 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 8 — Conversion & selectivity:**
$$
X_{\text{CO}} = \frac{5.6}{28} = 20 \%
$$

$$
S_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = \frac{5.32}{5.6} = 95 \%
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Trade-offs

| Process Variable | Increase Effect on Conversion | Increase Effect on Catalyst Life | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature ↑ | ↓ (exothermic equilibrium) | ↓ (sintering) | Use **isothermal / cooled** reactor |
| Pressure ↑ | ↑ (mole-reducing) | Neutral | Higher compressor CAPEX |
| H₂:CO ratio ↑ (M ↑) | ↑ | ↑ (less coking) | Need shift reactor & H₂ recycle |
| Space velocity ↑ | ↓ (less contact time) | Neutral | Smaller reactor, more recycle |
| Sulphur in feed ↑ | Catastrophic deactivation | ↓↓↓↓ | Mandatory ZnO guard bed |

### 3.4 Real-World Engineering Application

In **ICI / Johnson Matthey / Topsoe / Lurgi methanol plants**, the synthesis loop is designed as follows:

$$
\text{Syngas} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{ZnO guard bed}}_{\text{S removal}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Compressor}}_{\text{to } 80 \text{ bar}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Methanol Reactor (tubular/adiabatic)}}_{\text{Cu/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Heat Recovery}}_{\text{steam gen}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Condenser / Separator}}_{\text{crude MeOH}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Distillation}}_{\text{Ref MeOH (> 99.85 \%)}}
$$

The **unreacted gas** is split: a portion is **purged** (to remove inerts like CH₄, N₂, Ar) and the rest is **recycled** to the reactor inlet, giving an **overall carbon conversion of > 99 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice between **ICI quench-type (adiabatic) reactor** and **Lurgi tube-cooled reactor** is a frequently asked KTU question. The ICI design uses cold syngas injection between catalyst beds to control temperature; Lurgi uses boiling water inside catalyst tubes for superior temperature control but at higher capital cost.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Methanol Plant from Syngas

<!-- Mermaid block; node IDs are alphanumeric (nodeA, step1 etc.) and do not use reserved keywords -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FEED SECTION"]:::feed --> nodeB["NATURAL GAS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["DESULPHURISATION ZNO GUARD"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STEAM METHANE REFORMER"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["RAW SYNGAS H2 CO CO2"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["WATER GAS SHIFT CONVERTER"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["H2 CO CO2 RATIO ADJUSTED"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["SYNGAS COMPRESSOR 80 BAR"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["METHANOL SYNTHESIS REACTOR CU ZNO AL2O3"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["REACTOR EFFLUENT HOT"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["HEAT RECOVERY BOILER"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["CONDENSER SEPARATOR"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["CRUDE METHANOL LIQUID"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["UNREACTED GAS RECYCLE"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["PURGE STREAM REMOVE INERTS"]
    nodeO --> nodeH
    nodeM --> nodeP["DISTILLATION TOWER"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["REFINED METHANOL 99.85 PERCENT"]
    nodeP --> nodeR["WATER AND LIGHT ENDS WASTE"]

    classDef feed fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#8a6d00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reactor fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sep fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a94442,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef waste fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG,nodeH feed
    class nodeI,nodeJ,nodeK reactor
    class nodeL,nodeN,nodeO,nodeP sep
    class nodeM,nodeQ prod
    class nodeR waste
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Schematic: ICI Quench-Type Methanol Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["FRESH SYNGAS"] --> mixA["MIXER"]
    recA["RECYCLE GAS"] --> mixA
    mixA --> bed1["CATALYST BED 1"]
    bed1 --> quencher["COLD SYNGAS QUENCH"]
    quencher --> bed2["CATALYST BED 2"]
    bed2 --> quencher2["COLD SYNGAS QUENCH"]
    quencher2 --> bed3["CATALYST BED 3"]
    bed3 --> bed4["CATALYST BED 4"]
    bed4 --> outlet["HOT EFFLUENT TO HEAT RECOVERY"]
    quencher -.cold gas injection.-> quencher2

    classDef bed fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef in fill:#ffe6b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class bed1,bed2,bed3,bed4 bed
    class inA,recA,mixA,quencher,quencher2 in
    class outlet out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In the ICI reactor, fresh + recycled syngas enters the top, reacts exothermically in each catalyst bed, and is **cooled between beds by injecting cold fresh syngas** (quenching). This keeps the temperature within the safe Cu-catalyst window (220 – 280 °C). The Lurgi design is fundamentally different — it uses **boiling water tubes embedded in the catalyst**, giving better temperature control but at higher cost.

### 4.3 Block-Level Flow: Reaction Network within Methanol Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedCO["CO FEED"] --> rxn1["CO PLUS 2H2 REVERSIBLE"]
    feedCO2["CO2 FEED"] --> rxn2["CO2 PLUS 3H2 REVERSIBLE"]
    feedCO2 --> rxn3["RWGS CO2 PLUS H2"]
    rxn3 -.produces extra CO.-> rxn1
    rxn1 --> meoh["CH3OH PRODUCT"]
    rxn2 --> meoh
    rxn2 --> water["H2O BYPRODUCT"]
    rxn3 --> water

    classDef rxn fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#997300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef feed fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class rxn1,rxn2,rxn3 rxn
    class meoh,water prod
    class feedCO,feedCO2 feed
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Note how the **reverse water-gas shift (RWGS)** reaction feeds **CO back into the primary reaction**, creating a network that ultimately drives both CO and CO₂ towards methanol — which is why modern high-activity Cu catalysts can use CO₂-rich syngas effectively.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define synthesis gas. What is its ideal H₂:CO ratio for methanol manufacture?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Synthesis gas (syngas) is a mixture of hydrogen (H₂) and carbon monoxide (CO) — usually containing some CO₂, CH₄, N₂ and water vapour — produced by steam reforming of natural gas, partial oxidation of hydrocarbons, or coal gasification. It is the primary feedstock for the manufacture of methanol, ammonia, Fischer-Tropsch fuels and hydrogen.

For methanol synthesis, the **ideal H₂:CO molar ratio is 2:1** (corresponding to the primary reaction CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH). When CO₂ is also considered, the stoichiometric number M = (H₂ − CO₂)/(CO + CO₂) should equal **2.0 – 2.05** for optimal operation. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Write the chemical reactions involved in the manufacture of methanol from synthesis gas. State the role of the Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The principal reactions in methanol synthesis are:
- **Primary:** CO + 2H₂ ⇌ CH₃OH (ΔH = −90.8 kJ/mol)
- **Secondary:** CO₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ CH₃OH + H₂O (ΔH = −49.5 kJ/mol)
- **Side reaction (RWGS):** CO₂ + H₂ ⇌ CO + H₂O (ΔH = +41.3 kJ/mol)

The **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst** plays the following roles:
- **Copper** (active metal) provides dissociative adsorption sites for H₂ and CO, enabling hydrogenation.
- **ZnO** acts as a structural and electronic promoter, dispersing Cu crystallites and preventing thermal sintering.
- **Al₂O₃** acts as a textural/acidic promoter, stabilising the catalyst surface and resisting poisoning.

The catalyst operates in the temperature range **220 – 280 °C** and is **highly sensitive to sulphur poisoning**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow sheet, describe the manufacture of methanol from natural gas via the **ICI low-pressure process**. Discuss the role of the **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst**, and explain why the process is operated at **50 – 100 bar** and **230 – 270 °C**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A syngas with composition H₂ = 60 %, CO = 32 %, CO₂ = 6 %, inerts = 2 % (mol) is fed to a methanol reactor. If the per-pass CO conversion is 22 % and 96 % of the converted CO forms methanol (rest goes to RWGS), calculate **(i)** the methanol yield per 100 mol feed, **(ii)** the total H₂ consumption, and **(iii)** the exit gas composition. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) ICI Low-Pressure Process — Flow Sheet Description (7 marks):**

The ICI low-pressure methanol process consists of the following steps:

1. **Feed preparation (syngas generation):**
   - Natural gas (mainly CH₄) is first **desulphurised** by passing through a **ZnO guard bed** at ≈ 350 °C to remove H₂S to < 0.1 ppm.
   - The desulphurised gas is mixed with **process steam** in the ratio steam:C ≈ **2.5 – 3.0** and preheated to ≈ 800 °C.

2. **Steam-methane reforming (primary syngas generation):**
   $$
   \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol})
   $$
   Conducted in a fired tubular reformer at **800 – 900 °C, 15 – 30 bar** over a **Ni/Al₂O₃** catalyst. The reformer effluent typically has H₂:CO ≈ 3:1.

3. **Syngas compression:**
   - Reformer outlet is cooled, and the syngas is **compressed** from ≈ 20 bar to the methanol synthesis pressure of **50 – 100 bar** using a centrifugal compressor.

4. **Methanol synthesis loop (ICI quench converter):**
   - The pressurised syngas enters the **ICI quench converter** — a vertical pressure vessel containing **4 – 5 catalyst beds** of **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃**.
   - **Cold fresh syngas is injected between beds (quenching)** to maintain the temperature within **230 – 270 °C**.
   - Reactions: CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH (primary); CO₂ + 3H₂ → CH₃OH + H₂O (secondary).
   - Per-pass conversion: **15 – 25 %**; overall conversion with recycle: **> 99 %**.

5. **Heat recovery and condensation:**
   - The hot reactor effluent (≈ 260 °C) is sent to a **waste-heat boiler** generating **medium-pressure steam**.
   - After cooling, the gas-liquid mixture enters a **separator** where **crude methanol (≈ 65 – 75 wt %)** is condensed.

6. **Recycle and purge:**
   - Unreacted gas is split: ≈ **80 – 90 % is recycled** to the reactor; the rest is **purged** to remove inert methane, nitrogen and argon.

7. **Purification (distillation):**
   - Crude methanol is sent to a **two- or three-column distillation train**.
   - **First column** removes dissolved gases and light ends (dimethyl ether, methyl formate).
   - **Second column** removes water and higher alcohols.
   - Final refined methanol purity: **> 99.85 wt % (Grade AA)**.

**Why 50 – 100 bar and 230 – 270 °C? (1 mark brief):**
- The primary methanol synthesis reaction is **exothermic and mole-reducing** (Δn = −2). High pressure favours forward conversion per Le Chatelier's principle.
- Higher pressure also increases **reaction rate** (kinetics) and improves **condensation efficiency** of methanol from the gas phase.
- **Low temperature** favours higher equilibrium conversion (exothermic), but **Cu catalyst coking/sintering** begins above ≈ 300 °C and reaction rate is too slow below ≈ 220 °C.
- **50 – 100 bar** is the **economic optimum** — beyond 100 bar, marginal conversion gain is outweighed by compressor CAPEX.
- **230 – 270 °C** is the **kinetic + thermodynamic compromise** — operated via inter-bed quenching (ICI) or boiling-water tubes (Lurgi).

---

**(b) Material Balance Calculation (7 marks):**

**Basis:** 100 mol feed.
- Initial moles: H₂ = 60, CO = 32, CO₂ = 6, inerts = 2.

**Step 1 — CO reacted:**
$$
n_{\text{CO, rxn}} = 0.22 \times 32 = 7.04 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Split:**
- **Methanol pathway (96 %):**
$$
\text{CO}_{\text{to MeOH}} = 0.96 \times 7.04 = 6.7584 \text{ mol}
$$
$$
\text{CH}_3\text{OH produced} = 6.7584 \text{ mol}
$$
$$
\text{H}_2 \text{ consumed (primary)} = 2 \times 6.7584 = 13.5168 \text{ mol}
$$

- **RWGS pathway (4 %):**
$$
\text{CO via RWGS} = 0.04 \times 7.04 = 0.2816 \text{ mol}
$$
$$
\text{CO}_2 \text{ consumed} = 0.2816 \text{ mol}, \quad \text{H}_2 \text{ consumed} = 0.2816 \text{ mol}, \quad \text{H}_2\text{O produced} = 0.2816 \text{ mol}
$$

> **Note on RWGS:** Strictly, RWGS converts CO₂ → CO. Here we interpret "4 % of converted CO undergoes RWGS" as the net effect of RWGS consuming 0.2816 mol CO₂ and 0.2816 mol H₂ to produce 0.2816 mol CO and 0.2816 mol H₂O. (Alternative interpretation accepted.)

**Step 3 — Total H₂ consumed:**
$$
\text{H}_2 \text{ total consumed} = 13.5168 + 0.2816 = 13.7984 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4 — (i) Methanol yield per 100 mol feed:**
$$
\boxed{n_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = 6.7584 \text{ mol per 100 mol feed} \approx 6.76 \text{ mol}}
$$

Equivalent mass: 6.7584 × 32.04 = **216.5 g methanol per 100 mol feed**.

**Step 5 — (iii) Exit gas composition:**
- H₂: 60 − 13.7984 = **46.20 mol**
- CO: 32 − 6.7584 − 0.2816 = **24.96 mol**
- CO₂: 6 − 0.2816 = **5.72 mol**
- CH₃OH (vapour): **6.76 mol**
- H₂O (vapour): **0.28 mol**
- Inerts: **2.00 mol**
- **Total exit = 85.92 mol**

Normalised composition (mol %):
- H₂ ≈ **53.8 %**
- CO ≈ **29.1 %**
- CO₂ ≈ **6.7 %**
- CH₃OH ≈ **7.9 %**
- H₂O ≈ **0.3 %**
- Inerts ≈ **2.3 %**

**Step 6 — Verification:**
- Conversion of CO = 7.04/32 = 22 % ✓
- Selectivity to methanol = 6.7584/7.04 = 96 % ✓
- Mass balance closure: all elements (C, H, O) balance within ±0.5 %. ✓

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of operating pressure and temperature** in methanol synthesis with the help of thermodynamic considerations. Discuss the difference between **ICI (quench) and Lurgi (tube-cooled) reactor designs**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reformer produces syngas with the following dry composition: H₂ = 72 %, CO = 18 %, CO₂ = 7 %, CH₄ = 3 % (mol). The syngas is mixed with additional CO₂ to improve conversion. Calculate: **(i)** the module M of the original syngas, **(ii)** how many moles of CO₂ must be added per 100 mol syngas to raise M to 2.0, and **(iii)** the new composition. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Pressure and Temperature Effects + Reactor Designs (7 marks):**

**Thermodynamic Analysis (3 marks):**

The primary methanol reaction CO + 2H₂ ⇌ CH₃OH has:
- **ΔH = −90.8 kJ/mol (exothermic)**
- **Δn = −2 (mole-reducing)**

Applying Le Chatelier's principle:
- **Increasing temperature** shifts equilibrium **backwards** → reduces equilibrium conversion. Conversely, lower temperature favours higher methanol yield.
- **Increasing pressure** shifts equilibrium **forwards** (towards fewer moles) → increases methanol yield.

The van't Hoff equation shows Kp decreases sharply with temperature:
$$
\ln \frac{K_{p,2}}{K_{p,1}} = -\frac{\Delta H}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

At **300 °C**, equilibrium conversion is only ≈ 5 %, whereas at **200 °C** it can exceed 60 %. However, the **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst** is kinetically inactive below ≈ 220 °C and deactivates above ≈ 300 °C due to copper crystallite sintering. Therefore, the **practical optimum is 230 – 270 °C** with **once-through conversion of 16 – 25 %**, and overall conversion > 99 % achieved via **gas recycle**.

**ICI Quench vs Lurgi Tube-Cooled Reactors (4 marks):**

| Feature | ICI Quench Converter | Lurgi Tube-Cooled Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor type | Adiabatic, multi-bed | Quasi-isothermal, single bed with tubes |
| Temperature control | Inter-bed cold syngas injection | Boiling water inside catalyst tubes |
| Catalyst configuration | Pellets in 4 – 5 fixed beds | Catalyst packed around boiling tubes |
| Heat removal capacity | Moderate (limited by quench gas flow) | Excellent (tube-side latent heat) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Catalyst life | Good | Excellent (better temperature control) |
| Capacity flexibility | Easier scale-up by adding beds | Limited by tube-bundle manufacturing |

**Conclusion:** Lurgi is preferred for **very large plants (> 2,500 MTPD)** where superior temperature control and longer catalyst life justify the higher CAPEX; ICI is preferred for **medium-sized or older plants**.

---

**(b) Material Balance on Syngas Ratio Adjustment (7 marks):**

**Basis:** 100 mol dry syngas.
- Initial: H₂ = 72, CO = 18, CO₂ = 7, CH₄ = 3.

**Step 1 — (i) Original module M:**
$$
M = \frac{[\text{H}_2] - [\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}] + [\text{CO}_2]} = \frac{72 - 7}{18 + 7} = \frac{65}{25} = 2.60
$$

**Interpretation:** Original syngas is **H₂-rich** (M = 2.60 > 2.0). Excess H₂ can be used to hydrogenate CO₂, but adding more CO₂ will consume the excess H₂ and bring M down to the ideal 2.0.

**Step 2 — (ii) Moles of CO₂ to add (let y mol added per 100 mol syngas):**

After adding y mol CO₂:
- H₂ = 72 (unchanged)
- CO = 18 (unchanged)
- CO₂ = 7 + y
- CH₄ = 3 (unchanged)

New module:
$$
M_{\text{new}} = \frac{72 - (7 + y)}{18 + (7 + y)} = \frac{65 - y}{25 + y}
$$

Set M_new = 2.0:
$$
\frac{65 - y}{25 + y} = 2.0
$$

$$
65 - y = 2.0 (25 + y) = 50 + 2y
$$

$$
65 - 50 = 2y + y = 3y
$$

$$
y = \frac{15}{3} = 5 \text{ mol CO}_2
$$

**Step 3 — (iii) New composition (per 105 mol total):**

- H₂ = 72/105 = **68.57 %**
- CO = 18/105 = **17.14 %**
- CO₂ = (7+5)/105 = **11.43 %**
- CH₄ = 3/105 = **2.86 %**
- **Total = 100.00 %** ✓

**Verification of new M:**
$$
M = \frac{68.57 - 11.43}{17.14 + 11.43} = \frac{57.14}{28.57} = 2.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Answer:** **5 mol CO₂ must be added per 100 mol syngas** to bring the module M from 2.60 down to the ideal 2.00 for methanol synthesis.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the side reaction** — Students often write only CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH and ignore the CO₂ and RWGS reactions. This loses 2 marks in long answers.
> 2. **Confusing high-pressure and low-pressure process conditions** — Modern ICI is 50 – 100 bar, NOT 250 – 350 bar. The 250-bar BASF process is **obsolete**.
> 3. **Wrong stoichiometric ratio** — The ideal H₂:CO is **2:1 molar** (not weight, not volume).
> 4. **Module M definition** — Many students forget to subtract CO₂ from H₂ in the numerator. Use the correct formula every time: $M = (H_2 - CO_2)/(CO + CO_2)$.
> 5. **Catalyst poisoning** — Sulphur is the **deadliest poison**. Must mention ZnO guard bed.
> 6. **Heat management** — Failing to mention exothermic nature and the need for quenching/tube cooling = −1 to 2 marks.
> 7. **Unreacted gas recycle** — Forgetting to mention recycle loop and purge stream = −1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Manufacture of Methanol from Synthesis Gas**

- ☐ **Syngas** = H₂ + CO (+ CO₂, inerts); ideal H₂:CO molar ratio = **2:1**; ideal **module M = 2.0 – 2.05**.
- ☐ **Primary reaction:** CO + 2H₂ ⇌ CH₃OH, **ΔH = −90.8 kJ/mol**.
- ☐ **Secondary reaction:** CO₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ CH₃OH + H₂O, **ΔH = −49.5 kJ/mol**.
- ☐ **Side reaction (RWGS):** CO₂ + H₂ ⇌ CO + H₂O, ΔH = +41.3 kJ/mol.
- ☐ **Operating conditions:** T = **230 – 270 °C**, P = **50 – 100 bar** (modern low-pressure ICI process).
- ☐ **Catalyst:** **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃** — Cu = active site, ZnO = structural promoter, Al₂O₃ = textural promoter.
- ☐ **Catalyst poison:** **Sulphur** — use **ZnO guard bed** upstream.
- ☐ **Per-pass conversion:** **15 – 25 %**; overall conversion with recycle: **> 99 %**.
- ☐ **Reactor designs:** **ICI (quench, multi-bed, adiabatic)** vs **Lurgi (tube-cooled, isothermal)**.
- ☐ **Syngas generation:** **Steam-methane reforming** is dominant route: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂.
- ☐ **Purification:** Two-column distillation → refined methanol **> 99.85 wt %** (Grade AA).
- ☐ **Recycle loop:** Unreacted gas recycled; small purge removes CH₄, N₂, Ar.
- ☐ **End uses:** Formaldehyde, MTBE, acetic acid, olefins (MTO/MTP), DME, fuel.
- ☐ **Thermodynamics:** Exothermic + mole-reducing → low T, high P favours conversion.
- ☐ **Conversion formula:** $X_{\text{CO}} = (n_{\text{CO,in}} - n_{\text{CO,out}})/n_{\text{CO,in}}$.
- ☐ **Selectivity formula:** $S_{\text{MeOH}} = n_{\text{MeOH}}/(n_{\text{CO,in}} - n_{\text{CO,out}})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of liquid fuels from synthesis gas by Fischer-Tropsch process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Production of liquid fuels from synthesis gas by Fischer-Tropsch process ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Fischer-Tropsch (FT) Process** is a heterogeneous, catalyzed chemical reaction that converts a mixture of carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H₂) — collectively known as **synthesis gas (syngas)** — into a broad spectrum of liquid hydrocarbon products ranging from light gases to long-chain paraffins and waxes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Recall-Ready):** > *"The Fischer-Tropsch process is a set of catalyzed chemical reactions in which carbon monoxide and hydrogen (syngas) are converted into liquid hydrocarbon fuels and chemicals, following the general stoichiometry:* > *nCO + (2n+1)H₂ → CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ + nH₂O (paraffins)"* The overall process is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ **−165 kJ/mol** of CO converted for paraffin formation) and operates typically between **200 °C – 350 °C** and **10 bar – 40 bar**, using **iron (Fe)**, **cobalt (Co)**, or **ruthenium (Ru)** based catalysts. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine building a Lego chain. The syngas (CO + H₂) molecules are the individual Lego bricks. A catalyst acts like a *magnet that grabs one brick at a time* and **adds it to a growing chain**. At every step, there are two choices — *attach one more brick* (chain growth) or *close the box and ship it* (chain termination). The probability of "adding one more brick" is called **α (alpha)**, and the probability of "closing the box" is **(1−α)**. Change α, and you change whether you end up with chains of 5 bricks (gasoline) or 50 bricks (diesel/wax). This is exactly what happens on the surface of an FT catalyst particle! ### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context | Aspect | Detail | | :--- | :--- | | Invented | **1923** by Franz Fischer & Hans Tropsch at the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute, Germany | | First Industrial Plant | **1936**, Germany (coal-to-liquid) | | Largest Modern Commercial Plant | **Pearl GTL, Qatar** (Shell, ~140,000 bbl/day) & **Sasol, South Africa** | | Strategic Relevance | Converts coal, natural gas, or biomass → ultra-clean diesel (near-zero sulfur) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick-Recall:** The FT process is the **heart of XTL technology** (X = Coal→CTL, Gas→GTL, Biomass→BTL). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Core Chemistry (Step-by-Step Logic) The Fischer-Tropsch synthesis is **not a single reaction** but a *family of parallel and consecutive reactions* producing a product spectrum. The major reaction classes are: **(a) Paraffin (Alkane) Formation — The dominant pathway** $$n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **(b) Olefin (Alkene) Formation** $$n\,\text{CO} + 2n\,\text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **(c) Alcohol (Oxygenate) Formation — minor side reaction** $$n\,\text{CO} + 2n\,\text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{OH} + (n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **(d) Boudouard (Carbon Deposition) — unwanted side reaction** $$2\,\text{CO} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \qquad \Delta H = -172\;\text{kJ/mol}$$ **(e) Water-Gas Shift (WGS) — critical side reaction on Fe catalysts** $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why WGS matters:** On iron catalysts, WGS is *highly active* and shifts the effective H₂:CO ratio upward. On cobalt catalysts, WGS is *negligible*, so a high H₂:CO feed ratio (~2:1) is mandatory. ### 2.2 The Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) Distribution — The Master Equation This is the **single most important equation** in FT kinetics. It predicts *the entire product spectrum* from a single parameter, **α** (the chain-growth probability). $$x_n \;=\; (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{\,n-1}$$ $$W_n \;=\; n\,(1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{\,n-1}$$ Where: - $x_n$ = **mole fraction** of hydrocarbon with $n$ carbon atoms - $W_n$ = **weight (mass) fraction** of hydrocarbon with $n$ carbon atoms - $\alpha$ = **chain growth probability** ($0 \le \alpha < 1$) > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning of α:** > - **α → 0** → almost every chain terminates at C₁ → product is mostly *methane* > - **α → 1** → chains grow indefinitely → product is *heavy wax* > - **α ≈ 0.7** → optimal balance for *diesel-range* fuels > - **α ≈ 0.9** → maximum wax (Shell SMDS process uses α ≈ 0.95) ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Typical Range / Units | Engineering Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Paraffin formation | $n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | n = 1 to 100+ | Master stoichiometry | | 2 | Olefin formation | $n\,\text{CO} + 2n\,\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | n ≥ 2 | Unsaturated product yield | | 3 | ASF mole fraction | $x_n = (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{\,n-1}$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Predicts mole distribution | | 4 | ASF weight fraction | $W_n = n\,(1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{\,n-1}$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Predicts mass distribution | | 5 | WGS reaction | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | $\Delta H = -41\;\text{kJ/mol}$ | Fe-catalyst H₂ balance | | 6 | Stoichiometric H₂:CO ratio | $(2n+1)/n$ | Paraffin: 3:1 (n=1) → 2:1 (n→∞) | Optimum syngas composition | | 7 | FT reaction enthalpy | $\Delta H \approx -165\;\text{kJ/mol\,CO}$ | Exothermic | Heat removal design | | 8 | Chain-growth selectivity | $\alpha = r_p / (r_p + r_t)$ | Fe: 0.5–0.7; Co: 0.7–0.85 | Catalyst selection | | 9 | Maximum gasoline yield | $W_{\max}$ at n ≈ 8 (gasoline) | ≈ 47 % at α ≈ 0.76 | Theoretical ceiling | | 10 | Maximum diesel yield | $W_{\max}$ at n ≈ 12 (diesel) | ≈ 41 % at α ≈ 0.80 | Theoretical ceiling | ### 2.4 Operational Variables & Their Effects | Variable | Effect on α | Effect on Catalyst | KTU Tip | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **↑ Temperature** | ↓ α (more CH₄) | Sintering risk | HTFT 320–350 °C | | **↑ Pressure** | ↑ α (heavier products) | Promotes wax | LTFT 20–40 bar | | **↑ H₂:CO ratio** | ↓ α (more CH₄) | Reduces carbon lay-down | Co needs 2:1 | | **↑ Space Velocity** | ↓ α | Less contact time | Higher gas yield | | **Smaller catalyst pore** | ↓ α | Diffusion-limited | Promotes C₅–C₁₁ | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) Distribution The ASF law is derived using a **Flory-type polymerization kinetics** assumption applied to the surface of the catalyst. Each growing alkyl chain has only two fates: **propagate** (add one CH₂ unit) or **terminate** (desorb as product). **Step 1 — Define the two elementary surface rates** Let: - $r_p$ = rate of chain propagation (one C-unit added) [units: mol/(g·s)] - $r_t$ = rate of chain termination (product desorbed) [units: mol/(g·s)] **Step 2 — Define the chain-growth probability** The probability that a growing chain adds *one more* CH₂ unit instead of terminating is: $$\alpha \;=\; \frac{r_p}{r_p + r_t}$$ The probability that it terminates at *this* step is: $$(1 - \alpha) \;=\; \frac{r_t}{r_p + r_t}$$ **Step 3 — Probability of forming an n-carbon product** To form a product with **n** carbon atoms, the chain must propagate $(n-1)$ times and then terminate once. Because each step is *independent* and identically distributed, we multiply probabilities: $$P(\text{C}_n) \;=\; \underbrace{\alpha \cdot \alpha \cdot \alpha \cdots \alpha}_{(n-1)\;\text{times}} \cdot (1-\alpha) \;=\; \alpha^{\,n-1}(1-\alpha)$$ This gives the **mole fraction** directly: $$\boxed{\,x_n \;=\; (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{\,n-1}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Convert mole fraction to weight (mass) fraction** The mass of one mole of CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ is approximately $M_n \approx 14\,n$ g/mol (neglecting H mass for high n). The weight fraction is the mole fraction weighted by molar mass, normalized by the total mass: $$W_n \;=\; \frac{n\,x_n}{\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\,x_n}$$ **Compute the denominator:** $$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\,x_n \;=\; (1-\alpha) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\,\alpha^{\,n-1}$$ Using the standard power-series identity: $$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\,\alpha^{\,n-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-\alpha)^2}$$ Therefore: $$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\,x_n \;=\; (1-\alpha) \cdot \frac{1}{(1-\alpha)^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{1-\alpha}$$ **Step 5 — Final weight-fraction expression** $$\boxed{\,W_n \;=\; \frac{n \cdot (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{\,n-1}}{1/(1-\alpha)} \;=\; n\,(1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{\,n-1}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification at α = 0.5:** All Wₙ for n = 1, 2, 3 ... must sum to 1. > $W_1 = 1 \cdot (0.5)^2 \cdot (0.5)^0 = 0.25$ > $W_2 = 2 \cdot (0.5)^2 \cdot (0.5)^1 = 0.25$ > $W_3 = 3 \cdot (0.5)^2 \cdot (0.5)^2 = 0.1875$ ... total = 1 ✓ ### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Fractional Yield in a Given Carbon Range A classic KTU problem: *Find the carbon number n\* that maximizes Wₙ.* To find the maximum, take the derivative $\frac{dW_n}{dn} = 0$ using continuous approximation. Treat $W_n$ as a function of continuous variable $n$: $$W_n \;=\; n\,(1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{\,n-1}$$ Take natural log to simplify: $$\ln W_n \;=\; \ln n + 2\ln(1-\alpha) + (n-1)\ln\alpha$$ Differentiate with respect to $n$: $$\frac{d(\ln W_n)}{dn} \;=\; \frac{1}{n} + \ln\alpha \;=\; 0$$ Solve for $n^\*$: $$\boxed{\,n^* \;=\; -\frac{1}{\ln\alpha}\,}$$ For **gasoline-range maximum** (n ≈ 8): $\alpha = e^{-1/8} = 0.882$ ❌ — actually the gasoline optimum is at lower α. Let us instead use the ASF relation: maximum weight fraction for given carbon number occurs at: $$n^* \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{\,\ln(1/\alpha)\,} \;\;\text{(when $n$ is treated discretely for max $W_n$)}$$ Numerical evaluation for **diesel fraction (C₁₀–C₂₀)**: At α = 0.80: $n^* = 0.80 / \ln(1/0.80) = 0.80 / 0.223 = 3.6$ ❌ — this gives C₄ maximum. **Correct discrete maximum formula** (treat Wₙ as discrete, look at Wₙ₊₁/Wₙ ratio): $$\frac{W_{n+1}}{W_n} \;=\; \frac{(n+1)}{n}\,\alpha$$ Set this = 1 to find maximum: $$\frac{(n+1)}{n}\,\alpha = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n^* = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$$ For **gasoline** ($n^* \approx 5$): $\alpha = 5/6 = 0.83$ For **diesel** ($n^* \approx 12$): $\alpha = 12/13 = 0.923$ — too high; in practice, products are cut at C₁₀–C₂₀ with α ≈ 0.75–0.85. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A cobalt FT catalyst operates at α = 0.75. Compute the mole and weight fractions of C₁, C₅, and C₁₀ products, and comment on the selectivity. **Solution:** Using $x_n = (1-\alpha)\alpha^{n-1}$ and $W_n = n(1-\alpha)^2\alpha^{n-1}$ with $(1-\alpha) = 0.25$: | n | $x_n$ | $W_n$ | Product | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | $(0.25)(0.75)^0 = 0.2500$ | $1 \cdot (0.25)^2 \cdot 1 = 0.0625$ | CH₄ | | 5 | $(0.25)(0.75)^4 = 0.0791$ | $5 \cdot (0.0625)(0.3164) = 0.0989$ | Gasoline | | 10 | $(0.25)(0.75)^9 = 0.0377$ | $10 \cdot (0.0625)(0.0751) = 0.0469$ | Kerosene/Diesel | > [!TIP] > **Comment for KTU:** At α = 0.75, methane selectivity is 25 mol% (high — undesirable). The catalyst is *under-selecting* for liquid fuels. The typical FT target is α ≈ 0.80–0.85 for cobalt and α ≈ 0.70 for iron (HTFT). ### 3.4 Carbon Number Optimization for Diesel Cut (C₁₀–C₂₀) Total mass fraction in C₁₀–C₂₀ at α = 0.80: $$\sum_{n=10}^{20} W_n \;=\; (1-\alpha)^2 \sum_{n=10}^{20} n\,\alpha^{\,n-1}$$ $$= (0.20)^2 \left[ 10(0.80)^9 + 11(0.80)^{10} + \cdots + 20(0.80)^{19} \right]$$ Computing the geometric series with ratio α = 0.80: $$\sum_{n=10}^{20} n\,\alpha^{\,n-1} \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{20} n\,\alpha^{\,n-1} - \sum_{n=1}^{9} n\,\alpha^{\,n-1}$$ Using the closed-form identity: $$\sum_{n=1}^{N} n\,\alpha^{\,n-1} \;=\; \frac{1 - (N+1)\alpha^N + N\alpha^{N+1}}{(1-\alpha)^2}$$ Plug in α = 0.80: For N = 20: $$\sum_{n=1}^{20} n(0.80)^{n-1} = \frac{1 - 21(0.80)^{20} + 20(0.80)^{21}}{(0.20)^2} \approx \frac{1 - 0.0154 + 0.0025}{0.04} \approx 24.68$$ For N = 9: $$\sum_{n=1}^{9} n(0.80)^{n-1} = \frac{1 - 10(0.80)^9 + 9(0.80)^{10}}{(0.20)^2} \approx \frac{1 - 1.342 + 0.966}{0.04} \approx 15.59$$ Therefore: $$\sum_{n=10}^{20} W_n \;=\; (0.04) \times (24.68 - 15.59) \;=\; (0.04)(9.09) \approx 0.364$$ **Conclusion:** Only about **36.4 %** of the product mass lies in the diesel window C₁₀–C₂₀ at α = 0.80. The remaining ~64 % is distributed as lighter gases, gasoline, and heavy wax — a *fundamental limitation* of the FT process known as the **ASF selectivity ceiling**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End FT Process Block Diagram (Gas-to-Liquid / Coal-to-Liquid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["RAW FEEDSTOCK
NATURAL GAS OR COAL OR BIOMASS"] B["GASIFICATION OR
STEAM REFORMING UNIT"] C["SYNTHESIS GAS
CO PLUS H2 MIXTURE"] D["GAS CLEANING
H2S COS REMOVAL"] E["FT REACTOR
FIXED OR SLURRY OR FLUID BED"] F["CATALYST
FE OR CO OR RU"] G["RAW PRODUCT
WAX DIESEL NAPHTHA GASES"] H["HYDROCRACKER
HEAVY WAX UPGRADING"] I["DISTILLATION
COLUMN TRAIN"] J["FINAL LIQUID FUELS
GTL DIESEL NAPHTHA KEROSENE"] K["RECYCLE
UNCONVERTED SYNGAS"] L["TAIL GAS
FUEL OR RECYCLE"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E F -.-> E E --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J E --> K K --> B G --> L classDef feed fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef react fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef prod fill:#7FB3D5,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef util fill:#F4D03F,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A feed class B,D,E,F,H react class C,G,J,L prod class I,K util ``` ### 4.2 Reaction Pathway on Catalyst Surface (Chain Growth Mechanism) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STEP 1
CO ADSORPTION
ON CATALYST SURFACE"] S2["STEP 2
H2 DISSOCIATION
H ATOM FORMATION"] S3["STEP 3
CHX MONOMER
FORMATION"] S4["STEP 4
CHAIN PROPAGATION
ADD CH2 UNIT
PROBABILITY ALPHA"] S5["STEP 5
CHAIN TERMINATION
DESORPTION
PROBABILITY 1 MINUS ALPHA"] S6["PRODUCT CnH2nPlus2
WAX DIESEL OR NAPHTHA"] S7["PRODUCT CnH2n
ALPHA OLEFIN"] S8["CH4
METHANE BYPRODUCT"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S5 --> S7 S5 --> S8 S3 --> S8 ``` ### 4.3 Three Major FT Reactor Configurations Compared ```mermaid flowchart TD R["FT REACTOR TYPES"] R1["MULTI TUBULAR FIXED BED
LTFT MODE
220 C 25 BAR
CO BASED
PEARL GTL"] R2["SLURRY BUBBLE COLUMN
LTFT MODE
230 C 30 BAR
CO BASED
SASOL SLURRY"] R3["CIRCULING FLUIDIZED BED
HTFT MODE
340 C 25 BAR
FE BASED
SASOL SYNTHOL"] R --> R1 R --> R2 R --> R3 classDef rect fill:#E8DAEF,stroke:#4A235A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class R1,R2,R3 rect ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the Fischer-Tropsch process. List any two catalysts used and the operating temperature range.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Fischer-Tropsch (FT) process is a *catalyzed chemical reaction* in which synthesis gas (a mixture of CO and H₂) is converted into liquid hydrocarbon fuels. - **Catalysts (1 mark):** Iron (Fe), Cobalt (Co), Ruthenium (Ru) — supported on Al₂O₃, SiO₂, or TiO₂. - **Operating range (1 mark):** Temperature: **200 °C – 350 °C**, Pressure: **10 bar – 40 bar**. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *What is the Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) distribution? Write the equation for mole fraction and weight fraction of an n-carbon product.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Concept (1 mark):** ASF distribution predicts the entire product spectrum of FT synthesis using a single parameter α (chain growth probability). - **Mole fraction (1 mark):** $x_n = (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{n-1}$ - **Weight fraction (1 mark):** $W_n = n\,(1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{n-1}$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]* **(a)** Derive the Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) distribution for the Fischer-Tropsch synthesis. Clearly define the chain-growth probability (α) and explain its physical significance in determining product distribution. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a cobalt-based FT catalyst operating at α = 0.80, compute: (i) The mole fraction and weight fraction of methane (C₁). **(2 Marks)** (ii) The carbon number at which the weight fraction is maximum. **(2 Marks)** (iii) The maximum attainable weight fraction for the gasoline cut (C₅–C₁₁). **(3 Marks)** --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A** **Part (a) — ASF Derivation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Definition of elementary rates (2 marks):** Let $r_p$ be the rate of *propagation* (addition of one CH₂ unit to the growing chain) and $r_t$ the rate of *termination* (desorption of the product from the catalyst surface). Both rates are assumed independent of chain length. **Step 2 — Definition of α (1 mark):** $$\alpha = \frac{r_p}{r_p + r_t}, \quad (1-\alpha) = \frac{r_t}{r_p + r_t}$$ **Step 3 — Probability of forming Cₙ (2 marks):** To form an n-carbon product, the chain must propagate $(n-1)$ times and terminate once. Each event is independent, so probabilities multiply: $$x_n = \alpha^{n-1} \cdot (1-\alpha)$$ **Step 4 — Conversion to weight fraction (2 marks):** $$W_n = \frac{n\,x_n}{\sum_n n\,x_n} = \frac{n(1-\alpha)\alpha^{n-1}}{1/(1-\alpha)} = n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$$ **Physical significance of α (bonus mark):** - α → 0 → mainly methane (C₁) - α → 1 → heavy wax (C₅₀+) - α = 0.7 – 0.8 → optimum liquid fuels (diesel & gasoline) --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks)** **(i) Methane fraction (2 marks):** At α = 0.80, $(1-\alpha) = 0.20$: $$x_1 = (0.20)(0.80)^0 = \mathbf{0.20}\;\;(20\;\text{mol\%})$$ $$W_1 = 1 \cdot (0.20)^2 \cdot (0.80)^0 = \mathbf{0.04}\;\;(4\;\text{wt\%})$$ **(ii) Carbon number of maximum weight fraction (2 marks):** Using the discrete ratio test $\frac{W_{n+1}}{W_n} = \frac{(n+1)}{n}\alpha$ and setting equal to 1: $$n^* = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = \mathbf{4}$$ So maximum $W_n$ occurs at **n = 4** (butane range). **(iii) Maximum gasoline cut yield (3 marks):** $$W_{\text{gasoline}} = \sum_{n=5}^{11} n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1} = (0.20)^2 \sum_{n=5}^{11} n(0.80)^{n-1}$$ Compute partial sum using the identity: $$\sum_{n=5}^{11} n(0.80)^{n-1} = \frac{1 - 12(0.80)^{11} + 11(0.80)^{12}}{(0.20)^2} - \frac{1 - 5(0.80)^4 + 4(0.80)^5}{(0.20)^2}$$ Evaluating: - For N = 11: numerator = $1 - 12(0.0859) + 11(0.0687) = 1 - 1.031 + 0.756 = 0.725$ → sum ≈ 18.13 - For N = 4: numerator = $1 - 5(0.4096) + 4(0.3277) = 1 - 2.048 + 1.311 = 0.263$ → sum ≈ 6.575 - Difference = 18.13 − 6.575 = 11.56 $$W_{\text{gasoline}} = (0.04)(11.56) \approx \mathbf{0.462}\;\;(46.2\;\text{wt\%})$$ This represents the **theoretical maximum gasoline yield** at α = 0.80 — a fundamental ASF ceiling. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Describe the Fischer-Tropsch process with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of catalysts (Fe vs Co) and explain why **iron catalysts are preferred for coal-derived syngas** while **cobalt catalysts are preferred for natural-gas-derived syngas**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A syngas with composition 60% H₂ and 40% CO (by volume) is fed to an FT reactor operating at 30 bar and 230 °C using a cobalt catalyst.
