For an α = 0.85 assumption: (i) Calculate the H₂:CO stoichiometric requirement for paraffin formation. **(2 Marks)** (ii) Determine the **single-pass conversion** required if the recycle ratio (recycle:feed) is 2:1 and overall CO conversion is 90%. **(3 Marks)** (iii) Which is the **bottleneck reactant** and why? **(2 Marks)** --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (Outline)** **Part (a) (7 Marks):** - FT flow diagram (refer SECTION 4.1): syngas preparation → gas cleaning → FT reactor → product recovery → hydrocracker → distillation (3 marks) - Catalyst comparison (3 marks): | Property | Iron (Fe) | Cobalt (Co) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | WGS activity | High | Low | | H₂:CO requirement | ~1:1 (CTL) | ~2:1 (GTL) | | Operating T | 320–350 °C (HTFT) | 200–240 °C (LTFT) | | Cost | Cheap | Expensive | | Sulfur tolerance | Low | Very low | | Preferred for | Coal (low H₂:CO syngas) | Natural gas (high H₂:CO syngas) | - *Reason (1 mark):* Coal syngas has H₂:CO ≈ 0.7 — Fe's WGS activity *generates extra H₂ in situ*, making it self-correcting. Natural gas syngas has H₂:CO ≈ 3 — Fe would over-shift to methane; Co is more selective and stable. **Part (b) (7 Marks):** (i) Stoichiometric H₂:CO for paraffin = $(2n+1)/n$. For n = 10 (diesel average): $$\frac{(2 \times 10 + 1)}{10} = \frac{21}{10} = \mathbf{2.1}$$ (ii) Recycle calculation: Let F = fresh feed, R = recycle = 2F, then total feed to reactor = F + R = 3F. Overall CO conversion = 90 % → CO converted = 0.9 × 0.4 × F = 0.36F (in mol units). Single-pass conversion = (CO converted)/(CO entering reactor) = 0.36F / (0.4 × 3F) = 0.36/1.2 = **0.30 or 30 %** (3 marks) (iii) **H₂ is the bottleneck reactant** (2 marks) because: - Available H₂:CO = 60:40 = 1.5:1 - Stoichiometric ratio = 2.1:1 - Required H₂ is greater than available → H₂ is limiting; some CO will leave unreacted. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the (1−α) factor** in the ASF formula — students often write $W_n = n\alpha^{n-1}$, losing **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Confusing mole fraction $x_n$ with weight fraction $W_n$** — note the extra $(1-\alpha)$ factor in the latter. > 3. **Drawing FT flow diagrams without arrow direction** — KTU examiners deduct for unlabeled streams (lose 1 mark per unlabeled stream, max 2). > 4. **Writing "Fe and Co" without specifying support materials** (Al₂O₃, SiO₂, TiO₂) — lose 0.5–1 mark. > 5. **In numerical problems**, failing to state the units of α (dimensionless) and to verify the constraint $\sum W_n = 1$. > 6. **Mis-stating FT as a single reaction** — it is a *family* of parallel reactions producing a spectrum; examiners deduct 1 mark if only one equation is given. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Last-Minute Revision Bullets:** > - FT = CO + H₂ → liquid hydrocarbons; **catalytic**, **exothermic** (ΔH ≈ −165 kJ/mol CO). > - Catalysts: **Fe (HTFT)**, **Co (LTFT)**, Ru (research only). > - LTFT: 200–250 °C, 20–40 bar, Co-catalyst → wax/diesel-dominant. > - HTFT: 320–350 °C, 25 bar, Fe-catalyst → gasoline-dominant. > - Reactor types: Multi-tubular fixed bed, slurry bubble column, circulating fluidized bed. > - **ASF distribution:** $x_n = (1-\alpha)\alpha^{n-1}$, $\quad W_n = n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$. > - α = chain-growth probability → high α = heavy products; low α = CH₄. > - **Maximum theoretical selectivity ceiling**: gasoline ≈ **47 wt%** (α ≈ 0.76); diesel ≈ **41 wt%** (α ≈ 0.80). > - **Side reactions:** WGS, Boudouard (carbon deposition), olefin/alcohol formation. > - Industrial plants: **Sasol (South Africa)**, **Shell Pearl GTL (Qatar)**, **Oryx GTL (Qatar)**. > - FT diesel is **near-zero sulfur**, ultra-high cetane (>70) → premium blending stock. > - WGS on Fe catalysts *self-corrects* low H₂:CO → ideal for **CTL** (coal-to-liquid). > - Co catalysts require H₂:CO ≈ 2 → ideal for **GTL** (gas-to-liquid). > - Process flow: Feedstock → Gasification/Reforming → Syngas → Cleaning → FT Reactor → Hydrocracker → Distillation → Liquid Fuels. ---

topics.title: Petrochemical products and their applications: first, second and third generation petrochemical products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Petrochemical products and their applications: first, second and third generation petrochemical products ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition of Petrochemicals **Petrochemicals** are chemical products derived from petroleum (crude oil) and natural gas. They form the chemical building blocks for a vast range of consumer and industrial goods. The petrochemical industry converts raw hydrocarbons from refining and gas processing into a cascade of value-added products, systematically classified into **First, Second, and Third Generation** petrochemicals based on their position in the production chain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The classification into **first, second, and third generation** petrochemicals is fundamental to understanding the petrochemical value chain. Every downstream product can be traced back through generations to a primary hydrocarbon feedstock (naphtha or natural gas). ### 1.2 The Three Generations — An Intuitive Analogy Imagine a tree: - **Roots** = Crude oil / natural gas (raw feedstock) - **Trunk & Main Branches** = First Generation Petrochemicals (the basic building blocks like ethylene and benzene) - **Smaller Branches & Twigs** = Second Generation Petrochemicals (intermediates like ethylene oxide and styrene) - **Leaves, Flowers & Fruits** = Third Generation Petrochemicals (final products like plastics, synthetic fibres, and rubbers that consumers actually use) Just as a tree transforms soil nutrients into useful fruits through successive biological stages, a refinery transforms crude oil into useful end-products through successive chemical processing stages. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Generation Classification:** > - **First Generation:** Primary chemicals directly obtained from feedstocks via cracking/reforming. Also called **primary petrochemicals** or **C1–C4 olefins and C6–C8 aromatics**. > - **Second Generation:** Intermediates manufactured by reacting first-generation products. Also called **primary intermediates or derivatives**. > - **Third Generation:** Finished end-products (polymers, fibres, detergents) made from second-generation intermediates. ### 1.3 Why This Classification Matters - **Economic Value:** Each generation adds value — typically **5–10×** the cost of the previous generation per unit mass. - **Process Planning:** Refineries and petrochemical complexes are designed around these generations to optimize integration. - **Marketing & Trade:** Product pricing and demand forecasting are generation-specific. - **Engineering Design:** Determines reactor types, separation equipment, and utility requirements. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Key Numbers to Memorize) - Global petrochemical production: over **500 million tonnes/year** of primary chemicals. - Ethylene is the highest-volume organic chemical produced worldwide (~**200+ million tonnes/year**). - Approximately **90%** of all manufactured goods contain at least one petrochemical derivative. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 First Generation Petrochemicals (Primary Petrochemicals) These are produced **directly** from the primary raw materials of petroleum and natural gas processing. downstream polymer plants to capture synergies (e.g., Kerala's BPCL Kochi Refinery, Reliance Jamnagar). - **Feedstock Flexibility:** The petrochemical industry is increasingly shifting from naphtha to **ethane** (from shale gas) for first-generation production, improving margins. - **Sustainability:** Petrochemical engineers now focus on **circular economy** principles — recycling third-generation polymers back to monomers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Degree of Polymerization from Monomer Conversion For a step-growth polymerization, let: - N₀ = initial number of monomer molecules - N = number of molecules remaining at time t (monomers + oligomers + polymer chains) - p = fraction of functional groups reacted (conversion) **Step 1:** The number of functional groups consumed = N₀ · p **Step 2:** Each condensation links two molecules, so the number of bonds formed = (N₀ · p) / 2 **Step 3:** The number of remaining molecules = N = N₀ − (N₀ · p)/2 $$N = N_0 \left( 1 - \frac{p}{2} \right)$$ **Step 4:** The number-average degree of polymerization: $$\text{DP}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0 \left( 1 - \frac{p}{2} \right)}$$ $$\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{p}{2}} = \frac{2}{2 - p}$$ **Step 5:** At p = 1 (100% conversion): $$\text{DP}_n = \frac{2}{2 - 1} = 2$$ This **Carothers equation** reveals the famous Carothers' paradox: at complete conversion of difunctional monomers, DPₙ is only 2. To achieve high molecular weight (DPₙ > 100), conversion must exceed **99%**. **Step 6:** For a more general form (functionality f): $$\text{DP}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p \cdot f}$$ ### 3.2 Analytical Numerical Example: Conversion vs. DPₙ **Problem:** In a PET synthesis, the polymerization has reached a conversion of p = 0.98. Find the number-average degree of polymerization (DPₙ). **Solution:** $$\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{p}{2}} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{0.98}{2}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.49} = \frac{1}{0.51}$$ $$\text{DP}_n \approx 1.96$$ **This is too low for commercial PET** (which needs DPₙ > 100). Therefore, step-growth PET synthesis must push conversion beyond **99.5%** to achieve useful molecular weight. **Check at p = 0.995:** $$\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.4975} = \frac{1}{0.5025} \approx 199$$ Now PET achieves commercial-grade mechanical strength. ### 3.4 Mass-Balance Walk-Through: Ethylene → PVC To produce **1 tonne (1000 kg)** of PVC: **Step 1:** PVC repeat unit = CH₂–CHCl, MW = 62.5 g/mol Moles of repeat unit = 1,000,000 / 62.5 = **16,000 mol** **Step 2:** Monomer Vinyl Chloride (C₂H₃Cl), MW = 62.5 g/mol Theoretical VCM required = 16,000 mol × 62.5 g/mol = **1,000,000 g = 1 tonne** (100% conversion basis) **Step 3:** Account for industrial conversion (~95%) and polymerization losses: $$\text{Actual VCM required} = \frac{1.00 \text{ tonne}}{0.95} \approx 1.053 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 4:** Ethylene required to make 1.053 t VCM (assuming 95% yield in VCM synthesis): $$\text{Ethylene} = \frac{1.053}{0.95} \approx 1.108 \text{ tonnes of ethylene}$$ **Conclusion:** Approximately **1.1 tonnes of ethylene** are required to manufacture **1 tonne of PVC**, a third-generation petrochemical product. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define petrochemicals. List any three examples each of first-generation olefins and aromatics with their molecular formulae.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Petrochemicals are organic chemicals derived from petroleum (crude oil) and natural gas that serve as the fundamental building blocks for a wide spectrum of industrial and consumer products. They are classified into three generations based on their stage in the production value chain. **Three first-generation olefins:** 1. Ethylene (C₂H₄) 2. Propylene (C₃H₆) 3. Butadiene (C₄H₆) **Three first-generation aromatics:** 1. Benzene (C₆H₆) 2. Toluene (C₇H₈) 3. p-Xylene (C₈H₁₀) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Explain the difference between second-generation and third-generation petrochemicals with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Second-generation petrochemicals** are primary intermediates manufactured from first-generation petrochemicals by single-step chemical reactions such as oxidation, chlorination, or alkylation. They are not end products but feedstocks for the next stage. *Examples:* - Ethylene oxide (from ethylene) - Vinyl chloride (from ethylene) **Third-generation petrochemicals** are finished end-products manufactured by polymerizing, condensing, or formulating second-generation intermediates. These are products that reach the final consumer. *Examples:* - Polyethylene (from ethylene) - PVC (from vinyl chloride) - Nylon 6,6 (from adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine) --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (ESE Pattern, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **(a) Discuss in detail the classification of petrochemicals into first, second, and third generations. For each generation, give five representative examples with their parent feedstocks and at least one end-use. (7 Marks)** **(b) With the help of a process flow diagram, explain the manufacturing of Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) from ethylene and chlorine. Show the major reactions involved. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Classification of Petrochemicals (7 Marks)** The petrochemical value chain is organized into three hierarchical generations, beginning from raw hydrocarbons and ending at consumer products. **1. First Generation (Primary Petrochemicals):** These are directly produced from petroleum fractions (mainly naphtha) via steam cracking and catalytic reforming. They form the core building blocks. | Example | Molecular Formula | Source Process | End-use | |---------|-------------------|----------------|---------| | Ethylene | C₂H₄ | Steam cracking | Polymerization, EO synthesis | | Propylene | C₃H₆ | Steam cracking / FCC | PP, acrylonitrile | | Butadiene | C₄H₆ | Steam cracking | SBR rubber, ABS | | Benzene | C₆H₆ | Catalytic reforming | Styrene, phenol, aniline | | p-Xylene | C₈H₁₀ | Xylene loop | PET, polyester fibre | **2. Second Generation (Intermediates):** Derived from first-generation products via single-step reactions. | Example | Parent Feedstock | Reaction | End-use | |---------|------------------|----------|---------| | Ethylene Oxide | Ethylene | Catalytic oxidation | Antifreeze, surfactants | | Vinyl Chloride | Ethylene | Chlorination + cracking | PVC | | Styrene | Ethylbenzene (from benzene) | Dehydrogenation | Polystyrene, SBR | | Phenol | Cumene (from benzene) | Peroxidation | Bakelite, bisphenol-A | | Terephthalic acid | p-Xylene | Oxidation | PET | **3. Third Generation (End Products):** Manufactured by polymerization or condensation of second-generation chemicals. | Example | From | Application | |---------|------|-------------| | Polyethylene (PE) | Ethylene | Carry bags, containers, pipes | | Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) | Vinyl chloride | Pipes, window frames, cables | | Polystyrene (PS) | Styrene | Foam cups, insulation | | PET | EG + PTA | Bottles, polyester fabric | | Nylon 6,6 | Adipic acid + HMDA | Tyre cords, carpets | **Conclusion:** The three-generation framework helps engineers trace the origin of any product, optimize production economics, and design integrated petrochemical complexes. --- **(b) Manufacture of PVC from Ethylene and Chlorine (7 Marks)** PVC is the third most widely used polymer in the world. The commercial route is the **ethylene-route** (also called the balanced VCM process). **Step 1: Direct Chlorination of Ethylene** $$C_2H_4 + Cl_2 \rightarrow C_2H_4Cl_2 \quad \text{(1,2-Dichloroethane, EDC)}$$ This is an exothermic addition reaction carried out in a liquid-phase reactor with FeCl₃ catalyst at 50–80°C. **Step 2: Pyrolytic Cracking of EDC** $$C_2H_4Cl_2 \rightarrow C_2H_3Cl + HCl \quad \text{(Vinyl Chloride Monomer, VCM)}$$ Carried out in a furnace at 480–520°C, giving VCM and HCl as by-product. **Step 3: Recovery of HCl and Oxychlorination Loop** $$C_2H_4 + 2 HCl + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow C_2H_4Cl_2 + H_2O$$ This converts the HCl back to EDC, eliminating waste and improving atom economy. This makes the process "balanced." **Step 4: Polymerization of VCM** $$n \, CH_2=CHCl \rightarrow -(CH_2-CHCl)_n-$$ Typically suspension polymerization in water with initiators (peroxides), dispersants (PVA), and temperature around 50–60°C. Molecular weight is controlled by chain transfer agents. **Process Flow Diagram (Already in Section 4.3) is referred to here.** **Applications of PVC:** Water pipes, electrical cable insulation, window profiles, flooring, blood bags, credit cards, vinyl flooring. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a) What are aromatic petrochemicals? Discuss the catalytic reforming process for the production of BTX (Benzene, Toluene, Xylene) from naphtha. List the major reactions involved. (7 Marks)** **(b) Describe in detail the manufacturing of Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) with balanced chemical equations. Mention its major applications in the textile and packaging industries. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Aromatic Petrochemicals & Catalytic Reforming (7 Marks)** **Aromatic Petrochemicals** are unsaturated cyclic hydrocarbons containing one or more benzene rings. The three most important are collectively called **BTX** — Benzene, Toluene, and Xylene (ortho, meta, para isomers). **Source:** BTX are produced by **Catalytic Reforming of Naphtha** (a C₆–C₁₂ fraction from crude oil distillation). **Catalytic Reforming Process:** - **Feed:** Heavy naphtha (C₆–C₁₂, 80–200°C boiling range) - **Catalyst:** Platinum (or Pt-Re, Pt-Sn) on chlorided alumina - **Temperature:** 480–525°C - **Pressure:** 5–35 bar (modern units use low pressure) - **Reactor type:** Semi-regenerative, cyclic, or continuous catalytic regeneration (CCR) **Major Reactions:** 1. **Dehydrogenation of Naphthenes:** $$C_6H_{12} \rightarrow C_6H_6 + 3 H_2$$ (Endothermic, the primary BTX-producing reaction) 2. **Dehydrocyclization of Paraffins:** $$n-C_7H_{16} \rightarrow C_7H_8 + 4 H_2$$ 3. **Isomerization of n-Paraffins to Isoparaffins:** $$n-C_5H_{12} \rightarrow i-C_5H_{12}$$ 4. **Hydrocracking (minor, undesirable):** $$C_{10}H_{22} + H_2 \rightarrow C_5H_{12} + C_5H_{10}$$ 5. **Dealkylation of Alkyl Aromatics:** $$C_6H_5C_2H_5 + H_2 \rightarrow C_6H_6 + C_2H_6$$ **Reformate Composition (typical):** - Aromatics (BTX): 60–65 wt% - Hydrogen: rich by-product (used in hydrotreating) - Liquid yield: 80–85 wt% of feed **Separation:** The reformate is fractionated in a BTX extraction column using solvents like **Sulfolane** or **NMP** to recover pure BTX. --- **(b) Manufacture of PET (7 Marks)** **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)** is a semi-aromatic polyester made by condensation polymerization of **Ethylene Glycol (EG)** and **Terephthalic Acid (PTA)** or **Dimethyl Terephthalate (DMT)**. **Raw Materials:** - EG: From ethylene oxide hydration (second generation) - PTA: From p-xylene oxidation (second generation) **Step 1: Esterification (or Transesterification if DMT is used)** $$HOCH_2CH_2OH + HOOC-C_6H_4-COOH \rightarrow HOCH_2CH_2OOC-C_6H_4-COOH + H_2O$$ This produces **bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET)** monomer at 240–260°C with a metal catalyst (Sb₂O₃ or Ti(OBu)₄). **Step 2: Polycondensation (Pre-polymerization + Finishing)** $$n \, BHET \rightarrow -[O-CH_2-CH_2-O-CO-C_6H_4-CO]_n- + n \, H_2O$$ This is carried out under high vacuum (1–10 mbar) at 270–290°C to drive the equilibrium towards high molecular weight. Final DPₙ > 100. **Number-Average Molecular Weight:** $$M_n = DP_n \times M_0 = 100 \times 192 \, g/mol = 19{,}200 \, g/mol$$ (where M₀ of PET repeat unit = 192.17 g/mol) **Applications of PET:** 1. **Packaging:** Beverage bottles (Coca-Cola, water bottles), food containers, films. 2. **Textile:** Polyester fibre for clothing, curtains, carpets (Dacron, Terylene). 3. **Industrial:** Tyre reinforcement, conveyor belts, audio/video tapes. 4. **Engineering Plastics:** Glass-reinforced PET for automotive parts. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing generations:** Many students incorrectly classify *polymers* (e.g., PE, PVC) as first generation. **Correct:** Polymers are **third generation**; only ethylene and propylene are first generation. > 2. **Forgetting balanced equations:** KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for unbalanced or missing chemical equations in Part B answers. > 3. **No process conditions:** A reaction equation without temperature, pressure, and catalyst mentioned is considered incomplete (lose 1 mark per major process step). > 4. **Diagram omission:** Even for Part B questions, a small **flow diagram or block diagram** earns 1–2 extra marks in the KTU valuation key. > 5. **Carothers' equation application:** When asked about polymer molecular weight, ALWAYS use the correct step-growth formula $\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p/2}$ and explain the **Carothers' paradox**. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Petrochemicals are classified into **three generations** based on their position in the value chain. - ✅ **First generation** = primary petrochemicals = **Olefins (C₂–C₄)** and **Aromatics (BTX)** + methanol/ammonia from syngas. - ✅ **Second generation** = primary intermediates = EO, EG, VCM, styrene, acrylonitrile, phenol, PTA, caprolactam, adipic acid. - ✅ **Third generation** = end products = plastics (PE, PP, PVC, PS, PET), fibres (Nylon, Polyester), rubbers (SBR, EPDM), detergents, agrochemicals. - ✅ Ethylene is the **highest-volume organic chemical** in the world. - ✅ **Steam cracking** of naphtha (800–900°C) produces olefins; **Catalytic reforming** of naphtha produces aromatics. - ✅ **Cumene process** is the major route to phenol (cumene → cumene hydroperoxide → phenol + acetone). - ✅ **Sohio process** is the major route to acrylonitrile (propylene + ammonia + oxygen). - ✅ **PET** is made by condensation of EG + PTA, releasing water. - ✅ **Nylon 6,6** is made from adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine. - ✅ **Carothers' equation** for step-growth polymerization: $\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p/2}$. - ✅ High molecular weight in condensation polymers requires **> 99% conversion**. - ✅ Polydispersity Index PDI = M_w / Mₙ; equals 1 for perfectly monodisperse polymer. - ✅ Petrochemical complexes integrate first, second, and third generation plants for maximum economic and energy efficiency. - ✅ The petrochemical industry is a cornerstone of modern life: > 90% of manufactured goods contain at least one petrochemical derivative. - ✅ KTU-favorite exam topics: (i) Definitions and examples of all three generations, (ii) Manufacturing of PVC/PET/PE/Nylon with equations, (iii) Catalytic reforming for BTX, (iv) Carothers' equation numericals.

topics.title: Production of ethylene, propylene, and butadiene by naphtha/gas cracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Production of ethylene, propylene, and butadiene by naphtha/gas cracking # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steam Cracking (Pyrolysis)** is the high-temperature thermal decomposition of saturated hydrocarbon feedstocks — typically **naphtha**, **ethane**, **propane**, or **natural gas liquids (NGL)** — in the presence of **dilute steam**, conducted inside externally-fired tubular reactors (cracking furnaces) at coil outlet temperatures of **800 °C – 900 °C** and very short residence times (**0.1 s – 0.5 s**), to produce a slate of light olefins dominated by **ethylene (C₂H₄)**, **propylene (C₃H₆)**, and **1,3-butadiene (C₄H₆)**. In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the process falls under **"non-catalytic, homogeneous, free-radical thermal cracking"** and is the **primary building block reaction of the entire petrochemical value chain**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Carbon Chain Unzipper" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a long pearl necklace (the paraffin molecule, e.g., *n*-dodecane C₁₂H₂₆). If you grab both ends and apply a sharp, fast pull (heat), the string snaps in the middle. But because the pearls are now loose, they re-arrange themselves into **shorter, double-clasped necklaces (alkenes)** and a few stray pearls (**methane, H₂**). Adding **steam** is like having a crowd of helpful bystanders who keep the pearls from immediately re-glueing back together — a process engineer calls this *dilution suppression of secondary reactions*. In real reactors, the pearls (**carbon atoms**) form shorter chains with **double bonds** — these are **olefins**. The double bond is what makes ethylene, propylene, and butadiene so reactive and valuable for making plastics. ## 1.3 The Three Target Olefins — A Quick Snapshot | Product | Formula | Carbon No. | Primary End-Use | Demand Driver | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Ethylene** | C₂H₄ | C₂ | Polyethylene (PE), EDC/VCM | World's largest tonnage petrochemical | | **Propylene** | C₃H₆ | C₃ | Polypropylene (PP), Acrylonitrile, Cumene | Second-largest petrochemical feedstock | | **1,3-Butadiene** | C₄H₆ | C₄ | SBR rubber, ABS, Nitrile rubber | Tyre & latex industry | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Name the three most important olefins produced by steam cracking and state their single largest downstream derivative."* — Be ready with the above table. ## 1.4 Why "Steam" is Added — Three Critical Functions 1. **Reduces partial pressure of hydrocarbons** → shifts equilibrium toward olefin formation (Le Chatelier's principle applied to bond-breaking). 2. **Suppresses coke formation** on tube walls — this extends furnace run-length from weeks to **60 – 90 days**. 3. **Carries sensible heat** into the reactor and reacts endothermically, evening out the temperature profile. The **Steam-to-Hydrocarbon ratio (S/HC)** is the single most important operating lever — typically **0.3 – 0.5 kg steam per kg feed for ethane** and **0.5 – 0.9 kg/kg for naphtha**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Free-Radical (Rice–Herzfeld) Mechanism — Step by Step Steam cracking proceeds via a **free-radical chain mechanism** first proposed by Rice and Herzfeld in the 1930s. The three stages are: ### Stage A — Initiation (C–C bond homolysis) The weakest **C–C bond** in a paraffin (typically the central bond, BDE ≈ **346 kJ/mol**) breaks homolytically to give two alkyl radicals. $$\text{R{-}R'} \xrightarrow{k_i, \Delta H \approx +346 \text{ kJ/mol}} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R'}^{\bullet}$$ ### Stage B — Propagation (β-scission — the key olefin-forming step) A primary alkyl radical undergoes **β-scission** to form an **α-olefin (ethylene)** and a smaller alkyl radical: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_\beta} \text{C}_m\text{H}_{2m} + \text{C}_{n-m}\text{H}_{2(n-m)+1}^{\bullet}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why ethylene dominates the product slate:** The β-scission of a *primary* radical preferentially produces **ethylene** because the C–C bond β to a primary radical is weakest. This is why **higher severity (higher COT) always increases ethylene yield at the expense of propylene and heavier liquids**. ### Stage C — Termination (radical recombination / disproportionation) Radicals annihilate each other to form stable products (paraffins or olefins), or abstract hydrogen from a feed molecule. $$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{RH}$$ $$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R'}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{R{-}R'} \text{ (recombination)}$$ ## 2.2 The Four Severity Levers — A KTU Must-Know Framework The entire operation of a steam cracker is controlled by **four interconnected knobs**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "KTCS" Levers of Severity:** > - **K**inetic severity function (KSF) > - **T**emperature (COT) > - **C**ontact / residence time (θ) > - **S**team ratio (S/HC) ### 2.2.1 Coil Outlet Temperature (COT) - Naphtha crackers: **820 °C – 870 °C** - Ethane crackers: **840 °C – 900 °C** - Higher COT → **higher ethylene, lower propylene, more methane + H₂** ### 2.2.2 Residence Time (θ) - Modern high-selectivity furnaces operate at **0.1 s – 0.3 s**. - Short residence time + very high COT gives the **highest selectivity** (moles of ethylene per mole of feed converted). ### 2.2.3 Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio (S/HC) - Acts as an **inert diluent**, lowering hydrocarbon partial pressure. - Higher S/HC suppresses secondary bimolecular reactions (Diels–Alder, cyclization, coke). ### 2.2.4 Severity (combined metric) Two empirical metrics dominate the industry: 1. **Kinetic Severity Function (KSF)** — defined by **Linden, Peck & Salerno (1985)**: $$\boxed{\text{KSF} = \int_0^{\theta} k_E(T)\, dt \approx \theta \cdot k_E(\text{COT})}$$ 2. **Propylene-to-Ethylene Ratio (P/E)** — the simplest operating diagnostic: $$\text{P/E Ratio} = \frac{\text{Propylene Yield (wt\%)}}{\text{Ethylene Yield (wt\%)}}$$ > Low severity → P/E ≈ **0.6 – 0.7** (chemical-grade propylene co-product). > High severity → P/E ≈ **0.3 – 0.45** (ethylene max mode). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table. These are the equations that get 14-mark answers written.** | # | Concept | Equation | Typical Units | Notes / KTU Tip | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | First-order cracking rate | $-r_A = k \cdot C_A$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Plug-flow reactor assumption | | 2 | Arrhenius for cracking | $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ | s⁻¹ | $E \approx 220 - 260$ kJ/mol | | 3 | Conversion | $X = 1 - C_A / C_{A0}$ | dimensionless | Always expressed on **mass basis** for feed-stock | | 4 | Selectivity | $S_{i} = \dfrac{Y_i}{X}$ | kg/kg | $Y_i$ = yield of product $i$ | | 5 | Yield | $Y_i = S_i \cdot X$ | wt% of feed | Most common KTU asked value | | 6 | Steam ratio | $\text{S/HC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{steam}}}{m_{\text{feed}}}$ | kg/kg | 0.3 – 0.9 range | | 7 | Residence time | $\theta = V / \dot{V}_f$ | s | Inside the radiant coil only | | 8 | Carbon-efficiency | $\eta_C = \dfrac{\sum (n_i \cdot \dot{m}_i)}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot n_{\text{feed}}}$ | dimensionless | For mass-balance check | | 9 | Standard gas constant | $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ | — | Memorise | | 10 | P/E ratio diagnostic | $\text{P/E} = \dot{m}_{C_3}/\dot{m}_{C_2}$ | kg/kg | Quick severity meter | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Plant capacity sizing:** Every 1000 kg/h of ethylene requires ≈ **3000 kg/h of naphtha** feed (≈ 33 wt% yield basis). - **Economic driver:** Cracker margins are quoted as **$/t ethylene**, making **selectivity** the single most monetised engineering parameter. - **Environmental relevance:** Decoking cycles, CO₂ footprint of furnace burners, and ethylene-to-green-hydrogen (electric cracking by BASF / Linde) are the active R&D frontiers. ## 2.5 Coke Formation — The Inevitable Penalty Side reactions always produce **coke** (poly-aromatic carbon), which fouls radiant tubes and reduces heat transfer. Mitigation involves: - Adding **sulphur compounds (DMDS, H₂S)** at **50 – 200 wppm** to passivate nickel in tube alloys. - Periodic **decoking** by steam–air burn-off (off-line every **40 – 90 days**). - Advanced metallurgy (e.g., **35Cr-45Ni** HP-mod alloys). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section develops the canonical derivation you would need to secure **full 14 marks** on a KTU ESE Part B question. Every algebraic step is explicitly written; nothing is skipped. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Conversion in a Plug-Flow Cracking Coil ### 3.1.1 Set-up Assume the cracking coil behaves as an **ideal plug-flow reactor (PFR)** with **no radial gradients**, isothermal at an effective average temperature $T_{\text{avg}}$, and the cracking reaction is **first order in the hydrocarbon**. The molar rate of disappearance of feed A is: $$-r_A = k \cdot C_A$$ ### 3.1.2 Material Balance on a Differential Slice Take a differential slice of coil volume $dV$ at position $z$. With cross-section $A_c$ and superficial velocity $u_s$: $$\dot{V}_f \cdot dC_A = -r_A \cdot dV = -k \cdot C_A \cdot dV$$ ### 3.1.3 Separate Variables $$\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \cdot \frac{dV}{\dot{V}_f} = -k \cdot d\theta$$ ### 3.1.4 Integrate from Inlet to Outlet Applying limits $C_{A0} \to C_A$ as $\theta: 0 \to \theta$: $$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{C_A}{C_{A0}}\right) = -k\theta$$ ### 3.1.5 Convert to Conversion Since $X = 1 - C_A/C_{A0}$: $$\ln(1 - X) = -k\theta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{X = 1 - \exp(-k\theta)}$$ This is the **master equation** for first-order cracking conversion. KTU may give you $k$ and $\theta$ and ask you to compute $X$ — plug directly. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Coupling with Severity ### 3.2.1 Substitute the Arrhenius Form Plug $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ into the master equation: $$\ln(1 - X) = -A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) \cdot \theta$$ ### 3.2.2 Define the Kinetic Severity Function $$\text{KSF} \equiv \theta \cdot k(T) = \theta \cdot A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ Then: $$\boxed{X = 1 - \exp(-\text{KSF})}$$ > [!TIP] > **Board tip:** For naphtha cracking, $E \approx 220$ kJ/mol. If a question gives $E$ in kcal/mol, **convert first** using 1 kcal = 4.184 kJ. A common student error is leaving the units mixed. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Balance for a Naphtha Cracker (Numerical) ### 3.3.1 Problem Statement A naphtha steam-cracking furnace processes **10 000 kg/h** of feed. The yields (mass basis) are: - Ethylene (C₂H₄): **33.0 wt%** - Propylene (C₃H₆): **14.5 wt%** - 1,3-Butadiene: **4.5 wt%** - Other (H₂, CH₄, BTX, fuel oil, coke): **balance** Compute (a) hourly production rate of each olefin; (b) the **propylene-to-ethylene mass ratio** (severity diagnostic); (c) the **carbon efficiency**. ### 3.3.2 Solution — Step 1: Mass Flow of Each Olefin $$\dot{m}_{C_2} = 0.330 \times 10\,000 = 3300 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{C_3} = 0.145 \times 10\,000 = 1450 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{C_4} = 0.045 \times 10\,000 = 450 \text{ kg/h}$$ ### 3.3.3 Solution — Step 2: P/E Ratio $$\text{P/E} = \frac{1450}{3300} = 0.439$$ **Interpretation:** This is a *high-severity* operation (P/E < 0.5). ### 3.3.4 Solution — Step 3: Carbon Efficiency Carbon content of feed (assume naphtha = C_nH_{2n+0.2}, average n ≈ 7.5 for full-range naphtha): Carbon balance must close: $$\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot w_C^{\text{feed}} = \sum_i \dot{m}_i \cdot w_C^i$$ Take feed carbon content $w_C^{\text{feed}} = 0.845$ (typical for naphtha). Take C wt% in olefins: C₂H₄ → $24/28 = 0.857$; C₃H₆ → $36/42 = 0.857$; C₄H₆ → $48/54 = 0.889$. Carbon out in target olefins: $$\dot{C}_{\text{out}} = (3300)(0.857) + (1450)(0.857) + (450)(0.889)$$ $$\dot{C}_{\text{out}} = 2828 + 1243 + 400 = 4471 \text{ kg/h}$$ Carbon in: $$\dot{C}_{\text{in}} = 10\,000 \times 0.845 = 8450 \text{ kg/h}$$ Carbon efficiency (target olefins): $$\eta_C = \frac{4471}{8450} = 0.529 \approx 52.9\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** ~53% of the carbon in feed appears as the three target olefins. The remainder exits as methane, fuel oil, BTX, and coke. Improving this number by 1% is worth millions of dollars annually at cracker scale. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effect of Severity on Product Distribution (Qualitative Analytical Matrix) | Severity | COT (°C) | θ (s) | Ethylene (wt%) | Propylene (wt%) | Butadiene (wt%) | P/E | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Low | 800 | 0.45 | 22 | 16 | 4.5 | 0.73 | | Medium | 840 | 0.30 | 28 | 15 | 4.5 | 0.54 | | High | 870 | 0.20 | 33 | 14 | 4.0 | 0.42 | | Ultra-high | 900 | 0.10 | 38 | 11 | 3.5 | 0.29 | > [!TIP] > **High-Yield KTU Insight:** The **butadiene yield peaks at medium severity**. Above ~870 °C it starts to *fall* because 1,3-butadiene is itself an unstable diene that undergoes secondary Diels–Alder and cyclisation into aromatics (BTX). ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Decoking Cycle Frequency (Thermal Balance) Coke deposition rate is empirically fitted as: $$\frac{d\delta}{dt} = k_c \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_c}{RT_s}\right) \quad\text{with } E_c \approx 150 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Where $\delta$ = coke thickness (µm) and $T_s$ = tube skin temperature. When $\delta$ reaches a critical value (≈ 5 – 8 mm), tube metal temperature approaches design limit (~1050 °C for HP-mod alloys) and decoking is triggered. This sets the **furnace run-length** at ≈ 60 – 90 days. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Industrial Steam-Cracker Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid block-diagram traces the complete path of naphtha from feed drum to the three target olefin product streams, including every major piece of process equipment found in an ethylene plant. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Feedstock
Naphtha Storage
Tank 40C] nodeB[Feed Pump
P101 Centrifugal] nodeC[Convection Section
Preheater Coils
120 to 600 C] nodeD[Steam Mixer
Dilution Steam
S by HC Ratio 0.6] nodeE[Cross-Over
to Radiant Section] nodeF[Radiant Section
Cracking Furnace
COT 840 C] nodeG[Transfer Line
Exchanger TLE
Quench to 450 C] nodeH[Primary Fractionator
Heavy Ends Column
Fuel Oil Draw] nodeI[Quench Water Tower
Dilution Steam Recovery] nodeJ[Multi-Stage
Compressor 5 Stage] nodeK[Caustic Wash Tower
Acid Gas Removal] nodeL[Demethanizer
C1 CH4 H2] nodeM[Deethanizer
C2 Splitter
Ethylene Product] nodeN[Depropanizer
C3 Splitter
Propylene Product] nodeO[Butadiene Extraction
ACN or NMP Solvent
1,3 Butadiene Product] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeD --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeN nodeN --> nodeO nodeF -.-> nodeOutA[Cracked Gas
to Quench] nodeM -.-> nodeOutB[Polyethylene
Grade Ethylene] nodeN -.-> nodeOutC[Polypropylene
Grade Propylene] nodeO -.-> nodeOutD[SBR Rubber
Grade Butadiene] classDef reactor fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef separation fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef product fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class F,G reactor class H,I,J,K,L,M,N,O separation class OutB,OutC,OutD product ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram (left → right):** The naphtha feed is vaporised and superheated in the convection section (heat recovery zone), mixed with dilution steam, then sent into the **radiant section** — this is where the **free-radical cracking chemistry** happens at ~840 °C for ~0.3 s. The cracked gas is **instantly quenched** in the TLE to stop secondary reactions, then sent through a sequence of **distillation columns** to separate the three target olefins. ## 4.2 Inside the Radiant Coil — Schematic of a Modern Millisecond Cracker ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeIn1[Inlet from
Convection Section
620 C] nodeCoil[Radiant Coil
8 to 12 Swaged Tubes
ID 30 to 60 mm] nodeBurner[Floor and Wall
Gas Fired Burners
Heat Flux 90 kW per m2] nodeOut[Outlet to TLE
COT 840 to 870 C] nodeSkin[Tube Skin Temp
Max 1050 C
HP Mod Alloy] nodeIn1 --> nodeCoil nodeBurner -.-> nodeCoil nodeCoil --> nodeOut nodeCoil -.-> nodeSkin classDef hot fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef alloy fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class Coil,Burner,Out hot class Skin alloy ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key numbers to memorise** (high-frequency KTU 2024 exam content): > - **Heat flux** at tube wall: **80 – 100 kW/m²** > - **Tube metallurgy:** HK-40 → HP-Mod → most modern 35Cr-45Ni > - **Number of passes:** 4 – 8 swaged passes per furnace to maximise heat transfer ## 4.3 Material Balance Map — Where the Carbons Go ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeFeed[Naphtha Feed

100 kg 100 percent C] nodeC2[Ethylene

C2H4] nodeC3[Propylene
14.5 kg
C3H6] nodeC4[1,3 Butadiene

15 kg
CH4] nodeH2[Hydrogen
1 kg
H2] nodeBTX[BTX Aromatics
12 kg
Benzene Toluene Xylene] nodeFO[Fuel Oil

C9 plus] nodeCoke[Coke
2 kg
Carbon] nodeAcid[Acidic Gases
