H2S CO2] nodeFeed --> nodeC2 nodeFeed --> nodeC3 nodeFeed --> nodeC4 nodeFeed --> nodeCH4 nodeFeed --> nodeH2 nodeFeed --> nodeBTX nodeFeed --> nodeFO nodeFeed --> nodeCoke nodeFeed --> nodeAcid classDef main fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef byp fill:#dcdcdc,stroke:#808080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class C2,C3,C4 main class CH4,H2,BTX,FO,Coke,Acid byp ``` > [!TIP] > **The 50% Rule of Thumb:** In a well-tuned medium-severity naphtha cracker, roughly **50% of feed carbon exits as the three target olefins (ethylene + propylene + butadiene)**, and the other 50% leaves as by-products. KTU loves asking students to construct this pie-chart-like breakdown. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define steam cracking. Why is dilution steam added to the hydrocarbon feed in a steam cracker? **Model Answer:** Steam cracking is the **thermal, non-catalytic decomposition of saturated hydrocarbons (naphtha, ethane, LPG)** in externally-heated tubular reactors in the presence of dilute steam, to produce light olefins — primarily ethylene, propylene, and butadiene. **Three roles of dilution steam:** 1. **Lowers hydrocarbon partial pressure** → suppresses secondary bimolecular reactions (Diels–Alder, oligomerisation) and pushes selectivity toward ethylene. 2. **Reduces coke deposition** on tube walls → extends furnace run-length from days to **60 – 90 days**. 3. **Acts as a heat carrier** → ensures uniform temperature profile along the coil. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the **Kinetic Severity Function (KSF)**? Why is it preferred over COT alone to characterise cracking severity? **Model Answer:** $$\text{KSF} = \int_0^{\theta} k(T)\, dt \approx \theta \cdot A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ KSF combines **time and temperature** into a single dimensionless metric of cumulative reaction exposure. COT alone ignores residence time — two furnaces operating at the same COT but with different coil lengths will give very different product slates. KSF gives a **more physically meaningful measure of cracking severity** and correlates better with the **propylene-to-ethylene (P/E) ratio**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Mass Balance + Reactor Design* **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Q4(a)+(b)]** A naphtha steam-cracking furnace is fed with **20 000 kg/h** of full-range naphtha (average molecular formula **C₇.₅H₁₇**, MW = 105.7). The coil outlet temperature is **860 °C** and the residence time in the radiant section is **0.30 s**. Reported yields (mass %) are: - Ethylene: 32 % - Propylene: 15 % - 1,3-Butadiene: 4 % - Methane + H₂ + others: balance #### (a) [7 marks] Calculate: 1. The mass flow rate of each of the **three target olefins** produced per hour. 2. The **propylene-to-ethylene (P/E) mass ratio**. 3. The **carbon efficiency** to the three target olefins (use $w_C^{\text{feed}} = 0.845$, and $w_C^{C_2H_4}=0.857$, $w_C^{C_3H_6}=0.857$, $w_C^{C_4H_6}=0.889$). #### (b) [7 marks] The cracking reaction is first order with $k = 4.2 \text{ s}^{-1}$ at 860 °C. Compute the conversion of naphtha in the coil. If the engineer raises the COT to 880 °C and the activation energy is **230 kJ/mol**, calculate the new conversion (assume residence time unchanged). --- ### **Model Answer — Question A** #### Part (a) **Step 1 — Mass flow of each olefin:** $$\dot{m}_{C_2H_4} = 0.32 \times 20\,000 = 6400 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{C_3H_6} = 0.15 \times 20\,000 = 3000 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{C_4H_6} = 0.04 \times 20\,000 = 800 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 2 — P/E ratio:** $$\text{P/E} = \frac{3000}{6400} = 0.469$$ **Interpretation:** P/E < 0.5 → this is a **high-severity operation** aimed at maximising ethylene. **Step 3 — Carbon efficiency:** $$\dot{C}_{\text{out}} = (6400)(0.857) + (3000)(0.857) + (800)(0.889)$$ $$\dot{C}_{\text{out}} = 5485 + 2571 + 711 = 8767 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\dot{C}_{\text{in}} = 20\,000 \times 0.845 = 16\,900 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_C = \frac{8767}{16\,900} = 0.519 \approx 51.9\%}$$ #### Part (b) **Step 1 — Conversion at 860 °C (given $k = 4.2$ s⁻¹, $\theta = 0.30$ s):** $$X_1 = 1 - \exp(-k\theta) = 1 - \exp(-4.2 \times 0.30) = 1 - \exp(-1.26)$$ $$X_1 = 1 - 0.2837 = 0.716 = \mathbf{71.6\%}$$ **Step 2 — New rate constant at 880 °C using Arrhenius:** $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$ with $E = 230\,000$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T_1 = 1133$ K, $T_2 = 1153$ K: $$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{1153} - \frac{1}{1133} = 0.000\,867 - 0.000\,883 = -1.53 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$-\frac{E}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = -\frac{230\,000}{8.314}\times(-1.53 \times 10^{-5}) = 0.423$$ $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{0.423} = 1.527$$ $$k_2 = 4.2 \times 1.527 = 6.41 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 3 — New conversion at 880 °C:** $$X_2 = 1 - \exp(-6.41 \times 0.30) = 1 - \exp(-1.923) = 1 - 0.146 = \mathbf{85.4\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Raising COT by only **20 °C** boosted conversion by **~14 percentage points**. This shows why temperature control at the coil outlet is the single most sensitive operating variable in a cracker. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Mechanism + Process Flow + Yield Trends* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q5(a)+(b)] — Internal Choice Alternative** #### (a) [7 marks] Explain the **free-radical (Rice–Herzfeld) mechanism** of steam cracking of naphtha. With the help of balanced equations, identify the three steps and explain which step is **responsible for ethylene formation**. #### (b) [7 marks] Draw and explain the **process flow diagram of a typical naphtha steam cracker** for the production of ethylene, propylene, and butadiene. State the function of any **five major equipment items**. --- ### **Model Answer — Question B** #### Part (a) **Step 1 — Initiation:** Homolytic cleavage of a C–C bond in a paraffin molecule gives two alkyl radicals: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \longrightarrow \text{C}_m\text{H}_{2m+1}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_{n-m}\text{H}_{2(n-m)+1}^{\bullet}$$ This step requires high energy because the C–C bond dissociation energy is approximately **346 kJ/mol**. It occurs in the **high-temperature radiant section (800 – 870 °C)**. **Step 2 — Propagation (β-scission) — the ethylene-forming step:** The primary alkyl radical undergoes β-scission — the bond **beta** to the radical carbon breaks: $$\text{CH}_3\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{–CH}_2^{\bullet}$$ The products are: - An **α-olefin (ethylene)** — the desired product - A smaller **primary alkyl radical** — which propagates the chain further. This **β-scission step is responsible for ethylene formation**. Repeating β-scission on successively smaller radicals produces ethylene repeatedly — which is why ethylene is always the dominant product, regardless of feed chain length. **Step 3 — Termination:** Two radicals combine (recombination) or one radical abstracts hydrogen from a stable molecule: $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \text{ (ethane)}$$ $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5^{\bullet} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} \text{ (n-butane)}$$ These termination products become part of the by-product stream and may even be **recycled back to the furnace** to improve overall olefin yield. #### Part (b) **Refer to Section 4 Mermaid block diagram for the full schematic.** Major equipment functions: 1. **Convection Section Coils** — Recover waste heat from furnace flue gas to pre-heat naphtha feed (liquid → vapour) and superheat dilution steam. Operates at 120 °C → 600 °C. 2. **Radiant Section Furnace** — The heart of the cracker. Tubular reactor where COT = 860 °C and θ = 0.3 s. External gas burners supply **80 – 100 kW/m²** of heat flux. 3. **Transfer Line Exchanger (TLE)** — Ultra-fast boiler that quenches the cracked gas from ~860 °C to ~450 °C in milliseconds, **freezing** the desired olefin product slate and generating **high-pressure steam (HP, ~100 bar)** for the steam turbine driver of the cracked-gas compressor. 4. **Primary Fractionator** — Separates heavy by-products (fuel oil, tars) from the cracked gas. Bottom stream = fuel oil; top stream continues to compression. 5. **Cracked-Gas Compressor (5-stage, ~30 – 35 bar discharge)** — Raises cracked-gas pressure so it can be liquefied and fractionated by distillation at near-ambient temperatures. 6. **Demethanizer / Deethanizer / Depropanizer Columns** — Sequential distillation train that separates methane, ethane/ethylene, propane/propylene, and C₄ streams. 7. **Butadiene Extraction Unit** — Uses a selective solvent (**ACN — acetonitrile** or **NMP — N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone**) in extractive distillation to recover pure 1,3-butadiene from the C₄ cut. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Confusing ethylene yield with ethylene selectivity.** Selectivity = yield / conversion. KTU specifically uses both terms. > 2. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** when using the Arrhenius equation. A common, costly slip-up. > 3. **Stating "high temperature favours ethylene" without explaining WHY** — always invoke the **β-scission primary-radical argument**. > 4. **Drawing a block diagram but failing to label the operating temperature** at each major unit. Examiners expect to see 120 °C, 600 °C, 860 °C, 450 °C, −30 °C annotations. > 5. **Mixing up mass % and mole %** when reporting yields. The KTU convention is **mass % for yields**, mole % only for gas-phase compositions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Print this section. Read it the morning of the exam.** - [x] Steam cracking = **non-catalytic, thermal, free-radical** decomposition at **800 – 900 °C** in the presence of dilute steam. - [x] **Three target products:** Ethylene (C₂H₄), Propylene (C₃H₆), 1,3-Butadiene (C₄H₆). - [x] **Mechanism:** Rice–Herzfeld — Initiation (homolysis) → Propagation (β-scission, makes ethylene) → Termination (radical annihilation). - [x] **Severity levers (KTCS):** Kinetic Severity Function (KSF), Temperature (COT), Contact time (θ), Steam ratio (S/HC). - [x] **Steam ratio (S/HC):** 0.3 – 0.9 kg steam/kg feed. Lower partial pressure → higher olefin selectivity → less coke. - [x] **Master equation for first-order cracking:** $X = 1 - \exp(-k\theta)$. - [x] **Arrhenius:** $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$, $E \approx 220$ kJ/mol for naphtha. - [x] **High severity → more ethylene, less propylene** (P/E ratio drops from ~0.7 to ~0.3). - [x] **Butadiene peaks at medium severity** — degrades above ~870 °C via secondary reactions. - [x] **Coke mitigation:** DMDS sulphur passivation, periodic steam–air decoking, HP-Mod metallurgy. - [x] **Industrial train:** Convection → Radiant → TLE → Primary Fractionator → Compressor → Demethanizer → Deethanizer → Depropanizer → C₄ extraction. - [x] **Conversion efficiency benchmark:** ~50% of feed carbon exits as the three target olefins. - [x] **Universal exam constants:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), 1 kcal = 4.184 kJ. - [x] **Master diagnostic:** $\text{P/E ratio} = \dot{m}_{C_3}/\dot{m}_{C_2}$ — your instant severity meter.

topics.title: Various methods of polymerization and their mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Various methods of polymerization and their mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymerization – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymerization** is the chemical process by which small repeating molecular units called **monomers** are covalently bonded together to form long-chain molecules known as **polymers** (from Greek: *poly* = many, *meros* = parts). In the context of petroleum refining technology, polymerization is critical for converting light olefinic gases (C₃, C₄ fractions from catalytic cracking/FCC) into high-octane gasoline blending components and into valuable liquid fuels.

The two principal classifications mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

1. **Chain-Growth (Addition) Polymerization** – Monomers add one at a time to a growing chain end carrying an active site (radical, cation, anion, or metal-coordinated species). No small molecules are eliminated.
2. **Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerization** – Any two species (monomer, oligomer, polymer) with complementary functional groups can react at any time. Small molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃) are typically eliminated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture – The Train vs. The Handshake:**
> - **Chain-growth polymerization** is like building a train: you hook one carriage (monomer) to the engine (active site) at a time, and the locomotive keeps moving forward, adding cars. The engine is the "chain carrier," and the train's identity is set the moment the engine starts.
> - **Step-growth polymerization** is like a crowded party where every two people can shake hands at any moment. Short chains meet other short chains and combine. Nobody is in a "special" role — the chain grows by random meetings of *all* species present (monomers, dimers, trimers, etc.). The party only ends when essentially every possible handshake has been made.

## 1.3 Monomer, Repeat Unit, and Degree of Polymerization

- **Monomer**: The smallest building block. Example: ethylene (CH₂=CH₂), propylene (CH₂=CH–CH₃), styrene (CH₂=CH–C₆H₅), vinyl chloride (CH₂=CHCl).
- **Repeat Unit (mer)**: The constitutional repeating unit incorporated into the polymer backbone.
- **Degree of Polymerization (DP or Xₙ)**: The *number* of repeat units in an average polymer chain.

$$X_n = \frac{\text{Molar mass of polymer (M}_n\text{)}}{\text{Molar mass of repeat unit (M}_0\text{)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (bolded for emphasis):**
> - **DP (Xₙ)** is **dimensionless**.
> - Typical commercial polymers have **Xₙ ≈ 10² to 10⁵**.
> - High molecular weight polymers require **Mₙ ≥ 10⁵ g/mol**.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (KTU Syllabus Map)

| Category | Sub-Types | Active Centre | Petroleum Relevance |
|----------|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| Chain-Growth | Free Radical | Radical (R•) | Polyolefins, polystyrene |
| Chain-Growth | Cationic | Carbocation (C⁺) | Polyisobutylene (butyl rubber), gas-to-liquid upgrades |
| Chain-Growth | Anionic | Carbanion (C⁻) | Specialty synthetic rubbers |
| Chain-Growth | Coordination (Ziegler–Natta) | Metal–C bond | HDPE, isotactic PP |
| Step-Growth | Polycondensation | Functional group | Polyesters, polyamides, phenolic resins |
| Step-Growth | Polyaddition | Functional group | Polyurethanes, epoxy resins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The question paper heavily emphasizes *free radical mechanism steps* (initiation, propagation, termination, chain transfer) and the *comparison between addition and condensation* polymerization. Mastery of these is non-negotiable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chain-Growth (Addition) Polymerization – Mechanism

Chain-growth polymerization proceeds through **four canonical stages**. These stages occur concurrently after initiation.

### Stage 1 — Initiation
A **radical initiator (I)** (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, AIBN) decomposes under heat or light to produce two primary radicals. The radical then adds to a monomer's π-bond, forming a monomer radical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Initiation sets the *kinetic clock* of the reaction. Without an initiator, most vinyl monomers are kinetically stable despite being thermodynamically capable of polymerizing.

### Stage 2 — Propagation
The active chain-end repeatedly adds monomer units. The active site is *regenerated* at every step — that is what makes the chain "grow."

### Stage 3 — Termination
Two active chains combine (coupling) or disproportionate, killing both active centres.

### Stage 4 — Chain Transfer
The active centre is transferred to another molecule (solvent, polymer, monomer, or transfer agent). The original chain is *dead*, but a new active centre is born.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind each step:** Initiation is rare and energetically costly (high Eₐ). Propagation is fast and low-barrier because the active centre is regenerated. Termination is bimolecular and diffusion-controlled. Chain transfer is the "side door" that limits molecular weight without stopping the reaction.

## 2.2 The Four Sub-Types of Chain-Growth Polymerization

### 2.2.1 Free Radical Polymerization
- Active site: **carbon radical (–C•)**
- Initiator: **peroxides, azo compounds (AIBN), redox systems**.
- Works for: **styrene, vinyl chloride, methyl methacrylate, butadiene, acrylates**.
- Mechanism: homolytic bond cleavage → radical addition → head-to-tail propagation.

### 2.2.2 Cationic Polymerization
- Active site: **carbocation (–C⁺)**.
- Initiator: **strong Lewis acids (BF₃, AlCl₃) with co-initiator (H₂O, HCl)**.
- Works for: **electron-rich monomers — isobutylene, styrene, vinyl ethers**.
- Strong electron-donating groups on the monomer *stabilize* the carbocation.

### 2.2.3 Anionic Polymerization
- Active site: **carbanion (–C⁻)**.
- Initiator: **strong bases / organometallics (n-BuLi, NaNH₂, Grignard reagents)**.
- Works for: **electron-withdrawing groups — styrene, butadiene, methyl methacrylate, acrylonitrile**.
- Often **"living"**: absence of termination step allows molecular-weight engineering.

### 2.2.4 Coordination (Insertion) Polymerization
- Active site: **transition-metal–carbon bond (e.g., Ti–C, Zr–C)**.
- Catalyst: **Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₄/AlR₃) or metallocenes**.
- Works for: **ethylene, propylene, 1,3-butadiene**.
- Provides **stereoregularity** (isotactic, syndiotactic) — controls tacticity.

## 2.3 Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerization – Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Carothers' Insight (1929):** In step-growth, *every* molecule carrying complementary functional groups can react with *any* other. High molecular weight is achieved only at *very high conversions* (typically > 99%).

### 2.3.1 Carothers' Equation (the cornerstone formula)

For a stoichiometrically balanced system (r = 1):

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

For a non-stoichiometric system with stoichiometric imbalance *r* (r = N_A/N_B, where N_A ≤ N_B):

$$X_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

where:
- **Xₙ** = number-average degree of polymerization
- **p** = fraction of functional groups reacted (conversion)
- **r** = stoichiometric ratio (≤ 1)

### 2.3.2 Kinetics of Step-Growth Polymerization
For an uncatalyzed, second-order, equal-reactivity system (A–A + B–B type):

$$-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k [A]^2$$

Integration gives:

$$\frac{1}{[A]} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + kt$$

But [A] = [A]₀(1 − p), so:

$$\frac{1}{(1-p)} = 1 + [A]_0 \, k t$$

## 2.4 Methods (Physical Implementation) of Polymerization

The KTU syllabus specifically names these physical *processes* (they are methods, not mechanisms):

1. **Bulk (Mass) Polymerization** – Monomer + initiator only. Pure polymer, but poor heat transfer.
2. **Solution Polymerization** – Monomer dissolved in solvent. Better heat removal, but solvent recovery cost.
3. **Suspension (Pearl) Polymerization** – Monomer droplets dispersed in water with stabilizer. Produces beads.
4. **Emulsion Polymerization** – Monomer emulsified with surfactant + water-soluble initiator. Very high molecular weight + high rate.
5. **Slurry/Gas-Phase Polymerization** – Used in Ziegler–Natta processes (polyolefin manufacture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this distinction matters:** The *mechanism* (free radical, ionic, etc.) and the *method* (bulk, emulsion, etc.) are *independent* axes. A free-radical polymerization can be carried out by bulk, solution, suspension, *or* emulsion technique. Examiners expect students to keep these axes separate.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Application |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| Degree of Polymerization | $X_n = M_n / M_0$ | Basic DP definition |
| Carothers (balanced) | $X_n = 1/(1-p)$ | Stoichiometric step-growth |
| Carothers (imbalanced) | $X_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ | Non-stoichiometric step-growth |
| Rate of Initiation (radical) | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | Radical generation from initiator |
| Rate of Polymerization | $R_p = k_p [M \bullet] [M]$ | Monomer consumption rate |
| Rate of Termination | $R_t = 2 k_t [M \bullet]^2$ | Bimolecular radical death |
| Steady-state [M•] | $[M \bullet] = \sqrt{R_i / 2 k_t}$ | Quasi-steady-state assumption |
| Kinetic Chain Length | $\nu = R_p / R_i$ | Average monomers per active centre |
| DP from chain transfer | $X_n = R_p / (R_t + R_{tr})$ | Effect of chain transfer on DP |
| Ceiling Temperature | $T_c = \Delta H / \Delta S$ | Threshold for polymerization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are *the* tools used in industrial reactor design. **R_p** sets the *production rate*; **R_t** and **R_{tr}** set the *molecular weight*. A polymerization engineer tunes **temperature, initiator concentration, and solvent choice** to hit a target **Xₙ** at a target **R_p**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FCC off-gas → gasoline-range liquids**: Catalytic oligomerization (a chain-growth variant) polymerizes C₃/C₄ olefins into C₆–C₁₂ gasoline with high octane number.
- **Polyethylene & polypropylene production**: Coordination polymerization (Ziegler–Natta) is the world's largest-volume polymerization process (≈ 200 million tonnes/year globally).
- **Synthetic lubricants**: Oligomerization of 1-decene (via cationic/BF₃ mechanism) yields polyalphaolefins (PAO) used as high-performance engine oils.
- **Adhesive and coating resins**: Step-growth polymerization of phenol–formaldehyde, urea–formaldehyde, and epoxy systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rate of Free-Radical Polymerization

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

### Step 1 — Write the elementary rate expressions

For the canonical elementary steps:

- Initiation: $I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2 R^{\bullet}$
- Radical + monomer: $R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} M_1^{\bullet}$
- Propagation: $M_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} M_{n+1}^{\bullet}$
- Termination: $M_n^{\bullet} + M_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} P_{n+m}$ (coupling or disproportionation)

The rate of initiation (radical generation) is:

$$R_i = \frac{d[R^{\bullet}]}{dt} = 2 f k_d [I]$$

where *f* is the **initiator efficiency** (fraction of radicals that successfully add to monomer).

### Step 2 — Apply the steady-state approximation (SSA)

Assume $[M^{\bullet}]$ is small and nearly constant during the reaction. Then:

$$\frac{d[M^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0$$

Therefore $R_i = R_t$:

$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the radical concentration

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

### Step 4 — Write the rate of polymerization

The rate of monomer consumption is governed by the propagation step:

$$R_p = -\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M^{\bullet}] [M]$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $[M^{\bullet}]$ from Step 3

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

### Step 6 — Derive the kinetic chain length $\nu$

By definition, $\nu$ is the *average number of monomer molecules added per active centre* before termination:

$$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]}{2 f k_d [I]}$$

Substituting $[M^{\bullet}]$:

$$\nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]} = \frac{k_p^2 [M]^2}{2 k_t f k_d [I]}$$

### Step 7 — Combine to obtain number-average DP

When *only* bimolecular termination is operative and no chain transfer occurs:

$$X_n = \frac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr}} \approx \frac{R_p}{R_t} = \nu$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The equations reveal that **doubling initiator concentration [I] quadruples R_p** (since $R_p \propto \sqrt{[I]}$), but it *reduces* the molecular weight by a factor of two. This is the classic **rate-versus-molecular-weight trade-off** in free-radical polymerization — a favourite viva question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Carothers' Equation (Step-Growth)

### Step 1 — Define conversion *p*

For an A–A + B–B system, let $N_0$ be the initial number of A groups (= initial number of B groups in a balanced system). After reaction, the number of A groups remaining is:

$$N_A = N_0 (1-p)$$

### Step 2 — Count the molecules

Initially there are $N_0$ monomer molecules (each has one A and one B). At conversion *p*, the number of A groups reacted is $N_0 p$. Each reaction consumes **two** functional groups (one A and one B) and thus the number of *new bonds* formed is $N_0 p$.

The number of polymer molecules remaining is:

$$N = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0 (1-p)$$

(Each bond formation reduces the molecule count by 1.)

### Step 3 — Compute Xₙ

$$\begin{aligned}
X_n &= \frac{\text{Initial molecules}}{\text{Remaining molecules}} \\
&= \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} \\
&= \frac{1}{1-p}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — For non-stoichiometric systems

Let $N_A$ and $N_B$ be the initial functional group concentrations with $N_A \le N_B$. The stoichiometric imbalance:

$$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} \le 1$$

Total initial functional groups: $N_A + N_B = N_A(1 + 1/r)$. The number of unreacted A groups at conversion *p* of A is $N_A(1-p)$. The number of unreacted B groups is $N_B - N_A p$. Total unreacted:

$$N_{\text{unreacted}} = N_A(1-p) + N_B - N_A p = N_A + N_B - 2 N_A p$$

The number of bonds formed is half the number of reacted groups: $N_A p$. The remaining molecule count is initial molecule count minus bond count:

$$N = N_0 - N_A p$$

After algebra (substituting $r = N_A/N_B$ and simplifying):

$$X_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Carothers)

**Problem:** Adipic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH) and hexamethylenediamine (H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂) react to form nylon-6,6. Find the conversion at which $X_n = 100$ for a stoichiometrically balanced mixture.

**Solution:**

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1-p} = 100$$

$$1 - p = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01$$

$$p = 0.99 = 99\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the famous "99% rule"** in step-growth polymerization: to get a polymer with $X_n = 100$, you need **99% conversion**; for $X_n = 200$, you need **99.5%** conversion. This is why industrial polycondensation reactors (e.g., for PET, nylon) are designed for *extremely* high conversions — even tiny residual monomers drastically reduce molecular weight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechanism Flow Diagram – Free Radical Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeI["Initiator I"] -->|k_d, heat| nodeR["Primary Radical R•"]
    nodeR -->|k_i, addition| nodeM1["Monomer Radical M1•"]
    nodeM1 -->|k_p, propagation| nodeM2["Active Chain Mn•"]
    nodeM2 -->|k_p, propagation| nodeM3["Longer Active Chain Mn+1•"]
    nodeM3 -->|k_t, termination coupling| nodeP["Dead Polymer Pn+m"]
    nodeM3 -->|k_t, termination disproportionation| nodeP2["Dead Polymer Pn + Pm"]
    nodeM3 -->|k_tr, chain transfer| nodeM4["Dead Polymer + New Radical C•"]
    nodeM4 -->|k_i, re-initiation| nodeM1
```

## 4.2 Comparison Architecture – Addition vs. Condensation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CG["Chain-Growth Addition"]
        CG1["Monomer M"] -->|radical/ionic addition| CG2["Active Centre M•"]
        CG2 -->|propagation| CG3["Growing Chain"]
        CG3 -->|termination| CG4["High MW polymer"]
    end
    subgraph SG["Step-Growth Condensation"]
        SG1["Monomer A-B"] -->|condensation + byproduct| SG2["Dimer A-B-A-B"]
        SG2 -->|condensation| SG3["Oligomer"]
        SG3 -->|condensation| SG4["Polymer + small molecule"]
        SG5["Monomer A-A + B-B"] -->|condensation| SG2
    end
```

## 4.3 Four Sub-Types of Chain-Growth Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Chain-Growth Polymerization"] --> nodeB["Free Radical"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Cationic"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Anionic"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Coordination Ziegler-Natta"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Initiator: Peroxide, AIBN"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Active site: C radical"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Monomer: Vinyl, styrene, MMA"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Initiator: BF3 plus H2O"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Active site: Carbocation"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Monomer: Isobutylene, vinyl ether"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Initiator: n-BuLi, NaNH2"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Active site: Carbanion"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Monomer: Styrene, butadiene, MMA"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Catalyst: TiCl4 plus AlR3"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Active site: Ti-C bond"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["Monomer: Ethylene, propylene"]
```

## 4.4 Industrial Polymerization Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF1["FCC C3-C4 Olefins"] -->|Feed pretreatment| nodeF2["Purified Olefins"]
    nodeF2 -->|Catalyst addition| nodeF3["Polymerization Reactor"]
    nodeF3 -->|Heat removal| nodeF4["Cooling System"]
    nodeF3 -->|Polymer slurry| nodeF5["Separation"]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6["Solvent Recovery"]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF7["Polymer Pelletization"]
    nodeF7 --> nodeF8["Final Polyolefin Product"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the process interactions from feed olefins to final polymer pellets — a typical industrial layout for Ziegler–Natta or metallocene-based polyolefin production in petroleum refining complexes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **addition** and **condensation** polymerization with two examples each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Addition (Chain-Growth) | Condensation (Step-Growth) |
|---------|-------------------------|----------------------------|
| Mechanism | Chain-wise addition at active centre | Stepwise reaction between any two species |
| Byproduct | **No** small molecule eliminated | Small molecule (H₂O, HCl, NH₃) eliminated |
| Monomer type | Vinyl monomers with C=C | Bifunctional monomers with –OH/–COOH/–NH₂ |
| Kinetics | Initiator-dependent; rapid chain growth | Reaction of all species; high conversion needed |
| Molecular weight | Build-up at once after short induction | Gradual increase with conversion |
| Examples | Polyethylene from CH₂=CH₂; Polystyrene from styrene | Nylon-6,6 from adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine; PET from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid |

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the **role of an initiator** in free-radical polymerization. Give two examples.

**Model Answer:**

An **initiator (I)** is a thermally or photochemically labile compound that decomposes to generate free radicals which *start* the polymerization chain. Its roles are:

1. **Generate primary radicals** that attack the monomer's π-bond to form the first monomer radical.
2. **Set the rate of initiation** ($R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$), which controls both the overall polymerization rate and the molecular weight.

Examples: **Benzoyl peroxide (BPO)** — decomposes at 60–80 °C to phenyl radicals + CO₂; **Azo-bis-isobutyronitrile (AIBN)** — decomposes at 50–70 °C to isobutyronitrile radicals + N₂.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** Discuss the **mechanism of free-radical polymerization** in detail. Derive the rate expression and explain the effect of **initiator concentration** and **temperature** on the rate and degree of polymerization.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism [7 Marks]:**

The free-radical polymerization of a vinyl monomer M proceeds through **four elementary steps**:

**Step 1 – Initiation:**
$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2 R^{\bullet}$$
$$R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} M_1^{\bullet}$$

The initiator (e.g., BPO) decomposes with rate constant $k_d$. A fraction *f* of the generated radicals successfully add to a monomer.

**Step 2 – Propagation:**
$$M_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} M_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

This step is repeated hundreds to thousands of times. The radical site is *regenerated* — that is the heart of chain-growth.

**Step 3 – Termination (two modes):**
- **Coupling:** $M_n^{\bullet} + M_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{t,c}} P_{n+m}$
- **Disproportionation:** $M_n^{\bullet} + M_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{t,d}} P_n + P_m$

**Step 4 – Chain Transfer (to monomer, solvent, polymer, or transfer agent CTA):**
$$M_n^{\bullet} + XH \xrightarrow{k_{tr}} P_n + X^{\bullet}$$

**Steps 5 & 6 – Re-initiation by transferred radical:**
$$X^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_{a}} XM^{\bullet}$$

The original chain dies; a new chain is born.

**(b) Rate Expression and Discussion [7 Marks]:**

Using the steady-state approximation, equating $R_i = R_t$:

$$\boxed{R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}}$$

**Effect of initiator concentration:**
- $R_p \propto \sqrt{[I]}$ — doubling [I] increases rate by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ times.
- $X_n \propto 1/\sqrt{[I]}$ — doubling [I] *halves* the molecular weight.

**Effect of temperature:**
- $k_d$ follows Arrhenius with very high activation energy (~125 kJ/mol) for peroxide initiators.
- $k_p$ has lower Eₐ (~20–30 kJ/mol); $k_t$ has very low Eₐ (~5–10 kJ/mol) due to diffusion control.
- Overall $E_a^{Rp} = E_{a,d}/2 + E_{a,p} - E_{a,t}/2 \approx 80\text{–}90$ kJ/mol.
- Rate increases sharply with temperature; $X_n$ *decreases* with temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Free Radical Mechanism:**
> 1. **Forgetting the steady-state approximation** when deriving $R_p$ → 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Confusing the two termination modes** (coupling vs. disproportionation) — explicitly state both.
> 3. **Missing the chain-transfer step** in the mechanism → 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Omitting the initiator efficiency factor *f*** in the rate expression.
> 5. **Not stating the units** of $R_p$ (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹) when writing the final equation.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]** Explain **step-growth (condensation) polymerization** with a suitable example. Derive the **Carothers equation** and discuss the effect of **stoichiometric imbalance** and **conversion** on the degree of polymerization.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-Growth Polymerization Mechanism [7 Marks]:**

In step-growth polymerization, **any** molecule bearing complementary functional groups can react with **any other**. The chain grows by *random meetings* of all species present, not by addition to a single active site. A representative example is the formation of **nylon-6,6** from adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine:

$$\text{n HOOC–(CH}_2)_4\text{–COOH} + \text{n H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH}_2$$
$$\longrightarrow \text{H[–NH–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2)_4\text{–CO–]}_n\text{OH} + (2n-1)\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Key features:
- **Bifunctional monomers** are required.
- A **small-molecule byproduct** (H₂O in this case) is eliminated — hence the name "condensation."
- The reaction proceeds through the same mechanism for dimer, trimer, oligomer, and polymer — *no* special active centre.
- Molecular weight rises **slowly** until very high conversions are reached.

**(b) Carothers' Equation Derivation [7 Marks]:**

Let $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules, $p$ = fractional conversion of functional groups, $N$ = number of molecules remaining at conversion $p$.

Each *new bond* formed reduces the molecule count by 1. Number of bonds formed = $N_0 \cdot p$. So:

$$N = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0(1-p)$$

Average degree of polymerization:

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

**Non-stoichiometric case:** Let $r = N_A/N_B \le 1$ where $N_A$ is the limiting functional group. After derivation:

$$X_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

**Effect of conversion (r = 1):**
- $p = 0.90 \Rightarrow X_n = 10$ (oligomer).
- $p = 0.99 \Rightarrow X_n = 100$ (polymer).
- $p = 0.999 \Rightarrow X_n = 1000$ (high polymer).

**Effect of stoichiometric imbalance (at $p \to 1$):**
$$X_n^{max} = \frac{1+r}{1-r}$$

A 1% excess of one monomer ($r = 0.99$) caps $X_n$ at $\approx 199$ — no matter how complete the reaction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Step-Growth & Carothers:**
> 1. **Confusing *p* with extent of reaction** — *p* is the *fraction* of functional groups reacted, not a percentage per se.
> 2. **Forgetting the (1+r) term** in the non-stoichiometric case — the simplified $1/(1-p)$ form is valid *only* for $r = 1$.
> 3. **Missing the 99% rule discussion** — examiners reward students who recognize the operational implication.
> 4. **Omitting the small-molecule byproduct** in the chemical equation → 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **No unit check** on $X_n$ — explicitly state it is dimensionless.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Polymerization Methods & Mechanisms:**

- ☐ Polymerization = monomer → polymer via covalent bond formation.
- ☐ Two major classes: **Chain-Growth (Addition)** and **Step-Growth (Condensation)**.
- ☐ Chain-Growth has four sub-types: **Free Radical, Cationic, Anionic, Coordination (Ziegler–Natta)**.
- ☐ Free-radical mechanism: **Initiation → Propagation → Termination → Chain Transfer**.
- ☐ Initiation is the rate-limiting, energy-expensive step.
- ☐ Propagation regenerates the active centre — that is the "chain."
- ☐ Termination modes: **coupling** (combines two chains) and **disproportionation** (H-transfer).
- ☐ Rate of polymerization: $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{f k_d [I] / k_t}$.
- ☐ $R_p \propto \sqrt{[I]}$; $X_n \propto 1/\sqrt{[I]}$ — *the rate–MW trade-off*.
- ☐ Step-growth: *any* species reacts with *any* other; high MW only at very high conversion.
- ☐ Carothers' equation (balanced): $X_n = 1/(1-p)$.
- ☐ Carothers' equation (imbalanced): $X_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$.
- ☐ The **99% rule**: $X_n = 100$ requires $p = 0.99$ for $r = 1$.
- ☐ Physical methods (orthogonal axis to mechanism): **Bulk, Solution, Suspension, Emulsion**.
- ☐ Coordination polymerization gives **stereoregular** polymers (isotactic, syndiotactic).
- ☐ Petroleum refining applications: **oligomerization of FCC C₃/C₄ olefins to gasoline; PAO lubricants; polyolefins**.
- ☐ Ceiling temperature $T_c = \Delta H / \Delta S$ — above this, polymerization is thermodynamically forbidden.
- ☐ For the KTU exam: always state the *units* of $R_p$ (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹) and the *dimensionless* nature of $X_n$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production processes of LDPE, LLDPE and HDPE; basic difference among the three and their applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production processes of LDPE, LLDPE and HDPE; basic difference among the three and their applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Polyethylene (PE) – The Workhorse Polyolefin

**Polyethylene (PE)** is a thermoplastic polymer produced by the addition polymerization of the monomer **ethylene (CH₂=CH₂)**. It is the most widely produced synthetic polymer in the world, classified into several families based on **density** and **molecular architecture**, which directly govern its mechanical, thermal, and processing behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Polyethylene grades are classified primarily by **density (g/cm³)** and **degree of chain branching**. The three principal commercial grades are **LDPE**, **LLDPE**, and **HDPE**, each produced by distinctly different catalytic and pressure regimes.

### 1.2 The Three Commercial Grades at a Glance

| Grade | Full Form | Typical Density (g/cm³) | Branching Pattern | Polymerization Type |
|-------|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **LDPE** | Low-Density Polyethylene | 0.910 – 0.925 | Long & short chain branches | Free-radical (high pressure) |
| **LLDPE** | Linear Low-Density Polyethylene | 0.915 – 0.940 | Uniform short chain branches | Coordination (low pressure) with comonomer |
| **HDPE** | High-Density Polyethylene | 0.941 – 0.965 | Essentially linear | Coordination (low pressure) Ziegler–Natta / Phillips / Metallocene |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Spaghetti Bowl" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture (Spaghetti Analogy):** Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti strands.
> - **HDPE** = straight, tightly packed, long spaghetti → strands slide past each other with difficulty → **high density, high strength, high crystallinity**.
> - **LDPE** = knotted, tangled spaghetti with many loops and branches → loose packing → **low density, soft, transparent film**.
> - **LLDPE** = mostly straight strands with small, evenly spaced kinks (comonomer "speed bumps") → better than LDPE in strength but easier to process than HDPE → **a structural compromise**.

This single mental image explains the entire property spectrum and is a favourite KTU viva question.

### 1.4 Why the Production Process Dictates the Product

The **pressure**, **temperature**, **catalyst system**, and **presence/absence of comonomer** during polymerization directly control the **branching topology** of the resulting polymer chain, which in turn governs:

- **Crystallinity** (X-ray diffraction measured, typically 40–80 %)
- **Density** (ASTM D792)
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI / MFR)** (ASTM D1238, g/10 min)
- **Mechanical strength** (tensile, impact)
- **Optical clarity** (haze %)
- **Chemical resistance**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** *Same monomer (ethylene), different process → entirely different product.* This is the central theme of KTU Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LDPE – High-Pressure Free-Radical Polymerization

**Process Route (ICI / Autoclave / Tubular Reactor):**

- **Pressure:** 1000 – 3000 atm (≈ 100 – 300 MPa)
- **Temperature:** 80 – 300 °C
- **Initiator:** Oxygen (O₂) or organic peroxides (e.g., *tert*-butyl peroxybenzoate)
- **Mechanism:** Free-radical addition polymerization
- **Comonomer:** None (homopolymer of ethylene)

**Why branching occurs (the "Why" behind the "How"):**

At extremely high pressures, the propagating radical chain undergoes **intra-chain "back-biting" transfer reactions**. The radical end curls back and abstracts a hydrogen from a methylene group 4 carbons back, generating a **short chain branch (ethyl/butyl)**. Long chain branches arise from chain transfer to a dead polymer molecule.

**Operating Cycle (ICI Autoclave Process):**

1. Ethylene compressed to ≈ 2000 atm in a multi-stage diaphragm compressor.
2. Mixed with initiator at reactor inlet.
3. Polymerization in a stirred autoclave (≈ 100 – 2000 L).
4. Conversion per pass ≈ 15 – 30 %.
5. Unreacted ethylene recycled.
6. Polymer melt extruded through a die, pelletized.

**Reactor Types (commonly asked):**

- **Autoclave (stirred tank)** – BASF, ICI process
- **Tubular reactor** – Lupolen process, length up to 1 km, higher conversion

### 2.2 LLDPE – Low-Pressure Solution / Gas-Phase Process

**Process Route (Solution, Slurry, or Gas-Phase):**

- **Pressure:** 1 – 100 atm
- **Temperature:** 80 – 250 °C (depending on process variant)
- **Catalyst:** Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₄/MgCl₂) or Metallocene
- **Comonomer:** 1-butene, 1-hexene, or 1-octene (3 – 10 mol %)
- **Mechanism:** Coordination–insertion polymerization

**The Comonomer Trick:**

The 1-olefin comonomer (e.g., 1-butene: CH₂=CH–CH₂–CH₃) inserts randomly into the otherwise linear chain, creating **short, uniform alkyl side groups**. This:

- Disrupts crystalline packing → lowers density
- Increases tear and impact strength dramatically vs. LDPE
- Maintains linear backbone → higher tensile strength than LDPE

**Commercial LLDPE Process Variants:**

1. **Solution process** (DuPont, Dowlex) – polymer dissolves in cyclohexane
2. **Gas-phase fluidized bed** (Union Carbide UNIPOL) – dominant modern process
3. **Slurry process** (Phillips Particle Form)

### 2.3 HDPE – Low-Pressure Coordination Polymerization

**Two Dominant Catalytic Routes:**

#### A) Ziegler–Natta Route

- **Catalyst:** TiCl₄ + Al(C₂H₅)₃ (triethyl aluminium) on MgCl₂ support
- **Pressure:** 1 – 50 atm
- **Temperature:** 50 – 100 °C
- **Process:** Slurry (in hexane) or Gas-phase
- **Density:** 0.940 – 0.965 g/cm³

#### B) Phillips Route (Chromium on Silica)

- **Catalyst:** CrO₃ / SiO₂ (or Cr/silica–titania)
- **Pressure:** 30 – 40 atm
- **Temperature:** 80 – 110 °C
- **Process:** Particle-form slurry or loop reactor
- **Density:** 0.945 – 0.965 g/cm³ (highest density)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Favourite:** *Why does Phillips catalyst give the highest density HDPE?* — Because the Cr/silica catalyst minimizes chain transfer, producing nearly perfectly linear chains with extremely high crystallinity (≈ 80–90 %).

#### C) Metallocene Catalysts (mHDPE)

- Single-site catalysts (e.g., Cp₂ZrCl₂ / MAO)
- Enable narrow molecular weight distribution (MWD ≈ 2.0) and tailored comonomer incorporation
- Used to produce specialty HDPE and LLDPE grades

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Remarks |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|---------|
| Degree of crystallinity (Xc) | $X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | % | $\rho_c$ = crystalline phase density, $\rho_a$ = amorphous phase density, $\rho_s$ = sample density |
| Density range rule | $\rho_{\text{HDPE}} > \rho_{\text{LLDPE}} > \rho_{\text{LDPE}}$ | g/cm³ | Direct consequence of branching |
| Melt Flow Index (MFI) | $\text{MFI} = \dfrac{\text{mass extruded in 10 min}}{\text{gram}}$ | g/10 min | Inversely related to Mw (viscosity) |
| Mark–Houwink equation | $[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{\,a}$ | dL/g | $K$, $a$ depend on solvent & polymer; used to find viscosity-average MW |
| Polymerization rate (radical) | $R_p = k_p \, [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | $k_p$ = propagation, $k_t$ = termination, $k_d$ = initiator decomposition, $f$ = efficiency |
| Number-average DP | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{R_p}{R_t}$ | – | Classic radical polymerization result |
| Comonomer incorporation | $\text{SCB} \propto \text{mol \% comonomer in feed}$ | – | Higher comonomer → lower density |
| Tensile strength (empirical trend) | $\sigma_{ts,\text{HDPE}} > \sigma_{ts,\text{LLDPE}} > \sigma_{ts,\text{LDPE}}$ | MPa | For a comparable MFI |
| Glass transition temp (Tg) | $T_g \approx -110 \text{ °C (all PE grades)}$ | °C | Branching has minor effect on Tg |
| Melting point (Tm) | $T_{m,\text{HDPE}} \approx 130 \text{–} 135 \text{ °C}$ ; $T_{m,\text{LDPE}} \approx 105 \text{–} 115 \text{ °C}$ | °C | Tm increases with crystallinity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always cite the **units** (especially g/cm³ for density and g/10 min for MFI) when answering numerical or comparison questions — failure to do so costs marks.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance

- The choice of LDPE / LLDPE / HDPE is the **single largest tonnage decision** in the global plastics industry (≈ 100 million tonnes/year).
- The **UNIPOL gas-phase process** revolutionized PE manufacturing by eliminating the need for solvent, drastically reducing CAPEX and energy footprint.
- Modern **bimodal HDPE** (made with two reactors in series) is the gold standard for **PE-100 pressure pipe** grades used in water and gas distribution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Density–Crystallinity Relationship (X-ray / Two-Phase Model)

**Starting assumption:** A semi-crystalline polymer is a two-phase mixture of a perfectly crystalline phase of density $\rho_c$ and a fully amorphous phase of density $\rho_a$.

Let $V_c$ = volume of crystalline phase, $V_a$ = volume of amorphous phase, $V = V_c + V_a$ (total volume). Let $m_c$ and $m_a$ be the corresponding masses.

**Step 1 — Express mass in terms of density and volume:**

$$
m_c = \rho_c \cdot V_c \qquad \text{and} \qquad m_a = \rho_a \cdot V_a
$$

**Step 2 — Volume fraction crystallinity ($v_c$):**

$$
v_c = \dfrac{V_c}{V_c + V_a} = \dfrac{V_c}{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $\rho_c$ and $\rho_a$ in terms of $V_c$ and $V_a$:**

From $V = V_c + V_a$ and $m = m_c + m_a$, the sample density is:

$$
\rho_s = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{\rho_c V_c + \rho_a V_a}{V_c + V_a}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_c = v_c V$ and $V_a = (1 - v_c)V$:**

$$
\rho_s = \rho_c \, v_c + \rho_a \, (1 - v_c)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v_c$:**

$$
v_c = \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mass (weight) fraction crystallinity $X_c$:**

Since $X_c = \dfrac{m_c}{m} = \dfrac{\rho_c v_c V}{\rho_s V} = \dfrac{\rho_c \, v_c}{\rho_s}$,

$$
X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c \,(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s \,(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the formula presented in the KTU cheat sheet above. For PE, $\rho_a \approx 0.855 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_c \approx 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**Numerical Example (typical LDPE):** $\rho_s = 0.918$ g/cm³

$$
v_c = \dfrac{0.918 - 0.855}{1.00 - 0.855} = \dfrac{0.063}{0.145} = 0.434
$$

$$
X_c = \dfrac{1.00 \times 0.434}{0.918} \times 100 = 47.3 \,\%
$$

LDPE crystallinity ≈ 40–50 %. HDPE (ρ ≈ 0.954) gives:

$$
v_c = \dfrac{0.954 - 0.855}{0.145} = 0.683
$$

$$
X_c = \dfrac{1.00 \times 0.683}{0.954} \times 100 = 71.6 \,\%
$$

HDPE crystallinity ≈ 70–80 %. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Free-Radical Polymerization Kinetics (LDPE context)

For initiation by thermal decomposition of initiator I → 2R• with rate constant $k_d$ and efficiency $f$:

$$
R_i = 2 f k_d [I]
$$

Propagation (rate-determining, rate constant $k_p$):

$$
R_p = k_p [M] [M \cdot]
$$

Termination (combination, rate constant $k_t$):

$$
R_t = k_t [M \cdot]^2
$$

**Steady-state on radicals:** $R_i = R_t$ →

$$
[M \cdot] = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_i}{k_t}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
$$

Substitute into $R_p$:

$$
\boxed{R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}}
$$

This is the **canonical rate expression** for free-radical polymerization. The factor of 2 is sometimes dropped when a "rate of radical generation" convention is used, but the KTU standard keeps it.

**Number-average degree of polymerization (under termination by combination):**

$$
\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{\tfrac{1}{2} R_i} = \dfrac{2 R_p}{R_i}
$$

**Key observation:** $R_p \propto [I]^{0.5}$ and $\bar{X}_n \propto [I]^{-0.5}$. Thus *increasing initiator decreases molecular weight* — a key process lever for MFI control in LDPE reactors.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Master Reference Table)

| Property / Process Parameter | LDPE | LLDPE | HDPE |
|------------------------------|------|-------|------|
| **Polymerization mechanism** | Free-radical | Coordination (Z–N / Metallocene) | Coordination (Z–N / Phillips) |
| **Pressure (atm)** | 1000 – 3000 | 1 – 100 | 1 – 50 |
| **Temperature (°C)** | 80 – 300 | 80 – 250 | 50 – 110 |
| **Catalyst** | Peroxide / O₂ | TiCl₄/MgCl₂ or metallocene + co-catalyst | TiCl₄/AlR₃ (Z–N) or CrO₃/SiO₂ (Phillips) |
| **Comonomer** | None | 1-butene, 1-hexene, 1-octene | Sometimes 1-butene (for LMDPE) |
| **Branching** | Long + short, irregular | Short, uniform | Essentially none (linear) |
| **Crystallinity (%)** | 40 – 50 | 50 – 60 | 70 – 80 |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 0.910 – 0.925 | 0.915 – 0.940 | 0.941 – 0.965 |
| **Tm (°C)** | 105 – 115 | 120 – 125 | 130 – 135 |
| **Tensile strength (MPa)** | 7 – 15 | 12 – 25 | 25 – 40 |
| **Elongation at break (%)** | 400 – 700 | 600 – 800 | 100 – 600 |
| **Impact strength** | Moderate | High | High (especially bimodal) |
| **Optical clarity** | High (transparent film) | Moderate (slight haze) | Low (opaque) |
| **MFI range (typical)** | 0.2 – 30 | 0.5 – 50 | 0.1 – 50 |
| **Reactor type** | Autoclave / Tubular | Gas-phase fluidized bed | Loop reactor / Gas-phase / Slurry |
| **Major producers (process)** | LyondellBasell (Lupolen T), ExxonMobil (tubular) | Dow (solution), ExxonMobil (gas-phase) | Borealis (Borstar), Chevron Phillips (Phillips) |

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Comonomer Effect on Density (LLDPE)

For LLDPE, the density drop per mol% of 1-butene incorporated follows an empirical relation:

$$
\rho = \rho_0 - k \cdot x_c
$$

where $\rho_0$ = homopolymer (HDPE) density, $x_c$ = mol fraction of comonomer, $k$ = empirical constant.

For 1-butene: $k \approx 0.024 \text{ g/cm}^3$ per mol%.

For 1-hexene: $k \approx 0.027 \text{ g/cm}^3$ per mol% (more effective density reducer per mole — because longer branch disrupts crystallinity more).

For 1-octene: $k \approx 0.030 \text{ g/cm}^3$ per mol%.

**Example:** Starting from $\rho_0 = 0.960$ (HDPE), adding 4 mol% 1-hexene:

$$
\rho = 0.960 - 0.027 \times 4 = 0.960 - 0.108 = 0.852 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

(Note: this linear approximation breaks down for very high comonomer levels; in practice LLDPE density is held in 0.915 – 0.940 with comonomer ≈ 3 – 8 mol%.)

### 3.5 Application Mapping (Why each grade exists)

**LDPE Applications:**

- Transparent **food packaging film** (bread bags, frozen food)
- **Squeeze bottles**, dispensing tubes
- **Wire and cable insulation** (flexible)
- **Liners** for tanks and ponds
- **Foamed PE** (LD-33 type) for cushioning

**LLDPE Applications:**

- **Stretch film** (pallet wrap) – replaces LDPE due to higher tear/puncture resistance
- **Heavy-duty shipping sacks**
- **Agricultural film** (greenhouse covers, mulch film)
- **Rotomoulded tanks** (with HDPE blend)
- **Injection-moulded lids, container closures**

**HDPE Applications:**

- **Blow-moulded bottles** (milk, detergent, shampoo) – the iconic opaque white jug
- **PE-100 pressure pipes** for water/gas distribution
- **Geomembranes** (landfill liners)
- **Crates, pallets, automotive fuel tanks**
- **Toys, household goods**
- **Bimodal HDPE** → high-performance pressure pipe (MRS 10 MPa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Memory Hook:** "LDPE = Limp & Clear", "LLDPE = Linear & Strong film", "HDPE = Hard & Dense jugs."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Molecular Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LDPE_ARCH ["LDPE - Long & Short Chain Branches"]
        direction LR
        nodeA["backbone segment"]
        nodeB["short ethyl branch"]
        nodeC["long chain branch"]
        nodeA --> nodeB
        nodeA --> nodeC
    end

    subgraph LLDPE_ARCH ["LLDPE - Uniform Short Branches"]
        direction LR
        nodeD["linear backbone"]
        nodeE["uniform butyl branch"]
        nodeF["uniform butyl branch"]
        nodeD --> nodeE
        nodeD --> nodeF
    end

    subgraph HDPE_ARCH ["HDPE - Linear, Minimal Branching"]
        direction LR
        nodeG["linear chain 1"]
        nodeH["linear chain 2"]
        nodeG -.-> nodeH
    end
```

### 4.2 LDPE High-Pressure Tubular Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    feedA[Ethylene Feed] --> compA[Primary Compressor]
    compA --> compB[Secondary Hyper Compressor]
    compB --> mixA[Initiator Injection Point]
    mixA --> reactA[Tubular Reactor 1 to 1.5 km]
    reactA --> coolA[Cooling Zone]
    coolA --> reactB[Reactor Section 2]
    reactB --> sepA[High Pressure Separator]
    sepA --> extrdA[Extruder and Pelletizer]
    extrdA --> prodA[LDPE Pellets]
    sepA --> recycA[Recycle Ethylene]
    recycA --> compA
```

### 4.3 LLDPE UNIPOL Gas-Phase Fluidized-Bed Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    feedB[Ethylene + Comonomer 1-hexene] --> compC[Compressor]
    catB[Catalyst Ziegler Natta or Metallocene] --> feedB
    compC --> reactC[Fluidized Bed Reactor 80 to 100 C]
    nitB[N2 for Fluidization] --> reactC
    reactC --> dischB[Product Discharge Tank]
    dischB --> purgeB[Purge Bin]
    purgeB --> extrdB[Extruder Pelletizer]
    extrdB --> prodB[LLDPE Granules]
    reactC --> recycB[Recycle Gas Compressor]
    recycB --> reactC
```

### 4.4 HDPE Slurry Loop Reactor (Phillips Process)

```mermaid
graph TD
    feedC[Ethylene Feed] --> compD[Compressor]
    feedD[Hexane Diluent] --> reactD[Loop Reactor]
    feedE[Hydrogen MW Regulator] --> reactD
    catC[CrO3 on SiO2 Catalyst] --> reactD
    compD --> reactD
    reactD --> flashD[Flash Tank]
    flashD --> dryD[Dryer]
    dryD --> extrdC[Extruder]
    extrdC --> prodC[HDPE Powder to Pellets]
    flashD --> recycC[Hexane Recovery]
    recycC --> reactD
```

### 4.5 Decision Logic: Choosing the Right PE Grade

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA{Need high transparency and flexibility?}
    startA -- Yes --> pickLDPE[Choose LDPE]
    startA -- No --> startB{Need tough thin film?}
    startB -- Yes --> pickLLDPE[Choose LLDPE]
    startB -- No --> startC{Need maximum strength and rigidity?}
    startC -- Yes --> pickHDPE[Choose HDPE]
    startC -- No --> pickMDPE[Choose MDPE Medium Density or Bimodal HDPE]
```

### 4.6 Property Pyramid of Polyethylenes

```mermaid
graph TD
    apexA[Highest Density and Strength]
    apexA --> tier1[HDPE Tier]
    tier1 --> tier2[LLDPE Tier]
    tier2 --> tier3[LDPE Tier]
    tier3 --> apexB[Highest Transparency and Flexibility]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE). Mention the polymerization process and any two applications.**

**Model Answer:**
LDPE is a thermoplastic polymer of ethylene with density ranging from 0.910 to 0.925 g/cm³, characterized by a high degree of long and short chain branching. It is produced by **free-radical addition polymerization of ethylene at high pressure (1000 – 3000 atm) and temperature 80 – 300 °C**, using oxygen or peroxide initiators in an autoclave or tubular reactor. The branching arises from intra-chain "back-biting" transfer reactions.

*Applications:* (1) Transparent food packaging film (bread bags, frozen food pouches); (2) Squeeze bottles and flexible wire-cable insulation.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between LDPE and HDPE on the basis of (i) polymerization method, (ii) branching, and (iii) density.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | LDPE | HDPE |
|-------|-----------|------|------|
| (i) | Polymerization method | Free-radical, high pressure (1000 – 3000 atm) | Coordination polymerization, low pressure (1 – 50 atm) using Ziegler–Natta or Phillips catalyst |
| (ii) | Branching | Long and short chain branches (irregular) | Essentially linear, negligible branching |
| (iii) | Density | 0.910 – 0.925 g/cm³ | 0.941 – 0.965 g/cm³ |

### 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the production process of Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE) by the ICI autoclave process with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the mechanism of branching.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the solution and gas-phase processes for the manufacture of LLDPE. What is the role of the comonomer, and how does it affect the final properties?

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**ICI Autoclave Process for LDPE:**

1. **Feed preparation:** Fresh ethylene from the cracking unit is combined with recycled ethylene. The combined stream is compressed in a primary compressor to ≈ 250 – 300 atm, then in a secondary hyper-compressor to ≈ 1000 – 2000 atm.
2. **Initiator injection:** Organic peroxide initiator (or oxygen) is injected at the reactor inlet.
3. **Polymerization:** The feed enters a stirred autoclave reactor (typically 100 – 2000 L working volume) maintained at 80 – 300 °C. Polymerization is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ –95 kJ/mol of C₂H₄). Heat is removed via an external jacket and internal cooling coils.
4. **Conversion and separation:** Single-pass conversion is 15 – 30 %. The reactor effluent (polymer melt + unreacted ethylene) passes to a high-pressure separator where unreacted monomer is flashed off and recycled.
5. **Pelletization:** The polymer melt is extruded through a die, cooled, and cut into pellets.
6. **Quality control:** MFI, density, and tensile properties are continuously monitored.

**Mechanism of branching in LDPE:**

- **Short chain branches (SCB):** Arise from "back-biting". The propagating radical end abstracts a hydrogen from the 4th carbon back, creating an internal radical that propagates a 2- or 4-carbon side branch. This is *intramolecular chain transfer*.
- **Long chain branches (LCB):** Arise from chain transfer to a dead polymer molecule, abstracting a backbone H and re-initiating a new chain that grows as a long pendant branch.

These branches disrupt crystalline packing, yielding low density and high clarity.

##### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**LLDPE Production Processes:**

**(1) Solution Process (e.g., DuPont Sclairtech, Dow Dowlex):**
- Ethylene and 1-octene (or 1-hexene) are fed with a hydrocarbon solvent (cyclohexane) into a series of stirred reactors at 200 – 300 °C and 50 – 100 atm.
- Catalyst: Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₄/MgCl₂) or metallocene with Al-alkyl co-catalyst.
- The polymer remains dissolved in the solvent. Hot solvent flashing concentrates the polymer solution, which is then pelletized.

**(2) Gas-Phase Process (e.g., Union Carbide UNIPOL):**
- Ethylene and comonomer (1-butene or 1-hexene) are fed to a fluidized-bed reactor operating at 80 – 100 °C and 20 – 30 atm.
- Catalyst particles are injected continuously; the bed is fluidized by recycle gas (ethylene + N₂).
- Polymer grows directly on the catalyst particles. Product is discharged intermittently into a purge bin to remove hydrocarbons, then pelletized.

**Role of the comonomer:**

The α-olefin comonomer (1-butene, 1-hexene, 1-octene) inserts into the growing chain at random intervals, creating short, uniform alkyl side branches. These short branches:

- Disrupt crystal formation → lower density and Tm
- Improve tear strength, impact resistance, and puncture resistance compared to LDPE
- Provide a **tunable density** by varying comonomer feed ratio
- Maintain a **linear backbone** → higher tensile strength and elongation than LDPE

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the Ziegler–Natta and Phillips catalytic processes for HDPE production. Compare the two on the basis of catalyst, density, and crystallinity.

**(b) (7 marks)** Tabulate the differences between LDPE, LLDPE, and HDPE in terms of structure, density, polymerization method, and applications. State the empirical relation between density and crystallinity.

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Ziegler–Natta Process for HDPE:**

- **Catalyst:** TiCl₄ supported on MgCl₂, co-catalyst Al(C₂H₅)₃ (triethyl aluminium).
- **Process:** Conducted as a slurry in a hydrocarbon diluent (hexane) in a loop reactor (e.g., Borstar) at 1 – 50 atm and 50 – 100 °C, or as a gas-phase process in a fluidized bed.
- **Polymer growth:** Coordinative insertion of ethylene at the Ti–C bond. Each Ti site grows one chain.
- **Density:** 0.940 – 0.965 g/cm³ (tunable by adding small amounts of 1-butene as comonomer → LMDPE / LLDPE-HDPE hybrids).
- **Crystallinity:** 70 – 80 %.

**Phillips Process for HDPE:**

- **Catalyst:** Chromium oxide (CrO₃) on a silica or silica–titania support, reduced and activated in situ.
- **Process:** Particle-form slurry in a loop reactor at 30 – 40 atm and 80 – 110 °C. No co-catalyst required; Cr is the active site.
- **Polymer growth:** Coordinative insertion; the Cr–C bond initiates each chain.
- **Density:** 0.945 – 0.965 g/cm³ (the highest among all PE grades due to near-perfect linearity).
- **Crystallinity:** 75 – 85 %.

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Ziegler–Natta | Phillips |
|-----------|---------------|----------|
| Catalyst | TiCl₄ / MgCl₂ + AlR₃ | CrO₃ / SiO₂ |
| Co-catalyst | Required (Al-alkyl) | Not required |
| Active metal | Ti | Cr |
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.940 – 0.965 | 0.945 – 0.965 |
| Crystallinity (%) | 70 – 80 | 75 – 85 |
| Reactor | Loop slurry / Gas-phase | Loop slurry (particle form) |
| Polymer morphology | Powder / granules | Spherical particles |

##### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Comparison Table — LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE:**

| Property | LDPE | LLDPE | HDPE |
|----------|------|-------|------|
| **Structure** | Branched (long + short) | Linear with uniform short branches | Linear, virtually unbranched |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 0.910 – 0.925 | 0.915 – 0.940 | 0.941 – 0.965 |
| **Polymerization method** | Free-radical, high pressure (1000 – 3000 atm) | Coordination, low pressure (1 – 100 atm) with comonomer | Coordination, low pressure (1 – 50 atm), Z–N or Phillips |
| **Crystallinity (%)** | 40 – 50 | 50 – 60 | 70 – 80 |
| **Tm (°C)** | 105 – 115 | 120 – 125 | 130 – 135 |
| **Tensile strength (MPa)** | 7 – 15 | 12 – 25 | 25 – 40 |
| **Major applications** | Packaging film, squeeze bottles | Stretch film, heavy-duty sacks, agri film | Bottles, pipes (PE-100), crates |

**Empirical relation between density and crystallinity:**

$$
X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100\%
$$

where $\rho_c \approx 1.00$ g/cm³ and $\rho_a \approx 0.855$ g/cm³ for polyethylene.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units! Units! Units!** — Students lose 1–2 marks by writing "density 0.92" without g/cm³.
> 2. **Don't confuse the catalyst roles:** Ziegler–Natta needs a *co-catalyst* (AlR₃); Phillips does not. Mixing these is a guaranteed mark-loser.
> 3. **"Comonomer in LDPE"** is a common wrong answer — LDPE is a homopolymer; branching comes from back-biting, not from a comonomer. Comonomer is the LLDPE trick.
> 4. **"LDPE is made by Ziegler–Natta"** is wrong. LDPE = free-radical; HDPE/LLDPE = coordination (Z–N / Phillips / metallocene).
> 5. **Always quote both** pressure *and* temperature ranges in process descriptions.
> 6. **Flow diagrams are compulsory** for full marks in 14-mark questions — practice drawing them with labelled streams (feed, recycle, product, catalyst).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Take-aways for KTU Module 4:**

- ✅ **Polyethylene family is classified by density and branching**, both of which are *direct consequences* of the polymerization process.
- ✅ **LDPE** → free-radical, **high pressure** (1000 – 3000 atm), branched, low density (0.910 – 0.925 g/cm³), transparent films.
- ✅ **LLDPE** → coordination, **low pressure**, with **1-olefin comonomer** (1-butene / 1-hexene / 1-octene) → uniform short branches → tough film grade.
- ✅ **HDPE** → coordination, **low pressure**, **Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₄/AlR₃)** or **Phillips (Cr/SiO₂)** catalyst → linear → high density (0.941 – 0.965 g/cm³) → bottles and pipes.
- ✅ Density order: **HDPE > LLDPE > LDPE**; Crystallinity order: same as density; Tensile strength order: same as density; Clarity order: **LDPE > LLDPE > HDPE** (inverse).
- ✅ **Free-radical kinetics:** $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{2 f k_d [I] / k_t}$.
- ✅ **Crystallinity formula:** $X_c = \rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a) / [\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)] \times 100\%$ with $\rho_c \approx 1.00$ and $\rho_a \approx 0.855$ g/cm³ for PE.
- ✅ **Phillips catalyst → highest density HDPE** because of near-perfect linearity.
- ✅ **Comonomer role in LLDPE:** disrupts crystallinity → tunable density, improved tear/impact strength.
- ✅ **Major industrial processes:** ICI autoclave / Lupolen tubular (LDPE); UNIPOL gas-phase / Dowlex solution (LLDPE); Borstar loop / Phillips particle form (HDPE).
- ✅ **Bimodal HDPE** (two reactors in series) → PE-100 pipe grade.
- ✅ **PE = largest tonnage polymer globally**; PE family represents > 35 % of all plastics produced.
- ✅ **Tm order:** HDPE (130–135 °C) > LLDPE (120–125 °C) > LDPE (105–115 °C); Tg ≈ –110 °C for all.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of PVC and polystyrene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production of PVC and polystyrene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** is a thermoplastic polymer synthesized by the free-radical addition polymerization of the vinyl chloride monomer (VCM, CH₂=CHCl). Its repeating unit is **–[CH₂–CHCl]ₙ–**, where *n* typically ranges from **500 to 1,500**, giving commercial grades a number-average molecular weight ($\overline{M}_n$) between **40,000 – 1,50,000 g/mol**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a long freight train where every alternate wagon is "decorated" with a chlorine atom hanging on its side. That is exactly what PVC looks like at the molecular level — a carbon backbone (the train) with chlorine "pendant groups" (the decorations) attached to every second carbon. The chlorine atoms make PVC naturally **flame retardant** and chemically resistant, which is why you see it in plumbing pipes, window frames, electrical cable insulation, and credit cards.

## 1.2 Polystyrene (PS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Polystyrene (PS)** is a vinyl aromatic thermoplastic polymer produced by the free-radical addition polymerization of the **styrene monomer (C₆H₅–CH=CH₂)**. Its constitutional repeat unit is **–[CH₂–CH(C₆H₅)]ₙ–**, with a glass transition temperature ($T_g$) of approximately **100 °C** and a density of about **1.04 – 1.06 g/cm³**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of polystyrene as a "backbone wearing a bulky backpack." The polymer chain is a simple –CH₂–CH– chain, but on every second carbon hangs a heavy, rigid **phenyl (benzene) ring** "backpack." Because the benzene rings are bulky and stiff, the chains cannot slide past each other easily — this makes polystyrene **rigid, transparent, and brittle**. When the polymer is "fluffed up" with a blowing agent (pentane), the same chemistry gives you the **styrofoam** that protects your new TV in its box.

## 1.3 Classification of These Polymers in the Petrochemical Value Chain

| Polymer | Monomer | Monomer Source | Global Rank (Demand) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PVC** | Vinyl Chloride (VCM) | Ethylene + Cl₂ / HCl + O₂ | **3rd largest** commodity plastic |
| **PS** | Styrene | Ethylbenzene (from Benzene + Ethylene) | **4th largest** commodity plastic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Both PVC and Polystyrene are textbook examples of **"petrochemical-derived addition polymers"** — meaning they are built entirely from the **C₂ (ethylene) and C₆ (benzene) building blocks** obtained from the cracking and reforming operations studied in the earlier modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Production of PVC – The Three-Stage Industrial Route

Modern industrial production of PVC follows a tightly integrated **three-stage process** known as the **"Balanced Ethylene-Chlorine Route"**:

### Stage 1: Production of 1,2-Dichloroethane (EDC) — The "Dimer"
EDC is the direct precursor to VCM. It is produced by **two complementary reactions** that together keep the process atom-efficient and HCl-balanced.

**(a) Direct Chlorination (Exothermic):**

$$CH_2{=}CH_2 + Cl_2 \longrightarrow ClCH_2{-}CH_2Cl \qquad \Delta H \approx -180 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- Carried out in liquid EDC at **40 – 90 °C**, 1 – 4 bar.
- Catalyst: **FeCl₃** (Lewis acid) or **SbCl₅**.
- Conversion per pass ≈ 99 %; selectivity ≈ 99 %.

**(b) Oxychlorination (Exothermic):**

$$CH_2{=}CH_2 + 2\,HCl + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \longrightarrow ClCH_2{-}CH_2Cl + H_2O \qquad \Delta H \approx -238 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- Carried out in a **fluidized-bed catalytic reactor** at **220 – 300 °C**.
- Catalyst: **CuCl₂ on Al₂O₃** support (the "Deacon" chemistry).
- Purpose: **Consumes the HCl by-product** of the VCM cracking step (Stage 2) — closing the chlorine loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Balanced Process" Logic:** The HCl formed when EDC cracks to VCM (Stage 2) is *exactly* the HCl consumed in the oxychlorination step (Stage 1b). Thus, only **make-up Cl₂** must be added to the plant. This integration — perfected by companies like **INEOS, Vinnolit, and Occidental** — is the global industry standard.

### Stage 2: Cracking of EDC to Vinyl Chloride Monomer (VCM) — The "Monomer"

$$ClCH_2{-}CH_2Cl \xrightarrow{\Delta} CH_2{=}CHCl + HCl \qquad \Delta H \approx +71 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Endothermic, gas-phase, free-radical chain reaction** (Rice–Herzfeld mechanism).
- Furnace temperature: **480 – 540 °C**, pressure **6 – 30 bar** (high pressure suppresses coke and favors selectivity).
- Residence time: **~10 – 30 seconds** (short — to avoid secondary cracking of VCM to acetylene).
- Conversion per pass: **50 – 60 %**; overall VCM selectivity **> 99 %**.

The hot gas is **rapidly quenched** with cold EDC to stop the reaction, then VCM is separated by **distillation**.

### Stage 3: Polymerization of VCM → PVC — The "Polymer"

Four industrial processes exist; their share is shown below:

| Polymerization Process | Global Share | Product Form | Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_w$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Suspension** | ~80 % | White powder (porous grains) | 60,000 – 1,50,000 |
| **Emulsion** | ~12 % | Latex / fine powder | 40,000 – 80,000 |
| **Bulk (mass)** | ~8 % | Porous powder | 50,000 – 1,00,000 |
| **Solution** | <1 % | Lacquer / solution | 30,000 – 70,000 |

**Suspension polymerization (dominant route) — key features:**
- VCM droplets dispersed in water (VCM : water ≈ 1 : 1.2).
- **Suspending agent:** Poly(vinyl alcohol) or methyl cellulose (0.05 – 0.1 %).
- **Initiator:** Organic peroxides such as **AIBN**, **Lauroyl peroxide**, or **Di(2-ethylhexyl)peroxydicarbonate** — decomposes at **45 – 70 °C** to give free radicals.
- Temperature controlled very tightly (± 0.5 °C) because it determines $\overline{M}_w$:

$$ \overline{M}_n = \frac{[M]_0 \cdot M_0}{f \cdot k_d \cdot [I]^{1/2}} \cdot \frac{1}{(k_p/k_t)^{1/2}} $$

- Reaction is **exothermic** (~ –96 kJ/mol VCM); heat removal is the limiting design factor.
- Pressure ≈ 5 – 12 bar.
- Polymer precipitates as fine particles; unreacted VCM is stripped and recycled.

## 2.2 Production of Polystyrene – The Three-Stage Industrial Route

### Stage 1: Alkylation of Benzene with Ethylene → Ethylbenzene (EB)

$$C_6H_6 + CH_2{=}CH_2 \xrightarrow{\text{acid cat.}} C_6H_5{-}CH_2CH_3 \qquad \Delta H \approx -113 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Liquid-phase Friedel–Crafts alkylation.**
- Catalyst: **AlCl₃ + HCl (gas)** or modern **zeolite Beta (Mobil/Badger process)** at **220 – 260 °C**, 30 – 40 bar.
- Side reaction: formation of **diethylbenzene (DEB)** — recycled back via transalkylation.
- Conversion of ethylene: ~100 %; EB selectivity: **> 98 %**.

### Stage 2: Dehydrogenation of Ethylbenzene → Styrene

$$C_6H_5{-}CH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} C_6H_5{-}CH{=}CH_2 + H_2 \qquad \Delta H \approx +124.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Endothermic, catalytic, gas-phase, fixed-bed adiabatic or isothermal reactor.**
- Catalyst: **Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) promoted with K₂O/Cr₂O₃/CeO₂** ("Shell 105" or "Styromax" type).
- Operating conditions: **600 – 650 °C**, near **atmospheric pressure** (vacuum or N₂/steam dilution used to shift equilibrium).
- Steam-to-EB molar ratio: **6 – 12 : 1** — provides heat, lowers partial pressures, and cokes the catalyst less.
- **Equilibrium-limited:** single-pass conversion ≈ **60 – 70 %**; selectivity to styrene **> 95 %**.
- Main by-products: benzene, toluene, **α-methylstyrene**, and coke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Steam?** Steam (i) supplies the heat of reaction endothermically in the adiabatic case, (ii) lowers the partial pressures of EB and styrene, shifting the equilibrium *toward* styrene (Le Chatelier), and (iii) reacts with deposited coke ($C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$) — a continuous *in-situ* catalyst regeneration by **gasification**.

### Stage 3: Polymerization of Styrene → Polystyrene

Styrene is polymerized by **free-radical addition** (no catalyst — thermal auto-initiation works above 100 °C, or chemical initiators like benzoyl peroxide are used).

$$n\,C_6H_5{-}CH{=}CH_2 \longrightarrow {-}[CH_2{-}CH(C_6H_5){-}]_n{-}$$

**Industrial Processes:**

| Process | Mechanism | Product | T (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bulk (mass)** | Continuous stirred-tank then tower | **GPPS, HIPS, EPS** (crystal PS) | 80 – 200 |
| **Suspension** | Bead form | EPS, IPS | 80 – 130 |
| **Emulsion** | Latex → coagulate | High-$\overline{M}_w$ PS | 50 – 90 |

**Bulk polymerization** dominates (~80 %) because no solvent/separation cost → high purity. Two stages:
1. **Pre-polymerization reactor (CSTR)** at 80 – 110 °C, conversion 30 – 50 %.
2. **Finishing tower** at 150 – 200 °C, conversion raised to **> 99 %**.

**Major product variants of polystyrene:**

- **GPPS (General Purpose PS):** Clear, brittle, $\overline{M}_w \approx 2 - 4 \times 10^5$.
- **HIPS (High Impact PS):** GPPS grafted with **5 – 10 % polybutadiene rubber** → opaque, tough.
- **EPS (Expandable PS):** Impregnated with **5 – 8 % pentane** as blowing agent → steam-expanded to foam.
- **SPS (Syndiotactic PS):** Metallocene-catalyzed → crystalline, $T_m = 270\ ^\circ C$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit of PVC | $-[CH_2{-}CHCl]_n{-}$ | — | Head-to-tail addition |
| Repeat unit of PS | $-[CH_2{-}CH(C_6H_5)]_n{-}$ | — | Atactic in free-radical route |
| Number-average DP | $\overline{DP}_n = \overline{M}_n / M_0$ | PVC: 600 – 1500, PS: 800 – 2500 | $M_0$ = monomer mol. wt. |
| Polydispersity index | $PDI = \overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n$ | Free-radical: 1.5 – 2.0 | $PDI = 1 + p$ for Flory |
| Conversion (mass basis) | $X = (m_0 - m)/m_0$ | Dimensionless | $m_0$ = initial monomer mass |
| Carothers equation (step) | $\overline{DP}_n = 1/(1 - p)$ | — | Not for chain polymers |
| Suspension polymerization rate | $R_p = k_p [M] \left(\dfrac{f\,k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Free-radical square-root law |
| VCM polymerization enthalpy | $\Delta H_p \approx -96$ kJ/mol | — | Highly exothermic |
| Styrene polymerization enthalpy | $\Delta H_p \approx -70$ kJ/mol | — | — |
| Glass transition, $T_g$ (PS) | Atactic PS, $\overline{M}_w \to \infty$ | $\approx 100\ ^\circ C$ | Fox–Flory relation |
| Glass transition, $T_g$ (PVC) | Commercial grade | $\approx 80 - 85\ ^\circ C$ | — |
| $T_g$ dependence on $\overline{M}_n$ | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K / \overline{M}_n$ | K constant | Fox–Flory |
| Heat of EDC cracking | $\Delta H \approx +71$ kJ/mol | — | Endothermic |
| Heat of EB dehydrogenation | $\Delta H \approx +124.8$ kJ/mol | — | Endothermic, $\Delta n_{gas} = +1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Fox–Flory equation** is the single most-tested formula in KTU polymer questions — it tells the student that *short polymer chains have lower $T_g$* because chain ends act as internal plasticizers. Use it confidently in any $\overline{M}_n$–$T_g$ numerical problem.
