* **Comparison with emulsion PBR:** Emulsion PBR (radical) gives only **≈ 60 % cis** plus a mixture of trans and 1,2-vinyl; **solution PBR is far superior in cis-content and resilience**, which is why it dominates tyre sidewall compounding.

#### Model Solution — (b)

* **(i) Reactor design:** A single large stirred-tank reactor, typically 10–30 m³, with **double jacket** carrying boiling liquid ethylene at atmospheric pressure. Agitator is low-shear to avoid crushing crumbs.
* **(ii) Role of methyl chloride:** Acts as a **slurry medium**—it dissolves monomers but **precipitates polyisobutylene** because PIB is insoluble in MeCl at −95 °C. This auto-precipitation removes polymer from the active site and prevents runaway.
* **(iii) Why isoprene:** Pure polyisobutylene is fully saturated and **cannot be vulcanized** with sulphur. Incorporating 1–3 mol % isoprene introduces C=C double bonds that act as **sulphur-curing sites** without sacrificing the air-impermeable backbone.
* **(iv) Liquid ethylene jacket:** Liquid ethylene boils at **−103.7 °C**; the **latent heat of vaporization (≈ 483 kJ/kg)** absorbs the polymerization enthalpy (≈ 85 kJ/mol) and maintains the reactor at −95 °C. The vaporized ethylene is recompressed, condensed, and recycled—an elegant **boiling-pool refrigeration** scheme.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing "hot SBR" with "hot polymerisation of styrene".** Hot SBR is still 50 °C, not 200 °C.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of mercaptan.** Many students write the recipe without mentioning that the mercaptan **controls molecular weight** by chain transfer. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Citing "aluminium chloride" as Ziegler–Natta.** AlCl₃ is a **cationic (Friedel–Crafts)** catalyst for butyl rubber; it is **not** a Ziegler–Natta catalyst.
> 4. **Skipping the −95 °C explanation in butyl rubber.** Examiners expect a thermodynamic/kinetic justification: at higher T, MW collapses due to chain transfer to monomer.
> 5. **In numerical problems, omitting the units or sign.** Always state mol % with the unit explicitly.
> 6. **Drawing "block diagrams" without arrows or labels** → 0 marks for the diagram portion.
> 7. **Not writing the Mayo–Lewis derivation origin.** Examiners in KTU 2024 want at least a one-line justification of the terminal model assumption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Module 4: SBR, PBR, Butyl Rubber**

* **SBR = emulsion copolymer of butadiene + styrene**; cold (5 °C, redox) and hot (50 °C, persulphate) grades.
* **Recipe has 9 components:** monomer, water, soap, mercaptan, peroxide, Fe/EDTA, sulfoxylate, phosphate, and activator.
* **Conversion is short-stopped at 60 %** to avoid branching and gel formation.
* **Coagulation is by H₂SO₄ + NaCl.**
* **PBR = solution homopolymer of 1,3-butadiene**; uses **Ziegler–Natta (Nd) or lithium catalysts**; cis-1,4 > 95 %.
* **PBR is a homopolymer**, not a copolymer—do not confuse with SBR.
* **Butyl rubber = cationic copolymer of isobutylene + isoprene (97/3)**.
* **Polymerization at −95 °C** in **methyl chloride slurry** with **AlCl₃** catalyst.
* **Liquid ethylene jacket** provides cryogenic cooling by latent-heat vaporization.
* **Tg values:** SBR ≈ −50 °C, PBR ≈ −108 °C, IIR ≈ −67 °C.
* **Densities** of all three are ≈ 0.91–0.94 g/cm³.
* **Butyl rubber has ≈ 8× better gas impermeability** than NR → inner tubes, innerliners.
* **Mayo–Lewis terminal model** governs SBR composition; $r_1 r_2 < 1$ → azeotropic tendency.
* **Chain transfer agent (mercaptan) controls $\bar{X}_n$** in SBR.
* **Catalyst removal (desashing)** is critical for oxidative stability in PBR.
* **SBR is the largest-volume synthetic rubber** (~45 % share); **PBR ~25 %**; **Butyl ~5 %** but **strategic**.
* **Tyre industry consumes ~70 %** of all synthetic rubber output.
* **Three Cs of butyl process:** **C**old, **C**ationic, **C**ryogenic.
* **Three Ss of SBR:** **S**tyrene, **S**oap, **S**hort-stop.
* **Three Ns of PBR:** **N**d catalyst, **N**arrow MWD, **N**-hexane solvent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of ABS plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Production of ABS plastics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene (ABS)** is a thermoplastic terpolymer manufactured by the polymerization of **styrene** and **acrylonitrile** in the presence of a **polybutadiene rubber** backbone. The elastomeric polybutadiene phase is dispersed as microscopic particles within the rigid styrene-acrylonitrile (SAN) matrix, producing a two-phase toughened amorphous polymer. ABS combines the chemical resistance and surface hardness of acrylonitrile, the toughness and impact strength of butadiene, and the processability, gloss, and rigidity of styrene, making it one of the most versatile engineering thermoplastics in the petrochemical industry. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Production of ABS plastics falls under petrochemical downstream products derived from the three C₃/C₄/C₈ monomers obtained as by-products of steam cracking in refineries and ethylene plants. ### 1.2 Typical Composition of ABS (Industrial Specifications) | Component | Weight % | Functional Contribution | |---|---|---| | Acrylonitrile (CH₂=CH–CN) | **15 – 35 %** | Chemical resistance, hardness, heat distortion temperature | | Butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂) | **5 – 30 %** | Impact strength, low-temperature toughness | | Styrene (C₆H₅–CH=CH₂) | **40 – 60 %** | Rigidity, gloss, processability, low cost | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Reinforced Concrete Analogy"** > Imagine building a house. The **styrene-acrylonitrile (SAN)** matrix acts like the **hard concrete walls** — rigid, smooth, and shiny. The **polybutadiene rubber particles** act like the **steel reinforcement bars (rebar)** embedded inside the concrete — flexible, tough, and able to absorb sudden shocks. When a hammer hits the wall (an impact load), the rigid SAN would normally crack, but the dispersed rubber particles absorb and dissipate the energy, preventing crack propagation. This is exactly how ABS behaves: a rigid plastic made tough by an embedded rubber phase. The three monomers are linked together through a **graft copolymerization** reaction, where SAN chains are chemically grafted onto the polybutadiene backbone. The grafted rubber particles form the "anchors" that bind the tough rubber phase to the rigid SAN matrix — without this grafting, the rubber would simply separate out and the material would lose its mechanical integrity. ### 1.4 Why ABS is Important in Petroleum Refining Technology ABS is a **high-value downstream petrochemical product**. Its three monomers (acrylonitrile, butadiene, styrene) are obtained from: - **Steam cracking** of naphtha (ethylene plants) — produces butadiene and styrene - **Ammoxidation of propylene** (Sohio process) — produces acrylonitrile - **Catalytic reforming** of naphtha — also yields benzene → ethylbenzene → styrene Thus, ABS represents a critical **value-added link** between refinery operations and the plastics manufacturing industry. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Chemistry of ABS Formation ABS is not a simple random copolymer. It is a **graft copolymer** consisting of: - A **polybutadiene (PB) rubber substrate** (the "backbone") - **Styrene-acrylonitrile (SAN) grafted chains** growing on the PB backbone - **Free SAN matrix** (ungrafted) filling the volume between rubber particles The grafting reaction mechanism is **free-radical polymerization**, typically initiated by peroxides or redox initiators. During the reaction, free radicals abstract allylic hydrogens from the polybutadiene chain, creating radical sites at which styrene and acrylonitrile monomers are polymerized into SAN branches that are **covalently bonded** to the rubber backbone. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Grafting Mechanism - **Step 1 — Initiation:** The initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, cumene hydroperoxide) decomposes thermally to yield free radicals. - **Step 2 — Chain transfer to rubber:** Free radicals (or growing SAN chains) attack the allylic C–H bonds of polybutadiene, abstracting hydrogen and creating a macro-radical site on the rubber chain. - **Step 3 — Grafting:** Styrene and acrylonitrile monomers add to the macro-radical, growing SAN branches outward from the rubber particle surface. - **Step 4 — Termination:** Two growing chains (or a chain with a radical) combine or disproportionate, ending the polymerization. - **Step 5 — Phase separation:** As SAN accumulates, the grafted rubber particles become incompatible with the surrounding SAN matrix, forming discrete dispersed rubber domains (~0.1 – 5 µm in size). - **Step 6 — Coagulation & isolation:** The latex is broken (coagulated), washed, and dried to obtain ABS resin powder. ### 2.3 Industrial Production Routes for ABS There are **four major commercial processes** for ABS manufacture: | Process | Description | Key Features | |---|---|---| | **Emulsion Polymerization** | Polybutadiene latex + styrene + acrylonitrile polymerized in water with surfactants | Most common; fine control of rubber particle size; produces high-impact grades | | **Mass (Bulk) Polymerization** | Polymerization in monomer melt, no water/solvent | Clean, low-cost; harder to control rubber morphology | | **Suspension Polymerization** | Monomers dispersed as droplets in water with stabilizers | Good heat removal; medium-impact grades | | **Continuous Mass / Mass-Suspension Hybrid** | Combination of mass prepolymerization followed by suspension finishing | Modern, energy-efficient, high-purity grades | ### 2.4 Detailed Process — Emulsion Polymerization Method (Industry Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The emulsion route accounts for approximately 70–80 % of global ABS production.** The product is often called **e-ABS** (emulsion ABS). **Raw Material Preparation:** - Polybutadiene latex (typically 5–10 % of total solids) - Styrene monomer (purified, inhibitor removed) - Acrylonitrile monomer (purified) - Deionized water - Emulsifier (sodium dodecyl sulfate, potassium oleate) - Initiator (potassium persulfate, redox system) - Chain transfer agent (tert-dodecyl mercaptan) to control molecular weight - Antioxidant (to prevent degradation) **Process Flow:** 1. **Charging the reactor** with polybutadiene latex, water, emulsifier, and a small amount of initiator. 2. **Gradual addition** of styrene and acrylonitrile monomers to the reactor at controlled temperature (**60 – 90 °C**) over several hours. 3. **Grafting** occurs as SAN polymer grows on the PB rubber particles; free SAN also forms in the aqueous phase. 4. **Coagulation:** The latex is fed to a coagulation tank where salts (CaCl₂, Al₂(SO₄)₃) and acid break the emulsion, causing ABS particles to precipitate. 5. **Washing** to remove residual salts and emulsifiers. 6. **Drying** in a fluidized-bed dryer or spray dryer to remove moisture (final moisture < 0.1 %). 7. **Compounding:** The ABS powder is melt-blended with additives — stabilizers, lubricants, pigments, flame retardants, antistatic agents. 8. **Pelletizing** through an extruder to produce uniform pellets. 9. **Packaging** for shipment to converters (injection molders, extruders). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Relationship | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Grafting efficiency | $G = \frac{\text{SAN grafted onto PB}}{\text{Total SAN formed}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates how much SAN is anchored to rubber | | Rubber particle size | $d_p = 0.1 - 5\ \mu m$ | µm | Affects impact strength and surface gloss | | Monomer conversion | $X = \frac{M_{polymer}}{M_{monomer, initial}} \times 100$ | % | Process efficiency indicator | | Heat of polymerization (acrylonitrile) | $\Delta H \approx -72\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | Reactor cooling design | | Heat of polymerization (styrene) | $\Delta H \approx -70\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | Reactor cooling design | | Heat of polymerization (butadiene) | $\Delta H \approx -73\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | Reactor cooling design | | Melt flow index (MFI) | Mass extruded in 10 min at 200 °C / 5 kg load | g/10 min | Processability indicator | | Vicat softening temperature | $T_{Vicat} \approx 95 - 110\ ^\circ C$ | °C | Heat resistance rating | | Notched Izod impact strength | $I_{Izod} \approx 100 - 400\ \text{J/m}$ | J/m | Toughness rating | | Glass transition (SAN matrix) | $T_g \approx 105 - 115\ ^\circ C$ | °C | Upper service temperature | | Glass transition (PB rubber) | $T_g \approx -85\ ^\circ C$ | °C | Lower service temperature | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The **grafting efficiency** is the single most important quality parameter in ABS manufacture. A low grafting efficiency (< 30 %) means the rubber phase is poorly anchored, leading to delamination, poor impact strength, and surface defects. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive industry:** dashboards, bumper housings, interior trim (leverages impact strength at low temperatures) - **Electronics:** computer monitor housings, printer casings, mobile phone bodies (leverages heat resistance and flame-retardant grades) - **Consumer goods:** LEGO bricks, helmets, luggage, toys (leverages toughness and colorability) - **Pipes and fittings:** drainage pipes, DWV systems (leverages chemical resistance) - **3D printing:** FDM filament (leverages dimensional stability and processability) The petroleum refinery therefore supplies the **feedstock monomers** (ethylene → ethylene oxide → butadiene; benzene → styrene; propylene → acrylonitrile), and the petrochemical plant converts them into the final ABS resin — a textbook example of **vertical integration in the petroleum value chain**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Removal Requirement in an ABS Polymerization Reactor This derivation shows how to size the cooling duty of a CSTR used for the emulsion polymerization of ABS. This is a **typical KTU Part B numerical problem**. **Given data:** - Reactor volume: $V = 10\ \text{m}^3$ - Feed monomer composition: 60 % styrene, 25 % acrylonitrile, 15 % butadiene (by mass) - Total monomer feed rate: $F_{m,in} = 1500\ \text{kg/h}$ - Monomer conversion per pass: $X = 0.85$ (i.e., 85 %) - Reaction temperature: $T = 75\ ^\circ C$ - Density of monomer mix: $\rho_{mix} \approx 880\ \text{kg/m}^3$ - Heat of polymerization values: - Styrene: $\Delta H_{St} = -70\ \text{kJ/mol}$ - Acrylonitrile: $\Delta H_{AN} = -72\ \text{kJ/mol}$ - Butadiene: $\Delta H_{BD} = -73\ \text{kJ/mol}$ **Step 1 — Determine the molar feed rates of each monomer.** Molar masses: - Styrene (C₈H₈): $M_{St} = 104.15\ \text{g/mol}$ - Acrylonitrile (C₃H₃N): $M_{AN} = 53.06\ \text{g/mol}$ - Butadiene (C₄H₆): $M_{BD} = 54.09\ \text{g/mol}$ Mass feed rates: $$ \dot{m}_{St} = 0.60 \times 1500 = 900\ \text{kg/h} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{AN} = 0.25 \times 1500 = 375\ \text{kg/h} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{BD} = 0.15 \times 1500 = 225\ \text{kg/h} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to molar flow rates.** $$ \dot{n}_{St} = \frac{900 \times 10^3\ \text{g/h}}{104.15\ \text{g/mol}} = 8641\ \text{mol/h} $$ $$ \dot{n}_{AN} = \frac{375 \times 10^3\ \text{g/h}}{53.06\ \text{g/mol}} = 7067\ \text{mol/h} $$ $$ \dot{n}_{BD} = \frac{225 \times 10^3\ \text{g/h}}{54.09\ \text{g/mol}} = 4160\ \text{mol/h} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the molar rate of polymerization (moles reacting per hour).** $$ \dot{n}_{rx,St} = X \times \dot{n}_{St} = 0.85 \times 8641 = 7345\ \text{mol/h} $$ $$ \dot{n}_{rx,AN} = 0.85 \times 7067 = 6007\ \text{mol/h} $$ $$ \dot{n}_{rx,BD} = 0.85 \times 4160 = 3536\ \text{mol/h} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the heat released by each monomer polymerization.** $$ \dot{Q}_{St} = \dot{n}_{rx,St} \times \vert \Delta H_{St} \vert = 7345 \times 70 = 5.142 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ/h} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{AN} = 6007 \times 72 = 4.325 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ/h} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{BD} = 3536 \times 73 = 2.581 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ/h} $$ **Step 5 — Total heat release rate (this equals cooling duty).** $$ \dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{St} + \dot{Q}_{AN} + \dot{Q}_{BD} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{total} = 5.142 \times 10^5 + 4.325 \times 10^5 + 2.581 \times 10^5 $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{total} = 1.205 \times 10^6\ \text{kJ/h} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to kW (for engineering specification of cooling jacket).** $$ \dot{Q}_{total} = \frac{1.205 \times 10^6}{3600} = 334.6\ \text{kW} $$ **Result:** The reactor requires a cooling duty of **approximately 335 kW** to maintain isothermal operation at 75 °C. This dictates the size of the cooling jacket, the chilled water flow rate, and the reflux condenser. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of ABS Production Processes | Criterion | Emulsion Process | Mass (Bulk) Process | Suspension Process | Mass-Suspension Hybrid | |---|---|---|---|---| | Continuous or batch | Continuous / batch | Continuous | Batch | Continuous + batch | | Water usage | High (solvent medium) | None | High (suspending medium) | Low | | Product purity | Lower (residual surfactant) | Very high | Medium | Very high | | Rubber particle control | Excellent | Difficult | Moderate | Good | | Grafting efficiency | 50 – 70 % | 30 – 50 % | 40 – 60 % | 60 – 80 % | | Capital cost | High | Lowest | Medium | High | | Energy consumption | Medium (drying) | Low | Medium | Low | | Environmental impact | Waste water to treat | Minimal | Waste water to treat | Low | | Typical application | High-impact, flame-retardant | High-gloss, plating | Medium-impact, color | Premium grades | ### 3.3 Worked Example: Grafting Efficiency Calculation **Given:** - Total SAN formed in reactor: $M_{SAN,total} = 850\ \text{kg}$ - Free (ungrafted) SAN recovered in matrix: $M_{SAN,free} = 250\ \text{kg}$ - SAN that has grafted onto PB: $M_{SAN,grafted} = 600\ \text{kg}$ **Solution:** $$ G = \frac{M_{SAN,grafted}}{M_{SAN,total}} \times 100 = \frac{600}{850} \times 100 = 70.6\ \% $$ A grafting efficiency of **70.6 %** is considered industrially excellent and produces a high-impact ABS grade. > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** For premium high-impact ABS, the grafting efficiency must exceed **60 %**. If it falls below 40 %, the product is rejected due to delamination and poor mechanical performance. ### 3.4 Worked Example: Composition Verification by Material Balance A production batch of ABS uses the following charges: - Styrene: 1200 kg - Acrylonitrile: 500 kg - Butadiene (added as polybutadiene latex, 35 % solids): 600 kg latex → 210 kg PB **Step 1 — Total mass of monomers charged:** $$ M_{total} = 1200 + 500 + 210 = 1910\ \text{kg} $$ **Step 2 — Composition by weight percent:** $$ \%St = \frac{1200}{1910} \times 100 = 62.8\ \% $$ $$ \%AN = \frac{500}{1910} \times 100 = 26.2\ \% $$ $$ \%BD = \frac{210}{1910} \times 100 = 11.0\ \% $$ **Step 3 — Verify against ABS specification:** - Styrene (62.8 %) — within 40 – 60 % range? **Slightly above** → gloss and rigidity will be higher. - Acrylonitrile (26.2 %) — within 15 – 35 % range? **Yes** → good chemical resistance. - Butadiene (11.0 %) — within 5 – 30 % range? **Yes** → moderate impact strength. **Conclusion:** This is a **high-gloss, medium-impact grade** suitable for injection-molded consumer goods. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ABS Molecular Architecture The following Mermaid block renders the **graft copolymer architecture** of ABS at the molecular level. The polybutadiene backbone is shown in the centre, with SAN grafted branches radiating outward, and free SAN matrix surrounding the rubber particle. ```mermaid graph TD A["POLYBUTADIENE BACKBONE"] --> B["GRAFTED SAN CHAIN 1"] A --> C["GRAFTED SAN CHAIN 2"] A --> D["GRAFTED SAN CHAIN 3"] A --> E["GRAFTED SAN CHAIN 4"] B --> F["STYRENE ACRYLONITRILE UNITS"] C --> G["STYRENE ACRYLONITRILE UNITS"] D --> H["STYRENE ACRYLONITRILE UNITS"] E --> I["STYRENE ACRYLONITRILE UNITS"] F --> J["FREE SAN MATRIX PHASE"] G --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K["RIGID PLASTIC MATRIX GIVES GLOSS AND RIGIDITY"] A --> L["RUBBER PARTICLE GIVES IMPACT STRENGTH AND TOUGHNESS"] ``` ### 4.2 Industrial Emulsion Process Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A["Monomer Storage
Styrene + Acrylonitrile"] --> B["Purification and
Inhibitor Removal"] C["Polybutadiene
Latex Tank"] --> B B --> D["Emulsion
Polymerization
Reactor CSTR"] D --> E["Coagulation
Tank Salt Acid"] E --> F["Washing
and Filtering"] F --> G["Fluidized Bed
Dryer"] G --> H["Compounding
Extruder Additives"] H --> I["Pelletizer
and Cooling"] I --> J["ABS Pellets
Packaging Storage"] D --> K["Heat Exchanger
Cooling Duty 335 kW"] K --> D ``` ### 4.3 Process Flow Logic (Step-by-Step Reading of the Diagram) - **Input stream A** brings purified styrene and acrylonitrile monomers from storage; stream **C** brings the polybutadiene latex substrate. - Both streams merge at the **purification unit (B)**, where residual inhibitors (e.g., MEHQ, TBC) and oxygen are stripped to prevent premature polymerization. - The purified feed enters the **emulsion polymerization reactor (D)**, a glass-lined or stainless-steel CSTR operating at 60 – 90 °C under nitrogen atmosphere. - The reactor's exothermic heat is removed by a **cooling jacket and external heat exchanger (K)**, returning coolant to the jacket. - The ABS latex exits to the **coagulation tank (E)**, where salts (CaCl₂) and sulfuric acid break the emulsion and precipitate ABS crumb. - The crumb is **washed and filtered (F)** to remove salts, surfactants, and unreacted monomers. - The wet crumb enters the **fluidized bed dryer (G)**, reducing moisture to below 0.1 %. - Dry ABS powder is fed to the **compounding extruder (H)**, where stabilizers, pigments, and additives are melt-blended. - The molten ABS is cut into uniform **pellets (I)** and packaged. ### 4.4 Value-Chain Integration Diagram: Refinery → ABS Resin ```mermaid graph TD A["Naphtha Feed to Steam Cracker"] --> B["Ethylene Plant Cracking Furnace"] B --> C1["Butadiene C4 Fraction Extraction"] B --> C2["Styrene from Benzene via Ethylbenzene Dehydrogenation"] B --> C3["Propylene to Acrylonitrile Sohio Process Ammonoxidation"] C1 --> D["ABS Polymerization Plant"] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E["ABS Resin Compounding and Pelletizing"] E --> F["End Products Automotive Electronics Consumer Goods"] ``` **Reading the value chain:** - **A → B:** Naphtha from the refinery enters the steam cracker. - **B → C1:** The C₄ fraction is extracted and purified to give **1,3-butadiene** (one of the three ABS monomers). - **B → C2:** Benzene (from reformate) is alkylated to ethylbenzene, then dehydrogenated to **styrene**. - **B → C3:** Propylene is ammoxidized with ammonia and air over a fluidized-bed catalyst to give **acrylonitrile**. - **C1 + C2 + C3 → D:** The three monomers converge at the ABS polymerization plant. - **D → E → F:** The ABS resin is compounded and sold to downstream industries. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List the three monomers used in the production of ABS plastics and state the function of each monomer in the final product.* **Model Answer:** The three monomers are: - **Acrylonitrile (15 – 35 %):** contributes chemical resistance, surface hardness, and heat distortion temperature. - **Butadiene (5 – 30 %):** contributes impact strength, toughness, and low-temperature flexibility via the polybutadiene rubber phase. - **Styrene (40 – 60 %):** contributes rigidity, gloss, ease of processing, and low cost via the SAN matrix phase. **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define "grafting efficiency" in the context of ABS manufacture. What is its typical industrial range?* **Model Answer:** Grafting efficiency is defined as the percentage of total SAN (styrene-acrylonitrile) formed during polymerization that is chemically grafted onto the polybutadiene backbone, expressed as: $$ G = \frac{M_{SAN,grafted}}{M_{SAN,total}} \times 100 $$ The typical industrial range is **50 – 70 %** for the emulsion process and **60 – 80 %** for the mass-suspension hybrid process. A value above 60 % is required for high-impact ABS grades. --- ### 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **forget to draw a process flow diagram** (PFD) when asked to describe a process. KTU examiners allocate **2–3 marks specifically for the diagram**. Always include a labelled block diagram of the emulsion polymerization process. > - Do **not confuse** the roles of the three monomers. Examiners frequently set 2-mark sub-questions asking "What does butadiene contribute?" — a wrong answer here costs 2 marks instantly. > - For numerical problems, students often forget to **convert units** between kg/h, g/mol, and kW. Showing every unit conversion step is mandatory for full marks. > - Do **not write "ABS is a copolymer"** — it is a **terpolymer with a graft copolymer structure**. The distinction is a 1-mark KTU favourite question. --- **Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **emulsion polymerization process** for the manufacture of ABS plastics. List the raw materials, the operating conditions, and the role of each additive used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A continuous CSTR is used to polymerize ABS from a feed containing 55 % styrene, 30 % acrylonitrile, and 15 % butadiene (mass basis) at a total feed rate of 2000 kg/h. The conversion is 80 %. Calculate the **total heat release rate in kW**, given $\Delta H_{St} = -70\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta H_{AN} = -72\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta H_{BD} = -73\ \text{kJ/mol}$. Atomic/molar masses: C = 12, H = 1, N = 14. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to (a):** **Raw Materials:** - Styrene (purified, inhibitor removed) - Acrylonitrile (purified) - Polybutadiene latex (5 – 10 % of total solids) - Deionized water (continuous phase) - Emulsifier — sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) or potassium oleate - Initiator — potassium persulfate (K₂S₂O₈) or a redox system (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂) - Chain transfer agent — tert-dodecyl mercaptan (TDM) - Antioxidant — hindered phenol (e.g., Irganox 1076) - Electrolyte for coagulation — CaCl₂, Al₂(SO₄)₃ - Acid for coagulation — H₂SO₄ **Operating Conditions:** - Reactor temperature: **60 – 90 °C** - Reactor pressure: atmospheric to slight vacuum - pH: 8 – 10 (alkaline) - Reaction time: 4 – 8 hours - Solids content of latex: 35 – 50 % - Monomer conversion: 90 – 99 % **Process Steps (with PFD):** 1. **Charging the reactor** with polybutadiene latex, water, emulsifier, and initiator. 2. **Gradual addition** of styrene and acrylonitrile over 3 – 5 hours. 3. **Grafting reaction** at 75 °C under nitrogen; SAN chains grow on PB rubber particles. 4. **Coagulation** with CaCl₂ and H₂SO₄ to break the emulsion. 5. **Washing** with deionized water to remove salts. 6. **Drying** in a fluidized bed dryer to < 0.1 % moisture. 7. **Compounding** with stabilizers, pigments, and additives in an extruder. 8. **Pelletizing** to uniform ABS pellets. **Block diagram (to be drawn by the student):** *Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the reference PFD.* --- **Model Solution to (b):** **Given:** - Total feed = 2000 kg/h - Mass fractions: $w_{St} = 0.55$, $w_{AN} = 0.30$, $w_{BD} = 0.15$ - Conversion $X = 0.80$ - $\Delta H_{St} = -70\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta H_{AN} = -72\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta H_{BD} = -73\ \text{kJ/mol}$ **Step 1 — Mass flow rates:** $$ \dot{m}_{St} = 0.55 \times 2000 = 1100\ \text{kg/h} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{AN} = 0.30 \times 2000 = 600\ \text{kg/h} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{BD} = 0.15 \times 2000 = 300\ \text{kg/h} $$ **Step 2 — Molar masses:** - Styrene C₈H₈: $M_{St} = 8(12) + 8(1) = 104\ \text{g/mol}$ - Acrylonitrile C₃H₃N: $M_{AN} = 3(12) + 3(1) + 14 = 53\ \text{g/mol}$ - Butadiene C₄H₆: $M_{BD} = 4(12) + 6(1) = 54\ \text{g/mol}$ **Step 3 — Molar flow rates:** $$ \dot{n}_{St} = \frac{1100 \times 10^3}{104} = 10577\ \text{mol/h} $$ $$ \dot{n}_{AN} = \frac{600 \times 10^3}{53} = 11321\ \text{mol/h} $$ $$ \dot{n}_{BD} = \frac{300 \times 10^3}{54} = 5556\ \text{mol/h} $$ **Step 4 — Moles reacted (X = 0.80):** $$ \dot{n}_{rx,St} = 0.80 \times 10577 = 8462\ \text{mol/h} $$ $$ \dot{n}_{rx,AN} = 0.80 \times 11321 = 9057\ \text{mol/h} $$ $$ \dot{n}_{rx,BD} = 0.80 \times 5556 = 4445\ \text{mol/h} $$ **Step 5 — Heat released:** $$ \dot{Q}_{St} = 8462 \times 70 = 5.924 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ/h} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{AN} = 9057 \times 72 = 6.521 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ/h} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{BD} = 4445 \times 73 = 3.245 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ/h} $$ **Step 6 — Total heat release rate:** $$ \dot{Q}_{total} = (5.924 + 6.521 + 3.245) \times 10^5 = 1.569 \times 10^6\ \text{kJ/h} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to kW:** $$ \dot{Q}_{total} = \frac{1.569 \times 10^6}{3600} = 435.8\ \text{kW} $$ **Final Answer:** The total heat release rate is **≈ 436 kW**, which must be removed by the reactor cooling system. --- **Question B (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Compare the **emulsion, mass, and suspension polymerization processes** for ABS manufacture on the basis of grafting efficiency, purity, water usage, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **grafting mechanism** in ABS manufacture. How does the grafting of SAN onto the polybutadiene backbone improve the impact strength of the polymer? Discuss the role of the rubber particle size in determining the final properties. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to (a):** | Process | Grafting Efficiency | Purity | Water Usage | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Emulsion | 50 – 70 % | Moderate (surfactant residue) | High | High-impact, flame-retardant, plating grades | | Mass (bulk) | 30 – 50 % | Very high (no surfactant) | None | High-gloss, transparent, medical grades | | Suspension | 40 – 60 % | Moderate | High | Medium-impact, color-compounded, general-purpose | | Mass-suspension hybrid | 60 – 80 % | Very high | Low | Premium automotive, electronic enclosures | The **emulsion process** dominates global production due to its excellent control over rubber particle morphology and superior grafting efficiency. The **mass process** is favoured for ultra-clean applications. The **mass-suspension hybrid** is the most modern, combining the cleanliness of mass polymerization with the morphology control of suspension finishing. **Model Solution to (b):** **Grafting Mechanism:** The grafting reaction is a **free-radical chain transfer** process. The initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) decomposes to give free radicals, which initiate polymerization of styrene and acrylonitrile in the aqueous phase. Growing SAN radicals attack the **allylic C–H bonds** of the polybutadiene backbone, abstracting a hydrogen and creating a **macro-radical site** on the rubber chain. Styrene and acrylonitrile then add to this macro-radical, growing SAN branches that are **covalently bonded** to the rubber particle. The result is a **graft copolymer** with a rubber core and SAN shell, dispersed in a free SAN matrix. **How Grafting Improves Impact Strength:** Without grafting, the rubber phase would simply be a mechanical mixture with the SAN matrix. Under an impact load, the rubber particles would debond from the matrix, and cracks would propagate easily — giving a brittle product. With grafting, the SAN branches act as **molecular anchors** that chemically bond the rubber to the matrix. When an impact load is applied, the rubber particles **crazing and shear yielding** absorb the energy and prevent crack propagation. The result is a tough, ductile material with very high impact strength (notched Izod 100 – 400 J/m). **Role of Rubber Particle Size:** - **Small particles (0.1 – 0.5 µm):** High surface gloss, moderate impact strength — used in plating and aesthetic grades. - **Medium particles (0.5 – 2 µm):** Balanced gloss and impact — general-purpose grades. - **Large particles (2 – 5 µm):** Maximum impact strength, lower gloss — used in high-impact structural parts. The optimal particle size is typically **0.5 – 1 µm** for most commercial applications, providing a balance of toughness, gloss, and processability. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam** - ABS stands for **Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene** — a **graft terpolymer**, not a simple copolymer. - Typical composition: **Acrylonitrile 15–35 %, Butadiene 5–30 %, Styrene 40–60 %**. - ABS is a **two-phase system**: rigid SAN matrix + dispersed polybutadiene rubber particles. - **Acrylonitrile** provides chemical resistance, hardness, and heat distortion temperature. - **Butadiene** provides impact strength, toughness, and low-temperature flexibility. - **Styrene** provides rigidity, gloss, and processability. - **Grafting efficiency** must exceed **60 %** for high-impact grades. - **Emulsion polymerization** is the dominant industrial process (70 – 80 % of global production). - The reaction is **free-radical** with chain transfer to the allylic C–H bonds of polybutadiene. - **Heat of polymerization** for each monomer is approximately **70 – 73 kJ/mol** (exothermic). - Reactor cooling duty is a **critical design parameter** — typical industrial reactors require several hundred kW of cooling. - Process steps: polymerization → coagulation → washing → drying → compounding → pelletizing. - **Mass-suspension hybrid** is the modern, premium route — high grafting efficiency, low waste water. - The three monomers are derived from refinery streams: **butadiene** from steam cracking C₄, **styrene** from benzene (reformate), and **acrylonitrile** from propylene (Sohio process). - Industrial uses: automotive, electronics, consumer goods, pipes, 3D printing filament. - **Pitfall:** Always say "**terpolymer**" and "**graft copolymer**" — never just "copolymer." - **Pitfall:** Always include a **PFD** in long-answer questions worth full marks. - **Pitfall:** Always **show unit conversions** in numerical problems (kg/h → mol/h → kW).

topics.title: Production of polyamide (Nylon 6 and Nylon 6,6), polyester and acrylic fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production of polyamide (Nylon 6 and Nylon 6,6), polyester and acrylic fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧵 MODULE 4 — PRODUCTION OF POLYAMIDE, POLYESTER & ACRYLIC FIBRES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Polyamide Fibres — Formal Definition

A **polyamide** is a macromolecule containing repeating amide linkages (**—CO—NH—**) along the polymer backbone. The two most commercially significant polyamide fibres are **Nylon 6** (a homopolymer of ε-caprolactam) and **Nylon 6,6** (a copolymer of hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid). Both are semicrystalline, thermoplastic, fibre-forming polymers belonging to the broader family of **engineering thermoplastics** derived entirely from **petrochemical feedstocks** (benzene, cyclohexane, butadiene, propylene).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 (OECHT615 / Module 4):**
> Nylon 6 and Nylon 6,6 are produced by **ring-opening polymerisation** and **step-growth polycondensation** respectively. The KTU examiner frequently tests the *monomer–polymer relationship* and the *feedstock–petrochemical link*.

### 1.2 Polyester Fibres — Formal Definition

**Polyester fibre** is a manufactured fibre in which the fibre-forming substance is any long-chain synthetic polymer composed of at least **85 % by mass of an ester of a dihydric alcohol and terephthalic acid** (ISO definition). The dominant commercial polyester is **poly(ethylene terephthalate)** — abbreviated **PET** — produced by the polycondensation of **ethylene glycol (EG)** and either **dimethyl terephthalate (DMT)** or purified **terephthalic acid (PTA)**.

### 1.3 Acrylic Fibres — Formal Definition

An **acrylic fibre** is a manufactured fibre in which the fibre-forming substance is any long-chain synthetic polymer composed of at least **85 % by mass of acrylonitrile units (—CH₂—CH(CN)—)** (ISO definition). The polymer is **polyacrylonitrile (PAN)**, obtained by **free-radical chain polymerisation** of the monomer **acrylonitrile (ACN)**. Acrylonitrile is produced industrially by the **Sohio ammoxidation process** of propylene.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Lego Principle"**
> Think of monomers as individual **Lego bricks** and the polymer as a **chain you build by snapping them together**.
> • In **Nylon 6** and **Nylon 6,6**, every "snap" releases a small **water molecule** (condensation) — like a click that expels a tiny drop.
> • In **Nylon 6**, one of the bricks is actually a **ring** (caprolactam) that opens up before joining — like a bracelet unclasping to become a straight stick.
> • In **acrylics**, the chain grows by **free radicals** — picture a crowd of unpaired electrons running along, grabbing monomers at random.
> • In **polyester**, two different bricks (acid + alcohol) snap together to release methanol or water.

### 1.4 Petrochemical Origin (Why this topic is in Petroleum Refining)

| Polymer | Key Monomer(s) | Petrochemical Building Block |
|---|---|---|
| Nylon 6 | ε-Caprolactam | **Cyclohexanone** (from **benzene/phenol**) |
| Nylon 6,6 | HMDA + Adipic acid | **Benzene → Cyclohexane/Butadiene** |
| Polyester (PET) | EG + PTA/DMT | **p-Xylene** (from reformate), **Ethylene oxide** |
| Acrylic (PAN) | Acrylonitrile | **Propylene** (FCC/steam cracker) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerisation Mechanism Classification

The four fibres in this module span **three distinct polymerisation mechanisms** — a favourite KTU short-answer topic.

### A. Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerisation
- **Nylon 6,6** and **Polyester (PET)** belong to this class.
- Any two molecular species (monomer, oligomer, polymer) can react with each other at any time.
- Molecular weight rises **slowly** until very high conversion (> 99 %).
- Each bond formed releases a small molecule (**H₂O** or **CH₃OH**).

### B. Ring-Opening Polymerisation (ROP)
- **Nylon 6** is the textbook example.
- The cyclic monomer **ε-caprolactam** is opened (via hydrolytic, anionic, or cationic initiation) to give a linear polyamide.
- Mechanistically behaves like step polymerisation in terms of molecular weight build-up, but **no small molecule is eliminated** (hence sometimes classified as "addition ring-opening").

### C. Chain-Growth (Free-Radical) Polymerisation
- **Acrylic (PAN)** belongs to this class.
- An initiator (e.g., AIBN, KPS, redox couple) generates radicals.
- Chain grows to very high molecular weight in **milliseconds**, then terminates.
- High molecular weight is achieved at **low conversion** (no need for 99 % conversion).

---

## 2.2 The Carothers Equations — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carothers' Equation is the single most important formula for step-growth polymerisation problems.** Memorise both forms (stoichiometric and non-stoichiometric).

### Definitions of Terms
- $\overline{DP}_n$ = **number-average degree of polymerisation** (average number of monomer units per chain)
- $p$ = **extent of reaction / conversion** (fraction of functional groups that have reacted)
- $N$ = total number of molecules initially present
- $N_0$ = number of molecules remaining at conversion $p$
- $r$ = **stoichiometric imbalance ratio** = $N_B / N_A$ (where A is the minority functional group, $r \le 1$)
- $\overline{M}_n$ = number-average molecular weight
- $M_0$ = average molecular weight of one structural repeat unit

---

### Master Formula Table

| # | Formula | Application | KTU Frequency |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Stoichiometric balance ($r = 1$); e.g., Nylon 6,6 with 1 : 1 acid : amine | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | Non-stoichiometric; one monomer in excess | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $\overline{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \times M_0$ | Converts DP to absolute molecular weight | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{M_0}{1-p}$ | Stoichiometric case, expressed in mass | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\overline{M}_w = M_0\,\dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$ | Weight-average MW for step polymerisation | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $\text{Dispersity } Đ = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = 1 + p$ | Molecular weight distribution width | ⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A real polyester reactor at 99 % conversion gives $\overline{DP}_n = 100$ (i.e., $\overline{M}_n \approx 19{,}200$ g/mol for PET). To reach fibre-grade $\overline{M}_n \approx 20{,}000$, the reactor must operate at $p \approx 0.995$. The viscosity at this point is enormous — this dictates the use of special **finishing reactors, wiped-film evaporators, or solid-state polycondensation (SSP)**.

---

## 2.3 Fibre-Spinning Fundamentals

All four polymer types are converted into fibre by **melt spinning** (Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, PET) or **wet/dry spinning** (PAN, since PAN decomposes below its melting point).

| Process | Applicable to | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Melt Spinning** | Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, PET | Polymer melts cleanly below degradation T |
| **Dry Spinning** | PAN (sometimes) | Polymer dissolved in DMF, extruded into hot air |
| **Wet Spinning** | PAN (most common) | Polymer solution (DMF/DMSO) extruded into coagulation bath |

**Key post-spinning operations (always mention in 14-mark answers):**
1. **Drawing** (orientation) — aligns chains, increases crystallinity, raises tenacity 5–10×.
2. **Annealing / heat-setting** — fixes dimensions, relieves internal stress.
3. **Texturing** — adds bulk/crimp for apparel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Carothers Equation (Stoichiometric Case)

### Problem Statement
For a step-growth polymerisation of a bifunctional monomer A—A + B—B (with A and B present in **equal stoichiometric amounts**), derive the relationship between the number-average degree of polymerisation $\overline{DP}_n$ and the fractional conversion $p$.

### Logical Steps

**Step 1 — Start with one mole of A groups (and one mole of B groups).**
Initially, total number of molecules present:
$$N_0 = N_{\text{AA}} + N_{\text{BB}}$$
For a stoichiometric mixture of bifunctional A—A and B—B in equal moles, the initial number of molecules is $N_0 = 1$ (normalised; per 1 mole of A—A plus 1 mole of B—B → 2 moles of molecules initially, but for simplicity we follow the Carothers convention per mole of A groups).

**Step 2 — Define conversion p.**
Let $p$ = fraction of A groups (or B groups, since they are equal) that have reacted at time $t$.

Number of unreacted A groups = $N_0(1-p)$.

**Step 3 — Each A group that reacted formed one new molecule of by-product (water) AND extended chains.**
When $p$ fraction of groups have reacted, the number of separate molecules (chains) remaining is proportional to the unreacted groups:
$$N = N_0(1-p)$$

> **Logic Check:** At $p = 0$ (no reaction), $N = N_0$ — correct, all monomers are free.
> At $p = 1$ (100 % reaction), $N = 0$ — correct, the whole system becomes one giant molecule in the limit.

**Step 4 — Define degree of polymerisation.**
$\overline{DP}_n$ is the *average number of original monomer units per polymer molecule*. Since the total number of monomer units is conserved (say, $N_0$ structural units), and they are distributed among $N$ chains:
$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{\text{Total structural units}}{\text{Number of chains}} = \frac{N_0}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 4.**
$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

This is the celebrated **Carothers equation for stoichiometric step polymerisation**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Nylon 6,6 — KTU Style 14-Mark Sub-Part)

> **Q (Module 4, 7 marks):** Nylon 6,6 is synthesised from hexamethylenediamine (HMDA, $M = 116.21$ g/mol) and adipic acid ($M = 146.14$ g/mol). If the reaction is carried to **99.5 % conversion** with **1 % stoichiometric excess of adipic acid** (to cap chains and control molecular weight), calculate the number-average degree of polymerisation and the number-average molecular weight of the resulting polyamide.

### Given
- $p = 0.995$
- Excess of adipic acid = 1 % → ratio $r = 0.99$ (acid:amine)
- Repeat unit mass of Nylon 6,6: HMDA + Adipic acid − H₂O
$$M_0 = 116.21 + 146.14 - 18.015 = 244.33 \;\text{g/mol}$$

### Step 1 — Use non-stoichiometric Carothers equation
$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

Substitute $r = 0.99$ and $p = 0.995$:
$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{1+0.99}{1+0.99 - 2(0.99)(0.995)}$$

Compute denominator:
$$1 + 0.99 = 1.99$$
$$2(0.99)(0.995) = 1.97010$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 1.99 - 1.97010 = 0.01990$$

$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{1.99}{0.01990} = 100.0$$

### Step 2 — Compute number-average molecular weight
$$\overline{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \times M_0 = 100.0 \times 244.33 = 24{,}433\;\text{g/mol} \approx 24.4\;\text{kDa}$$

### Step 3 — Verification by stoichiometric equation (no excess, $r = 1$)
For comparison, if perfectly stoichiometric:
$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1-0.995} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200$$

Hence a **1 % stoichiometric excess halves the degree of polymerisation** — this is the **industrial molecular-weight control technique** universally employed in commercial Nylon 6,6 and PET plants.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Number of By-Product Molecules Released

For a step-growth polymerisation producing **water** (e.g., Nylon 6,6, Nylon 6 by hydrolysis equilibrium):

Number of bonds formed = number of H₂O molecules released = $N_0 \cdot p$

For Nylon 6,6 at 99.5 % conversion starting from 1 mole of HMDA + 1 mole of adipic acid → 2 moles of structural units → 1.99 moles of water released per mole of diamine.

> **Mass-balance check (engineering use):** In a 100 t/day PET plant producing PET from DMT + EG, the methanol release is enormous — recovered in a distillation column and recycled. KTU examiners sometimes ask why this mass balance matters.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism × Fibre × Conditions

| Parameter | Nylon 6 | Nylon 6,6 | PET | PAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polymerisation Type** | Ring-opening (hydrolytic) | Step-growth condensation | Step-growth condensation (trans-esterification) | Free-radical chain |
| **Monomer(s)** | ε-Caprolactam | HMDA + Adipic acid | EG + DMT/PTA | Acrylonitrile |
| **By-product** | None (ring opening) | H₂O | CH₃OH (DMT route) or H₂O (PTA route) | None |
| **Catalyst** | Water (hydrolytic) / Na (anionic) | None required | Sb₂O₃ / Ti(OBu)₄ | AIBN / KPS / Redox |
| **Reactor Type** | VK tube (continuous) | Autoclave (batch) | CSTR train + finisher | CSTR or continuous stirred |
| **Typical $\overline{M}_n$ (g/mol)** | 15,000–30,000 | 12,000–20,000 | 20,000–40,000 | 50,000–150,000 |
| **Spinning Method** | Melt | Melt | Melt | Wet (DMF/DMSO) or Dry |
| **Melting Point (°C)** | 215–220 | 255–265 | 250–260 | Decomposes ~300 |

---

## 3.5 Acrylonitrile Stoichiometry (Ammoxidation — Petrochemical Anchor)

The **Sohio process** converts **propylene + ammonia + air** to acrylonitrile:

$$\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH—CH}_3 + \text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{bismuth-phosphomolybdate, 400-460 °C}} \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH—CN} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Selectivity to ACN** ≈ 75–80 %; by-products are HCN and acetonitrile (both recovered as valuable chemicals — a key **integrated petrochemical complex** concept).

PAN polymerisation:
$$n\,\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CHCN} \xrightarrow{\text{AIBN, 50-70 °C}} \left(\text{—CH}_2\text{—CH(CN)—}\right)_n$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Nylon 6,6 Production (Melt Route)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Benzene from Reformate] --> A2[ Catalytic Hydrogenation to Cyclohexane ]
    A2 --> A3[ Oxidation to KA Oil Cyclohexanol + Cyclohexanone ]
    A3 --> A4[ Nitric Acid Oxidation to Adipic Acid ]
    B1[ Ammonia ] --> B2[ Hydrogenation of Adiponitrile ]
    A4 --> C1[ 60 percent Aqueous HMDA Solution ]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[ Stoichiometric Mixing with Adipic Acid in 80 percent N2 atmosphere ]
    C2 --> C3[ Autoclave Polymerisation 220 to 280 degree C 20 bar ]
    C3 --> C4[ Flash and Pelletiser ]
    C4 --> C5[ Melt Spinning at 280 degree C ]
    C5 --> C6[ Drawing 4x to 5x at 120 degree C ]
    C6 --> C7[ Nylon 6 6 Fibre ]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Nylon 6 Production (Caprolactam Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phenol or Cyclohexane Feed] --> P2[ Oxidation to Cyclohexanone ]
    P2 --> P3[ Beckmann Rearrangement with Hydroxylamine sulfate gives Cyclohexanone Oxime ]
    P3 --> P4[ Acid Catalysed Rearrangement to epsilon Caprolactam ]
    P4 --> P5[ Purification Vacuum Distillation ]
    P5 --> P6[ Continuous VK Tube Hydrolytic ROP at 250 to 270 degree C with 0.5 to 5 percent water ]
    P6 --> P7[ Monomer Recovery and Pelletisation ]
    P7 --> P8[ Melt Spinning 260 to 280 degree C ]
    P8 --> P9[ Drawing and Heat Setting ]
    P9 --> P10[ Nylon 6 Fibre ]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Polyester (PET) Fibre Production

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[ p Xylene from Catalytic Reformer ] --> Q2[ Oxidation to Terephthalic Acid or Esterification to DMT ]
    Q2 --> Q3[ Ethylene Oxide hydration to Ethylene Glycol ]
    Q3 --> Q4[ Ester Interchange with DMT at 180 to 220 degree C Mn or Co Acetate Catalyst ]
    Q4 --> Q5[ Prepolymer Stage 240 to 270 degree C ]
    Q5 --> Q6[ Finisher Polycondensation 270 to 285 degree C Sb2O3 Catalyst high vacuum ]
    Q6 --> Q7[ Melt Spinning through Spinneret ]
    Q7 --> Q8[ Drawing over Heated Godets ]
    Q8 --> Q9[ Heat Setting and Winding ]
    Q9 --> Q10[ PET Polyester Fibre ]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Acrylic (PAN) Fibre Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[ Propylene from FCC or Steam Cracker ] --> R2[ Ammoxidation Sohio Process Bi P Mo O Catalyst 400 to 460 degree C ]
    R2 --> R3[ Acrylo Nitrile + HCN + Acetonitrile By Products ]
    R3 --> R4[ Purification Distillation Column ]
    R4 --> R5[ Free Radical Polymerisation in DMF or DMSO Solution AIBN 50 to 70 degree C ]
    R5 --> R6[ PAN Dope Solution 20 to 25 percent solids ]
    R6 --> R7[ Wet Spinning into DMF Water Coagulation Bath ]
    R7 --> R8[ Drawing 6x to 10x in Hot Water and Steam ]
    R8 --> R9[ Washing and Drying ]
    R9 --> R10[ Crimping and Cutting to Staple ]
    R10 --> R11[ Acrylic Fibre ]
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture — Interlinkage of Petrochemical Streams

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Refinery
        F1[ Naphtha from Crude Distillation ]
        F2[ Catalytic Reformer C6 to C8 Aromatics ]
        F3[ FCC Unit Propylene Rich C3 Stream ]
        F4[ Steam Cracker Olefins and Aromatics ]
    end
    subgraph Petrochemical Complex
        F1 --> F4
        F2 --> P1[ Benzene Extraction ]
        F2 --> P2[ p Xylene Crystallisation ]
        F3 --> P3[ Acrylonitrile via Ammoxidation ]
        F4 --> P4[ Ethylene to Ethylene Oxide to EG ]
    end
    P1 --> Z1[ Nylon 6 6 Plant ]
    P1 --> Z2[ Nylon 6 Caprolactam Plant ]
    P2 --> Z3[ PET Polyester Plant ]
    P3 --> Z4[ PAN Acrylic Plant ]
    P4 --> Z3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B, Q1(a)]**
> **Q1 (3 marks).** Distinguish between **addition polymerisation** and **condensation polymerisation** with one example each from the four fibres covered in Module 4.

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | Addition (Chain-growth) | Condensation (Step-growth) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Active centre (radical/ion) adds monomers sequentially | Any two molecules react with each other |
| By-product | **None** | **Small molecule** (H₂O, CH₃OH, HCl) |
| Molecular weight build-up | Rapid, early in reaction | Slow, only at very high conversion |
| Initiator needed? | Yes (radical initiator) | No (heat/catalyst sufficient) |
| Example from Module 4 | **Acrylic (PAN)** — free-radical polymerisation of acrylonitrile | **Nylon 6,6** — HMDA + adipic acid → releases H₂O |
| Second example | — | **PET** — EG + DMT → releases CH₃OH |

*(Valued at 3 marks: 1 mark for mechanism distinction, 1 mark for by-product, 1 mark for correct example.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1(b)]**
> **Q2 (3 marks).** Write the chemical equation for the **Sohio ammoxidation of propylene** and state the catalyst and typical temperature range.

**Model Answer:**
$$\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH—CH}_3 + \text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH—CN} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- **Catalyst:** Bismuth–phosphomolybdate (Bi–P–Mo–O) on silica support.
- **Temperature:** **400 – 460 °C**.
- **Pressure:** 1 – 3 atm (atmospheric or slightly above).
- **Industrial significance:** This single reaction supplies **> 90 % of the world's acrylonitrile**, the monomer for PAN acrylic fibre.
*(Valued at 3 marks: 1.5 marks for balanced equation, 0.75 mark for catalyst, 0.75 mark for temperature.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, ESE Pattern, 14 marks]**
> **Module Choice Question:** *Answer **any one** of the following:*

---

### **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of Nylon 6,6 from petrochemical feedstocks**. List the major reactions, the by-products removed at each stage, and the spinning method used to convert the polymer into fibre. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polycondensation reactor produces Nylon 6,6 with a **number-average molecular weight of 19,500 g/mol**. The repeat unit has a formula weight of **226 g/mol**. If the stoichiometric ratio $r = 0.98$ (slight excess of adipic acid), calculate the **fractional conversion $p$** achieved in the reactor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Manufacture of Nylon 6,6 (7 marks)**

*Steps to write (each is approximately 1 mark):*

1. **Feedstock isolation:** Benzene (from reformate/naphtha) is catalytically hydrogenated to cyclohexane.
2. **Adipic acid synthesis:** Cyclohexane → KA oil (cyclohexanol + cyclohexanone) → **nitric acid oxidation → adipic acid**. By-product: N₂O, Cu/N-containing effluent.
3. **HMDA synthesis:** Adipic acid → adiponitrile (ammoniation) → **catalytic hydrogenation → HMDA**.
4. **Salt formation:** HMDA + adipic acid → **"Nylon salt"** (1 : 1 aqueous solution of the ammonium salt, 60 % w/w).
5. **Polycondensation:** Salt is concentrated, charged to a **stainless-steel autoclave** under N₂ at **220–280 °C, 20 bar**. Water is removed continuously. By-product: **H₂O** (removed and recycled).
6. **Spinning:** Polymer melt (at 280 °C) is extruded through a spinneret, quenched, and **drawn 4–5×** to orient the chains. Subsequent heat-setting fixes properties.

*(A flow diagram matching Section 4.1 above should accompany the answer. 1 mark reserved for the flow diagram.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

Given: $\overline{M}_n = 19{,}500$ g/mol; $M_0 = 226$ g/mol; $r = 0.98$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\overline{DP}_n$:**
$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} = \frac{19{,}500}{226} = 86.28$$

**Step 2 — Apply the non-stoichiometric Carothers equation:**
$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \frac{1+0.98}{1+0.98 - 2(0.98)\,p} = \frac{1.98}{1.98 - 1.96\,p}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $p$:**
$$86.28\,(1.98 - 1.96\,p) = 1.98$$
$$170.84 - 169.11\,p = 1.98$$
$$169.11\,p = 168.86$$
$$p = \frac{168.86}{169.11} = 0.9985$$

**Step 4 — Express as percentage:**
$$p = 99.85\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to **subtract water** when calculating $M_0$ (HMDA + Adipic − 18). The value 226 g/mol already includes this subtraction; if a student uses the sum (262) the answer halves.
> 2. Inverting the Carothers equation — it should be $\overline{DP}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$, **not** $(1+r-2rp)/(1+r)$.
> 3. Reporting $p$ as a fraction (0.9985) without converting to % (99.85 %) — loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. Confusing $r$ definition: $r$ = minority/majority = 0.98 (i.e., amine in *excess* of acid → reverse of the usual industrial practice). Either convention is acceptable if used consistently.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **ring-opening polymerisation of ε-caprolactam** to give Nylon 6. Discuss the role of water as an initiator, the function of the **VK (vertical kneader) tube reactor**, and the spinning method used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Acrylic fibre is produced by **free-radical polymerisation of acrylonitrile**. With the help of a labelled flow diagram, describe the production of acrylonitrile from propylene, the polymerisation step, and the **wet-spinning** process. Mention **two major by-products** of the ammoxidation reaction and how they are handled. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline (Key Points)

**(a) Ring-opening polymerisation of ε-caprolactam (7 marks)**

- **Mechanism — Hydrolytic ROP:** Water opens caprolactam to give aminocaproic acid, which then attacks another caprolactam ring. Equilibrium: linear polymer + caprolactam ↔ chain-extended polymer; typically 8–10 % caprolactam remains at equilibrium.
- **Catalyst/initiator:** Water (hydrolytic, used industrially) OR anionic initiators (Na, Grignard) for "fast" RIM Nylon 6.
- **Reactor:** **Vertical Kneader (VK) tube** — a continuous plug-flow reactor with internal agitators that scrapes viscous melt from the walls; residence time 12–24 h; temperature 250–270 °C.
- **Monomer recovery:** The 8–10 % unreacted caprolactam is extracted with hot water and recycled.
- **Spinning:** Melt spinning at 260–280 °C through spinneret; draw ratio 3.5–5×; heat-set at 180–190 °C.

**(b) Acrylic fibre process (7 marks)**

- **Ammoxidation:** Propylene + NH₃ + O₂ → ACN over Bi–P–Mo–O catalyst at 400–460 °C. Equation as in Section 3.5.
- **By-products:**
  * **HCN** — recovered by absorption in alkaline solution, converted to **methacrylamide** or to **NaCN** for gold mining. Modern plants integrate HCN into methyl methacrylate lines.
  * **Acetonitrile** — recovered by distillation, used as a process solvent in pharmaceutical and fine-chemical industries.
- **Polymerisation:** ACN + co-monomers (methyl acrylate ~7 %, itaconic acid/sodium allyl sulphonate ~1–2 %) in DMF/DMSO solution; AIBN initiator; 50–70 °C; conversion ~70–80 %.
- **Wet spinning:** Dope extruded into **DMF–water coagulation bath** (40–60 °C); counter-current solvent recovery; **drawing 6–10× in boiling water/steam**; drying; crimping; cutting to staple. (For flow diagram, mirror Section 4.4.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Question B:**
> 1. **Do NOT write a free-radical mechanism in excessive detail** for PAN — the KTU examiner awards 1 mark for "free-radical / AIBN initiator" and 1 mark for "co-monomers for dyeability". Excessive chain-mechanism writing wastes time.
> 2. **Forgetting the by-products HCN and acetonitrile** — a classic 2-mark item that students skip.
> 3. **Confusing wet spinning with dry spinning** for PAN — PAN *can* be dry-spun but industry overwhelmingly uses **wet spinning**. Always state "wet spinning into DMF–water bath".
> 4. **Not mentioning co-monomers** — pure PAN is undyeable; commercial acrylic fibre always contains 5–10 % neutral co-monomers (methyl acrylate, vinyl acetate) and 1–2 % ionic co-monomers. KTU explicitly tests this in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 30-Second Recall List for Module 4**

- [ ] **Nylon 6** = ring-opening of ε-caprolactam; **no small molecule** released (ring opens).
- [ ] **Nylon 6,6** = HMDA + adipic acid → **H₂O released**; autoclave, 220–280 °C.
- [ ] **PET** = EG + DMT (CH₃OH released) **OR** EG + PTA (H₂O released); two-step: ester interchange then polycondensation; **Sb₂O₃** catalyst in finisher.
- [ ] **PAN** = acrylonitrile from **propylene + NH₃ + O₂ (Sohio)**; free-radical polymerisation; **wet-spun from DMF**.
- [ ] **Repeat-unit masses:** Nylon 6 ≈ 113; Nylon 6,6 ≈ 226; PET ≈ 192; AN ≈ 53.
- [ ] **Carothers equation** — stoichiometric: $\overline{DP}_n = 1/(1-p)$. **Non-stoichiometric:** $\overline{DP}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$.
- [ ] To get $\overline{M}_n \approx 20{,}000$ g/mol, conversion must exceed **99 %** — that is why finishing reactors / SSP are needed.
- [ ] **Slight stoichiometric excess** of one monomer **halves** the $\overline{DP}_n$ — this is how molecular weight is *deliberately controlled*.
- [ ] All four polymers derive from **petrochemical feedstocks**: benzene / cyclohexane / p-xylene / propylene — coming from the **reformer, FCC, and steam cracker** of a petroleum refinery.
- [ ] **Spinning methods:** Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, PET → **melt spinning**; PAN → **wet spinning** (occasionally dry).
- [ ] **Drawing** (orientation) is essential to develop fibre tenacity — typically 4–5× for nylons, 6–10× for acrylics.
- [ ] Acrylic fibre is **always a copolymer** (pure PAN is hard to dye); common co-monomers: methyl acrylate, vinyl acetate, sodium allyl sulphonate.
- [ ] **By-products of Sohio process:** HCN (recovered) + acetonitrile (recovered as solvent).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — OECHT615 Petroleum Refining Technology (KTU 2024 Scheme).**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of phenol formaldehyde resins
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT615 - PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production of phenol formaldehyde resins

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Phenol Formaldehyde Resins (PF Resins)** are the oldest and most commercially significant class of **thermosetting synthetic polymers**, first synthesized by **Leo Baekeland in 1907** (commercialized as **Bakelite**). They are produced by the step-growth polycondensation reaction between **phenol (C₆H₅OH)** and **formaldehyde (HCHO)** in the presence of an acidic or basic catalyst.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** PF resins are condensation polymers formed by the reaction of phenolic compounds with aldehydes (predominantly formaldehyde), yielding three-dimensional cross-linked network structures upon curing, characterized by high thermal stability, chemical resistance, and mechanical strength.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of Phenol Formaldehyde resin production like **baking a cake that hardens permanently**:

- The **flour and eggs** (phenol + formaldehyde) are the raw ingredients.
- The **baking powder or yeast** is your **catalyst** (acid for Novolac, base for Resol).
- As you mix and bake, the ingredients **link together** in a permanent web (cross-linking).
- Once baked, you **cannot melt it back** into batter — it's a **thermoset** (irreversible), unlike a chocolate bar (thermoplastic) which can be re-melted.

This is why PF resins are used in **electric switches, brake linings, and pan handles** — they must not soften under heat.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Resin Type | Catalyst | P:F Molar Ratio | Storage | Cure |
|------------|----------|-----------------|---------|------|
| **Novolac** | Acidic (HCl, Oxalic, H₂SO₄) | < 1 : 1 (excess phenol) | Stable, thermoplastic | Requires hardener (HMTA) |
| **Resol** | Basic (NaOH, NH₃) | > 1 : 1 (excess formaldehyde) | Self-curing (limited pot life) | Heat-cured directly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** Novolac resins are **linear/branched** and require a second-stage curing with **hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA)**, while Resol resins are **pre-cross-linked** and cure directly upon heating.

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Chemical Metrics

- **Density of cured PF resin:** **1.24 – 1.32 g/cm³**
- **Service temperature range:** **−80 °C to +200 °C** (continuous), up to **300 °C** (short term)
- **Formaldehyde boiling point:** **−19 °C** (handled as 37% aqueous solution called **formalin**)
- **Phenol melting point:** **40.5 °C**, boiling point: **181.7 °C**
- **Heat deflection temperature:** **150 – 175 °C**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reaction Chemistry — The Core Mechanism

The PF reaction proceeds in **two fundamental stages**:

### Stage 1: Methylolation (Addition Reaction)
Formaldehyde adds to the *ortho* and *para* positions of phenol, forming **hydroxymethylphenols (methylol phenols)**.

$$\text{Phenol} + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{Hydroxymethyl Phenol}$$

Specific intermediates:
- **2-Hydroxymethylphenol** (*ortho*-methylol phenol)
- **4-Hydroxymethylphenol** (*para*-methylol phenol)
- **2,4-Bis(hydroxymethyl)phenol** (both positions)
- **2,4,6-Tris(hydroxymethyl)phenol** (all three positions — only with excess HCHO)

### Stage 2: Condensation (Ether and Methylene Bridge Formation)

Methylol groups react with each other to form:
- **Methylene bridges:** $\text{–CH}_2\text{–}$ (dominant under acidic conditions)
- **Dimethylene ether bridges:** $\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–}$ (dominant under basic conditions)

These bridges create the **cross-linked 3D network** responsible for the rigid, infusible character of cured PF resin.

## 2.2 Process Steps — Detailed Walkthrough

### Step 1: Resin Reactor Charging
- **Phenol**, **formalin (37% HCHO)**, and **catalyst** are charged into a **stirred jacketed reactor** (typically stainless steel or glass-lined).
- Reactor equipped with **reflux condenser**, **agitator**, **thermometer**, and **sampling port**.

### Step 2: Reaction Stage
- Temperature controlled at **70 – 100 °C** for Resol (base-catalyzed) or **80 – 100 °C** for Novolac (acid-catalyzed).
- Exothermic reaction — temperature controlled via **cooling water circulation** in jacket.
- Reaction time: **2 – 6 hours** depending on desired molecular weight.

### Step 3: Neutralization
- For Novolac: acid is neutralized with **NaOH or Ca(OH)₂**.
- For Resol: base may be partially neutralized with **acetic or formic acid** to stabilize pH.
- pH adjusted to **6.5 – 7.5** for stability.

### Step 4: Dehydration (Water Removal)
- Water from formalin and condensation is removed under **vacuum (50 – 100 mmHg)** at **80 – 120 °C**.
- Achieved in a **vacuum dehydrator** or directly in reactor.

### Step 5: Finishing
- Molten resin is cooled, flaked, ground, and sieved.
- For Resol: resin is typically supplied as **liquid solution (50 – 70% solids)**.
- For Novolac: supplied as **solid flakes/granules** to be compounded later with HMTA.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol/Formula | Typical Value | Unit |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|------|
| Molar ratio (Novolac) | $P/F$ | 0.75 – 0.90 | mol/mol |
| Molar ratio (Resol) | $F/P$ | 1.2 – 3.0 | mol/mol |
| Reaction temperature | $T$ | 80 – 100 | °C |
| Reaction time | $t$ | 2 – 6 | h |
| Catalyst (acid) | — | 0.1 – 2.0 | wt% of phenol |
| Catalyst (base) | — | 0.5 – 3.0 | wt% of phenol |
| Degree of polymerization | $\overline{DP}$ | $\dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | — |
| Carothers equation (P:F <1) | $\overline{X_n}$ | $\dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | — |
| Water removal | vacuum | 50 – 100 | mmHg |
| pH (final resin) | — | 6.5 – 7.5 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **Carothers equation** is the **most asked formula** for PF resin calculations — especially in problems involving gel point and average degree of polymerization.

## 2.4 Catalysis — Why It Matters

- **Acid catalysts** (HCl, H₂SO₄, oxalic acid, p-toluenesulfonic acid): Favor **methylene bridge** formation → linear/branched **Novolac**.
- **Base catalysts** (NaOH, KOH, NH₃, Ba(OH)₂): Favor **methylol group** formation → branched **Resol** that cures on heating.
- **Oxalic acid** is preferred over HCl in industry because it does not corrode reactors.

## 2.5 Industrial Applications

| Application | Resin Used | Reason |
|-------------|-----------|--------|
| Molding compounds (Bakelite) | Novolac + HMTA | High heat resistance |
| Plywood adhesives | Resol | Water resistance, fast cure |
| Laminates (circuit boards) | Resol | Electrical insulation |
| Foundry sand binders | Resol | High strength |
| Coatings & varnishes | Resol | Chemical resistance |
| Brake linings/clutch pads | Novolac + fillers | Friction, heat stability |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Carothers Equation for Novolac (P:F Molar Ratio < 1)

The **Carothers equation** relates the **number-average degree of polymerization** ($\overline{X_n}$) to the **stoichiometric ratio (r)** and the **extent of reaction (p)**.

For a system with $N_A$ molecules of phenol and $N_B$ molecules of formaldehyde, define:

$$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} \quad \text{(assuming } N_A < N_B \text{ for Novolac)}$$

The total number of molecules initially:

$$N_0 = N_A + N_B$$

At extent of reaction $p$, the number of molecules remaining:

$$N = N_A(1-p) + N_B - pN_A = N_A + N_B - pN_A = N_0 - pN_A$$

Therefore, the number-average degree of polymerization:

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A + N_B - pN_A}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $N_B$:

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{r + 1}{r + 1 - pr} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

The factor of 2 arises because **each methylene bridge consumes 2 reactive sites**.

### Solved Numerical Example (KTU Style — 7 Marks)

**Problem:** A Novolac resin is produced with **Phenol : Formaldehyde = 5 : 6 (mol)**. Find $\overline{X_n}$ at **p = 0.85**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify stoichiometric ratio:
$$r = \frac{N_{\text{Phenol}}}{N_{\text{HCHO}}} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833$$

Step 2 — Apply Carothers equation:
$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \frac{1+0.833}{1+0.833-2(0.833)(0.85)}$$

Step 3 — Compute denominator:
$$1 + 0.833 - 2(0.708) = 1.833 - 1.416 = 0.417$$

Step 4 — Final calculation:
$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{1.833}{0.417} \approx 4.40$$

**Answer:** $\overline{X_n} \approx 4.4$ — a low-molecular-weight oligomer typical of Novolac.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gel Point Prediction

The **gel point** ($p_c$) is the critical extent of reaction beyond which an infinite 3D network forms (i.e., the resin becomes insoluble and infusible). For PF systems, the Flory-Stockmayer condition gives:

$$p_c = \frac{1}{[r(f-2)+2]^{1/2}}$$

where $f$ is the **average functionality** of phenol (= 3, due to 2 ortho + 1 para positions) for reaction with difunctional HCHO.

For Novolac with $r = 1$ (P = F):
$$p_c = \frac{1}{[(1)(3-2)+2]^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577$$

This means **gelation occurs at ~58% conversion** for equimolar Novolac — a critical design parameter.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Effects

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on $\overline{X_n}$ | Effect on Cure Speed | Effect on Final Properties |
|-----------|--------------------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Formaldehyde (F) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Faster | More cross-links, brittler |
| Phenol (P) | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Slower | Tougher, more flexible |
| Acid catalyst | Modest ↑ | Faster cure (with HMTA) | Darker color |
| Base catalyst | ↑ Significantly | Moderate | Better mechanical strength |
| Reaction temperature | ↑ Increases | ↑ Faster | Risk of premature gelation |
| Reaction time | ↑ Increases | — | Higher viscosity, lower flow |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phenol–Formaldehyde Resin Production Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phenol Storage Tank] --> C[Stirred Jacketed Reactor]
    B[Formalin 37 percent Tank] --> C
    D[Catalyst Acid or Base] --> C
    C --> E[Reflux Condensation Stage 80 to 100 C]
    E --> F[Neutralization Tank pH 6.5 to 7.5]
    F --> G[Vacuum Dehydrator 50 to 100 mmHg]
    G --> H{Novolac or Resol}
    H -->|Novolac| I[Flaker and Grinder]
    H -->|Resol| J[Resin Solution Storage]
    I --> K[Compounding with HMTA and Fillers]
    J --> L[Direct Shipping as Liquid Resin]
    K --> M[Molding Press 150 to 180 C and 20 to 30 MPa]
    L --> N[Customer Industries Plywood Adhesives Coatings]
    M --> O[Finished PF Molded Articles Bakelite]
```

## 4.2 Reaction Pathway Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Phenol C6H5OH] -->|Stage 1 Methylolation| Q[Hydroxymethyl Phenols Ortho and Para]
    Q -->|Stage 2a Acid Catalyst| R[Methylene Bridge CH2 Formation]
    Q -->|Stage 2b Base Catalyst| S[Dimethylene Ether Bridge CH2 O CH2 Formation]
    R --> T[Novolac Linear Branched Polymer]
    S --> U[Resol Branched Prepolymer]
    T -->|Add HMTA and Heat| V[Cured 3D Crosslinked Network]
    U -->|Direct Heat Curing| V
    V --> W[Final Thermoset PF Resin Product]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Novolac vs Resol Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NOV[NOVOLAC PROCESS Acid Catalyzed]
        NA[Phenol Excess] --> NB[Mix with Formaldehyde]
        NB --> NC[Add Acid Catalyst HCl or Oxalic]
        NC --> ND[React at 90 C for 3 to 5 h]
        ND --> NE[Neutralize with NaOH]
        NE --> NF[Vacuum Dehydrate]
        NF --> NG[Solid Novolac Flakes]
        NG --> NH[Compound with HMTA 8 to 15 percent]
        NH --> NI[Mold and Cure at 160 C]
    end

    subgraph RES[RESOL PROCESS Base Catalyzed]
        RA[Formaldehyde Excess] --> RB[Mix with Phenol]
        RB --> RC[Add Base Catalyst NaOH or NH3]
        RC --> RD[React at 80 C for 2 to 4 h]
        RD --> RE[Adjust pH to 7]
        RE --> RF[Vacuum Concentrate to 60 percent Solids]
        RF --> RG[Liquid Resol Resin]
        RG --> RH[Cure Directly at 130 to 150 C]
    end
```
