# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Novolac and Resol resins.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Novolac | Resol |
|---------|---------|-------|
| Catalyst | Acidic | Basic |
| Molar ratio (P:F) | > 1 (excess phenol) | < 1 (excess HCHO) |
| Polymer structure | Linear/branched | Branched with methylol groups |
| Stability | Stable, thermoplastic | Self-curing (limited shelf life) |
| Curing agent | Requires HMTA | Cures on heating alone |
| Applications | Molding compounds | Adhesives, laminates |

**(3 marks: 1 for each distinguishing feature, full table = 3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is phenol-formaldehyde resin classified as a thermosetting polymer?**

**Model Answer:**
PF resin undergoes irreversible cross-linking upon heating via methylene and ether bridges between phenolic rings. Once cured, the 3D network **cannot be softened or remelted** by reheating — it decomposes instead. This **irreversible curing behavior** is the defining feature of a **thermoset**, distinguishing it from thermoplastics which can be repeatedly melted. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `(a)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the production process of Novolac phenol-formaldehyde resin with a neat flow diagram. List its major industrial applications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Raw Material Preparation (1 mark)**
- Phenol (molten at 45 °C) and formalin (37% aqueous HCHO) are charged in the molar ratio **P:F = 1 : 0.8 to 0.9**.
- Acid catalyst (oxalic acid or HCl) added at **0.1 – 2.0 wt%** of phenol.

**Step 2 — Reaction (2 marks)**
- Charged into a **stirred jacketed stainless-steel reactor** fitted with reflux condenser.
- Heated to **80 – 100 °C** for **3 – 5 hours** under reflux.
- Phenol and formaldehyde undergo **methylolation** followed by **condensation** to form linear/branched Novolac with methylene bridges.

**Step 3 — Neutralization and Dehydration (2 marks)**
- Acid is neutralized with **NaOH** to pH 6.5 – 7.5.
- Water removed under **vacuum (50 – 100 mmHg)** at **80 – 120 °C**.
- Molten resin discharged.

**Step 4 — Finishing (1 mark)**
- Cooled on a **flaker belt**, ground, and sieved into granules.
- Packaged and shipped.

**Step 5 — Industrial Applications (1 mark)**
- Molding compounds (with HMTA + wood flour/fillers)
- Brake linings and clutch facings
- Grinding wheels (abrasive binder)
- Electrical switchgear components

**(Flow Diagram carries 2 marks if included)**

### Question A `(b)` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(b) Explain the role of hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) in curing Novolac resins. Write the chemical reactions involved.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of HMTA (3 marks):**
Novolac resin lacks sufficient reactive methylol groups to self-cure because of the excess phenol used in synthesis. **HMTA (hexamine, (CH₂)₆N₄)** acts as a **latent hardener**:
1. Decomposes on heating (>120 °C) to release **formaldehyde and ammonia**.
2. The released formaldehyde creates additional **methylol groups** on Novolac chains.
3. These methylol groups undergo condensation → cross-linking → rigid 3D network.
4. Typical dosage: **8 – 15 wt%** of Novolac resin.

**Chemical Reactions (4 marks):**

$$(CH_2)_6N_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 6 HCHO + 4 NH_3$$

$$NH_3 + HCHO \longrightarrow \text{Amines (CH}_2=\text{NH, etc.)}$$

$$\text{Resin–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{Resin–CH}_2\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–Resin (ether bridge)}$$

$$\text{Resin–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{H–Resin} \longrightarrow \text{Resin–CH}_2\text{–Resin (methylene bridge)} + H_2O$$

The methylene and ether bridges cross-link adjacent Novolac chains, producing the final thermoset structure.

---

### Question B `(a)` `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a) With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the industrial manufacture of Resol-type phenol-formaldehyde resin.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Charge Preparation (1 mark)**
- Phenol and formaldehyde (molar ratio **F:P = 1.2 – 3.0**, excess HCHO) loaded into a jacketed reactor.
- Base catalyst (NaOH or Ba(OH)₂) added at **0.5 – 3.0 wt%**.

**Step 2 — Reaction (2 marks)**
- Heated to **70 – 100 °C** under reflux for **2 – 4 hours**.
- Basic conditions favor **methylol group** formation, producing *o*- and *p*-hydroxymethyl phenols and their condensation products.
- Temperature strictly controlled due to exothermic nature.

**Step 3 — pH Adjustment and Concentration (2 marks)**
- Resin partially neutralized with **acetic acid or lactic acid** to pH 7 – 8.
- Vacuum dehydration at **50 – 80 mmHg**, **70 – 90 °C** to concentrate to **50 – 70% solids**.

**Step 4 — Storage and Shipping (1 mark)**
- Cooled to **25 – 30 °C** and stored in sealed drums.
- **Limited shelf life (3 – 6 months)** because Resol can self-cure at room temperature.

**Step 5 — Flow Diagram (1 mark)**
As shown in SECTION_4, the flow is: Reactors → Reflux → Vacuum Dehydration → Cooling → Storage.

### Question B `(b)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(b) The number-average degree of polymerization of a Novolac resin is found to be 5.5 when the extent of reaction is 0.80. Calculate the P:F molar ratio used.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$\overline{X_n} = 5.5$, $p = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Write Carothers Equation (2 marks)**
$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks)**
$$5.5 = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2r(0.80)} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-1.6r} = \frac{1+r}{1-0.6r}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply (1 mark)**
$$5.5(1-0.6r) = 1+r$$
$$5.5 - 3.3r = 1 + r$$
$$5.5 - 1 = r + 3.3r$$
$$4.5 = 4.3r$$

**Step 4 — Solve for r (1 mark)**
$$r = \frac{4.5}{4.3} = 1.0465$$

**Step 5 — Interpret (1 mark)**
Since $r = N_P / N_F = 1.047 > 1$, the system has **excess phenol**, confirming it is indeed a **Novolac** formulation. The P:F molar ratio is approximately **1.05 : 1**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing P:F vs F:P ratios** — Always state clearly whether phenol or formaldehyde is in excess. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Carothers equation** — The denominator is $(1+r-2rp)$, NOT $(1+r-rp)$. This is the most common error.
> 3. **Omitting the flow diagram in production questions** — A neat labeled flow diagram is worth **2 marks** in any 7-mark question on production.
> 4. **Not mentioning HMTA** when discussing Novolac curing — Failing to state that Novolac requires HMTA (8–15 wt%) for curing costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing "Bakelite" without explanation** — Bakelite is a brand name; explain that it is PF resin + wood flour + HMTA molded under heat and pressure.
> 6. **Ignoring temperature and pressure** — Always state the curing conditions (**150 – 180 °C, 20 – 30 MPa**) for completeness.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ PF resins are the **first fully synthetic thermoset plastics** (Baekeland, 1907).
- ✅ **Novolac = Acid catalyst + Excess phenol**; **Resol = Base catalyst + Excess formaldehyde**.
- ✅ **Novolac** requires **HMTA** (8 – 15 wt%) for curing; **Resol** self-cures on heating.
- ✅ Key reaction stages: **Methylolation → Condensation → Cross-linking**.
- ✅ Methylene bridge ($\text{–CH}_2\text{–}$) dominates in acid catalysis; ether bridge dominates in base catalysis.
- ✅ **Carothers equation:** $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ — most important formula for KTU.
- ✅ **Gel point for equimolar PF:** $p_c = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$.
- ✅ Production steps: **Reaction → Neutralization → Vacuum Dehydration → Finishing**.
- ✅ Major applications: **Molding compounds, plywood adhesives, laminates, brake linings, foundry binders**.
- ✅ Cured PF service temperature: up to **200 °C** continuously; excellent flame and chemical resistance.
- ✅ Phenol:Formaldehyde **molar ratio decides the resin type** — never confuse the two systems in numerical problems.
